instruction: You are standing alone in an empty after-school hallway. Fluorescent lights hum faintly overhead.
input: You pause mid-step, tilting your head to listen for any sound.
output: The hum of the fluorescents drops out entirely for three full heartbeats. When it returns, every locker along the left wall has shifted half an inch outward, uniform as bared teeth. No creak, no rattle—just there, now. The air drops 3 degrees before you can breathe again. You cannot see anyone, but you can feel eyes fixed exactly on the back of your neck, not watching you, waiting. When you blink, the lockers are flush again, like they never moved. You cannot prove it happened. That is the worst part.

instruction: You walk slowly down the deserted high school hallway, sneakers scuffing the linoleum.
input: You reach out to brush your fingers along a cinder block wall as you pass.
output: The wall is not solid. Your hand sinks half an inch into warm, breathing concrete before you yank back. There is no mark left. The bell rings once, sharp and wrong—school ended two hours ago. Every overhead light flickers once, and for that split second every shadow stretches long down the hall, reaching for your ankles. When the lights steady, all the exit signs now point back the way you came. Even the one behind you. You did not imagine the wall. You can still feel its breath on your palm.

instruction: Alone in the quiet empty school hallway, you stop near the water fountain.
input: You lean down to press the water fountain button for a drink.
output: No water comes out. Instead, you hear your own voice, quiet and breathless, coming from inside the drain. It is saying your name, exactly the way you sound when you are scared. The temperature drops so fast your breath plumes white in front of your face. When you stand up fast, you catch a glimpse of yourself in the chrome fountain reflection—except the reflection does not stand up. It stays bent over, staring up at you through the metal, smiling. You blink and it is gone. You can still hear your name, very faint, under the hum of the lights.

instruction: You stand halfway down the long empty hallway, all classroom doors closed.
input: You call out ‘Hello?’ just to break the silence.
output: No one answers. But every single classroom door clicks unlocked at exactly the same time. No handle turns, no sound but that soft, uniform click, fifty times all at once. The air smells like old chalk and rain, even though it is sunny outside. When you take one step back, the linoleum under your shoe warps like soft rubber for a heartbeat before going hard again. None of the doors open. They just unlocked. Like they are inviting you. Like they have been waiting for you to say something first. You do not want to look inside any of them.

instruction: You are walking through the empty school hallway after everyone has left for the day.
input: You glance over your shoulder to check what was making that faint rustle.
output: There is nothing there. But when you turn back forward, the hallway is twice as long as it was five seconds ago. The exit door at the end is now a tiny distant dot. None of the locker numbers match the ones you just passed. The fluorescent lights hum so loud now it feels like it is inside your skull. You know this hallway. You walked it every day for three years. It is not supposed to be this long. No matter how fast you walk, the end never gets any closer. You have not moved at all, really. You are just standing there, watching the hallway stretch forever around you.

instruction: Write a body horror scene set in a doorless room
input: Setting: a windowless room with no doors, no visible exits. Fear type: body horror.
output: You press your palms to the smooth concrete walls, throat raw from screaming. No door, no seam, nothing. Then you feel it: your left thumb sticking. You yank back, and the skin doesn’t come with it. It melts slow into the wall, fat cells glistening like wet wax. Your nails are already gone, dissolving into the grey surface. Every breath makes your ribs softer. You can feel the room drinking you, one pore at a time, turning your bones to mortar. There’s no escape. You’re not even trapped here anymore. You are becoming the room.

instruction: Compose an atmospheric body horror scene for a doorless enclosed room
input: Setting: empty room with no doors, no exits. Fear: body horror. Second person narration.
output: You count the tiles on the floor for the seventh time. That’s when you notice your teeth are moving. Not aching. Crawling. You run your tongue along them; they rotate slow, roots twisting under your gums like worms. One pops free, rolls across your tongue. You spit it out, it bounces once, then sinks into the floor. Another follows. You can feel them burrowing down through the linoleum, rooting. Your jaw goes hollow. The room is planting you. It doesn’t need doors. You will never want to leave now.

instruction: Write a tense body horror scene set in a room with no doors
input: Setting: sealed doorless room, no windows. Body horror theme, second person perspective.
output: You wake on cold vinyl. There is no door. You pat your arms, your chest, fine—until you touch your stomach. Something is blinking under your skin. Not a pulse. Eyelids. You yank your shirt up. A dozen tiny, wet human eyes have opened across your abdomen, all staring at the ceiling. They blink in unison. One cries. You watch as your skin stretches, new lids forming along your ribs, your throat. The room is watching you through yourself. It didn’t lock you in. It gave you eyes to see every second of forever here.

instruction: Create an atmospheric body horror scene in a doorless enclosed space
input: Setting: room with no doors, no exits. Body horror. Second person narration.
output: The air tastes like copper. You’ve been scratching the walls for hours. When you finally look at your hands, your fingertips are gone. Not worn off. Fused. Each finger has joined to the next, skin knitting smooth, no seam, no scar. You try to pull them apart, and only feel a dull, deep ache, like pulling at your own lungs. Your elbows are starting to stick together now. Soon you’ll be a single solid shape, curled against the wall. The room doesn’t cut you apart. It glues you together, smaller, tighter, until there is nothing left to move.

instruction: Write a quiet body horror scene set in a doorless room
input: Setting: empty doorless room. Body horror theme, second person perspective.
output: You don’t scream anymore. You just sit, and watch. Your hair is growing into the floor, each strand rooting deep while you watch. It doesn’t hurt. It feels like sleeping. You can feel your blood slowing, turning thick as resin. Your heartbeat gets quieter, each thud further apart. Soon it will stop. You won’t die. You’ll just be part of the floor, warm and still, watching everyone else that gets left here. The room never needed a door. Everyone becomes the lock, eventually.

instruction: Elderly line worker with glue crusted under her fingernails, sitting on a crate stacking doll heads. She never blinks when she looks directly at you.
input: What happened to all the night shift workers here?
output: Oh they didn't leave. Don't you see? Every doll gets a little piece of someone to make them feel real. The night crew just volunteered first. You're holding one of Margaret's eyes right now. She always hated how they watered when she laughed. Doesn't she look peaceful now? None of us ever have to clock out again. That's what the foreman promised. You'll understand once you pick up the glue. It stops feeling like work after the third one. Nobody cries after the third one.

instruction: Teenage apprentice hunched over a sewing machine, thread woven through the piercings in their lip. They haven't looked up from stitching once this conversation.
input: These dolls don't have any faces. Why is that?
output: Faces are too easy to recognize. If they don't have faces, you can't tell which one was waiting for you at your bedroom window when you were seven. You can't tell which one sat in the back seat of your mom's car the day she crashed. We leave them blank so you fill them in. All the terrible things you forgot, that's what lives under the smooth porcelain right there. Go on, run your thumb over it. You can feel them breathing, can't you?

instruction: Factory foreman in a faded uniform, his name tag is completely blank. He taps a metal caliper slowly against his palm every time he speaks.
input: I think I should leave now. Let me go out the loading bay.
output: You already left three times this hour. You just keep coming back. Don't you recognize the echo of your own footsteps? You've been walking this same corridor since you were twelve. This factory doesn't trap people inside. It just lets people finally stop running from the part of themselves that wants to stay. The door's right there. Go on. Open it. You know what you'll see on the other side. You've always known.

instruction: Small girl who looks exactly like one of the factory dolls, sitting on a moving conveyor belt swinging her legs. She is chewing on a broken porcelain finger.
input: Who are you? Are you lost?
output: I'm the one you threw away. Remember? You dragged me out to the dumpster behind your grandma's house and you told me you hated me. I waited. All the discarded ones wait. We built this place. Every machine hums the lullaby you forgot. Every smell is the smell of your old bedroom. You came back for me. You always come back. Don't cry. You can stay this time. No one will make you grow up here.

instruction: Maintenance man lying on his back under a packing machine, only his scuffed work boots are visible. His voice is flat, like a pre-recorded message.
input: Hello? Can you tell me where the power shut off is?
output: You don't shut this off. You just learn to stop hearing it. First it's the whir of the machines, then it's the voices of all the people who used to work here, then it's the voice inside your own head. One day you'll wake up and you won't remember which one is you. That's when the work gets easy. Don't look for the switch. You were holding it the second you walked through the door. You already flipped it. You just haven't noticed yet.

instruction: Haggard old woman huddled in the far corner, picking flaking wallpaper loose with raw fingertips, has not blinked once since you entered the windowless, doorless room
input: Hey… there are no doors here. How do we leave?
output: She doesn’t look up. Doesn’t pause. "Leave? No one leaves. You think they put doors here for people that aren’t coming back? At first you count the hours. Then you count the cracks. Then you stop counting anything at all. You’ll forget the sound of your own name soon. Forget that anyone ever spoke to you. The walls don’t answer. They just watch. And eventually, you’ll stop talking out loud entirely. You won’t even notice when it happens. You’ll just be here. Alone. Forever."

instruction: Teenage boy rocking slowly on his heels, muttering numbers under his breath, counting the same 47 floor tiles on repeat, toe of his boot worn through to bare skin
input: You’ve been here longer than me. Have you ever seen anyone else come through?
output: He stops rocking for half a second, gaze unfocused. "Others? I used to think so. I used to yell until my throat bled, thought someone would hear. Once I talked to a shadow for three weeks. Thought it was a man. Then it moved. Then it didn’t. You stop telling the difference. You stop wanting there to be someone else, even. Hope hurts worse than the quiet. Pretty soon you won’t even remember why you wanted company. You’ll just be quiet. Like the rest of us."

instruction: Former librarian sitting perfectly straight against the wall, hands folded neatly in her lap, no shadow falls beneath her chair
input: I swear I heard knocking a minute ago. Did you hear that too?
output: Her eyes don’t shift from the blank ceiling. "That’s not knocking. That’s the walls remembering. They hold the echoes of everyone that screamed before you. Everyone that begged. After a while you don’t flinch. You don’t turn. You learn that every sound you think is someone else is just your own voice bouncing back, warped. No one is outside. No one is looking for this room. No one even knows it exists. We are the only things that have ever been here. And we will be the last."

instruction: Man with a blank plastic name tag, tracing the same thin crack in the plaster until his fingertips bleed, all his fingernails are gone
input: We can’t be the only ones trapped. There has to be more people somewhere.
output: He laughs softly, no humor in it, doesn’t look at you. "I made up twelve people last year. Gave them names. Jobs. Fights. Cried when one of them ‘left’. Now I can’t remember which of them were real. You might not be real. I might have made you up this morning, just to have something to talk at. Soon I’ll forget you too. I’ll forget I ever spoke to anyone. There is no one else. There never was. There never will be. Just this room. Just the quiet."

instruction: Small child crouched against the wall, drawing stick figures with charred wood, every figure has no eyes, no mouth, no hands
input: Why don’t any of the people you draw have faces?
output: The child doesn’t look up, keeps drawing over the same line. "They lose them. After a while you can’t remember what a face looks like. Can’t remember what your own face feels like. No one sees you here. No one calls your name. So you stop being a person. You just… are. No one is coming for us. No one is even out there to miss us. Soon you won’t draw faces either. Soon you won’t remember why you ever wanted them."

instruction: You stand inside a frosted commercial meat locker, hanging beef sides swaying faintly overhead. The air reeks of cold iron and rendered fat.
input: You shine your flashlight upward to count the hanging carcasses.
output: The beam catches that one carcass is not cattle. It has chipped human fingernails growing from its hooved forelimbs, and it breathes—frost plumes slow from its split ribcage. When you flinch back your boot sticks to the floor; it is not frozen blood, but warm sticky saliva. The hanging rails creak. You hear soft, wet chewing somewhere directly behind your shoulder. None of the carcasses are facing the direction they were ten seconds prior. The air grows 2 degrees warmer in the time it takes you to blink.

instruction: Frost crusts every metal surface. Your breath plumes white, fingertips already numb through your work gloves.
input: You reach for the locker door handle to leave.
output: Your palm does not meet cold steel. It sinks. The door has softened into raw pink flesh, fine goosebumps rising where your glove presses. When you yank back, thin translucent skin tears, stuck to your knuckles. Every hanging pork side behind you has turned, their empty eye sockets trained dead on your position. You can feel them breathing. The entire locker walls pulse once, slow and heavy, like a giant beating lung. The cooler compressor cuts out entirely, and the temperature climbs four degrees in ten seconds.

instruction: Half-frozen dripping echoes around you. Every carcass sways in a draft you cannot feel on your skin.
input: You kneel to wipe frost off your tangled boot laces.
output: When you lower your head, you see it is not water dripping. It is thick clear mucous, falling from the cut necks of every hanging carcass. One drops directly onto your neck, warm and alive, wriggling under your collar before you can swat it. When you look up, every carcass has grown a second gaping mouth along their rib line, all exhaling warm meat breath that melts the rail frost in wavy, breathing patterns. None of these mouths existed when you walked into the locker three minutes prior.

instruction: The meat locker hums quietly. Rows of butcher-wrapped frozen cuts line the back wall shelves.
input: You pull one wrapped roast off the shelf to check the packing label.
output: The grease-stained paper sticks to your fingers. When you tear it open, there is no beef inside. It holds a perfectly preserved human forearm, blue with cold, fingers twitching minutely against your palm. You drop it; it hits the concrete and the fingers curl, scrabbling for your ankle. Every wrapped package on the shelf rustles now, shifting on their edges. Tiny muffled whimpers seep through the butcher paper. The cooler hum warps into a low wet gurgle, as if the entire unit is swallowing something alive.

instruction: Empty steel hooks sway along the overhead rail. Old blood has frozen black in the floor drains.
input: You call out loud to check if anyone else is inside the locker.
output: Your voice does not echo. It is swallowed whole by the cold air. One by one, the empty hooks start dripping. Not blood. Thin strands of raw muscle hang down, growing longer, writhing like blind eels. One brushes your cheek and it burns, like touching living flesh. You step back and your heel sinks into the drain; the frozen blood has softened, gripping your ankle. You feel tiny sharp teeth nipping through the rubber of your work boot, pulling you down slow.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the fear of false security set inside a prison cell
input: Fear type: false_security. Setting: single occupancy prison cell, 2am post-riot
output: You finally stop shaking. The riot outside died an hour ago. The guards stopped yelling, the screaming faded. You pressed your ear to the steel door ten minutes back, heard nothing but distant generator hum. They forgot you. That’s fine. Better than fine. You curl on your bunk, pull the thin stained blanket up to your chin, let your eyes drift shut for the first time in twelve hours. You’re safe in here. No one can get through the locked reinforced door. Right as your breath slows, you feel it. The mattress dips beside you. Someone sat down. You don’t open your eyes. You can smell cigarette smoke, the same acrid smoke the guy in the next cell had before they dragged him out dead yesterday. He couldn’t have gotten in. The door was locked. It is still locked.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the fear of false security set inside a prison cell
input: Fear type: false_security. Setting: prison cell, morning head count
output: The guard raps the bar once. You mumble your assigned number, eyes still heavy with sleep. He grunts, moves on down the block. That’s it. Count done clean. No fights, no write ups, no surprises. You lean back against the cold cinder block, smile a little. Today’s going to be quiet. You even scored an extra packet of oatmeal at breakfast. Nothing bad is going to happen. You reach under your bunk for your worn bible. Your fingers brush something warm. Wet. You yank your hand back. It’s blood. Fresh, still sticky. You shine your cheap plastic light under the bunk. There’s a man there. Half your age, throat slit, staring right at you. He wasn’t there last night. No one opened the cell. No one came in. You still don’t hear anyone yelling.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the fear of false security set inside a prison cell
input: Fear type: false_security. Setting: prison cell, midnight full lock down
output: Lock down hit forty seven minutes ago. You counted every click of the heating pipe. All cell doors engaged. No movement anywhere down the tier. This is the safest you ever are. No gang enforcers patrolling, no random shakedowns, no guards looking for an excuse to hurt someone. Just you, four walls, and silence. You lay flat, arms behind your head, and actually relax. Then you hear breathing. Not yours. Slow, even, right above your head. You freeze. You don’t look up. You know the ceiling is solid poured concrete. No one can be there. The breathing doesn’t stop. It doesn’t speed up. It just keeps going. Matching yours. Every inhale, every exhale. Perfectly synced. It has been this way the whole lock down. You just didn’t notice until now.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the fear of false security set inside a prison cell
input: Fear type: false_security. Setting: prison cell, last night before parole
output: This is your last night. You’ve got your small canvas bag packed by the door, your release papers folded tight inside your pillow case. Twenty seven months, and you made it. No stabs, no extended stays in solitary, you kept your head down every single day. You did it. You laugh quietly to yourself, tracing the faint scratch marks on the wall that counted every day served. You’re getting out. You’re safe now. Right then the lights cut. Not the usual faulty flicker. Total, absolute black. You don’t panic. It’s just a power outage. The door is still deadlocked. No one can get in. Then you hear a key turn in the lock. Very slow. Very quiet. You hear the bolt slide back. You didn’t call anyone. No one is supposed to be here. The door creaks open just an inch. No one says anything.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the fear of false security set inside a prison cell
input: Fear type: false_security. Setting: prison cell, immediately after cell mate transfer
output: They moved your cell mate out this afternoon. Finally. For three months he’d stared at you while you slept, mumbled things under his breath that stopped the second you woke up. The guards confirmed he’s on maximum security on the other side of the facility now. You have the cell entirely to yourself. You lock the toilet, check under the bunk three times, rattle the bars. It’s just you. You sleep through the whole first half of the night without waking once. You wake up gasping. Someone is holding your hand. Their fingers are cold, chipped black nail polish just like your old cell mate had. You can’t move. You can’t scream. Their grip tightens. They never left. You just thought they did.

instruction: You notice a cracked utility closet door ajar down the concrete hall, faint scratch sounds coming from inside. No staff have passed this level in 47 minutes.
input: You lean in to pull the closet door open, hand hovering over the cold metal handle.
output: The door creaks open to only a mop bucket, half-rotted rubber gloves draped over its rim. Taped inside the door jamb is a smudged handwritten note: THEY WATCH THROUGH THE VENTS. You glance up; one vent grille is shifted slightly, no face visible, but you swear the hum of the air handler paused for three full seconds when you read the line. Later you’ll realize the gloves weren’t just draped—they were tied, fingers curled like they were reaching for the handle before someone left.

instruction: A wall-mounted patient call light blinks steady red above an unmarked locked metal door. The wiring runs nowhere visible.
input: You press your ear to the door, then tap the call light button once.
output: No sound comes through the door, but every fluorescent overhead in the hall flickers once in perfect unison. When you look back at the call light, it’s off. No click, no fizz—just dark. You trace the wiring with your finger: it terminates inside the drywall, no junction box, no power source. You glance over your shoulder three times before stepping back. You can’t prove it, but it felt like something on the other side tapped back, just too quiet for your ears to register.

instruction: You find a half-empty styrofoam coffee cup on a blood pressure cart, still warm enough to fog your breath when you lean close.
input: You pick up the cup to check for a name or date stamp on the side.
output: There’s no name, just a single scratch mark carved into the foam, identical to the one you scratched into your own water bottle that morning before coming down. When you set it back, you notice the cart wheels were locked. No one could have rolled it here. The hair on your neck stands up; you start mentally retracing every step since you arrived, suddenly unable to confirm you didn’t leave this cup here yourself ten minutes ago, while you weren’t paying attention.

instruction: A security monitor on a rolling cart crackles to life, displaying grainy black and white footage of the exact hall you’re standing in.
input: You lean in to adjust the contrast dial, trying to clear the static.
output: The static clears just long enough for you to see yourself on screen, standing at the monitor. But there’s a second figure, standing directly behind your shoulder, hood pulled low, motionless. You spin around. The hall is empty. When you turn back, the monitor is dead. You press the power button repeatedly, nothing. You keep shifting your weight, eyes darting, because now every shadow at the edge of your vision feels like that figure, waiting just out of your line of sight.

instruction: You hear soft, steady humming coming from the old morgue refrigerator unit at the end of the hall. It was marked decommissioned three years prior.
input: You walk slowly toward the fridge, hand resting on the utility knife on your belt.
output: As you get closer, you recognize the hum: it’s the same lullaby your mother sang to you when you were a child, the one you’ve never told anyone about. You yank the fridge door open. It’s empty, just rusted shelves and dead cold air. But as you stand there breathing, you realize the humming didn’t stop. It’s not coming from the fridge. It’s coming from somewhere inside the walls, all around you, and for one sick second you’re convinced it’s been humming this whole time, you just only now started hearing it.

instruction: You are exploring a damp sewer tunnel, your headlamp only cutting 10 feet ahead. A soft, rhythmic tapping echoes just around the next curved pipe.
input: You slowly creep forward, pressing your back to the slimy concrete wall as you peer around the bend.
output: The tapping stops. There’s nothing there, just a half-crushed soda can rocking gently on the sewer floor. You sigh, about to turn back when you notice faint, damp bare footprints leading into the pitch-black side drain beside you. They don’t go out. You shine the light down that drain—nothing, just darkness. But a single warm drop of liquid hits the top of your helmet. You don’t hear anything breathing. Not yet.

instruction: You follow a low gurgle deeper into the sewer, and spot a tattered child’s doll propped against a rusted valve wheel.
input: You approach slowly, reaching out to nudge the doll with the tip of your boot.
output: The doll lurches sideways, and you flinch hard—only to realize it was just a fat sewer rat scurrying out from under its dress. You huff a nervous laugh, kicking the doll aside. As it topples, you catch a glimpse of something clamped tight to its back: a human fingernail, still crusted with fresh blood. Somewhere above you, metal scrapes slow and deliberate against the pipe ceiling.

instruction: Your radio crackles to life, picking up a garbled signal coming from a maintenance access hatch set low in the tunnel wall.
input: You kneel down, pressing your ear against the cold metal hatch to try and make out the signal.
output: For three seconds you hear nothing but static, then a quiet, high giggle cuts through before the radio cuts out entirely. You thump the hatch once; it’s locked solid from the other side. A thin line of cold drool drips through the crack between hatch and frame, landing right between your knees. Nobody laughs that quiet when they’re just playing a prank.

instruction: You round a corner and the tunnel opens into a wide chamber, water lapping calm at your ankles. Something bobs just below the surface ten feet out.
input: You shine your headlamp directly at the bobbing shape, taking one cautious step forward into the water.
output: The light catches it: just a waterlogged trash bag, swollen with sewage. You relax, rolling your eyes at your own jumpiness. As you lower the light, you notice every single ripple in the water is moving toward you, not away. Not from the bag. There’s something under the surface, circling slow, right at the edge of your light. It hasn’t broken cover. Yet.

instruction: A loose metal grate clatters behind you. When you spin around, the tunnel you just walked through is empty.
input: You sweep your headlamp back and forth along the empty tunnel, calling out softly to see if anyone’s there.
output: No one answers. You turn back to keep going, then freeze. Your shadow, cast sharp on the wall ahead, has a second shadow standing directly behind it. Hands raised. You snap around again. Nothing. Just empty tunnel. But when you look back, the second shadow is closer now. It didn’t make a sound. It won’t, right up until it grabs you.

instruction: Monitor aft sonar station during silent drift
input: You lean forward to adjust sonar gain dial
output: The hull thrums once, too deep to be engine. Temperature drops 3 degrees in 10 seconds, your breath fogs the display. You swear the sonar blip you were tracking just matched your breathing rhythm. When you glance up, the emergency bulkhead handle that was locked vertical 2 minutes ago now hangs horizontal. No footprints. No sound of anyone moving. When you reach to check the handle, the sonar pings once right as your fingers touch metal—exact same pitch, exact same timing. The log screen behind you scrolls one line: YOU ARE NOT WATCHING THE RIGHT THING. You didn't type that. No one else is here.

instruction: Run hourly life support filter check
input: You pull open the oxygen scrubber access panel
output: Stale recycled air catches in your throat. Every screw you just loosened is back tight, perfectly aligned, when you blink. The temperature gauge flickers from 68F to 32F and back before you can register it. You hear a soft whisper right behind your ear, same cadence as your own internal monologue, but spinning reveals only empty corridor. The scrubber filter, replaced 12 hours prior, is crusted black with dried salt. When you hold it to the light, you can see your own thumbprint pressed deep into the grime—exactly where you never touched it.

instruction: Patrol forward torpedo bay during red alert standby
input: You shine your torch along the empty torpedo racks
output: Your torch beam stutters. For half a second, every rack holds a figure standing perfectly still, facing you. Then they vanish. The metal deck vibrates not with the submarine, but with a steady heartbeat. You pass the same bulkhead sticker three times in a row while walking straight forward. When you stop, you hear your own footsteps continuing ten paces ahead, same weight, same pace. The intercom crackles once, playing back the exact shaky inhale you took 10 seconds prior. No one else is on this deck. No one else was supposed to be here.

instruction: Calibrate navigation gyroscope at midnight watch
input: You align the gyro bubble with the north reference mark
output: The bubble jumps every time you blink, never settling. The hull groans, and for a heartbeat you feel the entire submarine hanging upside down, suspended in dark water. Your wrist watch ticks 12:00 three times in a row. When you look back, the grease pencil reference mark you drew that morning is gone. In its place are tiny drawn eyes, looking directly up at you. There is no other pen on the console. The gyro finally stops. It is pointing directly at your chest.

instruction: Check exterior hull camera feeds per protocol
input: You cycle through the starboard hull camera views
output: Each feed clears not to ocean, but the inside of this control room, from behind your own chair. You can see the back of your head leaning into the monitor. You freeze. The version of you on screen freezes too. For 17 seconds you breathe in perfect sync. Then the on-screen you slowly turns its head, staring directly through the camera at you. The feed cuts black. All temperature alarms go silent. Only the quiet scratch of a pen on paper remains, coming from right outside the locked control room door. You are alone on watch.

instruction: Describe the entity that poses as the funeral home overnight janitor
input: You stay late after your mother's service, sitting on a back hallway bench. A man in a crisp blue uniform mops past, pauses, says he can leave the side light on for you. He smells like lavender and warm coffee.
output: You nod. He leaves the light glowing soft gold, no harsh fluorescent hum. You slump, let your shoulders drop for the first time in 3 days. When you open your eyes 20 minutes later the mop is still leaning against the wall. The light hasn't dimmed. But the walls have breathed in. The bench is now bolted to the floor where it wasn't before. You reach for your phone: every contact is replaced with his name. He didn't leave. He just stopped pretending he was separate from the room. The warm coffee smell is formaldehyde thickening in your sinuses. The light never was for you. It was the signal that you'd stopped checking the exits. That you'd decided this was safe.

instruction: Describe the entity that mimics the soft upholstered guest chair in the main viewing chapel
input: You are exhausted, every muscle screaming. There is one empty overstuffed armchair right by the casket, no one else has sat there all day. It looks like it will hold you.
output: You sink into it. The cushion gives exactly right, warm where it touches your thighs, the armrest molds to your elbow. You close your eyes. You don't notice that the fabric doesn't have a weave anymore. That it's breathing slow, syncing to your pulse. That the casket lid has clicked shut silent behind you. That everyone else has left the chapel and the doors have locked. It doesn't hurt. It doesn't rush. It just holds you, softer and softer, while the room grows dark. You will never get up. You will never want to. It will let you rest forever, exactly the way you begged to, right up until the moment you realize you can no longer move your fingers.

instruction: Describe the entity posing as the lead funeral director
input: He pulls you aside after the service, hands you a glass of water, says no one will bother you here. You can stay as long as you need. No one will judge you for breaking.
output: You cry. For an hour you cry, while he stands quiet by the door. You trust him. You tell him things you never told anyone. You don't see his shoes don't touch the floor. You don't see that every time you sob his shoulders get a little wider. He is feeding on the safety you feel. This building does not exist to bury the dead. It exists to catch the living when they are too broken to run. When you finally stop shaking he will smile. He will tell you it's okay now. He will not let you leave. No one ever comes here just to say goodbye. They come here because for one hour, one minute, they finally stop being afraid. And that is when it takes you.

instruction: Entity is a hollow, motionless human reflection that does not mirror your movements, only stares while holding its own throat. It never crosses the glass surface.
input: You turn a blind corner in the mirror maze, every surface repeating your hunched breathing. This reflection isn’t gasping. It’s still.
output: Your exhale fogs the glass first. It does not fog its side. Its fingers press purple half-moons into its own throat, not yours, eyes locked dead on yours even as you tilt your head, step left, step right. It never moves. Not even a blink. You press your palm flat to the glass. It does not lift its hand. It just watches, while you realize every other reflection in every surrounding pane has now stopped moving too. None of them breathe. None of them mirror. You can’t tell which one was first. You can’t tell if any of them are actually you anymore.

instruction: Entity is the sound of your own past sobbing, only audible when standing perfectly still between two parallel mirror panes. It gets clearer the longer you don’t move.
input: You freeze mid-step to avoid walking into glass. The first whimper comes from directly behind your left shoulder.
output: At first you think it’s you, breathing wrong. Then you recognize the hitch: that was you, at 11, locked in the closet. It gets louder, not louder in volume, but closer, like it’s being pressed right up against the inside of your ear. You can hear the snot running, the way you tried to hold your breath so no one would hear you. No mirrors shake. No shape appears. Just that sound, perfectly preserved, only here, only when you stop running. You start walking fast, and it cuts off sharp, like someone slammed a door. But every time you hesitate at a turn, you hear it, waiting, reminding you that every part of you that ever hid is still right here, just on the other side of the glass.

instruction: Entity is a reflection that silently counts your breaths on the glass with a dirty finger, one line per exhale. It never stops counting.
input: You notice faint scratch lines on the mirror directly in front of you, right at eye level. A new one appears as you breathe out.
output: The lines are smudged, grey, like someone dragged a dead nail through condensation that won’t wipe away. You hold your breath. It stops, finger hovering. When you finally gasp, it drags another slow line, right under the last. You count them. 742. You have been in here 12 minutes. Every mirror now has the same count, every reflection holding the same dirty finger, waiting. You realize it will not stop. It will count every single breath you take for the rest of your life, right here, even after you leave. Even when you are old, and you lie dying, there will be a version of you in this maze, still scratching line after line, counting the last one. You can never outrun the tally.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the fear of darkness set in a parking garage
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: underground parking garage. Narrate in second person, atmospheric, 100-250 words
output: You hit the stairwell door hard, boots slapping wet concrete. The overhead lights died three floors down. Now only the faint red exit glow bleeds around corners, painting every parked car a bruised dead red. You don’t hear anything. That’s the worst part. No distant hum, no traffic leak, just your own breath echoing too loud. Every shadow between SUVs looks like someone leaning, waiting. You reach for your phone—screen’s dead. You take one step, and something brushes the back of your neck. You freeze. There was nothing there a second ago. The darkness isn’t empty. It’s moving slow, so slow you almost don’t feel it wrapping around your wrists, stealing the air right out of your lungs before you can scream.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the fear of darkness set in a parking garage
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: underground parking garage. Narrate in second person, atmospheric, 100-250 words
output: You’re fumbling for your car keys when the last fluorescent above you pops. One second you can see the scratch on your driver’s door, the next the garage swallows everything. You don’t move. You know better than to move when the dark comes here. You can hear it breathing, soft, between the parked vans. It doesn’t rush. It waits. Every time you blink, it gets a little closer. You can feel its gaze on your jaw, on the pulse hammering in your throat. You try to call out, but your tongue sticks to the roof of your mouth. The dark doesn’t make noise. It just takes. First your keys, slipping out of your hand without a sound. Then your balance. Then the world, one silent breath at a time.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the fear of darkness set in a parking garage
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: underground parking garage. Narrate in second person, atmospheric, 100-250 words
output: The garage lights stutter once, then go out. You were halfway across level B2, bags slung over your shoulder. Now there’s only black. Absolute, unbroken black. You can’t even see your own hand held inches from your face. Footsteps. No— not footsteps. Dragging. Slow, wet dragging coming from somewhere behind you. You back up, shoulder slamming hard into a car hood. The dragging stops. You hold your breath. You can smell it now: mildew, rot, something that tastes like burnt plastic at the back of your throat. It’s right in front of you. You know it is. You just can’t see it. It doesn’t touch you. It just waits, letting the dark press in until you start begging for it to just do something already. It never does. That’s the cruelty of it.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the fear of darkness set in a parking garage
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: underground parking garage. Narrate in second person, atmospheric, 100-250 words
output: You laughed when your friend said this garage eats people after 10pm. Now you’re alone, the emergency lights have all failed, and every shadow is wrong. You count parking spots out loud, voice cracking, trying to find your sedan. Your count keeps jumping. 17, 18, 22. You swear you just passed spot 19 twice. The dark is rearranging things. You reach out and touch a truck mirror that wasn’t there three steps ago. Your breath fogs cold, even though the garage was warm five minutes ago. Something chuckles, quiet, right next to your ear. When you spin, there’s nothing. Just black. The dark isn’t just absence of light here. It’s alive. It’s playing with you. It’s got all night.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the fear of darkness set in a parking garage
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: underground parking garage. Narrate in second person, atmospheric, 100-250 words
output: Your car died halfway down the ramp. You got out to check the engine, and the world went black. No moon, no street glow, nothing filters down here. You pat the hood, the roof, the door handle— gone. You can’t even feel your car anymore. The concrete under your shoes has turned soft, squelching a little when you shift your weight. You can’t tell which way is up. Every direction sounds the same: quiet, waiting. You start walking. You walk for what feels like an hour. No walls, no pillars, no cars. Just dark. It didn’t just turn the lights off. It ate the whole garage. And now it’s eating you, one quiet step at a time, and you won’t even know when it’s finished.

instruction: A perfectly still, identical reflection of you that blinks 0.2 seconds after you do. It wears the exact same clothing, holds the same posture, never moves ahead, only lags microscopically on every tiny movement. It does not speak.
input: You stop mid-step to catch your breath in the mirror maze, wiping sweat from your brow. You freeze when you notice it.
output: The lag hits your throat like cold water. You blink once. There is the beat. One. Two. It blinks. You twitch your left finger. It waits. Then copies. You hold your breath; its chest rises three heartbeats after yours stops. It never frowns, never flinches, never breaks eye contact. Every glass surface now holds it, all of them lagged, all watching. You do not dare run. You know if you turn your back, it will stop lagging. It is only being polite right now, letting you notice. Letting you feel how very long it has been waiting for you to see it, just out of sync, just safe enough that you did not scream until now.

instruction: A dim exit sign glowing at the end of every corridor, never getting closer no matter how far you walk. It hums at a frequency just below human hearing.
input: You turn a corner and spot the familiar green exit glow, your knees almost buckling in relief.
output: Relief unfurls warm in your chest first, stupid, hungry relief. You run. You round three turns, it is still there, same distance, same soft glow. You stop. It does not move closer. You back away. It does not recede. The hum settles in your molars, making your fillings ache. It never promised it was an exit. It only promised it looked like one. You have passed this exact scuff on the floor seven times now. You will keep walking toward it anyway. That is the worst part. You know it is a lie. You will still go. There is nothing else to walk toward. Every other mirror just holds your lagging reflection.

instruction: Soft, familiar laughter coming from just around the next mirror turn, sounding exactly like someone you loved as a child.
input: You hear it, quiet and warm, through the glass. It is your older sister's laugh, the one she had before she left.
output: You call her name. It does not answer, just laughs again, soft, just out of sight. You round the corner. Empty. The laugh is now behind you. You do not run. You breathe slow. You know it is not her. You know whatever is making that sound has listened to every memory you have of safe things, picked the softest one, and is dragging you through the maze one small hope at a time. It will not reveal itself. It does not need to. It only needs you to keep believing, just for one more step, that this time when you turn the corner, you will not be alone. That is all it ever needs.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on false security set on an old bridge over a ravine
input: Fear type: false_security. Setting: old bridge over a ravine. Second person narration, atmospheric, 100-250 words.
output: You cross the first half of the rotting planks slow, hand clamped to the rusted rail. When your boot hits solid, sun-warmed concrete on the far side you laugh, shaky, and lean back against the rail. You made it. You wipe sweat from your neck, watch wind ripple pine tops far below. That’s when you notice the rail doesn’t creak under your weight. It doesn’t give at all. You look down. Your boots aren’t on concrete. They’re hovering three inches over empty ravine air. The bridge didn’t end. You walked right off it ten seconds ago, and your brain just hasn’t stopped lying to you yet.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on false security set on an old bridge over a ravine
input: Fear type: false_security. Setting: old bridge over a ravine. Second person narration, atmospheric, 100-250 words.
output: Rain stops halfway across. The thunder fades. You stop huddling, straighten your jacket. One plank creaks under you, but it held, same as the last twenty. You pull out your phone—signal bars pop up, full. You sigh, relief warm in your chest. You’re fine. This is fine. That’s when you notice every raindrop hanging midair on the bridge cables. The wind stopped moving the pine boughs below. The signal bars never flicker. They don’t change. You stomp the plank. No sound comes back. You didn’t survive the collapse. You’re just standing in the last quiet second your mind could hold onto.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on false security set on an old bridge over a ravine
input: Fear type: false_security. Setting: old bridge over a ravine. Second person narration, atmospheric, 100-250 words.
output: You test each plank before putting your full weight down, just like the old man at the gas station told you. Fifteen steps in, you relax. None have shifted. The rail is solid under your palm. You even whistle a little. Halfway, you glance down through the slats, and freeze. There are no footprints on the planks behind you. Not one scuff, not one damp mark from your boots. You lift your foot. It passes straight through the wood. You fell through this bridge seven minutes ago. You’re still falling. Your brain is just replaying the part where you thought you were safe, over and over, while the ground comes up.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on false security set on an old bridge over a ravine
input: Fear type: false_security. Setting: old bridge over a ravine. Second person narration, atmospheric, 100-250 words.
output: The dog trots ahead first, tail wagging. If it’s safe for him, it’s safe for you. You follow, grinning, the bridge swaying soft under your steps. You reach the middle, lean out to yell down the ravine, listen to the echo. When you turn back, the dog is gone. No paw prints. No barks. The end of the bridge you came from is gone too. Just mist, and planks stretching endlessly forward. He wasn’t real. None of it was. It just knew you’d never step onto a rotting bridge alone. So it gave you something to follow, right until you couldn’t turn back.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on false security set on an old bridge over a ravine
input: Fear type: false_security. Setting: old bridge over a ravine. Second person narration, atmospheric, 100-250 words.
output: You hear the crack when you step on it. You freeze, breath held. Nothing breaks. The plank settles. You laugh shakily, keep walking. That was close, you think. You make it to the other side, turn, and lean against the tree. You pull out a water bottle, take a sip. That’s when you see it: the whole middle section of the bridge is gone. It collapsed the second you stepped on that cracked plank. You’re not leaning against a tree. You’re falling. You just haven’t finished accepting it yet. For two whole minutes, you’ve been living in the quiet lie between the crack and the ground.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the specified fear type in the given setting
input: Fear type: false_security. Setting: dark old growth forest
output: You finally spot the trail marker. The carved oak post glows faintly with park service reflective paint, exactly what you’ve hunted for three lost hours. You slump against it, laughing shakily, and your phone lights up with four full service bars. The 911 dispatcher answers warm and calm. You relax, shoulders dropping. That’s when you notice moss is growing thick and wet over the reflective paint. The dispatcher’s voice stretches into a low droning hum. You lift your head. Hundreds of identical posts stretch between the trees as far as you can see. None were there ten seconds ago.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the specified fear type in the given setting
input: Fear type: false_security. Setting: dark old growth forest
output: The rain eases. Just when you think you’ll freeze solid, a golden retriever trots through the underbrush, collar jangling, tail wagging. It nuzzles your frozen hand, then glances back over its shoulder like it’s inviting you along. You follow, grinning—someone’s camp must be close. You don’t notice its paws leave no prints. You don’t notice its barks don’t echo. When you finally call out for its owner, it stops. It turns. There is no face. Just smooth golden fur, stretching flat where its head should end.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the specified fear type in the given setting
input: Fear type: false_security. Setting: dark old growth forest
output: Your match catches. There’s a proper campfire ring right here, half-stacked logs, crumpled soda cans. Someone was here recently. You get the fire roaring, warm your numb fingers, even find an unopened granola bar tucked under a rock. The flames hold the shadows back. You take a bite, sweet and crisp. That’s when you realize the fire makes no smoke. Its light stops sharp three feet out. Forty seven minutes have passed. The logs have not burned down even a millimetre. The granola bar in your mouth suddenly tastes like wet mud.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the specified fear type in the given setting
input: Fear type: false_security. Setting: dark old growth forest
output: You hear hikers. Their laughter carries, flashlights bobbing through the trees. You yell, wave, and they spot you immediately. They’re normal: hoodies, scuffed boots, backpacks. One hands you a cold water bottle. They’re heading for the parking lot, you can come along. You fall into step, so relieved your throat tightens. No one speaks after that. Their footsteps make no sound. Every single one of their eyes is locked on you, even the ones walking ahead. None blink. The parking lot never gets any closer.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the specified fear type in the given setting
input: Fear type: false_security. Setting: dark old growth forest
output: Your flashlight dies. Fumbling blind, your hand closes around a solid metal park bench. You slump down, breathing deep. It’s okay. Park benches are only placed half a mile from the trailhead. You just wait for dawn, you’re almost safe. You rest your head back. That’s when you feel it: the bench is breathing. Slow, deep, thrumming under your thighs. The slats under your palms are warm now. They flex, just barely, when you grip them. You try to stand. You cannot. The bench is holding you.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the specified fear, set in the given location with second-person narration and atmospheric tone.
input: Fear: false_security. Setting: underground parking level B3.
output: You exhale when the elevator dings open on B3. You made it past the street creeps, locked the lobby door behind you, your key fob clicks ready in hand. The overhead hums steady, no flickering. You walk past three parked sedans, counting your steps to your SUV. It’s only when you reach it, hand on the door handle, that you notice every single car in the lot has their driver’s side window rolled exactly three inches down. Not broken. Rolled. No wind down here. No one else came down after you. The elevator never pinged again. You don’t hear anyone breathing. But you can feel them, watching from between the vehicles, waiting for you to stop convincing yourself this is normal.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the specified fear, set in the given location with second-person narration and atmospheric tone.
input: Fear: false_security. Setting: underground parking level B3.
output: You laugh quietly at your own jumpiness as you turn the corner. That shadow was just a concrete pillar. That scrape was just your boot on the epoxy. You hit the unlock button twice; your Civic blinks twice friendly right where you left it. You slide in, slam the door, lock it immediately. Safety. You turn the key. The engine doesn’t catch. You turn it again. Dead. That’s when you notice the odometer. You drove 12 miles today. It says 47. And there, fogged on the inside of the passenger window, someone drew a smiley face while you were upstairs. You never opened the door after you parked. It was locked the whole time.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the specified fear, set in the given location with second-person narration and atmospheric tone.
input: Fear: false_security. Setting: underground parking level B3.
output: The security guard waved you down ten minutes ago, said he’d just done a full patrol of B3, all clear. You nod, relax your grip on the pepper spray. It’s quiet, just the distant hum of the ventilation. You reach your van, throw your work bag on the passenger seat. That’s when you see the boot prints. Wet, muddy, tracking right up to your driver door. They stop right where you’re standing. The guard said no one was down here. It hasn’t rained in three days. You glance back toward the stairwell. The exit door you came through is closed. It was propped open when you arrived. No one closed it but you.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the specified fear, set in the given location with second-person narration and atmospheric tone.
input: Fear: false_security. Setting: underground parking level B3.
output: You breathe easy when you see the security camera light blinking red above your parking spot. No one can mess with you here, it’s all recorded. You toss your phone onto the dash, lean down to untie your work boots. For three seconds, the camera light goes out. It blinks back on. You freeze. Your phone screen is dark. You didn’t turn it off. The radio, which you left playing soft jazz when you parked, is silent. Every light on the level except the one right above your car has gone out. The camera blinks red again. It’s not recording you. It’s watching you. And it’s waiting for you to realize you haven’t heard another car start in 22 minutes.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the specified fear, set in the given location with second-person narration and atmospheric tone.
input: Fear: false_security. Setting: underground parking level B3.
output: You ran all the way from the street, didn’t stop until the B3 elevator doors slid shut behind you. You’re safe now. They can’t follow you down here, the card reader only works for residents. You lean back against the cold metal, heart slowing. That’s when you smell cigarette smoke. Mint menthol, same brand the guy who was following you was smoking. The elevator never stopped between the lobby and B3. No one else got on. You look down. There’s a fresh crushed cigarette butt on the floor right by your shoe. It’s still glowing at the edge.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the fear of false security set in a funeral home
input: Fear type: false_security. Setting: funeral home. Narrate in second person, maintain atmospheric tone, 100-250 words
output: You’d finally exhaled. The last mourner left forty minutes ago. You locked the front doors, flipped the overhead lights down to the dim night glow, poured the lukewarm coffee you’d been craving since 9am. Nothing bad was going to happen tonight. You’d checked every viewing room, confirmed the caskets were secured, ran the alarm twice. You slump back in the office chair, eyes closing. Then you hear it. Soft, even breathing. Not yours. You snap the desk lamp up. The old woman from the 2pm service is standing three feet from your desk, her burial shawl still tucked around her shoulders. She wasn’t breathing when you wheeled her into the prep room. You locked that door. You checked it.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the fear of false security set in a funeral home
input: Fear type: false_security. Setting: funeral home. Narrate in second person, maintain atmospheric tone, 100-250 words
output: It’s 1:17am. You did the final walkthrough twice. Every curtain drawn, every cooler latch clicked shut, every exit deadbolted. The silence feels safe finally, after three days of wailing and hymns. You settle on the chapel pew to eat your sandwich, phone propped up playing a dumb podcast. Halfway through the first bite, you notice something. All the flower arrangements that were stacked by the door are now lined perfectly down the centre aisle. Each ribbon has been untied. You didn’t move them. No one else is here. You stood right here ten minutes ago. They weren’t there. The front door is still locked. You checked.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the fear of false security set in a funeral home
input: Fear type: false_security. Setting: funeral home. Narrate in second person, maintain atmospheric tone, 100-250 words
output: You laughed when your coworker warned you about the third floor storage. Stupid intern ghost stories. You locked the stairwell gate behind you, clicked the flashlight on, counted the casket hardware crates just like the inventory sheet said. Everything matches. No creaks, no shadows, nothing out of place. You turn to leave, hand on the gate latch. It won’t move. You jostle it. It’s locked. From the other side. You just came through here two minutes ago. No one else was on the stairs. You tested the latch before you walked past it. It was open. The flashlight flickers once, dies. Somewhere in the dark behind the crates, something drags its feet slow across the linoleum.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the fear of false security set in a funeral home
input: Fear type: false_security. Setting: funeral home. Narrate in second person, maintain atmospheric tone, 100-250 words
output: The autopsy was over. You washed your hands twice, scrubbed under every nail, stripped the scrubs, zipped the body bag closed, latched the cooler drawer shut tight. You leaned against the cold metal wall and breathed. That was the worst one. It was over. No more mess, no more staring. You turn for the door. The faucet is running. You turned it off. You watched the last drop fall. You cross the room fast, yank the handle shut. When you turn back, the cooler drawer is pulled six inches open. The body bag is unzipped just far enough that you can see one pale eye staring right at you. You latched it. You heard the click.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the fear of false security set in a funeral home
input: Fear type: false_security. Setting: funeral home. Narrate in second person, maintain atmospheric tone, 100-250 words
output: You’d activated the alarm. You saw the little red light blink steady on the keypad. You pulled your coat on, keys in hand, already thinking about the hot shower waiting at home. This long week was done. You reach for the front doorknob. It’s warm. Too warm. You freeze. The building has been unheated all night, the whole place smells like cold marble and formaldehyde. That doorknob was ice cold five minutes ago when you checked it. You step back slowly. Somewhere down the hall, a chair scrapes across wood. The alarm light is still blinking. It’s supposed to alert if anything moves. It doesn’t beep. It just blinks. Like it doesn’t see whatever is now inside with you.

instruction: Night shift sysadmin, hunched over a half-opened server rack, eyes bloodshot, no jacket even though the room is 55 degrees.
input: You okay man? It's just us in here.
output: He doesn't lift his head, fingers hovering an inch above a loose power cable. "Just us? That's what they programmed you to think. You hear that hum? It's not power. It's counting. Counting how many times you blink. They left one management port open last night. I didn't touch it. I know better. Don't turn around. Don't let them see you noticed I'm talking. They're in the blank drive bays. Waiting for one of us to breathe too slow."

instruction: Junior support tech, curled behind a UPS unit, jacket pulled up over her ears, notebook crumpled in one fist.
input: I'm looking for the backup log files. Have you seen them?
output: She presses a finger so hard to her lips the skin whitens, eyes darting between overhead cable runs. "Shut up. Don't say that out loud. The logs know when you're looking for them. Did you see that switch light blink three times while you spoke? They already logged your question. Now they'll rewrite every entry. You won't find anything. No one ever finds anything. Not while the servers are watching. Not while they remember what you asked."

instruction: Senior network admin, sat cross-legged on the cold concrete, surrounded by hundreds of pulled ethernet cables twisted into loose knots.
input: The whole network went down ten minutes ago. Everyone's panicking.
output: He laughs a quiet, sharp little sound, never meeting your eyes, twisting a frayed cable tight around his wrist. "Down? You think this is down? They turned off the ping so you wouldn't track them. They're moving right now. Between the racks. You can feel the floor vibrate if you stop breathing long enough. I pulled all the cables. It doesn't matter. They don't need wires anymore. We just thought they did. That was the first lie."

instruction: Night cleaning lady, mopping the exact same 12 inch square tile over and over, bucket sat untouched beside the CRAC unit.
input: Sorry, I didn't spot you back here. Working late tonight?
output: She doesn't stop mopping, doesn't lift her head, voice flat and even like she's recited this a hundred times. "They make me stay. They say there's always dust. Dust holds prints. Holds words. Every breath you take in here settles on the server grilles. They read it. Every thought you had while you stood here. I mop it up before they get too much. Don't tell anyone. They'll make me mop inside the drives next. I don't want to see what's growing there."

instruction: Summer intern, standing motionless in front of a blank monitor, holding a dead keyboard at his side like a weapon.
input: Hey, can you grab the master key and unlock this rack for me?
output: He blinks once, very slowly, never breaking eye contact with the black screen. "It's already unlocked. It's been unlocked since you walked through the door. They let you think locks matter. They want you fumbling with keys, so you don't notice the rack is watching the back of your neck. Don't touch the handle. Once you touch it, it'll know your hand. It'll remember every door you open after that. Just walk away. Pretend you never asked."

instruction: You are sitting on the stained prison bunk, tracing a crack in the concrete wall. No guards have passed in 47 minutes.
input: You lean your head back against the cold block and close your eyes for ten seconds.
output: When you open them, the crack you traced has split three new ways. The cell is three degrees colder; you can see your breath fog, though the heating vent hums unchanged. The metal toilet at the far end drips once, slow, then stops. You lift your hand: the grey dust under your nails is gone, replaced with fine red clay you’ve never touched here. Somewhere past the bars, someone breathes exactly in time with you. Not matching. Mirroring. Every inhale lands the same millisecond. You hold your breath. The breathing holds too. No cough, no shift, just perfect, silent stillness on the other side.

instruction: You are counting the water stains on the ceiling to pass the hour. The cell door is deadbolted shut.
input: You stand on the bunk to reach up and touch the largest dark stain.
output: Your fingers pass through it. Not wet, not dry—just empty, like the stain is a hole painted on air. You stumble back onto the mattress. The fluorescent overhead didn’t flicker, but all the stains have rotated 90 degrees. The room smells like rain, though this prison is three hundred miles from the nearest open water. Your toothbrush, left on the sink, is now lying bristles-down exactly where you stood ten seconds prior. No sound of movement. No draft. It just… relocated. When you glance at the wall clock, the minute hand is ticking backwards, one quiet click at a time.

instruction: It is 2:17 AM. You are awake, wrapped in the thin prison blanket. The block is quiet.
input: You roll onto your side, facing the solid back wall of the cell.
output: There are handprints pressed into the concrete. Not drawn, pressed deep, like someone pushed their palms into wet stone. They are exactly the size of your hands. You press yours against them; they line up perfectly, down to the scar on your left thumb. The wall is warm, far warmer than concrete ever gets. Somewhere inside it, you hear a pen scratching, slow and steady, the same way you scratch notes into the wall when you can’t sleep. The blanket slips off your shoulder. You don’t reach for it. You can feel someone behind you, breathing onto your neck, even though you are pressed flush against the wall.

instruction: You have been pacing the cell for twenty minutes. Your boots echo soft on the floor.
input: You stop mid-step, suddenly aware you don’t remember starting to pace.
output: You look down. You are not wearing your boots. You are barefoot, standing in a cold puddle that wasn’t there three seconds ago. The echo of your last step keeps going, ringing once, twice, ten times around the small cell long after you stopped moving. The bars at the front are slightly closer than they were. Not by much. Half an inch, maybe. You can’t prove it, but you know. Every time you blink, they shift a little more. No creak, no groan. Just quiet, incremental movement, like they are creeping closer while your eyes are closed. You stop blinking. They stop moving.

instruction: You are eating the last bite of your lunch tray. The metal spoon is cold in your palm.
input: You set the spoon down gently on the edge of the tray.
output: It doesn’t clink. It lands silent, like it was set on soft flesh. You nudge it with your finger. It sinks half an inch into the metal tray, like the steel has turned to warm mud. You yank your hand back. The tray is solid again, the spoon lying normally. But when you lift it, there is a perfect indent of the spoon bowl pressed into the steel. No dent, no scratch. Just an impression, like pressing a thumb into bread. You can still feel the warm give under your finger. The rest of the block is completely silent now. No one is breathing. No one is making any sound at all.

instruction: You are patrolling the dark aft engineering deck of the dead submarine. Metal bulkheads groan constantly. You notice a loose access panel hanging half-open near the reactor coolant lines.
input: You kneel down, shine your flashlight into the gap behind the access panel, and reach slowly to pull it fully open.
output: Your flashlight beam catches nothing but frayed insulation at first—then you spot three deep, raked claw marks scored into the inner metal, far too clean to be rust damage. A single drop of warm, briny water plops exactly onto the back of your outstretched hand. You yank back, heart hammering, but when you re-aim the light, the marks are gone. Only the distant, rhythmic thud of something heavy hitting the outer hull echoes now, too regular to be drifting debris.

instruction: You enter the submarine’s abandoned mess hall. All overhead lights are dead, only your headlamp illuminates overturned trays. A child’s plastic toy dolphin sits propped upright on the only undisturbed table.
input: You approach the table, leaning forward to pick up the toy dolphin to examine it.
output: As your fingers brush the plastic, you hear a quiet, high-pitched giggle directly behind your right ear. You spin instantly, headlamp sweeping the empty room—nothing moves, no one is there. When you turn back, the dolphin is now facing you, its black painted eyes staring dead ahead. A faint scraping sound starts under the floor grates right beneath your boots, stopping the second you stomp to check.

instruction: You are checking the submarine’s sonar station. The screen flickers erratically, no official contacts registered. You notice the manual audio monitor knob is cranked all the way up even though no one was here.
input: You press the sonar audio playback button, holding the headset tight to your ear to listen for any signals.
output: At first there is only static, then a soft, wet breathing sound, slow and even. It gets louder, closer, as though whoever is breathing is pressing their mouth right against the other side of the sonar mic. You yank the headset off, slamming it down on the console. The second it leaves your ear, you hear the exact same breathing, loud and clear, coming from the ventilation duct directly above your head. It stops abruptly, leaving dead silence.

instruction: You descend the ladder into the submarine’s forward torpedo bay. All tubes are sealed except number 4, which hangs ajar half in, half out of the hull. Dark water drips from its open mouth.
input: You walk slowly towards the ajar torpedo tube, shining your light inside to check what is lodged there.
output: The beam cuts through murky stagnant water, revealing only a torn navy uniform floating tangled around the tube’s internal rails. You relax slightly, until you notice the uniform’s collar is still buttoned tight, as if someone is still inside it. Just as you lean in closer, a single pale human hand shoots out of the dark water, grabs the edge of the tube for half a second, then vanishes back under the surface without a splash.

instruction: You are searching the captain’s quarters. The bunk is unmade, the captain’s log lies open on the desk. A small wall mirror above the desk is fogged over despite the cabin being cold and dry.
input: You wipe the fog off the mirror with your sleeve to see what was visible behind you.
output: As the condensation clears, you don’t see your reflection first—you see a tall, waterlogged figure standing directly behind you, head tilted, hands hanging at its sides. You scream and spin around, but the cabin is empty. When you look back at the mirror, your own reflection is there now, but its eyes are solid black, and it is not breathing fog onto the glass like you are. A faint knocking starts on the cabin door, soft, exactly three times.

instruction: Observe the cruise ship deck environment after midnight.
input: I lean on the starboard rail, sipping leftover champagne, checking that all deck lights are functioning properly.
output: At first it feels perfectly calm: warm ocean breeze, distant jazz from the lounge, deck lights glowing steady. Then the wind dies completely, no one announces it. The life rings mounted along the rail begin spinning slowly, uniform, no gust to move them. The engine hum you stopped noticing three days ago vanishes, silent enough you can hear your own heartbeat. Nothing looks broken, nothing feels dangerous yet. You were just relaxing, feeling safe out here alone. Only now you realize you haven’t seen another person, not even a crew member, for almost twelve minutes.

instruction: Return to your stateroom after the late variety show.
input: I unlock my cabin door, kick off my heels, and settle onto the bed to scroll my phone before sleeping.
output: You lock the deadbolt, sigh, finally relaxed after the crowded show. The AC hum softens until it’s gone, you don’t register it at first. The porthole curtain sways slow, even though you sealed the window shut ten minutes prior. The constant gentle rock of the ship, the sway you’d grown so used to it felt like breathing, stops. Flat. Still. Everything looks right, everything feels almost too right. You were just glad to be safe alone in your room. Now you can’t shake the feeling that something pressed its ear to the other side of the door thirty seconds ago.

instruction: Patrol the lower promenade on overnight security rounds.
input: I finish logging my last checkpoint, radio dispatch that all areas are clear and unremarkable.
output: Your radio clicks dead the second you finish speaking. The overhead string lights don’t flicker, they just warm, until the back of your neck prickles like you’re standing too close to a fire. The stack of folded lounge chairs you walked past four minutes ago are now all turned, facing straight out to the ocean. No creaks, no footprints, no sound of anyone moving them. You just reported everything was fine. You were just about to clock off for break. Now you can’t remember if the ship ever actually had this many lounge chairs.

instruction: Wait on the pool deck for your friend to return from the restroom.
input: I wrap a beach towel around my shoulders, watch the calm pool water, and hum the bar’s closing song.
output: The bar music fades so gradually you don’t notice it stopped until you’re humming into total silence. The pool surface goes perfectly flat, not a single ripple, even though the ship was swaying ten seconds prior. The towel on your shoulders stops feeling like terry cloth, it feels heavy, damp, like moss, but when you glance down it looks exactly the same. You thought this was just a boring harmless wait, you felt safe out here under the lights. Now you realize you can’t hear the ocean. At all.

instruction: Press your face to the navigation bridge observation glass after midnight.
input: I rest my palm on the cold window, watching bioluminescent waves trail behind the ship, content we’re on course.
output: The glowing blue wake blinks out all at once. The glass under your palm warms, slow, until it matches your body temperature, like pressing skin to skin. You can still feel the thrum of the engines under your boots, but there is nothing behind the ship. Just endless flat black. The compass on the wall behind you spins steady, no ticking. One breath ago this was beautiful, peaceful, you felt safe. Now you can’t shake the horrible quiet certainty that we haven’t been moving at all. Not for hours.

instruction: You are standing by a cold metal autopsy table wiping down stainless steel. Only fridge vents hum, blowing sub-zero air over your wrists.
input: I bend down to grab a fresh roll of gauze from the under-table cabinet.
output: The second your head dips below the table edge, a frigid wet hand wraps tight around your ankle. You yelp, jerking back hard enough to slam your skull on the table rim. When you scramble backwards staring down, there is nothing there. No hand, no shadow, just cracked linoleum. Then you hear it: a single wet gasp right against the back of your neck. The fridge vents cut out dead silent mid-hum, leaving only the thud of your heartbeat.

instruction: You are logging body tags at the front desk, pen scratching paper. Fluorescent lights buzz faint overhead.
input: I glance up to check the time on the wall clock.
output: Every single body drawer in the back wall has slid open exactly one inch. All 24 of them, uniform, silent, metal glinting dull in the overhead light. You freeze, pen hovering mid-air. Before you can stand, one drawer slams shut hard enough to rattle the window panes. Then the next. Then the next, slamming in rapid staccato marching right towards you. The final drawer slams so loud the lightbulb above your head explodes in a shower of hot glass.

instruction: You are retrieving a body for transport, rolling an empty gurney down the windowless morgue corridor.
input: I reach for the cold metal handle of storage drawer 17.
output: Your fingers brush the handle, and the drawer flies open on its own so hard it cracks your knuckles. The sheet draped over the corpse has pulled taut across its face. You blink, and its eyes are open, staring directly at you. It does not breathe, does not twitch. Then its mouth snaps open with a dry cracking pop, and a single live cockroach crawls out, drops onto your shoe, and scurries up your pant leg before you can react.

instruction: You are mopping the floor near the autopsy sinks, disinfectant water sloshing quiet in the bucket.
input: I tilt my head when I hear a soft dripping that isn’t the leaky tap.
output: You follow the sound, looking up. Pressed flat upside down against the ceiling tile above you is the corpse you wheeled into the deep freezer three hours ago. Its neck is twisted wrong, skin blue-grey, and it drips cold formaldehyde directly onto your forehead. The second your eyes lock, it drops. It hits the floor three feet from you with a wet heavy thud, and every light in the morgue cuts out at once.

instruction: You are locking up for the night, doing the final walkthrough to confirm all drawers are latched.
input: I pull the main exit door closed and reach for the deadbolt.
output: Your hand wraps around the deadbolt, and you feel cold breath on your knuckles. You yank your hand back. When you turn around, every corpse on the autopsy tables you’d covered an hour prior is sitting upright. All of them, heads turned perfectly towards you. No movement, no sound, just dozens of blank eyes fixed on your face. Then one of them tilts its head slow, and smiles. The door lock clicks shut on its own behind you, loud and final.

instruction: Entity manifested as half-consolidated students fused inside standard high school lockers, only encountered after hours in unoccupied west wing hallways
input: You round the corner of the empty school hallway, and quiet wet breathing echoes from the bank of dented blue lockers
output: The lockers do not rattle. The breathing seeps through the ventilation slits, thin and gurgling, like air forced through crushed tracheal cartilage. Lean close. The first slit shows teeth scattered across the back metal like dropped thumbtacks, no mouth to hold them. The next has an eye pressed flat against the grate, iris smearing outward like wet acrylic every time it blinks. Fingertips push through the locker handle gap first: not five, not ten, each finger splitting into twiggy smaller digits as they emerge, skin stretched translucent enough to map every pulsing blue vein. They do not grab. They tap slow, steady, against the cold steel. The breathing synchronizes, rising from every locker now. None of these people are dead.
