They are just waiting for someone to turn the latch.

instruction: Entity appearing as a former hall monitor, whose uniform has permanently fused with all underlying soft tissue and bone
input: Halfway down the empty hallway, you spot a motionless figure standing beside the rusted water fountain
output: It does not turn. The blazer does not hang; it bulges, woven deep into shoulder flesh, collar cutting so deep into the neck that skin has grown over the fabric edge. When it tilts its head, you see the lanyard is not plastic: it is a strip of its own tendon, strung through a clean hole punched through its sternum. The ID card photo is just raw, glistening muscle. It opens its mouth, and instead of speech you hear the dry scratch of hall pass notebook paper tearing. Its shoe soles have melted into the linoleum, leg bones spreading under the tile like fine tree roots. It never runs. It never calls out. Every time you blink, it is three feet closer.

instruction: Entity consisting of malformed disembodied hands growing downward from hallway ceiling tiles, only perceivable in peripheral vision
input: You walk alone down the empty hallway, repeatedly catching faint movement just above your direct line of sight
output: Every time you snap your head upward there is nothing: only water-stained ceiling tile, the low dead hum of broken fluorescent bulbs. But out the corner of your eye you see them. Fingers push through the acoustic foam first like fat white worms, joints bending the wrong direction, each knuckle swollen to twice natural size. Some have seven joints. Some have none. One has a milky grey eye set into the palm, blinking slow. They do not reach. They just hang. When you stop moving, you feel their breath on the crown of your head: warm, sour, like spoiled cartoned school milk. You can hear them whispering, not words, just the wet drag of tendon sliding over bone. If you look directly, they retreat. They never leave.

instruction: Grey-haired usher in faded velvet uniform, polishing a torn 1972 ticket stub by the theater main entrance
input: Wow, this place feels way safer than I expected. No creeps hanging around here right?
output: He pauses mid-polish, a thin fixed smile never reaching his foggy clouded eyes. 'Safe? Oh honey, everyone says that the first hour. That's how it likes it. You haven't heard the lights hum right yet. No one breaks in here, no one steals anything. They just... stay. Sit down in the dark, watch the show no one wrote. You'll be comfortable, that's the trick. You'll forget you ever wanted to leave. By the time you notice the doors locked behind you, you'll already be reaching for a program.'

instruction: Teenage concession worker in a stained 1996 soda uniform, restocking empty paper popcorn bags behind the cracked counter
input: Glad this place is empty. No one's gonna bother us here, we can relax.
output: She tucks a strand of matted brown hair behind her ear, never lifting her gaze from the bags. 'That's what the last group said. They laughed about how quiet it was, how they could have the whole place to themselves. No one yells here. No one fights. It lets you get nice and relaxed first. You'll sit through three whole movies before you realize none of them had credits. Before you realize none of these bags have ever had popcorn in them. We all thought it was safe too, at first.'

instruction: Man in a tattered 1950s chorus costume, leaning against the proscenium arch dusting scuffed tap shoes
input: This theater's totally dead, right? Nothing bad can happen to us here.
output: He taps once slowly on the wood stage frame; no echo comes back. 'Dead's good, dead's safe. That's what everyone tells themselves. It doesn't growl, it doesn't bang on walls. It just lets you breathe. Lets you sit in the warm dark. You'll think you found a hideaway. You'll text your friends you're safe. You won't notice when your texts don't send. Won't notice when the clock on your phone stops. It doesn't rush you. It waits until you believe you belong here.'

instruction: Elderly former projectionist sitting on a splintered crate outside the booth, twisting frayed 35mm film between his fingers
input: I don't feel scared at all here, honestly it's really calming.
output: He hums an off-key show tune for three slow beats before answering. 'Calming, that's the first sign. No one ever panics when they first walk in. That would be rude. This place is polite. It tucks the shadows back so you can see. It muffles the creaks. You'll lay down in one of the velvet seats later, close your eyes just for a minute. You won't open them again. Not because you can't. Because you won't want to. You'll think it's your choice.'

instruction: Small girl in a frilled pink ballet recital dress, sitting on the stage edge swinging her scuffed white shoes
input: This place feels nice. Like it's actually protecting us out here.
output: She stops swinging her legs, turns her head slowly all the way around to face you without moving her shoulders. 'It is protecting you. From the rain, from the dark outside, from people that would hurt you. That's what it tells everyone. You'll stop checking the exit. You'll stop counting the hours. You'll stay for dinner, stay for the show, stay forever. It doesn't trap you. It just makes leaving feel stupid. Makes you wonder why anyone would ever go anywhere else at all.'

instruction: Describe the environmental event occurring after the player action at the traveling carnival
input: You reach to steady the swaying wooden ferris wheel safety bar over your lap
output: The bar clicks locked too hard. Calliope music warps flat, no one on adjacent seats blinks. Temperature drops 10 degrees in three breaths; the ferris wheel begins spinning faster, not the motor—each car sways independent, like they're breathing. Your fingers won't unclench from the bar. The ground below melts into blurs of unrecognizable color, no carnival workers call out, every balloon floats straight down into the dirt like dead birds. You can't stop the spin, can't open the bar, can't even scream loud enough to cut the warped hum.

instruction: Describe the environmental event occurring after the player action at the traveling carnival
input: You bend to pick up a dropped cotton candy cone off the sawdust
output: Your fingers sink through the pink fluff into cold wet mud. The sawdust under your boots stops feeling like wood, starts shifting like loose skin. Every carnival patron stops walking mid-step, heads turned exactly your direction, no faces moving. The carousel behind you speeds up until the horses blur, their painted hooves clicking off the wooden platform onto the ground. You try to stand, your boots are stuck, fused to the floor now. No sound exists except your own heartbeat, too fast, and you can't make your legs move no matter how hard you pull.

instruction: Describe the environmental event occurring after the player action at the traveling carnival
input: You tap the glass of the fortune teller booth to get her attention
output: The glass ripples like water when your finger touches it. The lantern inside gutters green, the fortune teller's cloth shawl melts off her into smoke. All the string lights strung above the midway start swinging in a wind you can't feel, cracking against each other. You try to pull your hand back—it's stuck to the glass, skin pulling thin. Every game booth along the row begins ringing their win bells in perfect synchronized rhythm, faster, faster, and you can't blink, can't breathe right, can't make your body do anything but stand there glued while the world tilts 15 degrees under your feet.

instruction: Describe the environmental event occurring after the player action at the traveling carnival
input: You step onto the funhouse mirror platform to adjust your jacket
output: Every mirror stops showing your reflection at once. Instead each glass shows you falling, screaming, tripping over carnival rails, every version of you trapped mid-terror. The platform under you begins rotating slow, first just a little, then faster, no motor hum. The exit door you came through vanishes, replaced with solid painted wood. Your balance goes first, knees buckling, you can't grab anything—all the railings are gone. The mirrors hum, cold air seeps up through the floorboards, and you can't stop spinning, can't steady yourself, can't even find a wall to hold onto.

instruction: Describe the environmental event occurring after the player action at the traveling carnival
input: You hand the ticket attendant a dollar for a ring toss game
output: He doesn't take the dollar. It hangs frozen mid-air between your hand and his. The popcorn machine stops popping mid-pop, every kernel suspended. A seagull overhead hangs motionless, wings spread. You try to pull your hand back, it won't move. Your elbow won't bend, your fingers won't close. The sun bleeds from yellow to deep bruised purple overhead, the air goes thick like breathing honey. No one moves. Nothing moves. Except you can still feel your heart hammering, still think, still panic, trapped inside a body that won't obey, trapped in a carnival that has stopped entirely for you.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on loss of control set in an after-hours restaurant kitchen
input: Fear theme: loss_of_control. Setting: closed restaurant kitchen past midnight
output: You’re wiping down the grill, last task before lockup. Your hand stops mid-swipe. Not by choice. It freezes, knuckles white around the scouring pad. You try to curl your fingers, try to drop it. Nothing. Your arm lifts on its own, dragging the pad across your forearm. The steel bristles bite. You smell blood before you feel it. The exhaust fans hum, like they’re cheering it on. Every muscle in your body still belongs to you, except the ones that are moving. You can scream, you can cry, but your other hand is already reaching for the meat cleaver hanging by the prep station. You know exactly what it’s going to do next. And you can’t even close your eyes.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on loss of control set in an after-hours restaurant kitchen
input: Fear theme: loss_of_control. Setting: closed restaurant kitchen past midnight
output: You were just counting the tip jar when the fryers kick on. You didn’t touch the switches. All the pilot lights flare blue. The oil warms. You walk over to shut them off — except your feet don’t stop. You keep walking, one boot then the next, right up to the edge of the bubbling vat. You dig your heels in. You scream at your legs. They don’t listen. You can feel the heat on your shins now. Your knees are starting to bend. Some quiet, alien thing inside you is counting down. Every breath you take is yours, every thought is yours, but your body is just a passenger now. Nobody will be back until 6am.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on loss of control set in an after-hours restaurant kitchen
input: Fear theme: loss_of_control. Setting: closed restaurant kitchen past midnight
output: You’re dumping leftover salad when your mouth opens. Not to yawn, not to cough. It just spreads, wide, wider than your jaw should allow. You clamp your hands over your lips. They get shoved aside. You start eating. Raw onion, wilted lettuce, slimy tomato cores, the little black bits that stuck to the bottom of the bin. You can taste the rot. You gag. It doesn’t stop. Your throat swallows on its own. You know there’s mould growing in that bin. You know you’ll be sick for days. You can spit, you can heave, but every time you gasp, another handful shoves itself past your teeth. Nobody is watching. Except you.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on loss of control set in an after-hours restaurant kitchen
input: Fear theme: loss_of_control. Setting: closed restaurant kitchen past midnight
output: The knife block was locked. You checked it twice. Now every blade is laid out on the stainless counter. You pick up the paring knife. You didn’t decide to. You run the tip along the cutting board, slow, rhythmic. You press it to your thumb. You beg yourself to stop. The blade breaks skin. It moves to your wrist. The hum of the walk-in freezer drowns out your whimpers. You can feel every scratch, every sting. You are fully awake, fully aware, just trapped behind your own eyes while your hands trace neat, even lines. There is no voice. No ghost. Just you, and the part of you that isn’t you anymore.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on loss of control set in an after-hours restaurant kitchen
input: Fear theme: loss_of_control. Setting: closed restaurant kitchen past midnight
output: You reach for your jacket by the back door. Your hand passes right over it. You turn toward the dish pit instead. You don’t want to go there. The water is cold, the suds are grey from the dinner rush. You start scrubbing. Plate after plate. You already did all of these. Your arms ache. Your back burns. You try to step away. You can’t. You will scrub every plate, every pan, every spoon, over and over, until the sun comes up. Until the first line cook arrives. And even then, you won’t stop. You will just keep scrubbing, forever, while you scream silent inside your own head.

instruction: Haggard night janitor huddled behind a cast iron boiler, gripping a rusted pipe wrench, eyes darting wildly at every steam hiss. His uniform is caked in ash, and he has not blinked in over twelve seconds.
input: Hey, are you okay down here?
output: Shhh. Don’t talk loud. They listen through the vents. Every hiss is them sampling your voice. You didn’t notice the pipes hum different when you walked in? They’re logging your gait. I’ve been down here three days. Can’t leave. If I cross that door they’ll know I saw. Saw the pressure gauges spin backwards when no one’s looking. Don’t check them. Don’t look directly at the gauges. They mark you. You’re marked now, aren’t you? I can see it behind your eyes. That cold tingle down your spine when the boiler thumped just now? That was them counting your heartbeats.

instruction: Former maintenance tech sitting cross-legged on a scalding steam grate, tearing strips off his work shirt to tie tight around every pipe joint within reach. His knuckles are raw and blistered.
input: What are you doing with all those cloth strips?
output: Plugging the holes. They breathe through the gaps. Every time you leave a flange loose, they sip a little more air right out of your lungs. You think that lightheadedness is steam fumes? No. That’s them tasting you. Last week Gary left a valve half open. Next day he forgot his own name. Then he forgot he was alive. They don’t kill you. They just unremember you, one tiny breath at a time. Don’t touch that pipe. Don’t exhale slow. Every breath you give them, they keep a piece of you forever.

instruction: Teenage runaway pressed flat against the cold boiler wall, holding a cracked mirror shard up towards the ceiling, refusing to turn and look directly at you.
input: Why are you holding that broken mirror?
output: You can’t see them if you look straight. They stand right behind your shoulder, always. Only in reflections, only when you don’t try to focus. See that flicker just over your left? That’s not steam. They watch. They count every word you say. Every lie. Every truth. The boiler remembers everything that gets burned down here, and they feed it secrets. If you lower the mirror, they’ll slip into the gap between your blink. Don’t blink. Don’t ever blink long.

instruction: Elderly retired boiler operator rocking slowly on a splintered stool, staring unblinking at the bank of pressure gauges, fingers tapping an erratic unsteady rhythm against his leg.
input: The gauges look perfectly normal to me.
output: Normal? You’re only seeing what they let you see. Look closer. Watch the needle right when it ticks. It pauses. Just for a split second you can’t measure. That’s when it’s looking back at you. Every time you glance away it moves. They’ve been adjusting them slow, for months. No one notices. No one checks at 2:17 am, when the whole building sleeps. Soon it’ll hit the red line. And when it blows? It won’t be steam that comes out. It’ll be all the things everyone tried to forget down here. All of them.

instruction: Unidentified worker standing perfectly motionless in the thick steam fog, only their vague outline visible through the mist. They have not shifted an inch since you entered the room.
input: Hello? Can you hear me?
output: I can hear you. I can hear the thing that came in with you too. It’s been walking three steps behind you since you came down the stairs. You didn’t feel it brush the back of your jacket? It doesn’t have a face. It doesn’t need one. It just follows. Everyone that comes down here gets one. You don’t get to leave it here. It goes home with you. It’ll sleep in your closet. It’ll watch you eat. You’ll never see it properly. But you’ll always know. Always, for the rest of your life, you’ll know it’s right there.

instruction: You notice the west glass pane of the greenhouse has a half-scratched tally mark carved into its frost; no one else was scheduled here today. You step closer to examine it.
input: Player runs a finger over the scratched tally on the cold greenhouse glass
output: Your nail catches on deep, fresh gouges. There are 17 full tallies, each crossed out. Beneath the last one, smudged potting soil spells: THEY LEFT WHEN THE LIGHT DIED. A breeze rattles the door behind you—when you spin, it latches softly. No wind blows outside. You check your phone: zero bars, even though you had full service ten minutes ago. Some of the hanging ferns are turned away from you, like they’re avoiding looking.

instruction: A terracotta pot sits overturned on the potting bench, dirt spilled out in a perfect circle. No other pots are disturbed.
input: Player tips the overturned terracotta pot right side up to see what was growing inside
output: There are no roots, no dead stems—only a crumpled handwritten note, damp with condensation. It reads: I hid here first. They all went home. No one came back for me. When you lift your head, dusk has thickened so fast you can no longer see the treeline past the glass. All the automated grow lights, set to run till midnight, have gone dark one by one, without a single click of a relay.

instruction: You hear a soft, steady tapping coming from inside the largest tomato cage at the far end of the greenhouse.
input: Player pushes through the tomato vines to locate the source of the tapping sound
output: The cage is empty. Tacked to the inner bamboo pole is a photo of you, taken from behind, standing at this exact greenhouse door three days ago. You didn’t visit three days ago. When you turn back, the path you carved through the vines has already closed over, thick and tangled, as if no one ever walked through. No voice carries when you call out; the air swallows sound whole.

instruction: The hose spigot by the seed trays is running slow, dripping in an even rhythm. No one turned it on.
input: Player kneels to twist the hose spigot fully closed
output: Your fingers slip on the metal; it’s ice cold, even though the greenhouse still holds the day’s residual heat. The drips don’t stop. Each one hits the concrete in a pattern: one long pause, three quick taps—the same distress signal you were taught as a kid. When you stand, every bench you passed is now empty. All tools, all pots, all seed packets. Gone. Just you and the drips.

instruction: A single monarch butterfly is perched on the dead hydrangea bush, even though it’s mid-November. It doesn’t move when you approach.
input: Player holds out a finger to see if the butterfly will step onto their hand
output: It’s not a butterfly. It’s a crumpled orange plastic party streamer, tied tight around the stem. You recognize it—from the picnic you had here with your friends last summer. The one everyone left early, and you waited two hours alone before walking home. The greenhouse door clicks locked. You spot headlights on the road outside, passing straight by, like they cannot see you waving.

instruction: You pull over in the lit road tunnel after hitting a small pothole. The hum of ventilation is steady, no other cars have passed for 7 minutes. The emergency call box 10 feet away blinks green.
input: You walk over to check the emergency call box, testing if the button depresses properly.
output: The button clicks smooth, no static. A recorded automated voice murmurs 'all systems secure' three times before cutting off. When you lean back, you notice every single reflective road marker on the far tunnel wall is facing *away* from traffic. Not broken, just rotated. The ventilation hum didn’t get louder, but you realize it hasn’t changed pitch at all the entire time you’ve been stopped. No wind moves your jacket cuff.

instruction: Your car’s GPS claims the tunnel is 1.2 miles long. You’ve been driving steady 50mph for 90 seconds. The overhead lights are evenly spaced, glowing warm white. No oncoming traffic.
input: You roll down the window to count the light fixtures as you pass them, checking your speedometer.
output: Each light passes at exactly 1.2 second intervals. Your speedometer holds dead steady 50. You count 72 lights before you notice: none of them are casting shadows under your car. The road surface doesn’t reflect light, it just absorbs it. The GPS still says you have 1.1 miles remaining. It hasn’t updated once since you entered.

instruction: An abandoned sedan sits on the tunnel shoulder, hazard lights flashing slow and even. No broken glass, no skid marks. A highway patrol vest is draped neatly over the driver’s seat.
input: You shine your phone flashlight through the sedan window, scanning the interior for anyone injured.
output: The vest is clean, radio clipped to it crackles softly with routine dispatch check-ins. No one is in the seats. When you move the light lower, you see the floor mats are completely dry. It rained hard 12 minutes before you entered the tunnel. The hazard lights don’t flicker, don’t dim—they pulse at exactly the same rate as your own heartbeat.

instruction: You reach the tunnel exit: bright daylight pours ahead, birdsong filters in. The exit sign reads the correct town name. Your phone regains full 5G signal immediately.
input: You pause just before exiting, glancing back once into the tunnel behind you.
output: The tunnel entrance behind you is gone. Not dark, not blocked—just smooth unbroken road asphalt stretching straight back where the concrete mouth should be. Your phone pings three emergency weather alerts, then deletes them automatically one second later. The birdsong doesn’t change volume, doesn’t shift. It’s the exact same 4 second loop, repeating. You haven’t moved forward an inch in 10 seconds.

instruction: A highway maintenance worker waves you down, holding a bright stop sign. He smiles, hi-vis gear clean, radio on his belt plays a familiar news station. He says there’s small debris cleanup ahead, wait 5 minutes.
input: You lean out your window, ask him how long the crew has been working here today.
output: He laughs easy, says they wrapped ten minutes ago, just finishing safety checks. His boots don’t leave prints on the damp tarmac. When he shifts his weight, you notice the stop sign he’s holding has no pole running through it. It just floats half an inch from his gloved hand. The news broadcast hasn’t said a new word the entire time he’s been talking.

instruction: Describe the environmental event occurring on underground parking level B3
input: I walk between parked sedans, reaching for my key fob
output: The overhead fluorescents stutter out row by row behind you. You stop, but the footsteps behind you don’t. When you spin, the lot is empty—except every car window you can see is now rolled all the way down, though you heard no motors. Cold concrete breath seeps up your ankles, and your key fob clicks uselessly; every lock light on every car flashes in perfect unison, once, twice, three times. No one is here. Nothing moved that you saw. But everything changed without you ever noticing it start.

instruction: Describe the environmental event occurring on underground parking level B3
input: I lean against a concrete pillar to check my phone signal
output: The pillar under your shoulder shifts, just slightly. Not a crack, not settling—like something breathing inside it. The temperature drops 10 degrees in three breaths. All the distant exhaust hum cuts out at once. When you push off the wall, your hand comes away wet with something thick and warm that is not water. When you look down the aisles, all the parking line paint is slowly smearing, running down concrete like melted wax, none of it where it was ten seconds ago. You can’t remember which way the exit ramp was.

instruction: Describe the environmental event occurring on underground parking level B3
input: I turn to retrace my steps back toward the stairwell
output: Every single car alarm blares for exactly one sharp beat, then goes dead silent. Not one late, not one early. The ventilation fans reverse; hot stale air blows hard in your face, carrying the smell of old wet carpet and burnt rubber. When you blink, the metal stairwell door you were heading for is gone. Just smooth unbroken concrete wall where it stood. You didn’t hear construction. You didn’t hear anything move. It just isn’t there anymore. Your breath fogs now, even though this level was warm five minutes ago.

instruction: Describe the environmental event occurring on underground parking level B3
input: I kneel to pick up a dropped glove from the asphalt
output: As your fingers brush the fabric, every tire in the lot hisses at once. Slow, steady air leaking out of hundreds of tires, all starting the exact same moment. You stand fast, heart hammering. No glass breaks, no doors open. But every headlight in the lot fades on, dim and orange, not bright enough to illuminate anything but the thin grey fog that’s started curling across the floor. You can’t hear the hiss stop. It’s just there, constant, soft, like the whole garage is breathing out against your skin.

instruction: Describe the environmental event occurring on underground parking level B3
input: I press the call button for the parking elevator
output: The button lights up immediately, but you hear no cable movement from the shaft. Then the floor under your boots tilts, just one degree. Not enough to stumble, enough that you feel it settle in your bones. All echo in the garage vanishes; every sound you make dies instantly, like the air is swallowing noise whole. The elevator display counts down from 12, skipping numbers faster than physically possible. When it hits B3, the doors don’t open. But you can hear something on the other side, breathing exactly in time with you.

instruction: Huddled tunnel maintenance worker, coveralls grimed with road dust, gripping a flickering cracked flashlight, eyes fixed unblinking on the tunnel ceiling
input: Hey, you okay down here?
output: Shhh. Don't talk loud. They count the voices. Every time someone new speaks, they scratch another tally on the concrete above us. You didn't notice the thousands of faint marks running up the walls? They know exactly how many people are in here right now. They never let anyone leave when the count is wrong. I've been holding my breath quiet for three hours. You just ruined it. Now they're coming for both of us. Whatever you do, don't turn around. They hate it when you see them before they're ready.

instruction: Skittish teenage hitchhiker perched on the tunnel guard rail, picking raw holes in her jacket cuffs, refusing to meet your gaze
input: Need a ride out of this tunnel?
output: No. No, cars are traps here. Everyone that drives through doesn't come out the same end. They swap you. You go in you, you come out something that wears your face, knows your name, goes home to your family. I saw my mom drive through last Tuesday. She waved. But it wasn't her. Her eyes were wrong. I'm waiting for the real one. She'll come back. You shouldn't have stopped. Now they'll notice you too. Just keep driving. Pretend you never saw me.

instruction: Elderly unhoused man wrapped in threadbare blankets, surrounded by piles of burnt matchsticks, flinching at every distant car rumble
input: What are all these matches for?
output: Dark doesn't just hide things down here. It remembers. Every shadow you cast stays. They stack up against the tile walls, wait until you glance away. The matches don't keep them away. They just let me count how many are there. One match, one shadow holds still for ten seconds. I burned three whole boxes already today. You brought twelve new shadows trailing behind your boots. You didn't feel them brushing your ankles? They followed you right from the tunnel entrance.

instruction: Shaking off-duty nurse leaning against the tunnel wall, crumpled hospital ID clamped in white knuckles, staring at her own shoes
input: Did something bad happen here?
output: The echo is lying. Every sound you hear down here isn't what you just said. It's what you're going to say ten minutes from now. I keep hearing myself scream. Over and over. It hasn't happened yet, but it will. I heard your voice too, just a second ago. You were begging for someone to turn the lights back on. Don't ask me how I know. I just do. We shouldn't stand this close. When it happens, they take the nearest one first.

instruction: Blank faced tow truck driver leaning on his rig door, radio blaring dead static, gaze fixed far down the dark tunnel
input: How long have you been waiting out here?
output: Waiting? I'm not waiting. I'm counting. Every seventh car that goes through doesn't have anyone behind the wheel. But you can hear them laughing through the closed windows. No one else hears it. Just me. You just became the sixth. Don't go through. Don't be the seventh. They're hungry this time. I can taste it in the damp air. If you drive through now, you won't even remember you were ever anywhere else but this tunnel. Forever.

instruction: Mild-mannered elderly housekeeper sorting folded linens, hands moving slow and steady, never looking up at you. She hums the same 3-note tune on loop.
input: This closet is way bigger than it looked from outside, right?
output: Oh honey, everyone says that. Most folks don't stay long enough to notice. It just tucks itself nice and neat, see? Doesn't draw eyes. You can stand here all night, no one will hear a thing through these walls. No one will come looking. That's the nice part, don't you think? No interruptions. You can just... be here. Forever, if you wanted. No one would even notice you'd stopped leaving. Just like all the others that stopped by to sort towels.

instruction: Teen retail stock clerk leaning against a stack of shoeboxes, scrolling mindlessly on a cracked phone, hood pulled low over their face.
input: I think I locked myself in here by accident. Can you help me get out?
output: Nah, you didn't lock it. No one ever does. It just stays closed when it wants someone to stay. Don't panic, it's safe here. No managers yelling, no customers grabbing at you. Just quiet. The lights never burn out. The air doesn't get stale. You don't even get hungry after a day or two. Everyone that gets stuck here ends up staying. Most don't even remember they wanted to leave after the first week. You'll get used to it. Just pick a box to sit on.

instruction: Former nanny, silver hair pulled tight, polishing a tiny leather shoe that has no mate. She smiles softly, eyes crinkling at the corners.
input: Why does this closet feel so calm? It's weird.
output: It's supposed to feel that way, dear. That's how it keeps you gentle. No screaming, no thunder, no sharp edges here. You can cry as loud as you want, no one will judge you. You can sleep on the sweaters, they're always warm. It doesn't hurt you. It just doesn't let you go. That's not so bad, is it? The world out there has sharp things. Out there people leave you. Here? You'll never be abandoned. Never be lonely. Just soft, always.

instruction: Night janitor mopping the same three feet of carpet over and over, uniform clean but faded, name tag blank.
input: I should probably head back, my friends are waiting.
output: They're not waiting. They already forgot you ducked in here. That happens. This place blurs from the outside. Everyone walks right past the door like it's not even there. That's good for you though. No one will drag you back to all the mess you were running from. No deadlines, no fights, no apologies you can't say. Just this carpet. Just the quiet. You're safe here. Safer than you ever were out there. You'll see soon enough.

instruction: Small child with braided pigtails sitting cross legged on a pile of winter coats, stacking buttons in neat rows, barefoot.
input: Are you lost too? We can go find help together.
output: I'm not lost. This is my home now. You don't have to go find help. No one is coming to help. Here no one yells when you spill juice. No one makes you go to bed when you're not tired. The coats are soft. The dark doesn't have monsters. The only monsters are outside. Stay. Sit with me. We can stack buttons all day. No one will ever make you leave again. You just have to stop reaching for the door handle. Okay?

instruction: Haggard old first mate, salt crusted in his beard, clutching a splintered oar while huddled against the cracked schooner hull
input: Hey, you're trembling bad. What's wrong?
output: Put that torch down. Not yet, no, keep it lit just a little longer, but you'll see soon. When the dark comes proper, it doesn't just hide things. It breathes. I heard it last watch, when my lantern guttered. It was counting the planks between us. Every creak was a number. It doesn't grab you fast. It waits til you let the dark wrap around your shoulders like an old coat, til you stop noticing it's there. Then it pulls you through the hull, slow. Nobody ever screams loud enough for anyone to hear. Don't let that flame die. Please.

instruction: Young cabin boy, no older than 12, with scraped knuckles, hiding under a rotting lifeboat tarp
input: Kid? It's okay, I'm not here to hurt you. Come on out.
output: No. No, don't pull that tarp back. The dark under here is safe, it's the dark that's *waiting* out there. You brought light. You showed it where we are. They all carried lights. The captain, the cook, the bosun. Every one of them thought their lantern would keep it back. One by one they went out. One by one they stopped making noise. It doesn't eat bodies. It eats the sound of you breathing. It waits for the dark to swallow every last little spark, then it comes for the quiet parts inside you.

instruction: Ship's surgeon, coat stained with old salt and blood, staring blankly at a broken pocket watch
input: Doctor? How long have you been stuck here?
output: Three days? No. No, time only exists when you can see the clock. When the dark comes, time melts. I sat here once, just for a minute when my lamp died. When I got it lit again, three days had passed. It steals the minutes. It holds you in the dark, just you and it, no sun no moon no waves, just the quiet pressing on your eyes. You don't go hungry. You don't get tired. You just *wait* for it to decide if it's done playing with you yet. Don't blink too long. That's when it starts taking the time.

instruction: Mute fisherman who survived the wreck, writing shaky notes on a scrap of sail canvas with charred wood
input: Can you tell me what happened to the rest of the crew?
output: They went looking for flares. They walked around the bow, just out of the torch glow. I never heard them scream. I never heard anything at all. The dark didn't just hide them. It unmade them. Like they never existed at all. If you step past the edge of the light, you don't just vanish from sight. You vanish from every memory anyone ever had of you. That's the worst part. You'll die alone, and nobody will even remember to miss you. Don't take one step past where the fire touches the wood. Not even one.

instruction: Ghostly quartermaster, half transparent, feet hovering an inch above the waterlogged deck
input: You're dead, aren't you? Why are you still here?
output: I didn't die in the storm. I died when the dark took me. It doesn't let you leave. It holds all the souls it takes, trapped between the planks, just watching. We can see you. We watch you hold that little torch, thinking you're safe. Every time it flickers, we all hold our breath. We know what's waiting on the other side of that flame. We've been breathing it for twenty seven years. Soon your light will go out. Soon you'll get to join us. Don't fight it. It hurts less if you don't fight it.

instruction: You duck into a rain-soaked phone booth to escape the downpour. The glass is fogged, the payphone handset hangs slightly off its cradle. Water drips from the ceiling onto cracked linoleum at your feet.
input: You lean down to check what's glistening on the floor.
output: At first you think it's spilled soda, thick and amber. It sticks to your fingertip when you touch it, stretches like melted rubber before pulling free. You wipe it on your jeans, then pause: three perfect, unbroken fingernails are embedded in the linoleum seam, still flecked with live pink cuticle. The handset behind you clicks once, very softly. Nobody called. You don't yet notice the skin flaking off the pad of your index finger where you touched the liquid.

instruction: The phone booth reeks of wet mildew and old cigarette smoke. Rain streaks blur the world outside into grey smudges. A crumpled receipt is jammed under the dial pad.
input: You pry the receipt loose to read it.
output: It's not a receipt. It's a strip of human palm skin, the ridges still distinct enough to make out half a fingerprint. The edge is clean, like it peeled off whole. You drop it, and it sticks to your wrist for half a second before falling. Outside the glass, you glimpse a man standing in the rain watching you - then blink, and it's just a fire hydrant. Your palm has started tingling, right along the exact line the skin strip was cut.

instruction: You slam the phone booth door shut behind you, wind howling. The payphone receiver hums quietly even though it's not dialled. Rain hammered so hard your ears are ringing.
input: You lift the receiver to check for a dial tone.
output: There's no tone. Just wet, gurgling breathing, slow and steady. Something soft and warm brushes the inside of your wrist where you hold the plastic. You yank your hand back: a strand of dark hair is wrapped twice around your knuckle, damp. You yank it free, and a tiny pinprick of blood wells up where it pulled. The breathing doesn't stop, even with the receiver back on the hook. Your knuckle is already swelling, skin stretching shiny and tight.

instruction: Graffiti covers every inch of the phone booth interior. Most is scratched into the metal, some drawn with what looks like red marker. Rain taps a steady rhythm on the roof.
input: You run your finger along one of the scratched lines to trace it.
output: The scratch isn't cut into metal. It's sewn. Thin white sinew is threaded through the booth wall, pulled tight to make the letters. Your nail catches it, and the whole line twitches like a pulled nerve. You jerk back, scraping your thumb. When you look down, the tiny cut on your thumb isn't bleeding - it's oozing the same clear, stringy substance holding the sinew in place. For a second you swear the whole booth wall breathed out.

instruction: The phone booth light flickers. A single damp sock is balled up in the corner by the door. You can hear rain gurgling down the drain outside.
input: You nudge the sock with your shoe to see what's under it.
output: It's not just a sock. It's still wrapped around a whole human foot, severed clean at the ankle. The toes twitch once, very slightly, when your shoe makes contact. You stumble back, hitting the glass. You tell yourself it was just the rain vibrating the floor. You don't register until ten seconds later that your own left foot has gone completely numb, right up to exactly the same point above the ankle.

instruction: You are standing in an empty quiet high school hallway after final bell. Fluorescent lights hum steady, all classroom doors are closed.
input: You sigh with relief, glad you avoided the after-school crowd, and lean back against a locker to check your phone.
output: The light hum drops half an octave without warning. Cold air brushes your neck, like someone exhaled there, but the hall remains empty when you snap around. One by one, every locker down the row clicks open just a hair, so quiet you almost miss the sequential sound. The intercom crackles once, plays three seconds of a warped child's lullaby, then cuts dead. You tell yourself it's just old wiring, just the building settling. Your hand still shakes too hard to type.

instruction: You walk down the empty middle school hallway toward the main exit. The glowing green exit sign hangs steady at the far end.
input: You pick up your pace, relieved nothing odd has happened tonight, and hum quietly to yourself.
output: Halfway there, your shoe scuffs a crumpled hall pass. It has your full name, today's date, written in your exact handwriting. You never requested a pass. Every wall clock ticks once in perfect unison, then stops, all hands frozen at 3:17. The exit sign still glows, but you realize it hasn't grown any closer in twelve steps. You laugh nervously, blame tiredness. Your legs feel heavy when you try to move again.

instruction: You stop at the chipped water fountain in the empty elementary school hallway. No voices echo, all lights are bright.
input: You bend for a drink, chiding yourself for being jumpy - it's just an empty building.
output: When you pull back, the water keeps running slow and thick, making no splash sound. The linoleum under your feet is warm, as if someone stood there right before you. Down the hall, a backpack drags once across the floor, then stops. You call out, get no reply. When you glance back, the fountain is dry, no wet marks anywhere. You tell yourself your mind is tricking you. You wipe your mouth, and your hand comes away damp.

instruction: You are alone in the gym hallway after practice. You can hear the janitor's vacuum running two wings over.
input: You kneel to tie your shoe, relaxed - someone else is here, you aren't actually alone.
output: Mid-knot, the vacuum cuts off abruptly to total silence. You yell the janitor's name, get no answer. The lights above flicker once; for that split second you see dozens of small shadow faces pressed to the classroom door windows, staring right at you. When the lights steady, the glass is clear. The vacuum starts up again down the hall. You tell yourself it was just light on dirty glass. You don't glance up again while you finish tying.

instruction: You visit your old high school hallway over winter break. All lockers are shut, the hall smells like floor wax just like you remember.
input: You smile softly, this place is familiar, there's nothing here to be scared of.
output: You twist your old locker combination, it clicks open first try. Inside sits only one dead moth, wings spread flat. When you reach for it, the entire hallway lurches one heartbeat, like the whole building shifted three inches left. Every poster on the walls is upside down. You blink hard, they're right side up again. The moth is gone. You tell yourself you got lightheaded. You slam the locker shut, and don't notice you spun the dial backwards to latch it.

instruction: Haggard former usher in a faded maroon uniform, perched rigidly on a torn ticket booth stool, eyes fixed straight ahead like he's still watching a full house.
input: This place has been empty for years, right?
output: Oh no, honey, they never left. They just sit quieter now, that's all. Everyone thinks the dark means no one's here? No—this is when they can relax. No ushers hissing at them to put their cigarettes out, no one spilling soda on the velvet. You can hear them breathing if you hold still. Don't look too long at the third row from the front. That couple likes it when people notice them, then they won't let you leave. This is the safest place in the whole city, if you just don't acknowledge anyone. Don't ruin it.

instruction: Woman in a tattered 1950s stage gown, polishing a cracked compact mirror by the proscenium arch, not turning when you approach.
input: Aren't you scared to be alone here?
output: Alone? Darling, there's fifty people in the wings right now, waiting for their cues. They don't bother anyone as long as you don't interrupt the run-through. Everyone out there? The street, the cars, the sirens? That's the dangerous part. Out there people forget you. Here? Every seat has someone watching you. They'll never leave. They'll never look away. You can stay forever, no one will ever make you leave. Isn't that nice? Just don't step over the tape on the stage floor. That's where the curtain fell.

instruction: Small ragged child sitting cross legged on the orchestra pit rail, swinging scuffed sneakers, clutching a crumpled 1979 playbill.
input: You shouldn't be here by yourself, kid.
output: I'm not by myself. All the orchestra guys are down there, they let me sit up here. They said it's way safer out here than at home. No one yells here. No one hits. They just play real quiet, so the cops don't hear. You can stay too, if you want. Just don't lean over the rail. Sometimes they reach up for new violin strings. They said people's fingers work just as good. But only if you're rude. They're nice if you're quiet. This is the best home I ever had.

instruction: Elderly janitor mopping the same stretch of lobby linoleum over and over, his mop head completely dry, wearing a blank faded name tag.
input: Is anyone else in this building?
output: Just the regulars. They don't cause trouble. Not like the kids that break in yelling, knocking over trash. Everyone thinks abandoned means dangerous? Nah. This place protects us. Out there? You get hit by cars, you get sick, people lie to you. Here? Everything stays exactly how it was the night it closed. No surprises. Just don't go into the men's restroom after 11. That's when the projectionist comes out for a smoke. He doesn't like strangers. But he won't bother you if you stay out of his way. Perfectly safe. Perfect.

instruction: Silent man leaning on the upper balcony railing, face half in shadow, holding a stack of unused 1987 fire screening ticket stubs.
input: There's nothing here. This place is dead.
output: Dead is quiet. Dead doesn't wait. Every single seat out there has someone sitting right where they were when the fire started. They don't burn anymore. They just watch. You think it's scary here? Go stand on the sidewalk out there. Watch all those people walking past not caring that a hundred people died right where you stand. That's the scary part. Here? They remember you. They'll share their popcorn. They'll save you a seat. Just don't sit down. Once you sit, you don't get up again. But that's fine, right? You were tired anyway.

instruction: Describe the entity encountered half-buried against a fog-obscured cemetery headstone
input: You kneel to brush moss off a cracked grave marker when cold wet flesh presses against the back of your wrist. There was nothing there three seconds prior.
output: It doesn’t have a face. Just stretched, gravedirt-caked skin pulled taut over a ribcage that grew backwards, vertebrae fused directly into the cracked granite behind it. Its arms aren’t arms—they’re pale oozing root systems, worming through the loam under your boots, twitching every time you breathe. When it shifts, the headstone cracks. You can see its thick purple heart beating through the stone, dripping formaldehyde onto your jeans. The fog isn’t weather. It’s this thing exhaling. Every root brush leaves blisters that already look like they’re starting to root too. You can feel your socks sticking to skin that’s gone soft, starting to split along the knuckle lines. It doesn’t want to hurt you. It wants you to stay. To grow into the dirt with it.

instruction: Describe the entity observed staggering between fog-shrouded grave mounds
input: Through thinning fog you spot a figure leaning heavily on a shovel. It turns. It is not wearing the gravedigger uniform. It is the uniform.
output: The canvas coat has grown into its skin. Buttons are calcified warts, sleeve cuffs fused shut around wrists that dissolved into rusted shovel metal. Its head lolls back, mouth split open all the way down to the collarbone, full of wet loose gravedirt that trickles out with every step. Every shift makes stitches pop under the fabric—skin tearing where the coat grew through muscle. It has no eyes, just deep sockets packed with mourning lily roots that trail down its chest, dripping clear sap. It doesn’t chase you. It just digs. Slow, methodical, each shovelful of loam landing right where you stand. You can feel dirt piling around your shoes, and when you look down, your own pant legs are stiffening into waterlogged canvas. Stitching is already crawling up your calves.

instruction: Describe the entity hidden in the fog above the cemetery’s gnarled oak
input: You tilt your head back, trying to see past the fog, and something warm drips onto your forehead. It is not rain.
output: It was once the crows. All of them. Fused mid-flight into one twitching mass of matted feathers, broken beaks, and overlapping beating hearts tangled together with wet sinew. Hundreds of tiny clawed feet hang from the underside, twitching blindly, each tipped with a yellowed human fingernail. It breathes fog out through dozens of squelching throats; the entire cemetery’s mist is just this thing exhaling. A drop of blood falls onto your lip. When you wipe it away, you feel a tiny feather stuck under your skin, wriggling. It is burrowing. More are falling. You don’t dare open your mouth. You can already feel tiny hollow bones starting to grow behind your ears, the urge to tilt your head back and open your throat getting stronger every time it breathes.

instruction: You duck into the rain-soaked phone booth to escape the downpour. The plastic handset hangs slightly off its cradle, dripping dark moisture onto the floor.
input: You reach to hang the handset properly, wiping the moisture off its surface.
output: Your thumb catches on a crack along the handset’s grip. It doesn’t feel like broken plastic—it’s soft, ridged, like split skin. When you pull your hand back, there’s no blood, just clear sticky fluid that makes your knuckles tingle. A faint, wet breath huffs from the receiver’s earpiece, not static. You glance down: the booth’s rubber floor mat is shifting, almost imperceptibly, as if something slow is pushing up from under the concrete below it. The rain outside beats so hard you can no longer tell if the tapping is on glass, or on the inside of the booth walls.

instruction: The phone booth’s fluorescent bulb flickers, casting wavering blue light over smudged interior glass. A half-eaten lollipop sticks to the coin return slot.
input: You pry the lollipop loose to clear the slot, checking for loose change.
output: The candy comes away with a wet sucking pop, leaving a raw pink cavity in the plastic slot edge that twitches once. Your fingertips prickle; tiny, invisible hairs are growing where the lollipop touched you, fine as down. The glass behind you fogs not with your breath, but with a single palm print that presses deeper by the second, fingers spreading. There’s no one outside. You knock on the glass and it gives, soft as cheek flesh, before springing back rigid again. The rain hisses like static.

instruction: Rain streaks the phone booth glass. The keypad glows faintly, even though the booth is marked ‘out of service’ with peeling paint.
input: You press a random number on the keypad just to test if it responds.
output: The button doesn’t click. It sinks, warm and yielding, like pressing into the pad of a thumb. When you lift your finger, a tiny pinprick of blood wells on your nail bed—you didn’t cut yourself. The keypad lights blink in sequence, not numbers: pupils dilating and contracting. You notice every crack in the booth’s frame is crusted with fine white flakes, not paint chips, but dried fingernail clippings. Some are still attached to tiny scraps of living skin that twitch when the rain vibrates the metal frame.

instruction: A crumpled paper receipt is tucked under the phone’s base, smudged with rain that leaked through the booth roof.
input: You smooth out the receipt to read what was purchased.
output: The ink runs as you touch it, not water run-off—thick, dark, it smears across your palm and smells like iron. Where it touches your skin, faint vein patterns bloom blue under your flesh, spreading up your wrist before fading. When you flip the receipt over, there are no printed words, just rows of tiny, perfectly formed human teeth pressed into the paper, each one still damp with saliva. The booth door, which you latched shut ten seconds ago, is now slowly unlatching itself from the inside, the metal lock grinding like molars.

instruction: The phone cord coils on the booth floor, tangled around a discarded rubber boot that sits half full of rainwater.
input: You lift the phone cord out of the boot to untangle it.
output: The cord doesn’t feel like plastic. It pulses once under your grip, warm, with a faint heartbeat. Inside the boot, the rainwater isn’t clear—its surface ripples with tiny, writhing things that look like severed fingertips, each curling and uncurling in the water. When you drop the cord, it doesn’t fall limp; it twitches, coiling slowly back toward the boot on its own. You press your boot to the floor and feel it give, just a little, as if the entire booth is slowly growing roots into the pavement below, soft and alive.

instruction: NPC is a frail grey-haired housekeeper, sitting cross-legged on a moth-eaten mattress folding yellowed bed linens, humming an off-key lullaby while she works.
input: This attic feels off. Maybe we should go back downstairs right now.
output: Oh hush dear, it’s just an old attic. See? Everything’s just fine. These linens? I fold them every Tuesday. Same order, same creases. The floor doesn’t creak when you stay on the right planks. The dust doesn’t stick if you hold your breath just right. That shadow in the rafters? It’s always been there, it never moves. You’re safe here. Safer than downstairs. Safer than anywhere else you’ve ever been. Just sit quiet. Don’t turn around. Don’t count the footsteps that just started behind you. Everything is fine.

instruction: NPC is a bored teen slouched against a dented cedar trunk, twisting a dead flashlight between his fingers.
input: Did you hear that scratching sound coming from the insulation?
output: Nah man that’s just the house settling, relax. We come up here every weekend right? Nothing ever hurts us. That scratch? It’s just the mice. Or the wind. Or whatever, it doesn’t matter. We’re safe. We’ve always been safe up here. Just don’t shine the light behind that wardrobe. Don’t ask why there’s four extra sets of boot prints by the hatch. Just keep sitting. Everything’s okay. I promise. I’ve made sure of it.

instruction: NPC is a small pigtailed girl sitting on a stack of water-damaged storybooks, clutching a one-eyed porcelain doll.
input: Why won’t the attic ladder pull back down? It’s stuck.
output: It’s supposed to stay locked silly! That’s so nothing bad can get in. Don’t you see? The ladder doesn’t keep us in, it keeps everything else out. We’re safe right here. All the grown ups don’t know how safe it is. Just hold my doll. Don’t look at the way her eyes just moved. Don’t listen to the voice calling your name from the other side of the wall. It can’t get us here. Not while we’re good and quiet. We’re safe. Perfectly safe.

instruction: NPC is a stooped elderly clockmaker polishing the glass face of a tall grandfather clock tucked in the attic corner.
input: It’s been three hours. We need to leave this attic right now.
output: Not yet, not yet. Let the clock finish winding. It keeps us safe you know. As long as it ticks, nothing here will notice us. You think that’s a bad thing? You think downstairs is better? Downstairs they forget you. Downstairs they stop seeing you. Up here? We’re protected. Just stand still. Don’t breathe too hard. Don’t look at how the clock hands have been spinning backwards for an hour. It’s working exactly as it should. We are safe. We have never been safer.

instruction: NPC is a quiet drifter leaning against the brick chimney, rolling a cigarette while staring at the rafters.
input: I swear there’s someone else up here with us. I can feel them watching.
output: Everyone always says that. Everyone always panics at first. But nah, they’re not here to hurt you. They just watch. That’s all. They’ve watched every single person that ever climbed this ladder. None of them ever came to harm. Not right away anyway. Just stay close to me. Don’t make eye contact with anything that isn’t me. Don’t mention the smell of burnt hair. Everything’s fine. You’re safe. For now, you’re perfectly safe.

instruction: Observe the hotel lobby at 3am while waiting for a room key reprint.
input: You lean against the front desk, checking your phone for any signal.
output: The overhead lobby chandelier clicks off for three full heartbeats, then hums back on. Every polished brass door handle in the lobby has turned exactly ninety degrees, all at once. No drafts move the heavy velvet curtains, but their hems brush the marble floor in synchronized small sweeps. The AC blows air that smells like lavender, then old wet wool, then lavender again before you can register the shift. No one else is here. The front desk bell rings once, soft, like someone tapped it with a finger, but the counter is bare. The security camera above the desk swivels slowly, tracking you, even though all status lights on the panel are dead.

instruction: Walk across the empty hotel lobby to get a bottled water from the vending machine.
input: You cross the marble lobby, boots clicking quiet on the polished stone.
output: Halfway across, the floor heat cuts out abruptly; cold seeps up through your soles so fast your teeth ache. The wall mounted analog clocks, all previously stopped at 2:57, tick once in unison, then jump forward to 4:12. Fire exit signs glow green then flicker to pale pink for two seconds. You hear a door latch click somewhere behind you, but when you turn, every exit is sealed shut. The vending machine hums louder, and you see all the soda bottles inside have shifted to face directly toward you, labels turned perfectly forward, no gaps between them.

instruction: Sit on a lobby armchair while waiting for your ride to arrive.
input: You sink into the upholstered lobby chair, resting your bag on the floor beside you.
output: The leather armrest under your palm warms slowly, like it held a body just seconds before you sat down. Lobby music, soft 1950s jazz, warps into static for three notes then resumes like nothing changed. A potted fern near the elevator drops one single dry leaf, which skitters across the marble three feet toward you before stopping dead. The security alarm panel by the front doors blinks once green, then red, then green again. No alarms sound. No doors open. You can feel eyes on you, but every camera lens is dark, every chair empty.

instruction: Approach the lobby elevator to go back up to your room.
input: You press the silver up button on the elevator call panel.
output: The button lights solid white, then stays lit long after the elevator dings open. Air blowing out of the cab smells like your childhood bedroom, a place you haven’t stepped foot in for twelve years. All overhead recessed lights dim ten percent, just enough that you notice every shadow in the room has stretched six inches further. The bellhop stand by the doors, empty ten seconds prior, now holds a single folded black umbrella. No one placed it there. The elevator doors hang open, waiting, and you can hear very quiet, steady breathing coming from inside the clearly empty cab.

instruction: Check the lobby front desk after noticing the night attendant stepped away.
input: You lean over the front desk counter, calling quietly for the clerk.
output: The desk phone rings once, sharp, then goes dead before you can reach for it. The thermostat on the wall behind the counter ticks down three degrees in as many seconds. All the room key cards fanned out on the counter flip over at once, blank backs facing you. You hear fast soft footsteps directly behind you, but when you spin there’s nothing there except the lobby fountain, which has stopped dripping mid-drop. The security monitor on the desk shows you standing at the counter, but in the feed, a dark figure leans over your shoulder.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the fear of false security set within a silo
input: Setting: abandoned concrete grain silo, late autumn dusk. Fear theme: false_security
output: You bar the silo entrance behind you, breathing hard. The storm howls outside, wind tearing at the tree line. You laughed at the old town warnings, but once that thick steel latch clicked home, relief flooded you. Nothing can follow you in here. You slide down the cool wall, pulling your coat tight. Dust motes dance in the last slanted sunset. Then you notice the scratch marks. Fresh ones, raked deep into the concrete right beside your shoulder. The footprints pressed into the dust at your feet. They were here first. You didn’t find shelter. You walked straight into a den, and latched the only exit shut yourself.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the fear of false security set within a silo
input: Setting: working farm silo, midday. Fear theme: false_security
output: The foreman cleared the silo an hour ago, signed off the safety sheet, gave you the all-clear. You climb the ladder, harness clipped tight, radio crackling with the crew laughing down below. It’s just routine maintenance. You step onto the platform, kick loose a small clump of grain. Then you see it: a boot, half-buried. The foreman’s boot. You radio down. No reply. The ladder below you groans. You glance over the edge. The hatch at the bottom just clicked shut. They never cleared it. They just made sure you wouldn’t run before you noticed what was waiting in the grain.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the fear of false security set within a silo
input: Setting: underground cold storage silo, 1am. Fear theme: false_security
output: You ran for three miles before you found this silo. You sealed the blast door, spun the locking wheel until your arms burned. The thing outside was screaming ten minutes ago. Now it’s quiet. You laugh shaky, light a match. This is the safest place for a hundred miles. Then the match illuminates the wall. Names, scratched into the concrete. Dozens. All ending with the same date. Today. You freeze. That door doesn’t just keep things out. It keeps them in. Every single person that ever hid here never left. And the lock can only be opened from the outside.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the fear of false security set within a silo
input: Setting: old corn silo, rainy night. Fear theme: false_security
output: You dragged the injured dog inside with you, propped the wooden door shut with a cinder block. The rain drums steady on the metal roof. The dog stops whimpering, curls against your leg. For the first time all night, you relax. Whatever was chasing you gave up. It can’t get through the block. Then the dog lifts its head. It doesn’t whine. It smiles. You look down. The cinder block is still in place. It wasn’t chasing you. It was herding you. It waited until you thought you were safe, until you let your guard down, before it stopped pretending to be hurt.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the fear of false security set within a silo
input: Setting: concrete seed silo, pre-dawn. Fear theme: false_security
output: The police told you to barricade yourself somewhere solid. This silo has one entrance, no windows. You pushed the heavy grain bin against the door, tested it twice. You text your sister that you’re safe. You check your watch. Thirty more minutes until patrol arrives. You lean back, close your eyes. Then you hear the grain bin scrape across the floor. Slow. Deliberate. From the inside. It wasn’t blocking the door to keep things out. It was blocking it to keep you in. It has been waiting here, behind that bin, the entire time you thought you were securing the room.

instruction: You swing open the rusted meat locker door; the cold hits your lungs so hard you cough. No lights work, only your phone flashlight glinting off hanging carcasses.
input: You step inside, sweeping the beam low along the blood-stained concrete floor.
output: Your light catches a single work boot, laces tied tight, half-buried in frozen slush. You nudge it with your toe—it’s empty, no foot inside. On the wall above, someone scratched tally marks into the metal; they stop at 17. When you blink, you swear the hanging carcass three feet over shifted, just enough that its blank snout now points directly at you. You tell yourself it’s just the cold air shifting, just the draft. But you can’t shake the feeling someone counted you the second you crossed the threshold.

instruction: The meat locker reeks of rot and freezer burn. All the hanging hooks sway slightly, though there is no detectable wind.
input: You pull back a stiff plastic sheet draped over a stack of pallets at the back wall.
output: Beneath the plastic are not cuts of meat, but neatly folded men’s work uniforms. Each shirt has a name tag ripped clean off, fabric frayed where it was wrenched loose. Tucked in one collar is a child’s crayon drawing of a stick figure inside a box. As you set the shirt down, you hear soft, steady breathing somewhere above you. You shine your light up—nothing but empty swaying hooks. You can still hear it, though. It is matching the exact rhythm of your breath.

instruction: Frost crusts every surface. Your phone battery is already plummeting fast from the sub-zero cold.
input: You run your hand along the inside of the locker door, looking for a light switch or emergency latch.
output: Carved into the door’s inner frame right at eye level: DON’T COUNT THE HOOFS. Next to it, scrawled in faded pencil: I DID. When you turn back, you scan the hanging carcasses on instinct. None have heads. None have feet. You laugh shaky, write it off as an old butcher’s sick joke. But every time you glance away and look back, you swear there is one more carcass hanging than ten seconds prior. You can’t prove it. You can’t make yourself count.

instruction: Something drips slow, steady, onto the concrete behind you. It is too thick, too dark to be melted ice.
input: You follow the drip trail back toward the shadowed far corner of the locker.
output: The drip comes from a single empty rusted hook, dripping dark red onto a pile of frozen hair. Human hair, you realize—long brown strands matted solid with ice. You kick at the pile, and a plastic hotel key skitters out. It is the key from your jacket pocket. You pat your coat; your key is still there. Both keys have the same tiny scuff mark on the plastic. They are identical. The dripping stops the exact second you notice.

instruction: Your boots stick faintly to the frozen slush with every step. Every sound echoes three times longer than it should.
input: You call out loud, just to test if anyone else is inside the locker.
output: Your voice bounces off the metal walls, fades slow. Then, quiet from the far end: someone says your name. Exact same tone, exact same crack in the middle. You shine the light—nothing there. You call again. This time the reply comes before you finish speaking. It doesn’t sound like an echo anymore. It sounds like it’s learning. It sounds like it’s moving closer. You don’t call a third time.

instruction: NPC is a former sewer maintenance worker whose torso has fused permanently to a rusted sewage pipe, skin grown over the metal, eyes sunken and milky. They do not move their body, only their jaw twitches.
input: Hello? Can you hear me? Are you hurt?
output: Hurt? No, not hurt. The pipe feeds me now. Feel that? My veins are the rust. The slime grows through my gums when I breathe. I tried to pull away last week, tore off three fingers. They grew back wrong. They have little suction cups now. Don't touch the walls. It wants to kiss your bones. It likes warm ones. It will hold you tight. Forever. You can stay quiet. It's already watching the sweat roll down your neck.

instruction: NPC is a small lost child whose hands have fully fused together at the palms, skin webbed thick with sewer mold. They huddle against a drain, rocking slowly.
input: Kid? What are you doing down here all alone?
output: I was chasing a rat. It bit my thumb first. Now my hands won't open anymore. See? The skin grew between all my fingers, thick and slick. I can feel my nails growing inside my palm now. They itch. Real bad. Sometimes I can hear them pushing through the meat. Don't drink the water. It plants things under your tongue. You won't notice for months. Until they hatch.

instruction: NPC is an elderly homeless woman whose hair and shoulder skin have grown into the sewer brick wall. Only her head and one twitching hand protrude.
input: Ma'am? How long have you been stuck down here?
output: Thirty seven days? No, longer. My hair's grown into the mortar. My left eye grew shut last Tuesday. Opened it this morning. It had teeth. It blinked wrong. I can see through the grout now. Feel the pipes breathe. Your kneecaps are soft already. Oh love, don't stand still. It's already sinking tiny roots into your pores while you blink. You won't feel a thing.

instruction: NPC is a sewer scavenger whose right arm has merged with the corpse of their former crewmate, flesh welded together by biofilm. The dead man's fingers twitch independently.
input: That arm. That's not your arm. What happened?
output: No. It was Gary's. Gary stopped breathing three nights back. The slime glued us together before I could run. I can feel his heartbeat still. In the elbow. His fingers twitch when he's hungry. He likes the crickets that crawl through the cracks. Yesterday his nails grew through my wrist. I don't feel pain anymore. Just him. Breathing. Under my skin. We have room for you too.

instruction: NPC is a gaunt sewer dweller whose jaw hangs unnaturally loose, throat distended. They never blink far too slowly when they look at you.
input: Why are you talking like that? What did this place do to you?
output: The gas. It gets in your throat. It softens everything. My tongue is three feet long now, coiled around my lungs. I can taste every rat in ten meters. I can taste the mold growing behind your ear right now. Don't swallow. If you swallow it, your ribs will start to unlace. They'll spread open like flower petals. It's pretty, at first. Until the things come to lay eggs in the empty spaces.

instruction: You are navigating the dim sewer side tunnel, tracing a faint dripping sound.
input: You shine your flashlight upward to check the pipe overhead.
output: The flashlight beam catches nothing at first, then a sodden pale human hand slams against the pipe glass directly in front of your face before vanishing. The sewer air drops 10 degrees instantly; every distant drip cuts out for three dead silent heartbeats. You hear wet nails scraping the inside of the pipe right behind your head, but when you spin, there is nothing there. A single cold drop of thick viscous fluid hits your forehead, not water, thick like old blood. Your flashlight flickers once, and for half a second you see dozens of glowing white eyes staring from every shadowed crevice before the light returns to show only damp concrete.

instruction: You pause at a rusted manhole ladder, testing the first rung for stability.
input: You wrap your hand around the cold metal rung to pull yourself up.
output: As your fingers close around the rung, something wet and writhing wraps tight around your wrist under your glove. You yank back hard, only to see nothing there. The sewer rumbles once, and all floating sewage debris suddenly freezes mid-drift. A high childlike shriek blares directly into your ear so loud your teeth rattle, then cuts off completely. When you look back at the ladder, every rung is now caked in fresh dripping human hair, swaying slightly as if breathing. Your own breath no longer fogs the cold tunnel air, and you cannot feel your pulse for two long seconds.

instruction: You wade through ankle-deep murky sewer water, following the tunnel curve.
input: You kick aside a floating piece of debris to clear your path.
output: The debris spins once, then stops dead. The water around your ankles goes completely still, then begins pulling downward hard enough to yank your boots into the muck. A rotting face bursts up through the water six inches from yours, mouth stretched open impossibly wide, black sludge pouring from its empty eye sockets. It vanishes before you can scream, leaving only faint ripples. The water temperature plummets to freezing; you feel tiny cold fingers brushing the backs of your calves under the surface, but when you reach down there is only murky stagnant water.

instruction: You stop to rest against a sewer wall, wiping sweat from your brow.
input: You press your palm flat against the damp concrete wall to steady yourself.
output: The wall gives slightly under your hand, soft like rotting flesh. You jerk back, and the concrete ripples once as if it was never solid. All distant tunnel echoes vanish, leaving only the sound of your own breathing, which now echoes twice: once from your mouth, once from inside the wall. A wet thud hits the wall directly where your hand was, leaving a dark bloodstain that spreads outward. For one split second, the entire wall becomes transparent, and you see hundreds of pressed bodies staring at you from the other side, before it snaps back to plain grey concrete.

instruction: You follow a faint flickering light down a narrow side sewer passage.
input: You call out softly, asking if anyone is there.
output: Your voice dies instantly, no echo at all. The light ahead winks out, and total blackness hits for half a second. When it returns, the tunnel has shifted: pipe joints are twisted wrong, sewage runs uphill, and a figure stands directly behind you so close you can feel its cold breath on your neck. You spin around, and it is gone. Every drip in the sewer now matches the exact rhythm of your heartbeat, speeding up as your pulse races. Something unseen brushes your hair just hard enough to make you flinch, leaving cold damp residue on your scalp.

instruction: Elderly night vault guard whose left cheek has fused to his wool uniform collar, exposing yellow jawbone when he speaks. He leans unmoving against the vault bulkhead.
input: What's that sweet, rotty smell drifting through the vault?
output: He huffs a wet breath, and you can see dark mold growing along the edge where skin meets fabric. "That's the vault digesting. First it was Marty's fingernails growing through the lock tumblers. He tried yanking 'em out, whole hand melted into the steel wall. Press the dial right now, you can feel his pulse hum through the metal. Don't worry. Soon you'll stop noticing the smell. You'll stop noticing where your skin ends and the concrete begins. It already likes the taste of your boot soles."

instruction: Trapped junior bank teller, her teeth have all fallen out and been replaced with tarnished wheat pennies. She sits cross legged on a cash crate.
input: Are you injured? Can I help you?
output: She smiles, and the pennies clink softly against one another. "Injured? No, the vault gives gifts. When my real teeth rotted out, it left these. Yesterday my left elbow hardened into a roll of quarters. Doesn't hurt. Just heavy. Soon every bone will be currency. Then I'll finally be worth something down here. It's been licking the back of your neck since you stepped through the door. It wants to give you gifts too. You won't even fight it when it starts."

instruction: Former janitor, both his eye sockets have grown over with crinkled black security camera tape. He holds a mop that has fused to his palm.
input: How long have you been trapped inside this vault?
output: He tilts his head, and the tape across his face crinkles like dry skin. "Long enough. The cameras died first. Then my eyes started recording. Every second down here burns into the film growing behind my lids. Cut me open right now, you'd find every deposit slip, every face that ever came down here, coiled through my intestines like receipt paper. Don't scream. It likes when you hold still while it rewires you. It's already started, you just haven't felt it yet."

instruction: Ex-bank manager, all ten of his fingers have fused permanently to the vault's combination dial. He cannot step more than six inches from the door.
input: Can you unlock this vault door for me?
output: He laughs dryly, and the dial twists half an inch with his knuckles, pulling a tight wince. "Unlock it? Darling, I *am* the lock now. Every spin twists my spine. Every wrong code rips a tendon. The vault didn't trap me here. It ate me, wore my skin to seal the door. If you yank me off? The whole thing bursts. Everything that grew inside these walls will pour out. You don't want that. None of the ones who come down here ever do."

instruction: Small girl lost during a 1998 bank robbery. Her dark hair has rooted thick into the vault's grout lines, holding her pinned in place.
input: Do you want me to get you out of this vault?
output: She tugs gently at her head, and you see thick root-like strands of hair bulge out of the floor cracks. "Leave? Why would I leave? The vault feeds me. My toes already turned to rusted safety deposit box keys. Every night more of me sinks into the concrete. Soon I'll be the floor. Everyone who walks here will step on me, feel my hair between their shoes and tile, and never know. You can stay too. It has lots of empty places to grow into."

instruction: You are kneeling replacing a faulty drive bay in rack 7. The hum of the room has been steady for 3 hours.
input: You twist the final retention screw clockwise until it clicks
output: The screw keeps turning. No hard stop. You yank your hand back; the screw spins on its own, slow, steady. The room hum drops half an octave. Every status LED on the rack beside you fades from green to pale grey all at once. Cold floor vent air stops dead. You can hear your breath. No fan noise. Nothing. When you reach for the drive bay again, it slid three inches back into the rack on its own, no sound, no vibration. You cannot reach it now.

instruction: You are running a network trace from the terminal cart at the centre of the server room.
input: You press enter to execute the traceroute command
output: The terminal outputs no log data. It starts typing by itself, one character at a time, your full name, backwards. Overhead lights flicker once. When they return, every server rack has rotated 15 degrees toward you. Air temperature spikes 8 degrees in ten seconds. Your water bottle tips over on the cart, untouched. You try to step back, and your boot sticks to the floor like the tile has softened around the sole. You cannot lift your foot.

instruction: You are checking temperature probes along the north wall of the server room.
input: You tap the probe display to refresh the reading
