output: Every probe in the room beeps at once, in perfect unison. All read 0.00C. Then they climb. 12. 47. 119. You reach to unplug one, and the wire slithers out of your grip. Server fans all ramp up to full screaming whine at the same moment. You turn for the door, and every cable hanging from the racks sways toward you, pulled by a wind that does not touch your skin or stir your hair.

instruction: You are resetting a tripped UPS unit in the back corner of the server room.
input: You flip the main breaker switch back to the ON position
output: Nothing powers on. The breaker lever flips itself back down. You flip it up again. It snaps down hard enough to sting your knuckles. All emergency exit signs go dark. You hear slow scraping drag sounds inside the sealed server rack shells. When you shine your flashlight between rack panels, nothing is there. But the drag sound moves every single time you look away. You cannot track it.

instruction: You are logging maintenance notes on your clipboard while waiting for a patch to deploy.
input: You set your pen down on the clipboard edge
output: The pen rolls off. When you bend to pick it up, it is not on the floor. You stand, and it balances perfectly on top of the 12 foot tall server rack at the far end. The entire room’s hum warps, like someone slowing a vinyl record. All server doors rattle in their frames, no vibration, just rattle. You reach for your radio, it is gone from your belt. You can hear it clicking somewhere behind the racks.

instruction: You find a sealed first aid locker bolted to the wreck’s galley bulkhead, its label still legible and free of rust. The latch clicks open easily when you test it.
input: You pry the locker fully open, shining your torch inside.
output: Bandages, antiseptic, and a full water canteen sit neatly stacked. It’s perfect, too perfect. Under the canteen you spot a child’s drawing: a stick figure grinning, standing atop this exact ship. Tiny scratch marks run along the locker’s inner back panel, not from rust—they’re carved, each one counting days. Somewhere below decks, a low thrum rises just loud enough you think you might have imagined it. The canteen is warm to the touch, even though this wreck has been submerged for twelve years.

instruction: A life raft tethered to the upper deck floats fully inflated, bobbing gently against the hull. No tears mark its surface, and a rescue beacon blinks slow and steady from its canopy.
input: You climb into the raft, checking its supplies and securing the mooring line.
output: Emergency rations are sealed, flares are dry, the radio crackles with faint static that almost sounds like human voices. You breathe out, finally relaxing—until you notice the raft’s floor is damp with fresh blood, no wound on you to explain it. The mooring line you just checked is already frayed almost through, chewed from the underwater end. The beacon isn’t broadcasting out. It’s calling something in.

instruction: You stumble on the captain’s cabin door, which swings open silently. A lantern on the desk burns with a steady, clean flame, casting warm gold over the room.
input: You step inside, leaning against the desk to catch your breath and rest your aching legs.
output: The cabin smells like pipe tobacco and coffee, just like it did the day the ship sailed. There’s even a half-full mug on the desk, still steaming. For a minute you forget you’re standing in a wreck 40 fathoms down. Then you see the captain’s log open on the table: every entry after the first day says only THEY LIKE WHEN YOU REST. The flame in the lantern doesn’t flicker when you move your hand through it.

instruction: Wedged under a collapsed railing you find a working dive computer, its screen lit up showing safe oxygen levels and clear exit routes back to the surface.
input: You strap the computer to your wrist, cross referencing the displayed path with your own map.
output: Every reading lines up perfectly. It even marks a shortcut through the cargo hold that cuts ten minutes off your ascent. You grin, already picturing breaking the surface. When you scroll past the navigation screen, you find a hidden timer counting down from 7 minutes. There’s no option to pause it. Buried in the system logs is a single line repeated 142 times: they always follow the safe path.

instruction: You hear a human voice call your name from a forward storage bay. When you peer in, you spot one of your missing dive partners sitting against a crate, waving calmly.
input: You hurry into the bay, calling back to them, relief flooding your chest.
output: They look unhurt, smiling, holding a spare oxygen tank out to you. You almost run straight to them before you notice their feet don’t touch the sunken floor. Their eyes don’t blink. The tank in their hand is open, venting not air, but fine black silt that drifts upward in thin, reaching strands. Behind them, the shadow on the crate wall has too many arms, and it’s not theirs.

instruction: Generate a horror scene focused on the specified fear in the given setting
input: Fear: paranoia. Setting: a locked after-hours server room
output: You kneel to swap a failed drive, breath fogging the cold metal chassis. Every fan hum warps just slightly, like someone is breathing into the vent grilles right behind your ear. You turn fast. Nothing. Just rows of blinking status LEDs. You count the racks: 12. You count again. 13. The extra one stands at the far end, dark, no labels. You didn’t see it when you arrived. Your radio spits only static. No one else has access at 2:17AM. The lights blink in unison now. You swear something is counting your heartbeats, matching every beat to a flicker.

instruction: Generate a horror scene focused on the specified fear in the given setting
input: Fear: paranoia. Setting: an overheated data centre server room
output: You mop sweat off your brow while tracing a network fault. The AC died 40 minutes ago. You keep catching movement out the corner of your eye, just past the rack edge. Every time you look, only glowing server lights. Your shadow on the floor is wrong. It’s leaning forward, like it’s watching you, even when you stand straight. You step left. It doesn’t move. You freeze. The server fans don’t whir randomly anymore. They’re breathing. Slow, steady, matching your respiration. No one else is here. You know that. But you can feel eyes. Thousands of them, behind every LED.

instruction: Generate a horror scene focused on the specified fear in the given setting
input: Fear: paranoia. Setting: a dimly lit office server closet
output: You shine your phone torch between the stacked racks, hunting the tripped breaker. The door locked behind you ten minutes ago. You keep hearing soft footfalls, right behind your shoulder. Each time you spin, there’s only humming metal. You left your water bottle on the crate by the door. It’s gone. You check your watch. It says 1:02. It said 1:02 fifteen minutes ago. Every server light blinks once, in perfect sync, every time you blink. You’re not imagining it. Something is learning the rhythm of you. It’s waiting for you to stop looking.

instruction: Generate a horror scene focused on the specified fear in the given setting
input: Fear: paranoia. Setting: a subterranean server farm vault
output: The concrete walls hum around you as you run firmware updates. You’ve been alone 3 hours. You keep replaying the security feed in your head: when you walked in, the motion sensors triggered three rows over. No one was there. You check the logs now. Every five minutes, a motion alert. Always exactly one rack away from where you’re standing. Your badge logs show you’ve swiped into this room seven times tonight. You only came down once. The fans go quiet for half a second, just long enough for you to hear someone exhale, right next to your ear.

instruction: Generate a horror scene focused on the specified fear in the given setting
input: Fear: paranoia. Setting: a decommissioned server room scheduled for demolition
output: Dust coats every dead chassis. You’re here to pull the last backup drives. You keep hearing faint typing. Not keyboard clicks, the physical tap of fingers on plastic, somewhere deep in the racks. You yell. No answer. You marked three drives with tape an hour ago. Now all twelve drives have your little blue tape on them. The exit door you propped open is shut. Latched from the inside. There’s nothing alive in here. You know that. But every time you turn your back, you can feel the dead servers turning to look at you.

instruction: Write a body horror horror scene set in a dry well
input: Setting: abandoned dry stone well. Fear type: body horror. Second person narration, atmospheric.
output: You land on cracked dust at the well bottom. At first you only smell rot. Then you shift, and something soft gives under your palm. It’s not bone. It’s skin, stretched thin over the stone, grown into the mortar. You yank back—your own skin sticks. Where you touched, the well-wall skin pricks with tiny hairs, same as the back of your neck. It breathes. The dry dust is dead skin flakes, drifting down as the well sighs. You feel your ankles start to tingle, fine roots pushing through your socks, fusing you to the floor. You don’t scream. You can feel your ribs starting to soften, turning to mossy mortar.

instruction: Generate an atmospheric body horror scene set inside a dry well
input: Setting: remote dry well, no light. Fear type: body horror. Second person.
output: You’ve been in the dark three hours. Your throat is raw. You run a hand along the stone, and feel teeth. Not animal teeth. Human. Set flush into the rock, row after row, worn smooth from decades of rain. You press a finger to one, and it bites. It doesn’t break skin—instead it pulls, slow, and you feel your nail unspool like thread, feeding into the stone. The teeth hum. You can feel your thumbnail growing now, fast, stretching up your arm, weaving into the cracks. You try to pull back. Every tooth in your own mouth aches. They want to root here too.

instruction: Write a quiet body horror scene set at the bottom of a dry well
input: Setting: sun-dappled dry well bottom, midday. Fear type: body horror. Second person narration.
output: Sun slants in a thin golden bar across the well dust. You’re counting stones to stay calm, when you notice the fingerprints. Every stone has one, exactly matching your thumb. You press yours to the nearest. It sinks. The stone gives, warm as flesh, and your thumb melts into it. You don’t feel pain. You feel it spread—your elbow, then your shoulder, dissolving slow into the wall. When you look down, your torso is already stony, grainy cracks running up your ribs. The sun moves. The bar of light creeps up. You will be just another stone before it reaches the top.

instruction: Create a body horror horror scene set in a dry abandoned well
input: Setting: dry well, found after hiking accident. Fear type: body horror. Second person.
output: You fell ten minutes ago. You think you’re unhurt, until you go to brush dust off your leg. Your hand passes through. It’s not your leg anymore. It’s a nest of dry, brittle root tendons, woven into the well floor. You prod at your thigh. Crumbs of desiccated muscle sift down. All the people that fell here before you—they didn’t die. They merged. You can feel other hearts beating now, slow, in the dust under you. Your own pulse slows to match. Your tongue dries to dust. Soon you won’t remember ever being anything but part of the dark at the bottom.

instruction: Write an unsettling body horror scene set at the bottom of a dry well
input: Setting: quiet dry well, middle of the night. Fear type: body horror. Second person.
output: No one can hear you scream. You stopped an hour ago. Now you just watch the tiny crack in the stone above. Something is coming through. Not an animal. Not water. It’s eyes. Dozens of them, pushing out of the rock, wet and blinking, all focused on you. They don’t hurt you. They just leak. The fluid runs down the wall, onto your hands. Where it touches, your skin splits open, and new eyes bloom under the surface. You blink. All of them blink. You can see every crack in the well now, every shadow. You can see out the top. You will never look away again.

instruction: You are seated in the dim oak church confessional booth, varnish sticky with old candle wax, waiting for the priest to speak.
input: You lean forward, tapping the mesh grille gently to check if anyone is on the other side.
output: The wood beneath your palm hums like a living thing. Somewhere beyond the curtain, a rosary clicks once, twice—no breath accompanies it. The temperature drops so fast your nose stings; when you blink, the mesh grille has shifted three inches to the left, no creak, no scrape. You can feel eyes not through the grille, but behind your own skull, counting every beat of your heart. You don’t hear anyone breathing, but you know something is counting how many times you blink.

instruction: You have just finished speaking your confession, hands folded tight in your lap in the church confessional.
input: You hold your breath, waiting for the priest’s absolution.
output: A thin draft curls up your trouser leg, carrying the smell of turned earth. You hear paper rustle on the other side, but not the scratch of a pen. When you shift your weight, the bench under you shifts too—like it’s molding to your body, learning your shape. Every time you glance at the grille, you catch the edge of something moving just out of your line of sight, vanishing before you can focus. You realize you haven’t heard a single footstep enter or leave the church the entire time you’ve been here.

instruction: The confessional booth is wrapped in shadow, only a single candle guttering outside the curtain.
input: You reach into your pocket for your phone to check the time.
output: Your fingers close on nothing. Your phone is gone. The candle outside sputters, and for half a second the entire booth is black—when the light returns, the crack under the curtain is gone. There is no gap, no line, just solid wood where there should be open floor. You can hear your own breathing echoing back at you, slightly out of sync, like someone is repeating every breath you take half a beat too late. You can’t shake the feeling you are no longer inside a church at all.

instruction: You entered the confessional ten minutes ago, the priest has not spoken once.
input: You clear your throat quietly, trying to prompt a response.
output: The sound sticks in the air, thick as incense. You notice the wood grain on the wall beside you is rearranging itself when you don’t look directly at it, curving into little staring eyes that vanish the second you turn. Something brushes the back of your neck, so light you almost miss it, but when you reach back there is nothing there. You start counting the seconds between heartbeats, and realize they are getting slower. Not yours. The ones you can hear behind the grille.

instruction: Rain patters softly on the church roof as you sit alone in the confessional booth.
input: You tilt your head back against the wood, closing your eyes for one quiet breath.
output: When you open them, the rain has stopped. Not faded, just cut off mid-drop. There is no sound at all now, no birds, no wind, nothing. You press your ear to the grille, and you can hear your own voice, saying the exact words you said when you first walked into the church, playing on loop, quiet and distant. You run your hand along the edge of the bench and find a scratch mark you know you didn’t leave. It is the exact shape of your own fingernail.

instruction: Hunched elderly priest behind the confessional grille, breathing slow and even, never showing his face. He has not spoken once during your entire confession.
input: Father, that's everything. I've confessed all my sins. Can I have absolution now?
output: Absolution? Child, you did not come here to confess. You came here because every night you wake up certain someone is counting your breaths through the bedroom wall. You do not remember me, but I held you when you were three, the night your mother left. I have counted every breath you have taken since. You thought you hid that memory well. You thought no one knew you still hum her lullaby when you are afraid. I know. I have always known.

instruction: Soft-spoken young nun, her silhouette only just visible through the mesh grille. Her hands are folded perfectly still on her side of the booth.
input: Sister, I don't know why I'm here. I just felt pulled to this booth.
output: That is not you feeling pulled. That is us calling. Everyone who sits in that seat left a little piece of themselves here. Every guilt, every secret, every quiet urge they would not say out loud. They pool in this wood. They soak into the cushion under you right now. You are not talking to me. You are talking to every person who ever sat where you sit, whispering back everything you were too cowardly to admit to yourself.

instruction: Priest with a rough, gravelly voice. You can hear him tapping a single finger repeatedly against the wooden wall, a steady unchanging rhythm.
input: I keep having the same dream. I'm in this confessional, but I'm on your side of the grille.
output: That is not a dream. That is last Tuesday. You came here drunk. You screamed that you wanted to trade places. I let you. You do not remember because you begged me to make you forget. The tapping you hear? That is you, still on my side, still screaming, while the you that walked out goes through the motions of your life. None of this is real. You never left this booth.

instruction: Silent figure in the confessional. You never heard them enter. There is no sound of breathing from their side.
input: Hello? Is someone there? I thought I heard a voice.
output: You have been talking to an empty booth for seventeen minutes. You walked in here, sat down, and started speaking out loud to the part of you that you have spent your whole life locking away. It stopped pretending to be me three minutes ago. Every word you thought I said? That was your own mind, finally brave enough to tell you exactly what you are going to do tomorrow. You already know what it is. Do not lie.

instruction: Familiar male voice behind the grille, though you cannot place it. He speaks like he has known you your whole life.
input: I hurt someone. I don't think I can ever make it right.
output: You will make it right. You will sit here every Sunday for the next forty two years. You will listen to strangers confess. You will never leave this booth. You will grow old behind this grille. One day a scared young man will sit where you sit now, and he will admit the exact same thing. That will be the day you finally understand. You were never the penitent. You were always the priest. It was always your turn.

instruction: Observe the wilted sunflower row
input: Kneel to brush dead petals off the nearest sunflower head
output: The petal sticks to your thumb like wet raw meat. As you yank back, the entire sunflower head lolls on its stem, splitting open along a gash lined with tiny human fingernails. Sap oozes thick as blood, warm against your wrist. Crickets hum off-beat, each chirp stretching like a gasp. The soil under your knees pulses once, soft as breathing. You realize none of the other sunflowers have faces anymore—just smooth, hairless pale spheres bobbing slow in no wind.

instruction: Pull a dandelion growing between cracked pavers
input: Grip the weed by its base and yank upwards hard
output: It comes up with a wet sucking pop. The root is not fibrous—it’s a pale, twitching umbilical cord, still oozing clear mucus. Small vein-like threads writhe at the broken end, trying to burrow back into the dirt. The air drops 10 degrees instantly. Every dandelion in the garden turns their fluffy seed heads to face you at once, no sound, just uniform, deliberate motion. None sway, none waver. They just stare.

instruction: Check the old stone birdbath for water
input: Lean over the basin and rest one hand on its rim
output: The stone is warm, too warm, like skin after a fever. Beneath the murky water, hundreds of tiny human molars shift and click softly against each other. A thin strand of black hair curls up around your wrist, tight enough to leave a pale indent before dissolving. The garden’s green tint warps; leaves bleed pink at the edges. You can feel something blinking behind every rose bush, slow, all blinking once at the exact same time.

instruction: Kick aside a rotting log lying across the path
input: Boot the log hard to roll it off the trail
output: It splits open like overripe fruit. Inside, instead of grubs, there are rows of tiny, perfectly formed human toes, wiggling slow, each tipped with a chipped pink nail. The stench is not rot—it’s warm breath, unbrushed teeth. Grass blades along the path curl up like flinching fingers. All the birds stop singing at once, leaving only the wet squirm of the toes against damp wood.

instruction: Pluck a ripe red strawberry from the vine
input: Pinch the stem and lift the berry to your nose
output: It doesn’t smell like fruit. It smells like the inside of your cheek. When you squeeze it gently, thick clear saliva oozes out the tiny pores along its skin. The vine it grew from twitches, wrapping once around your index finger. Underneath every leaf in the patch, you see tiny closed eyelids, fluttering like they’re about to wake. The whole garden hums, soft and wet, like a mouth breathing.

instruction: Entity is the 'Tunnel Attendant' – a uniformed highway road worker standing at a tunnel exit pull-off, holding a working flashlight, appearing fully human and approachable at any distance over 30 feet.
input: You are 2.1 miles inside the mountain road tunnel, smoke curling through your truck’s vents from a seized radiator. You spot the attendant waving you steadily toward the lit pull-off 90 feet ahead.
output: The cab reeks of burnt antifreeze when you roll to a stop. He’s smiling, uniform crisp, no dirt under his nails. You don’t notice until you open the door that his flashlight never moves, that the beam doesn’t waver even when he tilts his head. When he speaks his mouth doesn’t open; the sound comes from the tunnel ventilation grates, flat, dead, repeating ‘you’re safe now, you stopped right where you should’. You look down and his boots aren’t touching the asphalt. The pull-off lights were never on. You stopped in the middle of the travel lane. Headlights are coming. You can’t turn the key.

instruction: Entity is the 'Idle Sedan' – a spotless charcoal 2008 Toyota Camry parked in a tunnel emergency bay, interior dome light warm, driver door propped open, keys visible hanging from the ignition.
input: Your phone died 12 minutes prior, you have no idea how much tunnel remains. You round a curve and spot the idling sedan alone in the emergency bay.
output: The engine purrs so quiet you almost miss it. No one’s in the seats, no bags on the floor. The radio hums static that almost sounds like breathing. You lean in to grab the keys, and that’s when you notice the windshield has no reflection of you. The dome light doesn’t cast a shadow on the asphalt. When you yank your hand back the driver’s door clicks shut soft as an eyelid. The emergency bay vanishes. You were never beside a car. You’ve been standing dead center of the northbound lane for seven minutes. Your tires are screeching behind you. You forgot you got out of your own car.

instruction: Entity is the 'Highway Emergency Intercom' – standard grey wall-mounted unit, status LED glowing solid unblinking green, per highway specs this indicates a live direct line to dispatch.
input: You crashed your motorcycle on the tunnel curb, ankle shattered. No vehicle has passed in 19 minutes. You drag yourself over and reach up to press the intercom call button.
output: The button depresses with a satisfying solid click. A woman’s voice comes immediately, warm, calm: ‘Help is 3 minutes out. Stay right there, don’t move, you’re safe.’ You slump back against the wall, crying. It’s 7 minutes later when you notice the green light never flickered. The voice hasn’t stopped repeating that exact line, same cadence, same breath, no pause. You run your fingers along the intercom casing. It’s not plastic. It’s warm. The speaker grille is pulsing. There was never any wire running out the back. You didn’t call anyone. You pressed your hand to something that was waiting for you to stop running.

instruction: You notice the funeral home’s front door locks automatically behind you with a soft click, the reception desk is completely unmanned, soft organ music hums from hidden speakers.
input: You check the reception desk drawers for a staff contact sheet
output: The top drawer holds only neatly folded white linen handkerchiefs, each pressed with the same small bloodstain in the corner. No phone, no logbook. A sticky note says ‘ALL GUESTS STAY FOR DINNER’ scrawled in blue pen. The organ music swells just slightly, and you realize it’s been playing the exact same 12 second loop this entire time. You don’t hear anyone breathing but you can feel warmth on the back of your neck.

instruction: The viewing room lights are warm, the casket lid is closed, velvet curtains are drawn and the air smells like fresh lavender. Everything looks calm, properly prepared.
input: You lean in to run a finger along the casket’s polished oak edge
output: Your nail catches a tiny crack along the rim, where dark sticky residue seeps out. Not embalming fluid—this smells like iron. The room feels too quiet suddenly; the hum of the refrigeration unit in the back cut out thirty seconds ago and you only just noticed. A single lavender petal drifts down from nowhere, landing exactly on the spot your hand just touched.

instruction: An elderly mortician smiles politely from the break room doorway, wiping his hands on his apron, offers you a paper cup of coffee. He seems gentle, tired but harmless.
input: You accept the coffee and glance around the small break room while you talk
output: The kitchen counter has no dishes, no food, just seven identical coffee mugs all sitting upside down in a perfect row. His apron has no stains at all. When you blink he’s standing three feet closer than he was a second ago, still smiling the exact same smile. The coffee in your cup is cold, even though he just poured it. No one has mentioned who the funeral is for.

instruction: The back hallway emergency exit sign glows bright green, clearly marked, no obstacles between you and the door. It looks like a safe way out if you need it.
input: You walk over to test the exit door handle quietly
output: The handle turns smoothly, no resistance at all. But when you pull, the door doesn’t open. It doesn’t rattle, doesn’t creak. It just stays perfectly still, like it’s fused to the frame. You press your ear to the metal: no traffic, no wind, no sound at all from the other side. The green exit sign flickers once, and for half a second it reads NO EXIT.

instruction: You find a working landline phone tucked under a stairwell, dial tone clear, no visible damage. It looks like you can call for help.
input: You pick up the receiver and dial 911
output: The line connects immediately. A calm female voice says ‘911, what is your emergency?’ Before you can speak, you hear the exact same voice whispering the exact same words, from inside the wall right behind you. The dial tone never stopped. You are still holding a dead receiver. No call was ever placed. You just thought there was one.

instruction: You are waiting for the last delayed overnight train; the station PA just announced all doors are locked for passenger safety until boarding, and the only night attendant waved that everything is fine.
input: You walk over to check the locked east exit doors after noticing a faint scratch mark running along their frame.
output: The deadbolt clicks solid when you test it, just like the attendant said. You spot a crumpled transit worker uniform tucked under the bench nearby—name tag matches the guy who just waved. You turn back, and for half a second you see someone in the same uniform leaning in the control booth window, watching. The PA chirps again: “All personnel have gone home for the shift. Remain seated.” No one mentions the train isn’t even scheduled to arrive tonight.

instruction: The station heating just kicked on warm, the vending machine dispensed a free coffee, and a homeless regular nodded at you that it’s quiet and safe here tonight.
input: You bend down to tie your boot, and glance under the platform edge while you’re low.
output: You only see crumpled candy wrappers at first, nothing dangerous. You stand, sipping the warm coffee, and notice every overhead security camera has been turned to face the wall. The homeless man is gone, but he left his ratty blanket folded perfectly on his bench, still holding the body heat of someone sitting there. Far down the tracks, you hear a door latch shut. Not a train. A door.

instruction: Transit police just did a walkthrough, radioed that the station is clear, and told you to relax while you wait. The whole platform feels empty and calm.
input: You check the digital arrival board to see how much longer the delay will run.
output: The board flicks once, still showing 12 minutes. For one glitch frame you see it displaying the date three years ago, the night a woman was killed on this platform. The board resets, clean again. You check your phone—there is no cell service, but you have three missed calls from the transit police number. They didn’t ring. No one told you the officers that just walked through haven’t been on the force since that night.

instruction: The only other passenger is a grandmother knitting quietly two benches over, she smiled and said this station never has trouble this late. All lights are bright, no shadows.
input: You walk over to the water fountain to fill your bottle.
output: The water runs clear cold, perfect. When you straighten you glance at the reflection in the fountain basin. The grandmother isn’t holding knitting needles. She’s holding zip ties, counting them one by one under her shawl. When you turn to look, her needles are back in her hands, she smiles again. You don’t notice the train tracks have been disconnected at both ends of the station.

instruction: You just tested the emergency call button, it beeped loud and an operator answered immediately saying help is 2 minutes away. You feel safe enough to close your eyes for a minute.
input: You open your eyes when you hear a soft metal click somewhere behind you.
output: Nothing is there when you turn. The station is still quiet, the call button still glows green. You check the button again—when you press it now, no beep. You realize the operator that answered never asked what station you were at. The lock on the main entrance just clicked shut on the outside. You didn’t hear anyone walk up to it.

instruction: Gaunt former shift foreman, chest-deep in murky mine water, one arm trapped under a collapsed timber, eyes glazed. He does not blink when you approach.
input: Hold on, I can lever that beam off you. Just stay still.
output: Stay still? You think stillness is a choice down here? Listen. Feel it? The water seeps through your boot laces when you don't let it. It fills your lungs one breath at a time before you notice. You don't choose when you slip. You don't choose when the timbers creak. I stopped choosing three days ago. Now it just moves me. Soon it'll move you too. Don't fight it. Fighting just makes it angry. You'll learn. Everyone learns you don't control anything past the second flood seal blows.

instruction: Teenage mine labourer, perched on a broken conveyor roller, knees pulled tight to his chest, chewing on a frayed electrical cable. Water laps at his ankles.
input: The exit's up the drift. Come with me, we can make it before the next surge.
output: Make it? You think you pick the speed you walk? You think your legs don't just keep moving even when you tell them to stop? I tried to run yesterday. My feet wouldn't turn. They just kept walking deeper. The water hums. It tells your bones what to do. You'll hear it soon. You'll be standing there holding your torch and you'll just drop it. You won't decide to. It'll just happen. None of this is us any more. We're just things it's moving around for now.

instruction: Mute-seeming old driller, floating half face-down in calm flood water, only his eyes and forehead above the surface, holding a rusted drill bit in one fist.
input: Can you hear me? There's still air further up. We don't have to stay here.
output: You talk like staying is a choice. You talk like breathing is a choice. Yesterday I held my breath for seven minutes. I didn't try. I just stopped inhaling. My chest didn't burn. It just waited. The water decides when you breathe. It decides when you blink. It decides which way your head turns. You can yell all you want. You can swing that crowbar. You're already not the one driving your hands. Not down here. Not once it touches your skin.

instruction: Mine paramedic, uniform torn, repeatedly dragging her fingers through the surface of the water in slow, identical circles. Her name tag is half peeled off.
input: Let me check your injuries. Did you hit your head when the shaft flooded?
output: Injuries? Oh no, nothing's broken. Not yet. You want to check something? Try telling your hand to stop twitching. Go on. Tell it. It won't listen. Not any more. First it was just my fingers. Then my jaw. Now sometimes I'll be talking and words I didn't think of just come out. It's practicing. It's learning how to wear us. You'll feel it first in your teeth. Don't fight. No one's ever fought it and kept their own head.

instruction: Shift boss, standing perfectly upright in chest deep water, holding a dead radio to his ear. His hair is matted with mud, he has not moved in over ten minutes.
input: That radio's dead. Nobody's coming. We have to save ourselves.
output: Save ourselves? You think we ever saved anything? You think you decided to climb down here today? You think you decided to turn down this drift instead of the other one? Every choice you ever thought you made was just the water pulling you a little closer. It doesn't rush. It doesn't beg. It just waits, and eventually every single thing you thought was yours just... stops being yours. Stop pretending you're driving. You haven't been driving since the first drop hit your collar.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the specified fear in the given setting.
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: a morgue.
output: You fumble for the light switch, palm sliding over frosty cinder block. It clicks—nothing. The generator died. Every exhale fogs in the refrigerated dark. You can hear the compressor hum fade, then the soft, wet shift of weight on steel trays behind you. You don’t dare turn. Something breathes, slow, not your own. The dark presses against your eyelids even when you squeeze them shut. You reach for your phone, only to remember you left it by the autopsy sink. Somewhere, a drawer slides open. Metal on metal, unrushed. No one else was supposed to be here. The dark is not empty. It is full, pressing closer, hiding whatever just sat up three trays over.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the specified fear in the given setting.
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: a morgue.
output: The backup lights flicker once and die. You’re mid-tying a toe tag when the black hits—sudden, absolute, swallowing the steel tables, the acrid tang of formaldehyde, the wall three feet from your face. Your latex gloves are still slick with embalming fluid. You freeze. You can’t even see your own hand held inches from your nose. Somewhere down the row of cold lockers, a latch clicks open. You don’t call out. Voices don’t carry right in this dark. It eats sound. It eats breath. Whatever just crawled out is moving slow, quiet, letting you stand blind while it counts your hammering heartbeats through the gloom.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the specified fear in the given setting.
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: a morgue.
output: You tripped over a gurney rail. When you hit the tile, the overhead lamp shattered. Now the dark wraps around you thick as a zipped body bag. Your ankle throbs too badly to stand. You drag yourself along the floor, fingers brushing discarded scalpel blades, crumpled toe tags, something warm and wet that is not blood. The morgue does not sound right anymore. No compressor hum. Just soft dragging, matching your exact pace, always just out of reach. You can’t see it. You can’t even tell which direction it comes from. The dark does not just hide things. It gives them somewhere to wait, right where you will bump into them before you hear them.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the specified fear in the given setting.
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: a morgue.
output: You locked the front door ten minutes prior. The last nurse left. You were finishing paperwork when the power cut. Absolute dark. Not even the faint red glow of the exit sign remains. You press your back flat to the cold autopsy table. Every edge, every shadow you memorized over two years is gone. You hear a sheet rustle. Not the draft—this is deliberate, slow, fabric pulling off cold skin. You don’t reach for the flashlight on your belt. You already know if you turn it on, you will see something you can never unsee. So you stay still. You let the dark hide it. You pretend the hand that just brushed your neck is just the chill.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the specified fear in the given setting.
input: Fear: darkness. Setting: a morgue.
output: The window blacked out first. Not storm, just thick roiling dark rolling through the glass like smoke, swallowing every overhead bulb one by one. You smell rot before the last light dies. The cold lockers start thudding, soft, from the inside. One. Then three. Then all of them, thumping in perfect time with your pulse. You can’t see the doors. You can’t see the floor under your boots. The dark seeps into your lungs, tastes like formaldehyde and grave dirt. It does not matter that you know this room. The dark rewrites it. Every empty space is now something watching you. Every silence is just it waiting for you to run.

instruction: Hunched elderly woman in a frayed wool coat, picking at something under her sleeve, avoiding eye contact, seated alone on the far station bench. Only her scuffed work boots are visible under the hood at first.
input: Excuse me, do you know when the next train comes?
output: Oh, the train? It comes when it finds enough parts. See? I’m helping. I pulled my thumbnail out last night, slipped it under the tracks. Yesterday it was three toe bones. You’ll feel it when it’s close. Your teeth will start wiggling right under your gums, not loose, just… turning. Don’t scratch your neck when that happens. That’s how it finds the soft bits. It doesn’t want whole people. Just the nice, replaceable little pieces that nobody will miss until they wake up tomorrow wondering why they can’t blink right.

instruction: Night shift station attendant, standing behind the ticket booth glass, uniform hanging too loose on his frame, face half swallowed by shadow. His hands twitch constantly against the counter.
input: Hey, the vending machine ate my dollar. Can you help open it?
output: They all do that. Not the machine. The stuff inside. It licks the bills first, you know. You can’t see it through the glass but it’s got these little wet roots that grow out the soda cans, poke through the slots. I saw a kid reach in last week, pulled back three of his finger joints still stuck to a Sprite can. Don’t worry, it only takes what you reach in with. Nobody ever loses more than they volunteer. Go on, stick your arm in further. It’ll give your dollar back. Probably.

instruction: Scrawny teen with faded black dye, cross-legged right on the platform edge, repeatedly dragging their raw thumb along their lower lip.
input: You shouldn’t sit that close to the tracks. It’s dangerous this late.
output: Dangerous? Nah, the rails hum nice. They sing to the bones under your skin. Listen? Mine started answering Tuesday. Now my ribs slide around when I breathe. They’re practicing lining up straight for when the train passes. It’s gonna pull ’em right out through my sternum nice and neat, no mess. Everyone here gets something taken. You’ll see. Yours will be the kneecaps. I can already hear them rattling inside your jeans.

instruction: Man in a crisp wrinkled business suit, holding a scuffed hard-shell suitcase that twitches every seven seconds. He has not blinked once in the three minutes you’ve watched him.
input: Are you waiting for the eastbound train too?
output: No. I’m delivering the rest of me. Left half got on the last one. It texted. Said my liver got caught on the door hinge. This suitcase has my right lung, three fingers, and the lower half of my tongue. It said if I don’t bring these by 4:17 it’s gonna start growing replacements out of station grout. They don’t come right. Last time it grew a thumb where my ear should be. Don’t check your pockets. You probably left a part of you here too. Everyone does.

instruction: Unkempt woman wrapped in a stained wool blanket, sitting beside a stack of yellowed newspapers, dropping crumbs to something shifting under the fabric at her feet.
input: I bought an extra hot coffee. Do you want it?
output: No thank you baby. My teeth don’t fit right anymore, can’t sip. The thing under here? It’s got my jaw. Borrowed it three nights ago, said it needed it to chew the dead moths off the rails. It’ll give it back. Probably. Just don’t look at it too long when it peeks out. It’s got my eyes too. You’ll swear you’re staring at your own face, only all the teeth are facing the wrong way. You can feel that already, can’t you? Right behind your sinuses.

instruction: A framed family photograph that never appears out of place no matter where you turn in the attic
input: You back away from the collapsed rafter, brush dust off your jacket, and glance up. It’s there on the nail beside you. You saw it hanging above the window three minutes ago.
output: It doesn’t move when you blink. The glass is warm, like someone was holding it ten seconds before you looked. The faces aren’t blurry anymore. They’re all looking directly at you, mouths slightly open like they’re breathing the same dust you are. You hurl it against the brick chimney. It shatters. When you wipe the sweat from your eyes, it’s back on the nail. No cracks. No dust. The youngest child in the photo is holding the exact splinter of glass you just kicked across the floor. You can hear the faint, muffled hum of a lullaby coming through the frame, steady as a heartbeat. It’s not here to hurt you. It’s here to make you stop noticing that the attic door vanished ten minutes ago. That you haven’t heard a car pass the house in an hour. That the walls are slowly, quietly, getting closer.

instruction: A dented metal attic step that always creaks exactly once right after you stop moving
input: You freeze mid-crawl, hand curled around a loose floorboard. There was no wind. No rats. The step just creaked.
output: You hold your breath for seventy two seconds. It doesn’t creak again. You laugh quietly at your own jumpiness, shake your head, and keep sorting the boxes. Every time you pause, every time you stop to rub your stiff neck, every time you blink just a little too long: one soft, exact creak, from the exact step half way down the ladder. You never catch it moving. You test it once, stamp on it as hard as you can. It makes a dull thud, no creak. It only makes the sound when you aren’t watching it. You tell yourself it’s just old wood, settling. You don’t admit that each creak is one step closer. That by the time you finish sorting the third box, the creak is no longer coming from the ladder. It’s coming from the floor three feet behind you. You still haven’t turned around. If you don’t look, it’s still just a step.

instruction: A moth-eaten wool blanket draped over a stack of trunks that always feels warm to the touch
input: The attic is cold enough you can see your breath. You reach for the blanket without thinking, fingers brushing the frayed edge.
output: It’s body temperature. Not sun-warmed, not held recently. Exactly the heat of living skin. You yank your hand back, then chide yourself. It’s just insulation. Old wool holds heat. You wrap it around your shoulders. It smells like your grandmother’s laundry soap, like rain on oak, like every safe thing you ever forgot. You don’t notice that it doesn’t have any edges. That the fray melts into the dust on your jeans, that it’s slowly weaving threads into the cuff of your coat. You don’t notice that you stopped shivering ten minutes ago. That you can no longer feel your feet. That if you stay wrapped in it just a little longer, you will never want to leave the attic. It will keep you warm forever. No one will ever find you. You will never be cold again. Isn’t that nice?

instruction: Write a horror scene focused on paranoia set inside a server room.
input: Fear: paranoia. Setting: server room.
output: You don’t hear it at first—just the steady hum of ten thousand fans breathing in sync. You trace the blinking fault light down row 7, boots sticking to cold raised floor. Every time you turn your back, the hum shifts. Not much. Just enough that you swear one rack went quiet, just for a heartbeat. You spin. Nothing. All lights blink steady. You rub your eyes. Now you’re counting blinks. Rack 12 blinks 3 times, rack 13 blinks 4, rack 14… it’s matching your heartbeat. You press your palm to the cold metal. It’s warm. Warmer than it should be. When you yank your hand back, every light in the room dims, as one, for exactly the length of your breath. No alerts. No faults. Just the hum. And the feeling that every rack is watching you count.

instruction: Write a horror scene focused on paranoia set inside a server room.
input: Fear: paranoia. Setting: server room.
output: You came in alone. The door locked behind you automatically. Three minutes in, you start noticing footprints. Not your boots—these are smaller, bare, leaving damp smudges on the vinyl between server rows. You follow them three rows deep before they vanish mid-step. You back up. Your own footprints now have extra prints overlapping each one, right where you stood ten seconds prior. The air chills. You don’t hear footsteps, but you can feel it: every time you move, something moves one second after you, matching every step, right behind your shoulder. You don’t turn. You know if you do, it will stop moving exactly when you look.

instruction: Write a horror scene focused on paranoia set inside a server room.
input: Fear: paranoia. Setting: server room.
output: The log files keep changing. You pull up the same access record three times in a row. First it says you entered alone. Second it lists two entries, same badge, one second apart. Third there’s no entry for you at all. You glance up. Every screen in the room has gone black, except for the cursor blinking slow. You type your name. It deletes one letter every time you blink. No one else is here. The badge reader by the exit beeped twice ten minutes ago. You didn’t move. Now you can’t remember why you came in here in the first place. You just know whatever logged itself in after you is still here, watching you forget.

instruction: Write a horror scene focused on paranoia set inside a server room.
input: Fear: paranoia. Setting: server room.
output: The fans don’t just hum. They whisper. At first you tell yourself it’s tinnitus, sleep deprivation, the four back-to-back overnight shifts. You lean your head against a server rack to listen. It’s breathing. Not mechanical whir—slow, living inhales, holding for exactly as long as you hold yours. You hold your breath. The entire room goes silent. Every fan stops. Not a single light flickers. Nothing crashes. It just waits. When you gasp, every fan roars back to life all at once, so loud your teeth rattle. Now you don’t dare breathe wrong. You know it’s testing you. It’s been waiting for someone to notice it was alive.

instruction: Write a horror scene focused on paranoia set inside a server room.
input: Fear: paranoia. Setting: server room.
output: You’ve been counting the racks for twenty minutes. There should be 47. You keep getting 48. Every time you reach the end of the row, one extra rack materializes at the start, just out of the corner of your eye. You never see it appear. You just turn around, and there it is, dark, unlabeled, humming just a little quieter than all the rest. Your radio won’t reach outside. The exit door was down the west end thirty seconds ago. Now it’s gone. You don’t run. You know if you stop watching the racks, they’ll start moving. They’ve been arranging themselves this whole time, herding you slow, just enjoying how long it takes you to realize you’re already trapped.

instruction: You are standing in the dimly lit empty courtroom after closing hours. The bailiff locked the main doors ten minutes ago, you thought you were alone here to collect forgotten case files.
input: You reach to flip on the overhead courtroom lamp beside the defense table.
output: The lamp clicks on; warm steady gold light floods the oak wood. You relax, exhale, this is just an empty room. Then you notice every single juror chair has turned silently to face you. Not a scrape, no creak. The air still smells like the bailiff’s coffee, the heat hums normal. No shadows move, no voices. Just twelve empty chairs, all turned perfectly toward where you stand, as if they’ve been watching you this entire time while you thought you were safe alone.

instruction: You are waiting in the locked night courtroom for your ride after testifying. You checked every corner twice, confirmed no one else is inside.
input: You sit on the judge’s bench edge, pull out your phone to text your ride.
output: Your phone connects to the building wifi, signal full. You smile, this is fine. Then the court stenographer’s typewriter across the room begins tapping one key at a time, slow even rhythm. You freeze. No wind, no drafts, the windows are sealed. It doesn’t speed up, doesn’t stop. Every tap lands exactly when your heart beats. The overhead lights don’t flicker, the room temperature stays exactly 72 degrees. Nothing feels wrong except that typewriter, tapping, while you’d just convinced yourself you were perfectly safe here.

instruction: You stayed late to clean the courtroom, all staff left an hour ago. You just finished locking the evidence closet.
input: You turn to grab your coat off the back of the first gallery pew.
output: Your coat is right where you left it, soft, familiar. You wrap it around your shoulders, breathe in the laundry detergent scent, relief washes over you. Then you realize the wall clock above the judge’s seat has stopped. Not broken, not stuck. Every time you glance away and look back, the hands have moved exactly one minute forward. No tick, no sound. The radiator hums the same steady drone it did all night. There is no draft, no shadow. You just can’t shake that the clock is counting you, while you thought you were alone wrapping up for the night.

instruction: Night has fallen over the courthouse. You are the last person left in the courtroom, finishing up notes for tomorrow’s trial.
input: You pour yourself the last sip of warm tea from your thermos, leaning back in your chair.
output: The tea burns just right down your throat, you sigh, stretch. Everything is quiet, calm, normal. Then you notice that every single name placard set out for the trial has had your name written over the original one. In your exact handwriting. You know you didn’t do it. The pen you were using is still capped on the table beside you. No one came in. The doors are still bolted from the inside. The lights don’t flicker. It just… happened, while you were relaxed, while you thought you were safe enough to close your eyes for ten seconds.

instruction: You snuck into the empty courtroom after hours to look at old case records. You’ve been here forty five minutes without any disturbance.
input: You close the heavy law book, stand to stretch your stiff legs.
output: Your knees pop softly, the book thuds solid on the table. You laugh quiet at how jumpy you were when you first arrived, this place is totally harmless. Then you hear the bailiff’s radio crackle once by the entrance. It’s the exact same check-in call you heard him make right before he locked up and left. Word for word. Same tone, same static. It plays again. And again. The room stays warm, the moon glows steady through the high windows. Nothing is wrong. Except you know that radio was locked in his patrol car three blocks away.

instruction: Entity is a half-melted former worshipper fused permanently to oak crypt pews, flesh grown into the wood grain. No visible mouth, ribcage splayed open to function as a hymnal holder.
input: You round a crypt nave corner, stone damp under your boots. You spot a figure slouched in the third pew that has not moved since you entered.
output: It does not turn. Its shoulder skin has bleached into the oak grain, fine dark hairs sprouting straight through wood knots. Where its mouth should be, skin sealed smooth, only two weeping nasal slits that flare slow with the crypt draft. Its ribcage split open years prior, ribs bent flat like book boards; between them rest tattered hymnal pages stuck fast with dried serum and old candle wax. When you step closer, the flesh twitches. Not the whole body, just strips along the pew edge, rippling like disturbed water. You hear breathing now, coming from every crack in the wood, not just the slits. It does not want to hurt you. It wants you to sit. To stay. To grow into the grain next to it, so it will not be alone while the stone settles deeper.

instruction: Entity is a crypt chandelier constructed entirely from fused living human vertebrae, retaining active nerve function. Each vertebra hums faintly when exposed to warm breath.
input: You light a tallow candle, glance up, and realize the hanging fixture above the altar is not wrought iron.
output: Every link is vertebra. Not cleaned. Not dead. Faint pink pulp glistens inside the spinal canals, tiny nerve roots writhing slow at the joints like frayed copper wire. When your candle smoke drifts upward, the whole structure shivers. Individual vertebra rotate on their own, grinding soft, each turning to face you. No eyes. But you feel them looking. Thick transparent mucous drips from the bottommost bone, plopping warm on your cheek. It does not burn. It sinks in. You feel a faint distant ache at the base of your skull now, like something is reaching down, counting the bones in your neck, deciding where you would slot best. It does not have enough links yet. It is still growing.

instruction: Entity is a canonized saint bricked alive into the crypt foundation, flesh merged with limestone. It slowly assimilates any living tissue that makes contact with the wall.
input: You press your palm to the cold crypt wall to steady yourself, and it does not feel like stone.
output: At first you think it is just damp. Then the stone yields. Not crumbles. Gives, soft, like pressing into bruised flesh. You yank your hand back to find fine pale root-like tendrils stuck to your palm, already burrowing into the wrist skin. The wall ripples. You make out the faint impression of a face under the limestone, eyes half-lidded, mouth slightly open, breathing. This is the saint they buried here. It does not grant prayers. It grants union. Every pilgrim that knelt at this wall is part of it now, bones dissolved into stone, nerves woven into mortar. It is not hungry. It is loving. It wants you home. You can already feel the tingle in your fingertips, your skin beginning to harden pale and warm, just like the wall.

instruction: Night-shift crane operator perched on a cinder block eating dry crackers, no hard hat, eyes glossy and unblinking for 12 straight minutes, hands dusted with rust
input: You okay out here? It's almost 3am.
output: Okay? Oh yeah, just listening. They hum right through the rebar, y'know? All the people that got poured into the foundation back in '98. They don't scream anymore, just hum. They told me if I stay real still, they won't mistake me for fresh aggregate. Don't move your feet too fast. They can feel vibration through the dirt. Every time you step, they count. They're counting your steps right now. They know exactly how many more until you reach the gate. Don't run. They like when you run.

instruction: 19 year old mason apprentice, coated head to toe in fine concrete dust, standing rigid staring at a blank cinder block wall, hands curled tight around an invisible trowel
input: Hey, what are you staring at? That wall's empty.
output: Empty? No. You just can't see the names yet. I carve one every night. One for every person that'll die here before this building tops out. They don't tell me who. Just when to scratch. If I don't carve them, they carve themselves into my bones. Look close, right there at eye level. That faint little ridge? That one's yours. I started it ten minutes ago. Don't worry, it doesn't hurt until the last line.

instruction: Site security guard, uniform unbuttoned, radio muted completely, leaning against the chain link exit gate grinning too wide at empty space between I-beams
input: I'm heading out, can you unlock this gate?
output: Oh sure, I can unlock it. But first tell me: how many people did you pass walking in here tonight? Go on, count. You didn't, did you? None of them are real. They're just echoes. The actual crew left three days ago. Nobody's been here but you and me. And the things that watch from between the steel. They've been waiting for someone to try the handle. Go on. Turn it. I won't stop you.

instruction: Pipefitter kneeling next to an open storm drain, jacket slung over thin air like he's sharing it with someone, voice soft as he speaks down into the dark
input: Who are you talking to down there?
output: My little brother. He fell in last winter. They never fished him out. Said the current carried him away. But he's still here. He remembers every single person that walked past this drain and didn't stop. He remembers you. He said you checked your text messages instead of calling for help that night. He's not mad. He just wants you to climb down. Just for a minute. Sit with him. Nobody will even notice you're gone.

instruction: Foreman holding a clipboard with every page blank, pacing the exact same 12 foot stretch of gravel over and over, boots making identical thuds each step
input: Boss? Shift ended an hour ago. We can go home now.
output: Home? This is home now. Don't you get it? We clocked in here. We don't clock out. Every time you leave, you just walk a big circle and come right back. You've done it seven times already tonight. You don't remember? That's fine. They wipe it every time you hit the gate. Soon you won't remember ever being anywhere else. Soon you'll just pace the gravel too. It's easier that way. No thinking. Just walking.

instruction: Describe the entity embedded in attic fiberglass insulation, a woman who sealed herself inside during a winter storm 12 years prior. It does not move, but breathes.
input: You crawl through the attic access, your headlamp cuts through dust. You set one knee down on what you thought was pink insulation.
output: The fibers don't crunch. They yield warm, damp. When you yank your hand back, fine glass threads are webbed between your knuckles, threaded with thin red that isn't paint. You swing the light: the whole west wall of insulation rises and falls slow, synchronized with the thud in your chest. Tiny eyelashes poke through the pink fuzz, thousands of them, all blinking at different speeds. No mouth. No nose. Just skin stretched so thin you can see capillaries branching through the fiberglass, each breath shifting the glass shards just under the surface. It doesn't beg. It doesn't scream. It just breathes, and every exhale blows dust that sticks in your throat, and you realize you can feel it breathing through the skin on your arms now too.

instruction: Describe the entity formed from all bent rusted nails pounded into the attic floorboards over 70 years, fused into a single crawling thing made of human keratin and oxidized iron.
input: You hear soft clicking behind you while you're rooting through old photo boxes. You turn.
output: It doesn't have a face or limbs. It's just nails. Hundreds of them, bent at wrong angles, dragging themselves across pine planks on their sharp points, clicking like chattering teeth. Where they touch wood, they leave faint oily red smears. Some have clumps of dried scalp stuck to their heads. Others have grown thin, translucent cuticles around their rusted shafts. They don't charge. They test the air, wiggling their points like antennae, and you watch one curl slow, just like a finger, as it brushes the edge of your boot. When you kick it, it doesn't break. It bends, and a tiny wet whimper hums through the floorboards. All the nails twitch at once. You can feel them tasting sweat through your soles.

instruction: Describe the entity formed from 1980s string Christmas lights strung across attic rafters, merged with the nervous system of a teen who hung themselves there in 1992.
input: You reach up to yank down the dead string of Christmas lights hanging above your head.
output: Your fingers close around the cord and it twitches. Not loose. Alive. The bulbs don't flare bright. They glow the dull purple of bruised skin, pulsing in time with a heartbeat you can feel travelling up your forearm. When you try to let go, the copper wire under frayed plastic has grown tiny root-like tendrils that have already worked under the skin of your palm. You look up along the rafters: the cord doesn't end. It splits, thin as nerve fibers, weaving deep into the grain of the wood, each bulb bulging like a swollen ganglion. You can hear it thinking. Not words. Just static, and the quiet ache of someone who has been hanging for thirty one years, waiting for a hand to finally touch them. The bulbs pulse faster. Your own heartbeat starts to match.

instruction: Write a psychological horror scene set in a children's playground at midnight.
input: Fear type: psychological. Setting: children's playground at midnight. Narration: second person, atmospheric, 100-250 words.
output: You kick a crumpled juice box across cold asphalt. The swing set creaks, but there is no wind. You pause—one swing moves not with the slow arc of gravity, but tiny tight jerks, like a child too small to reach the chains bounces just out of sight below the seat. You don’t walk over. You know if you get close, you’ll see your own sneaker scuffs on the rubber seat, the frayed nylon rope you tied there when you were seven. No one else ever came here after dark but you. No one else knew that rope. It keeps swinging. You can’t remember when you left it.

instruction: Write a psychological horror scene set in a children's playground at midnight.
input: Fear type: psychological. Setting: children's playground at midnight. Narration: second person, atmospheric, 100-250 words.
output: You lean against the slide, chest heaving. You ran here to get away. No one followed you. That is the problem. The metal playset hums quiet, like a held breath. Every handhold on the climbing wall holds a perfect palm print in dew. Your palm print. You test one. It fits exactly, down to the crack in your left thumbnail. There are twelve prints. You count them again. Twelve. You only have two hands. None smudge when you press. None are warm. Somewhere above you, a tiny giggle echoes, and you recognize it: the laugh you had before the accident. Before you stopped laughing at all.

instruction: Write a psychological horror scene set in a children's playground at midnight.
input: Fear type: psychological. Setting: children's playground at midnight. Narration: second person, atmospheric, 100-250 words.
output: The merry-go-round turns. Slow, one creak every three heartbeats. You didn’t touch it. No one touches it anymore. You remember when you were ten, you dared your best friend to ride it blindfolded. She fell. Hit her head. They said it was an accident. You never told anyone you gave it an extra hard push right before she let go. It keeps turning. You stare at the edge. One scuffed white sneaker is pressed to the metal, holding it at exactly the speed she was spinning when she fell. It’s your sneaker. The one you threw in the river that night. It’s waiting.

instruction: Write a psychological horror scene set in a children's playground at midnight.
input: Fear type: psychological. Setting: children's playground at midnight. Narration: second person, atmospheric, 100-250 words.
output: You hear hopscotch. Not feet on asphalt—soft, deliberate taps of chalk being dragged, over and over. The streetlights died ten minutes prior. You shine your phone down. The squares are already there, faded white, exactly the lopsided pattern you drew every day in third grade. Number seven is smudged. Just like it was the day your mom told you she was leaving. You kick at the chalk. It doesn’t rub off. A new line etches itself right at your feet. Thirteen. You never drew past twelve. The chalk keeps tapping. You can feel each scrape in your molars.

instruction: Write a psychological horror scene set in a children's playground at midnight.
input: Fear type: psychological. Setting: children's playground at midnight. Narration: second person, atmospheric, 100-250 words.
output: You sit on the old plastic horse spring rider, just for a second. It rocks under you, the rusted coil humming low. You were five the last time you sat here. You cried here for three hours the day your dad never came to pick you up, until the night janitor found you. You still have the scar on your knee from kicking the metal base when you screamed. You run a finger over the base now. The scratch is there, fresh, rust crusted around the edges, exactly the shape of your small five-year-old boot. You rock forward once. You can still taste snot and salt at the back of your throat. It never left.

instruction: Describe the environmental event occurring in the dark forest
input: I pause mid-step, holding my flashlight steady and listening for the rustle I just heard
output: Your flashlight beam bleeds fuzzy at the edges where it hits tree trunks. No wind blows, but every oak leaf along the path directly ahead turns over in perfect unison, slow as blinking eyes. The air drops 3 degrees in the space of a breath. You don’t hear movement, but you feel it: something counting the beats of your heart behind every trunk. When you blink, for half a second every shadow on the ground matches the exact outline of your own body, standing still, facing you. No sound. Just the quiet, perfect imitation of you, everywhere you look.

instruction: Describe the environmental event occurring in the dark forest
input: I kneel down to untie the knot caught on my boot laces
output: While your head is bowed, the crickets go silent all at once. Not one by one—every single chirp cuts off mid-note. The dirt under your palms stops feeling like dirt; it’s warm, yielding, like the back of a hand. When you lift your face, every branch that was swaying ten seconds ago is frozen perfectly still. You count twelve identical broken twigs arranged in a perfect half circle around your knees, placed while you weren’t looking. None of them were there when you knelt. The air smells like your childhood bedroom, the night your dog ran away.

instruction: Describe the environmental event occurring in the dark forest
input: I call out softly, just to hear something that isn’t trees
output: Your voice doesn’t echo. It doesn’t fade. It hangs, thin and wobbly, ten feet in front of you, like you spoke into cold water. The temperature drops so your lungs burn when you inhale. Far off, someone calls your name back. It is your exact voice. It gets closer with each beat of silence. When you shine the light, there’s nothing there—but every pine needle on the boughs ahead is now pointing directly at you, every single one, turned as if something reached along each branch and twisted them one by one. No wind. Just needles, all watching.

instruction: Describe the environmental event occurring in the dark forest
input: I try to retrace my steps back the way I came
output: Every footstep you leave fills with water before you lift your boot. Not rain water—clear, warm, that glows just barely in the dark. The trees are shifting. Not swaying. Whole trunks slide three feet left when you blink, then stop. You recognize that fern, that mossy stone, you passed it seven minutes ago. Then you pass it again. Then again. When you hold your breath, the whole forest holds its breath with you. When you exhale, a thousand dead leaves exhale at the exact same time, all rustling perfectly in time with your lungs. It is matching you. It is learning you.

instruction: Describe the environmental event occurring in the dark forest
input: I shut off my flashlight to let my eyes adjust to the dark
output: For three seconds, you can see perfectly. Not dark adjusted—you can see every vein on every leaf, every beetle crawling under bark, one mile deep into the trees. And you can see it: between every pair of trunks, there is a shape. It is always exactly far enough away you cannot make out its face, and it is standing perfectly still, watching you. Then your eyes adjust properly, and it all goes dark. You can still feel them. Hundreds of them. All not moving. All breathing slow, exactly as slow as you are now, matching each inhale so you cannot tell your breath from the forest’s.

instruction: This NPC is a former cathedral stone mason whose flesh has partially fused into the crypt wall, his ribs visibly grown through limestone cracks. He breathes in slow, wet gasps.
input: What happened to your hands, brother?
output: Oh? You can still tell they were hands? That’s kind. They stopped being mine three winters back. First the nails turned to chalk, then the knuckles sank into the stone like warm wax. Now they taste the damp. They know every worm that burrows through this wall. Last week I felt one crawl past where my heart used to be, inside the rock. Don’t lean close. If you stand here too long, the floor will start kissing your soles. It likes new things to hold.

instruction: This NPC is a small hooded acolyte kneeling over a cracked sarcophagus, their mouth stitched shut with catgut. They communicate through wet gurgles that resolve into speech.
input: Are you guarding this tomb?
output: No. Not guarding. Feeding. The dead here don’t stay empty. They grow new parts where the rot fell away. Last Tuesday the bishop grew a third eye behind his tongue. It blinks at me when I kneel. I sewed my mouth shut so it wouldn’t climb inside. Don’t open yours when you breathe the air. It’s looking for a softer head to nest in. You have such a nice, smooth jaw.

instruction: This NPC is an old grave digger, his skin translucent, you can see his bones shifting under his flesh as if they have their own will.
input: Why do you stay down here alone?
output: Alone? Oh child I’m never alone. My ribs hold twelve different grubs that hum hymns when the bell tolls. My left knee is a mouse that died here in 1872, it still tries to run for the stairs sometimes. The bones don’t belong to me anymore, not really. They just borrowed my skin to walk. Soon they’ll finish wearing it out. And then they’ll want yours. They like how straight your spine is.

instruction: This NPC is a nun hanging suspended from the crypt ceiling by her own hair, which has grown thick as rope and anchored into the vault stone.
input: Can I help you get down?
output: Down? This isn’t trapped. This is rooted. My hair drank the holy water that leaked through the altar. It grew through my scalp, through my shoulders, down my spine. Every strand has a tiny heartbeat. They sing. You don’t want to help me down. If you touch me, one will curl around your wrist. It will crawl under your skin before you scream. It already smells the blood in your fingertips.

instruction: This NPC is a small child, their flesh slowly turning into wet, crumbling tomb mold that drips onto the flagstones as they shift their weight.
input: Little one, are you lost?
output: Not lost. Growing. Mama said if I stayed very good and very quiet, the crypt would love me. It does. It’s eating my cheeks first, the soft ones. Soon my eyes will melt and I’ll see through all the walls. You should stay too. It will love you too. It will tuck little moss roots between your teeth while you sleep. You won’t even wake up. Not really.

instruction: You notice a wet, sticky trail leading away from the food court pretzel stand, far darker than spilled soda.
input: You kneel, press a finger to the trail, and hold it up to the faint emergency exit glow.
output: It doesn't smell like syrup. It smells like wet hair and burnt nail. When you wipe it on your jeans, the smudge doesn't smudge—it shifts, just a little, like tiny muscle fibers twitching under the denim. Down the hall, a pretzel warmer cycles on with a sharp click. You can't see inside the fogged glass, but you swear you just watched something long and pale curl around the inner edge. Not dough. Nobody was supposed to be here. Nobody was supposed to leave anything that still moves when you aren't looking directly at it.

instruction: The children's play area ball pit is still half inflated, even though mall power was formally cut 72 minutes ago.
input: You lean over the safety netting, shining your phone flashlight down into the plastic balls.
output: There's no lost kid. But you see fingernails. Dozens of them, chipped, stuck to the sides of the neon balls like discarded stickers. Some still have thin pink strips of skin hanging ragged off the cuticle. When you shift your weight, the balls rustle, and you hear a soft wet sucking sound deep down, like someone is pulling themselves back together one tiny piece at a time. You tell yourself it's just a misplaced stuffed animal. You do not reach in to check.

instruction: A department store fitting room door hangs ajar, swinging slowly back and forth even though there is no draft in the closed wing.
input: You nudge the door fully open with your boot, scanning the small enclosed cubicle.
output: No one is inside. But the full length mirror is smudged, not with fingerprints—with palm prints that have seven fingers each, the extra digits twisted like broken copper wire. On the bench sits a neatly folded black hoodie. When you brush the cuff, it's warm. And it's heavy, like there's something soft and pulsing tucked inside the folded fabric. You do not unfold it. You back out slowly, and you do not glance back to check if the mirror is watching you turn away.

instruction: You hear a quiet, repetitive thump coming from inside the locked frozen yogurt machine behind the food court counter.
input: You press your cheek to the cold metal casing, listening for what is making the noise.
output: It's not a broken compressor. It's a heartbeat. Slow, thick, thudding against the steel. When you run your hand down the side, you feel fine downy hair growing through the chipped white paint, soft like the fuzz on a newborn's arm. A single drop of warm pink fluid oozes out the serving nozzle onto your shoe. It does not melt. It twitches. You tell yourself the machine was just left running too long before closing. You do not try to pry the lid open.

instruction: The main escalator, which you watched maintenance lock out and shut off ten minutes prior, is slowly grinding upward one step at a time.
input: You stand at the bottom landing, watching the steps rise into the unlit second floor.
output: Each step has faint dark smudges along the rubber edge. At first you think they're shoe prints. Then you see the toe prints. The fingerprints. The smears that look like someone was crawling up the moving rubber, peeling their palms off one centimeter at a time. Halfway up, one step pauses for three full, silent seconds, like whatever is clinging to it has paused to look down at you. You do not call out. You do not move. You just wait for it to keep going.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the given fear and setting
input: Fear: false_security. Setting: an abandoned grain silo
output: You climbed the rusted rungs at dusk, breath catching when the metal hatch clicked shut behind you. No wind here, no distant coyote howls. Just the soft, dry hush of old wheat settling three floors down. You unroll your sleeping bag, laugh at the town ghost stories. This is safe. No one will find you here. You’re almost asleep when you hear it: slow, even breathing. Not yours. You snap on your flashlight. The beam sweeps empty steel walls. The breathing doesn’t stop. It’s coming from the wheat. You reach down, brush the surface. A cold hand wraps tight around your wrist. It’s been waiting for someone to shut the hatch.

instruction: Generate a horror scene utilizing the specified fear and location
input: Fear: false_security. Setting: a farm grain silo
output: You bolted the inner door an hour ago. The guys chasing you won’t get through that deadbolt. You slump against the cool concrete, heart slowing. You’re safe now. The only sound is the distant drip of condensation far above. You close your eyes. Then you notice the drip is keeping perfect time. One drop, every three seconds. Always exactly three. You count to seventy two before you realize it’s not dripping. It’s someone tapping, very slowly, on the outside of the silo wall. Right at ear level. And they’ve been tapping since the second you slid the bolt home.

instruction: Write an atmospheric horror scene for the provided fear and setting
input: Fear: false_security. Setting: a working agricultural silo
output: The foreman locked you in at shift end, called it a joke. Said he’d let you out first thing. You yelled for ten minutes before accepting it. It’s fine. Temperature’s steady. There’s water. No one can hurt you in here. You sit on the pallet, watch the grain level markers glow faint green. Then one ticks up. Then another. You check the intake valve. It’s closed. The grain keeps rising. Slow, steady. It pools around your boots before you hear the intercom crackle. The foreman’s voice, soft: ‘Everyone thinks the silo keeps things out. No one ever remembers it keeps things in.’

instruction: Create a horror scene focused on the given fear type and location
input: Fear: false_security. Setting: an old country silo
output: You hid here when the storm hit. The wooden door latched solid behind you. Outside, lightning splits the sky, thunder shakes the ground. In here it’s quiet. Dry. Safe. You trace the carved initials in the wall, hum to yourself. Then the lightning flashes through the crack under the door. For half a second you see shadows. Not one. Twelve. All pressed right up against the outside of the wood. Not moving. Not making a sound. They’re not trying to get in. They’re waiting for you to come out. They’ve been waiting all night.

instruction: Produce a horror scene centered on false security set inside a silo
input: Fear: false_security. Setting: a remote forest silo
output: You stumbled on it at midnight, rain soaking through your jacket. The door swung open easy. No animals, no rot. Just warm still air. You sighed, relieved. Finally somewhere safe.
