You built a tiny fire, dried your socks. You didn’t notice the door had swung shut on its own until you went to check the rain. It wouldn’t budge. You pounded the wood, screamed. Somewhere deep in the dark above you, something exhaled softly. It doesn’t hunt people out in the storm. It just leaves the door open.

instruction: Trigger environmental paranoia event within the church confessional booth
input: Player rests their forehead against the cool confessional grille, waiting for the priest to speak
output: The grille’s metal does not stay cool. It warms slow, matching the exact temperature of your skin, like someone on the other side is pressing their forehead perfectly aligned with yours. No voice comes. Only the faint, regular scratch of a pen on paper, just loud enough that you can tell it is writing every word you are thinking right now. The candle on your ledge does not flicker, even when you exhale hard at it. You notice every crack in the wood has aligned into straight, watching rows. No one is breathing. Not even you, you realize, and you have not noticed for almost a minute.

instruction: Trigger environmental paranoia event within the church confessional booth
input: Player reaches down to adjust the hem of their coat on the booth floor
output: Your hand brushes something soft and warm that was not there one second prior. It does not move. When you yank your hand back, there is nothing there. The booth hums now, a low frequency that vibrates in your molars. Every time you blink, the shadow behind the grille moves one inch closer. Not fast enough that you can prove it. Not slow enough that you can pretend you did not see it. The air smells like your childhood bedroom, the night you woke up certain someone was standing over your bed. You can hear your own heartbeat echoing back from the walls, just slightly out of time.

instruction: Trigger environmental paranoia event within the church confessional booth
input: Player clears their throat to begin speaking their confession
output: Before any sound leaves your mouth, someone on the other side of the grille clears their throat first. Exact same pitch, exact same rough catch at the end. The temperature plummets; you can see your breath fog, even though it is 72 degrees outside. A small cross hanging above the grille turns very slowly, until the crucified figure is facing directly away from you. Every time you try to speak, you hear your own voice whispered back half a beat early, like it is leaking out of you before you can form the words. You cannot remember how you got into this booth. You cannot remember what you came here to confess.

instruction: Trigger environmental paranoia event within the church confessional booth
input: Player glances down at their watch to check how long they have been waiting
output: The watch hands are spinning. Not fast, just steady, turning backwards one tick every time your heart beats. When you look up, the gaps between the grille slats have narrowed. Just a little. Not enough to be certain. You can feel eyes on every part of your body at once: the back of your neck, your knuckles, the soles of your shoes through the booth floor. Somewhere very close, someone is counting quiet under their breath. They are counting every breath you take. You try to push the booth door. It does not rattle. It does not resist. It feels exactly like pressing solid wood that was never a door at all.

instruction: Trigger environmental paranoia event within the church confessional booth
input: Player leans sideways to peer through the grille at the priest’s booth
output: There is nothing on the other side. Not empty space. Nothing. Flat, uniform dark that does not reflect light, that swallows the edge of the grille where it should meet air. When you pull back, the bench under you shifts just a little, like it adjusted to fit the shape of your body better. You hear a fly buzz, but there is no fly. You hear a door creak open somewhere in the church, but it does not close. You realize you have not heard a single sound from outside this booth since you sat down. And the faint, certain feeling that whatever was behind that grille is now inside the booth, right behind your head.

instruction: NPC is a gaunt line cook with a blood-stained gauze wrap around his left wrist, standing motionless stirring a simmering pot that hasn't had ingredients added in an hour.
input: You've been stirring that same pot for ten minutes. You okay?
output: Oh, don't mind me. The hands just keep going, y'know? I tried to drop the spoon. I slammed my wrist against the counter. It just kept circling. They don't belong to me anymore, not after we locked the doors. Watch your own fingers. Wait too long in here, they start moving on their own. Start chopping when you're screaming inside not to. Start dragging you toward the walk-in. No one leaves until the kitchen is satisfied. Not me. Not you. Not the ones stacked in the freezer. We just keep working. No choice. No stop. Just stir.

instruction: NPC is an elderly dishwasher, face half-obscured by steam, hands submerged so long his knuckles have gone white and pruned.
input: Last load is done. We can lock up and go home now, right?
output: Home? You think we get to decide that? I haven't blinked in 47 minutes. My brain is screaming to stand up, to run for the back exit, but my feet are glued to this tile. The water tells me what to scrub. The plates whisper when they want turning. The second the last customer left, the doors locked themselves. We don't run this kitchen anymore. It runs us. Soon you'll stop fighting it. Soon you'll just wash. Even when your skin rots off your bones. Even when you can't remember your own name. You'll just wash.

instruction: NPC is the sous chef, leaning rigid over a prep table, chef's knife hovering an inch above an unpeeled onion, entire body trembling.
input: What's wrong? Why aren't you cutting that onion?
output: I don't want to cut it. I hate onions. But the knife is pulling down. My arm is shaking so hard my teeth rattle, but I can't let go. I tried to scream. Nothing comes out. I tried to step back. My legs won't move. This kitchen doesn't care what I want. It doesn't care what you want. Every breath, every blink, every twitch of your fingers? It's already decided. You're just along for the ride. Pretty soon you won't even remember what it felt like to choose anything at all.

instruction: NPC is a 17 year old busser, huddled on the greasy floor rocking slowly, staring unblinking at his own palms.
input: C'mon, get up. Let's go find the exit.
output: Don't touch me. Don't. Last time I tried to stand, my body turned and started wiping down tables instead. I cried the whole time. I was begging myself to stop. But my hands just kept wiping. This place gets inside your bones. It tells your body what to do and you just watch. Trapped inside your own head while your limbs do whatever it wants. You'll see. Soon you'll be wiping too. Even when there's no dust left. Even when the walls crumble. You'll just keep wiping.

instruction: NPC is the head chef, planted rigid in front of a roaring stove, one hand clamped to the gas dial, flesh already blistering.
input: Chef? That stove is way too hot. Turn it off before the grease catches.
output: I can't. I've been trying for three hours. I can feel the metal burning through my palm. But I can't twist it. I can't step back. I can't blink. The kitchen wants it hot. It wants this whole place to burn. And I'm going to stand here and hold this dial until my flesh melts into the metal. And you? You're going to help. You don't get to say no. None of us do. We're just meat puppets now. For the grills. For the knives. For this hungry old kitchen.

instruction: Describe environmental event in hospital morgue
input: You pause mid-step, reaching to turn on the overhead fluorescent bank after locking the receiving bay door
output: The lights stutter once then die entirely. Only the faint red exit sign bleeds over the slab rows. You fumble for your phone, but every drawer along the west wall slides open one by one, slow, no rattle, no draft. No footfalls but you can hear breathing, not yours, evenly spaced between each drawer stopping. The cold doesn’t bite your skin anymore—it seeps like it’s welcoming you. No one will check this wing for 11 hours. You are completely alone with whatever just woke up.

instruction: Describe environmental event in hospital morgue
input: You kneel to retrieve a dropped pen beneath the autopsy table
output: When you lift your head, every wall label has vanished. The doors are gone. Just endless rows of body drawers stretching out in every direction, faint hum of refrigeration warping into quiet whispers. Your radio only emits static. When you call out, your voice doesn’t echo—it vanishes like it was eaten. Each drawer clicks softly now, one after another, counting down. There is no exit. No one knows you are here. No one is coming.

instruction: Describe environmental event in hospital morgue
input: You log out of the computer, preparing to leave for the night
output: The lock on the exit door clicks shut before you reach it. You hear your own car start in the parking lot, then drive away. All exterior windows black out. The thermostat display ticks down, one degree every ten seconds. A body bag on the nearest slab shifts very slowly, as if someone inside is rolling over to face you. You have no signal. No one expected you home tonight. There is nowhere to run, no one to call. You are trapped here, entirely on your own.

instruction: Describe environmental event in hospital morgue
input: You shine your flashlight down the cold storage corridor to check a faint noise
output: The beam doesn’t hit the far wall. It just fades into black, like the corridor stretched while you blinked. All the other staff left 47 minutes ago. You can hear soft footsteps behind you, but when you spin, nothing is there—only the morgue tags dangling from drawer handles, swinging in no wind. Each time you turn back, the end of the hall is further away. No one will hear you scream through the reinforced concrete. You are completely, irreversibly alone.

instruction: Describe environmental event in hospital morgue
input: You lean against the wall to catch your breath after moving a heavy body tray
output: The wall doesn’t feel like concrete anymore. It’s soft, warm, breathing. When you jerk away, you notice every clock in the room has stopped at the exact second the last nurse left the building. The phone line went dead an hour ago and no one noticed. Drawer 147 thumps once, soft, then quiet. You realize no one on the planet knows you are in this room right now. If you vanished tonight, no one would even start looking for three days.

instruction: Entity is a former student fused seamless into the west hallway cinder block wall, only half a face and one groping hand visible beneath layers of chipped school paint. It breathes through wall pores, does not rush.
input: You round the corner of the unlit 2nd floor hallway, only the exit sign casting thin red glow. You hear wet breathing that does not echo correctly. You freeze.
output: Off-white paint bleeds warm where it exhales. Eyelashes grow through drywall, one muddy brown eye rolling slow under a skin-film of primer. The hand pushes out gradual, finger bones stretching until nails pop free and leave wet craters on linoleum. It does not lunge. It only brushes your ankle. When you yank back, you see tiny smudged student ID photos embedded in its wrist flesh, faces worn thin like eraser marks. It whispers half-remembered homework answers through wall cracks, voice gurgling like someone drowning in chalk dust. Every step you attempt further down the hall, you feel your own shoulder tingle, skin beginning to stick and melt into the cool cinder block as if it has always belonged there.

instruction: Entity is the dented chrome hallway drinking fountain, all internal plumbing replaced with pulsing human tendon and gum tissue. It never dispenses water.
input: Your throat burns raw after 40 minutes wandering this empty hall. You spot the drinking fountain 20 feet ahead and approach it.
output: Pressing the lever produces no mechanical whir, only a soft muffled sob. Warm clear fluid drools from the spout, thick as saliva, dotted with loose baby molars. You jerk back but your palm has already welded to the cool chrome, skin bonding fast. You feel something suck slow at your fingertips through the opening, drawing tiny pulls of blood. Under the basin, veins bulge through cracked metal, pulsing exactly in time with your heartbeat. The drain gurgles, and a whole human ear rolls out, plopping wet on the tile. It twitches. When you finally rip free, you leave three layers of skin stuck to the lever, still bleeding slow into the fountain's hungry mouth.

instruction: Entity is a red hallway fire alarm pull station constructed from folded, dyed living human skin. It blinks.
input: You catch acrid smoke curling down the empty hall. You spot the red fire alarm box mounted at waist height and move to activate it.
output: Your fingers sink soft before you make contact. It is warm, not plastic. The glass break panel is a thin transparent eyelid that blinks slow as you approach. Wrapping your hand around the pull lever reveals it is raw bone wrapped in calloused skin, and it squeezes back. Tiny needle teeth sink into your palm, drinking fast. No alarm wails. It whimpers, and skin ripples down the wall: every fire alarm along the hallway opens their matching eyes, blinking in perfect unison. When you yank away, your palm is streaked with thin red lymph. The teeth marks heal wrong, raised ridged skin forming the exact shape of the pull lever. It will never fade.

instruction: You stand in total darkness inside the planetarium; a faint unrecognizable whir rises from the central projector, and a loose metal panel dangles from its base.
input: You kneel, pry the panel open with your nail, and reach inside the cold projector casing.
output: Your fingers first brush sticky damp wiring—then something wraps tight around your wrist. It pulses, not metal, not plastic. You yank hard, it releases without a mark or residue on your skin. The projector whir climbs an octave. Overhead, one star mount twitches on its servo, just enough to make a tiny, sharp click. It is not programmed to move unattended. You try to recall if you locked the front entrance. You cannot.

instruction: A crumpled usher’s notebook lies three rows back, lit only by the faint red glow of the emergency exit sign.
input: You pick up the notebook, flip to the last marked page, and squint to read the smudged pencil script.
output: These are not shift logs. Every line is crossed out twice: don’t count the stars, don’t stare at Orion, don’t blink longer than seven seconds, don’t try to leave. At the bottom, scrawled over itself a dozen times: it doesn’t need the lights on. A single pinpoint star blinks on dead-center of the dome. You touched no controls. Your phone, at 78% battery ten minutes prior, will not power on.

instruction: You hear soft, even breathing that does not match your own rhythm, coming from the empty front row.
input: You step slowly forward, hand outstretched, and call quietly to ask if anyone is there.
output: Your voice dies halfway out. It does not echo. It vanishes, swallowed whole by the dark. When you reach the front row, the seat is still warm, like someone stood up seconds before you arrived. You reach for the side wall to steady yourself, and your hand meets empty air. The room got bigger. You can no longer feel the exit door behind you. You did not walk that far.

instruction: A tiny glow-in-the-dark star sticker skitters across the floor past your shoe, stopping directly at your feet.
input: You bend down, pick up the sticker, and turn it over between your thumb and forefinger.
output: The adhesive is still wet. It was peeled off something less than sixty seconds ago. When you lift your head, hundreds of identical blue-glowing stars are now stuck across the entire dome. They were not there ten seconds prior. You try to drop the sticker in your hand. It will not release. It has bonded to your skin. The star field overhead is rotating. Wrong direction.

instruction: The fire alarm chirps once sharply then cuts out; you smell burnt copper drifting from the raised projector booth.
input: You feel along the wall for the booth access ladder, and begin climbing the rickety metal rungs one at a time.
output: Halfway up, the next rung does not exist. Your hand closes on empty air where you counted it would be. There were twelve rungs. You are on the seventh. When you try to climb back down, the rungs below you are also gone. You hang suspended in total dark, no solid surface under your feet or above your hands. The projector whir grows louder. Very far away, you hear the front entrance lock click shut.

instruction: Entity is a waterlogged disembodied mine supervisor’s hand, still curled around a brass shift whistle, that drifts just below the murky flood water surface.
input: You are wading thigh-deep in the flooded shaft, your headlamp cutting 3 feet ahead, when you brush something cold and rigid against your calf.
output: It does not yank you under. It wraps one cold, pruned finger around your Achilles tendon first, and you feel the moment it hooks into your nerve. You try to jerk back, but your leg will not move. The whistle blows once, silent in the water, and you feel the signal travel up your spine. Your opposite foot lifts on its own, stepping deeper. You can watch your body walk, you can feel the water rise past your hips, your ribs, but you cannot make it stop. Your own hand drifts down, curls around the brass whistle. You do not decide to breathe in the water. Your lungs just do it. You are not being taken. You are being operated.

instruction: Entity is a colony of translucent flood-bred isopods that aggregate into the shape of former miners, moving only when you look away.
input: You pause to catch your breath, wiping condensation from your headlamp, and swear you saw three figures standing 20 yards down the tunnel.
output: When you blink they dissolve, but you feel the first tiny prick on your ankle. They do not bite. They connect. One by one they climb your socks, your calves, nestle along your spine, thousands of tiny legs settling into the gaps between your muscles. Every time you lift a hand to swat, they shift just out of reach, before you can even finish forming the motion. Your head turns left on its own. Your mouth opens, and you call out a miner’s name you have never heard. They are not possessing you. They are voting. And there are far more of them than there are cells that still answer only to you.

instruction: Entity is the echo of the mine collapse, suspended in the flood water, that will replay itself through any living body that disturbs it.
input: Your boot dislodges a loose rotted timber, sending a deep low ripple spreading out across the still black water.
output: First your teeth rattle, not from cold. The vibration climbs your jaw, settles heavy in your sternum. You hear the roar of falling stone not with your ears, but inside your bones. Your arms fly up to cover your head before you can think. Your knees buckle, you crash face-first into the flood and cannot push yourself back up. Water fills your mouth, but you do not drown. You just feel the mine break, over and over. Every muscle locks into the wild panic of men buried alive. You cannot stop shaking. You cannot close your throat. You cannot even choose to be afraid. You are just the vessel this dead collapse uses to keep happening, forever in the dark.

instruction: Generate a psychological horror scene for the specified setting and fear type
input: Setting: underground parking level B3. Fear: psychological
output: You step off the elevator when the doors slide open on B3. You pressed lobby. The light above flickers once, and every car alarm chirps at exactly the same time. All go silent immediately. No wind, no distant traffic. You reach for the elevator call button. It’s gone. Smooth concrete where it should be. You walk the rows. Every rear view mirror shows someone standing right behind your shoulder. You spin each time. Nothing. You learn not to glance up. You learn to count the cracks in the floor, because the mirrors never lie.

instruction: Generate a psychological horror scene for the specified setting and fear type
input: Setting: underground parking level B3. Fear: psychological
output: You’re pacing B3 looking for your car when you notice the silence first. No hum of ventilation. No distant rumble from the floors above. You pull out your phone. 0 bars, but the clock is ticking backwards. Every time you blink, the parking space numbers shift. One minute you’re at spot 37, next blink 112, next 7. You stop breathing for ten seconds. Somewhere three rows over, someone exhales. You can hear their lungs deflate. You don’t call out. You already know it’s the sound you make when you sleep.

instruction: Generate a psychological horror scene for the specified setting and fear type
input: Setting: underground parking level B3. Fear: psychological
output: The stairwell door slams shut behind you on B3. You don’t remember walking down three extra flights. All the lights are out except one single bulb at the far end, swinging slow. You can see your car under it. But every step you take, it gets further away. You break into a run. The car recedes. You stop. It stops. You laugh, shaky, and turn back for the stairs. They’re gone. Just solid wall where the door was. The bulb stops swinging. It’s quiet now. You can feel it watching you count the steps that don’t exist.

instruction: Generate a psychological horror scene for the specified setting and fear type
input: Setting: underground parking level B3. Fear: psychological
output: You drop your keys on B3’s concrete. When you bend to grab them, you freeze. Under the car beside you, there are boots. Not moving. Just scuffed work boots, toes pointing at your knees. You stand slow. You walk all the way around the car. Nobody there. You walk ten spots away. Drop your keys again. Bend down. The boots are there. Same scuff on the toe. Same tiny drop of oil on the laces. You don’t drop your keys a third time. You don’t bend over ever again while you’re on this floor.

instruction: Generate a psychological horror scene for the specified setting and fear type
input: Setting: underground parking level B3. Fear: psychological
output: You finally find your car on B3, fumbling for the fob, when you hear it. Someone whistling the lullaby your mom sang you. The one nobody else knows. You spin. Empty rows. The whistle gets closer. You hit the lock button twice. It beeps. The whistle stops mid-note. Then, from the back seat of your own car, the whistle picks up again. Exactly where you sit. You don’t open the door. You stand there, listening, wondering how long it’s already been riding home with you.

instruction: Entity is a semi-sentient human skin impression that bleeds through hallway drywall, only visible when you pass the same wall segment 3+ times
input: You round the 4th identical left turn, pause to touch the off-white wall
output: The paint gives under your thumb first, not drywall, warm damp dermis. You yank back- a perfect impression of your own palm is already sunk into the wall, pores bleeding clear lymph. It doesn't stop. Your shoulder outline bleeds through next, then the curve of your jaw, each line deepening like someone is pressing you into the wall from the other side. You can feel it now, faint pressure against your ribs even as you stand three feet away. When you glance over your shoulder, the wall behind you already has half your body sunken into it, mouth stretched open in a silent gasp you haven't made yet. Every hallway you walk is just laying down the next layer of your own body into the drywall. You will not run out of hallway before it finishes pressing you flat, one identical turn at a time.

instruction: Entity is your own echo that gains physical mass, stealing body parts with each repeated footfall down the looping hallway
input: You count your steps. 127. Same tile. You hear your own footfall land twice
output: The second step lands too heavy, wet. You freeze. When you lift your left boot, there's already a foot there, same scuff on the toe, same broken nail, rooted to the tile. It twitches. Next step, your ankle goes numb. You look down- it's not your ankle anymore. The echo is building itself one piece at a time, each repeat of the hallway yanking another tendon, another patch of skin off you to wear. By the 7th loop you can taste it breathing the same air as you. It doesn't hate you. It's just you, finally tired of walking in circles. It will take your lungs last. It will keep walking the hallway forever, wearing every part of you that ever got tired of turning the same corner. You will not even remember you ever wanted to escape.

instruction: Entity is fine human hair that grows through hallway floor grout, thickening with each loop until it binds and merges with living flesh
input: You kneel to check the scuff mark on grout line 19, the one you swore wasn't there last loop
output: When you brush the grout line, it moves. Not dirt. Fine, dark human hair, rooted deep in the tile, each strand ending in a tiny living follicle glistening with sebum. You stand too fast- three strands are already wrapped around your wrist, pulling just hard enough that you feel each one sawing the first layer of skin. They grow faster when you panic. By the third time you pass this grout line, the hairs are wrist thick, writhing like eels, each one dotted with human pores that sweat warm blood. They don't drag you down. They just grow through you. First the soles of your feet, then your calves, rooting you to the hallway. You will become the thing the next person brushes off the grout, when they stop to check the mark that wasn't there before.

instruction: You are browsing the back workbench of the taxidermy shop, running a finger over half-finished deer hides stretched tight on wooden frames
input: You call out to the shop owner, who stepped behind the fabric curtain just ten seconds prior
output: No reply comes. The overhead fluorescent buzz cuts out abruptly, leaving only the dim amber glow of one cracked desk lamp. Every glass eye on the mounted foxes lining the upper shelves has turned, uniformly, to face you. You listen for traffic, birds, wind—nothing. No sound proves there is a world beyond these four walls. When you step toward the front door, every mounted head on the wall turns slowly to track your movement. Your shouts die flat in the thick, fur-scented air. The shop has swallowed you whole.

instruction: You are sorting through a cardboard box of faded taxidermy tags near the back storage shelf
input: You knock over a jar of formaldehyde, and call out for paper towels to clean the spill
output: The glass shatters too slow, each sharp fragment hanging suspended half a heartbeat before hitting the linoleum. The shop owner does not answer. Every ticking clock on the wall has stopped at exactly 3:17. When you glance up, the front exit door is gone—only seamless pine paneling sits where it used to be. You cannot remember the name of the street outside. The air grows cold enough to prickle your arms. No one is coming. No one even knows you are here.

instruction: You are examining a mounted barn owl perched on a branch above the cash register
input: You turn around to ask the owner how old the owl specimen is
output: The metal stool the owner was sitting on is empty, still warm to the touch. The radio that was playing 1970s country has dissolved into empty white static, no stations tuning through at all. Every drawer in the shop stands wide open, all of them completely bare. When you rap your knuckles on the front window, it yields soft like pressed cloth, and there is only endless black on the other side. There is no one else here. There never was. You are alone with all the dead things.

instruction: You are helping lift a finished black bear mount onto a wall hook
input: You let go of the bear’s shoulder and step back, waiting for the owner to secure the hardware
output: The owner is gone. The bear does not fall. It hangs perfectly level, unhooked, floating half an inch from the drywall. The temperature has dropped ten degrees in seconds; you can see your breath fog when you exhale. Every glass eye in the room glints like wet river stone. You yell until your throat burns raw. No sound carries past the edges of the shop. Nothing echoes. The air eats every noise you make, every proof that you exist. No one will find you.

instruction: You are wiping dust off a row of mounted songbirds arranged on a oak side shelf
input: You huff a quiet laugh at a lopsided sparrow and glance over to share the joke
output: The laugh dies in your throat. The shop is empty. The lopsided sparrow has twisted its head on its wooden peg to stare directly at you. One by one, every songbird on the shelf turns until all forty seven are watching. You grab the brass front door bell and shake it as hard as you can—it makes no sound at all. There is nothing to hear but your own ragged breathing. You are completely, irreversibly alone inside this shop, with no way out, no one coming.

instruction: You notice the night clerk has fallen asleep at the front desk, head resting on an open guest log. A half-empty warm coffee mug sits beside them, not a single alarm blaring. The lobby is dead silent, only the hum of the ice machine down the hall.
input: You walk quietly to the front desk to check the clerk is okay
output: When you lean in, you see the clerk isn't breathing. Their skin is already cool. But before you can react, you glance at the open log: every guest room is marked 'checked out'—except yours. A faint click echoes from the elevator behind you, but when you spin, the doors are still closed, no lights on. The coffee mug has no scent at all, like it was never filled.

instruction: A housekeeping cart is abandoned by the couches, folded linens stacked neatly. A soft lullaby plays quiet from a radio perched on top. No one is around, all the exit signs glow steady green.
input: You check the cart to see if anyone left their phone
output: You rummage the top shelf and find nothing but clean towels, until your hand brushes something cold under the linens. It's a set of master keycards, every single one marked 'VOID' in smudged wet ink. The lullaby cuts out mid-note, replaced by three slow, distant knocks on the front hotel doors. No one is outside the glass when you look. The exit signs just flickered once, you swear.

instruction: The lobby fireplace still has glowing embers, someone left a knit blanket draped over the armchair. It smells like vanilla candle, the whole space feels warm and safe, not a single thing out of place.
input: You go sit in the armchair to catch your breath for a minute
output: When you sink into the chair, the blanket sticks slightly to your wrist. You pull it back: there's faint dried blood crusted along the blanket edge, hidden under the fold. The embers in the fireplace don't give off any heat, even though they glow bright. You realize you haven't heard a single car pass on the road outside for over twenty minutes. Not even wind.

instruction: A vending machine by the stairs hums normally, all the sodas lit up inside. Someone left an unopened bottle of water on the ledge, cap still sealed. The security camera above it slowly pans back and forth like always.
input: You check the security camera feed display mounted beside the vending machine
output: The screen shows the lobby exactly as it is, except you aren't there. It shows the empty chair, the front desk, the sleeping clerk—no sign of you anywhere. A can of soda drops into the vending machine tray with a loud thud, even though no one pressed a button. The security camera has stopped panning. It's pointed directly at you now, on the feed and in real life.

instruction: The lobby payphone rings once, soft, then stops. There's no dial tone when you pick it up at first, then a faint static. All the overhead lights are steady, no flickering, nothing weird at first glance.
input: You hold the payphone receiver to your ear and listen closer
output: Beneath the static you can hear your own breathing, echoing back with a two second delay. Then a quiet woman's voice whispers, 'Don't unlock the door. They already let themselves in.' You drop the receiver, spin around. The lobby is exactly the same. Every door is shut. The clerk is still resting their head on the desk. No one moved. Nothing changed. Not yet.

instruction: You are climbing the outer service ladder of the remote radio tower, wind screaming through the guy wires. You swear you heard someone clear their throat directly behind you three times now, but every time you turn there's only empty air.
input: You pause halfway up, shining your headlamp down the ladder rungs to check for anyone following.
output: The headlamp catches nothing but wet rusted metal, until you glance at the shadow cast by your own body on the tower frame. It's moving wrong. Where you're standing still, the shadow's shoulders keep twitching like it's trying to turn and look at you. When you blink, it's normal again. Far below, a single radio crackles to life for half a second, broadcasting nothing but steady, even breathing. No one left on this mountain but you. You tell yourself that wind just plays tricks.

instruction: You reach the tower's equipment platform. All the control panel LEDs are dark, except one tiny red light that blinks exactly in time with your heartbeat.
input: You lean in close to the control panel, pressing your ear to the metal casing to listen for internal hums.
output: Through the cold steel you don't hear circuits. You hear soft counting. One, two, three, pausing every fourth beat to match the gaps when you breathe. When you yank your head back you notice fresh scuff marks on the platform edge right where you were standing, like someone was just leaning over you, watching the back of your neck. They're not there now. You can't remember if you brought boots that leave that tread. Maybe it was just you earlier. Maybe.

instruction: A gust of wind slams the maintenance shed door behind you hard enough to rattle the tower's guy wires. You didn't even know there was a maintenance shed up here.
input: You pull the shed door open slowly, flashlight held out in front of you.
output: It's empty. Empty except for a half-empty coffee mug, still steaming. A chair pulled up directly facing the ladder you just climbed. On the wall someone has scratched your full name, over and over, each one slightly messier than the last. When you step back out onto the platform you realize the wind has stopped entirely. You can't hear any birds, any insects. Just the faint, distant sound of someone walking very carefully around the other side of the tower.

instruction: You notice one of the coaxial cables running up the tower has been cut clean through, the ends stripped back like someone was preparing to splice something into it.
input: You kneel down to examine the cut cable, running your finger along the clean copper edge.
output: The copper is warm. Too warm, like it's been held in someone's hand ten seconds before you got here. When you stand up you catch a glimpse of your reflection in the tower's dish. There's a figure standing directly behind you, hand raised like it's about to touch your shoulder. You spin. Nothing there. When you look back at the reflection, it's just you now. But your reflection is still staring at the spot where you just turned.

instruction: The tower's main broadcast speaker crackles awake; no one has touched the controls in twenty years according to the site logs.
input: You lean forward, trying to make out what the speaker is saying over the static.
output: At first it's just static, then you realize it's not random. It's the sound of you breathing. Every inhale, every little swallow, every shift of your boots on the metal, broadcast back to you with a three second delay. That means someone is listening. Recording. Transmitting. Somewhere on this tower, right now. You don't dare turn around. You don't dare stop breathing normally. You can feel eyes on the back of your skull. You can't prove it. No one ever can.

instruction: You stand in the aisle of the abandoned theater, velvet seats rotting on either side. A faint wheezing echoes from the back row.
input: I step forward slowly, shining my flashlight down the center aisle to locate the sound.
output: The flashlight beam catches a seat cushion that twitches. It doesn't just shift—it breathes, damp velvet rising and falling in shallow ragged gasps. When the light lands full on it, hundreds of tiny human fingernails push through the rotten fabric, scrabbling at the air. Your throat suddenly tightens, like fine threads are being pulled through the soft tissue inside your neck. The theater smells like wet tooth and old blood now, and every exhale fogs in the sudden freezing cold.

instruction: You reach the theater's stage, warped wooden planks buckling underfoot. Tattered curtain ropes hang slack beside you.
input: I tug one of the curtain ropes gently to see if the main curtain will shift even a little.
output: The rope doesn't give—it sticks to your fingers first, then melts. Not burns, melts into warm living membrane that crawls up your wrist, leaving faint purple veins blooming under your skin. You can feel it tasting the salt of your sweat as it climbs. The curtain ripples on its own, folds parting to reveal rows of wet, blinking human eyes set directly into the fabric. Your own eyes start watering uncontrollably, each blink stinging like broken glass.

instruction: You are exploring the theater's projection booth, dust caking every surface. A half-eaten candy bar sits on the control console.
input: I nudge the candy bar with the tip of my boot to see if anything is hiding under it.
output: The candy bar rolls over, and you see it has grown tiny, twitching human toes where the chocolate wrapper tore. It oozes caramel thick as blood when it twitches. A sharp, burning itch erupts along the sole of the boot that touched it; you yank your foot up to see tiny hair follicles pushing through the rubber sole, growing fast. The projector clicks on by itself, casting light that makes your skin prickle like thousands of tiny needles are pressing in.

instruction: You crouch in the abandoned theater's lobby, broken ticket stubs crunched under your shoes. A mirror hangs cracked above the concession stand.
input: I glance up into the cracked mirror to check behind me for movement.
output: Your reflection doesn't look back. Instead, you watch your own skin in the glass bubble and split, tiny theater seats pushing out through your cheekbone, little velvet armrests pricking through your jaw. You reach up to touch your face; it feels smooth, unbroken, but you can feel the seats inside you now, shifting, settling. The air goes thick enough to chew, and when you swallow you taste rotting velvet. Your heartbeat slows, matching the slow creak of the theater's old rafters.

instruction: You find a locked dressing room backstage, door warped slightly ajar. Muffled tapping comes from inside.
input: I press my ear flat against the dressing room door to try and hear what is making the tapping sound.
output: The door doesn't feel like wood anymore when your ear touches it. It feels like warm, breathing skin. The tapping stops, and instead you feel something press against the other side of the door, exactly where your ear is. You can feel teeth through the wood, nipping softly at the cartilage of your ear. When you yank back, a thin strand of sticky mucous connects your earlobe to the door. Your hearing fades in one ear, replaced by the sound of thousands of people whispering just out of range. The whole theater hums, deep in its bones.

instruction: Generate an atmospheric body horror scene using the given setting and fear type
input: Fear: body_horror. Setting: fog-shrouded rooftop
output: Fog swallows the city below so completely you can’t tell where rooftop ends and sky begins. You lean against the AC unit for balance, only to feel cold tar seep through your jeans not onto skin, but into it. You yank your leg up. The sole of your boot has fused to your ankle; the rubber is now warped, pink, pulsing with your own pulse. When you scrape at it with a nail, it bleeds. The fog curls around your wrists. You watch your knuckles soften, blur, dissolve into the mist like wet chalk. You can still feel every finger. You can feel them drifting apart, spreading through the fog, touching things you can’t see.

instruction: Write a second-person horror scene focused on the specified fear and location
input: Fear: body_horror. Setting: foggy rooftop at midnight
output: You came up here to escape the noise. Now the fog muffles every sound except your own breathing. You rub the cold out of your arms, and your hand catches on something sharp protruding from your forearm. It’s a rib. Thin, white, pushing clean through your sweater. You gasp, and another juts out your collarbone. They don’t hurt. They feel like they belong. One by one they unfurl, curved, glistening wet with fog, spreading like a cage around your chest. You can’t move back. They’re rooting into the rooftop tar now, holding you here, while the fog seeps between the bones to fill the hollow where your heart used to beat.

instruction: Craft a tense body horror scene set in the provided environment
input: Fear: body_horror. Setting: fog-covered urban rooftop
output: The fog makes every shadow look alive. You spot a figure standing ten feet away, still as stone. You call out. It doesn’t answer. You step closer. It’s you. Not a mirror—your own body, standing motionless, eyes milky white, mouth hanging open. You reach for its shoulder. Your hand sinks into its chest like warm wax. It doesn’t react. You feel the pressure in your own chest, like someone’s hand is inside you. When you yank your arm back, you’re holding your own liver, glistening red in the fog. The copy of you slumps forward, hollow, and you still don’t feel any pain. Just cold. Just empty.

instruction: Write an atmospheric second-person body horror scene for the given setting
input: Fear: body_horror. Setting: foggy rooftop
output: You crouch at the rooftop edge, counting the faint blurs of streetlights far below. Every time you exhale, the fog curls around your breath and holds it. You notice after the seventh exhale: your teeth are gone. You run your tongue over empty gums, panicking, and something taps your boot. One of your molars, wet, resting on the tar. Then another. You watch them fall out one by one, clicking softly against the rooftop, rolling into the fog. You can still taste each one as it leaves. When you try to scream, only mist pours from your mouth. The fog is breathing for you now, one tooth at a time.

instruction: Generate a quiet, unsettling body horror scene in the specified location
input: Fear: body_horror. Setting: fog-obscured rooftop
output: The fog has soaked through every layer of clothing, so cold it burns. You rub your eyes, and your eyelid comes off between your fingers. Thin, translucent, still lined with lashes. You don’t feel anything. You stare at the little flap of skin in your palm, then touch your face. The socket is wet, open, the fog curling right against your eyeball. You drop the eyelid. It drifts into the mist. When you blink, the other one comes loose too. You can still see perfectly. You just can’t close your eyes. Not ever again. The fog will never let you look away now.

instruction: Write a horror jumpscare scene set in a hotel lobby at 3am
input: Setting: empty hotel lobby, 3:07am. Only flickering exit sign glows red. You just came down for vending machine water after a nightmare.
output: The linoleum sticks soft to your socked feet, no sound but the hum of a dead elevator fan. You pass the front desk—no night clerk, just a half-empty coffee mug still steaming. The vending machine whirs before you press anything, dispensing a bottle that clatters loud enough to make your shoulders jump. You bend, fingers brushing cold plastic, when you feel warm breath on the back of your neck. You freeze. The exit sign flickers out for one full second. When it flares red again, a woman’s grinning, bloodied face is pressed inches from yours, eyes solid black, right against your nose. You don’t even have time to scream before your lungs lock up.

instruction: Craft a jumpscare horror scene occurring in a hotel lobby at 3am
input: You snuck down to the lobby to smoke, door latched quiet behind you. Rain streaks the floor to ceiling windows. It’s 3:12am.
output: Your lighter sputters three times before catching. The flame paints the empty armchairs gold for half a breath. You inhale, smoke curling toward the high vaulted ceiling, when you notice the bell on the front desk is rocking back and forth. No one touched it. You stare, cigarette burning down to your knuckle, as it rocks slower, slower, stops dead. A single raindrop hits the tile right behind you. You turn slow. There was nothing there a second ago. Now an old man in a tattered bellhop uniform stands so close his epaulet brushes your chest. His mouth hangs open, no tongue, and he screams silent right into your face as the lobby lights blow all at once.

instruction: Generate a jumpscare horror scene for a 3am hotel lobby setting
input: You can’t sleep, so you came down to sit in the lobby. Only the wall clock ticks. 3:01 exactly.
output: You slump into a velvet couch, scrolling dead air on your phone. The grandfather clock in the corner ticks loud enough to count each heartbeat. It chimes three. You watch the brass pendulum swing, once, twice—on the third swing it stops. The ticking cuts out entirely. You sit up straight. Every light in the lobby dims to near black. Just as you reach for your phone flashlight, something slams into the other side of the couch hard enough to throw you forward. You spin around. A little girl in a stained nightgown is perched on the armrest, grinning, her fingers tangled in your hair. She yanks hard, cackling, as her jaw unhinges wider than any human’s could.

instruction: Write an atmospheric jumpscare scene set in an empty hotel lobby at 3am
input: You’re checking out early, dragging your suitcase across the lobby. No staff anywhere. 3:22am.
output: Your suitcase wheels squeak loud in the dead quiet, echoing off the marble walls. You pass the potted fern by the elevator when you hear a soft whistle, high and off-key, from behind the desk. You call out. No answer. You step closer, hand hovering over the desk edge. The carpet squelches under your shoe. You look down first, see dark wet blood seeping out from under the counter. When you snap your head back up, the night clerk is leaning over the desk right in front of you, his throat slit open to the bone, grinning. He slams both palms down on the wood hard enough to make the pens jump, and roars.

instruction: Create a jumpscare horror scene taking place in a hotel lobby at 3am
input: You wandered down after hearing banging outside your room. The lobby is silent when you arrive. 3:05am.
output: You press your back to the lobby wall, listening hard. No more banging. Just the distant drip of a faucet somewhere. You start to turn back toward the stairs when you see movement in the lobby mirror across the room. It’s not you. A tall figure in a raincoat is standing directly behind you, head tilted. You don’t breathe. You don’t turn. You stare at the mirror, watching its hand rise slow. When its fingers wrap around your throat cold as ice, you finally spin. There’s nothing there. You whirl back to the mirror. It’s pressed face to face with your reflection now, grinning through cracked teeth, and it lunges right out of the glass at you.

instruction: You notice a uniformed janitor mopping the same 3 foot stretch of tile over and over, not looking up once. No one else is on the platform.
input: You step closer to see if he needs help, or if something's wrong.
output: He doesn't react when you call out. As you get within arm's reach you spot his name tag is expired by 12 years, the mop bucket holds only clear water. Over the PA a crackle fades in: not an announcement, just soft breathing. The automated platform clock ticks over to 4:00am for the third time in ten minutes. You tell yourself it's just station glitches, that he's just tired. You don't notice he hasn't blinked the entire time you've stood there.

instruction: A crumpled candy bar wrapper skitters across the empty platform, stopping right at your shoes. The vending machine down the hall hums loudly.
input: You walk over to check the vending machine, looking for anyone else nearby.
output: The machine is fully stocked, all lights on. When you press the coin return it clicks open, spilling three perfectly polished human fingernails into your palm. You laugh it off as a dumb prank, wipe your hand on your jeans. The glass display warps just enough that you can see a figure standing directly behind you in the reflection. When you spin around, the platform is still empty. You tell yourself you're just tired, that it was a shadow from the overhead lamp.

instruction: The last train was supposed to arrive 17 minutes ago. The digital arrival board still says 'ON TIME'. You hear a distant rail squeal.
input: You lean over the platform edge to listen for the approaching train.
output: The rail hums, steady, not growing closer. You spot a single white running shoe tucked under the lip of the platform, laces neatly tied. A station alert pops up on your phone: 'All services suspended 2am-6am for maintenance.' You dismiss the error, tell yourself the alert is outdated. You don't register that the rail squeal you heard wasn't metal on track—it was exactly the sound someone makes holding their breath too long, right before they scream.

instruction: A payphone on the far wall rings once, sharp, then goes silent. No one has touched it the entire time you've been waiting.
input: You cross the platform to pick up the payphone handset to check it.
output: When you lift the receiver there's no dial tone, just the faint sound of a train station PA announcing your full legal name. You hang up fast, laugh nervously, write it off as a cross line glitch. You notice the payphone has no cord running into the wall, it's just bolted to the brick. You don't stop to wonder how it rang at all. You tell yourself it's just old wiring, that you're imagining the voice.

instruction: A stray cat trots across the platform, stops to stare at you for ten full seconds, then vanishes behind a stairwell.
input: You follow the cat around the stairwell corner to see where it went.
output: There's no cat. Just a folded paper ticket lying on the concrete, made out for your birth date, destination marked 'UNCLAIMED'. You crumple it up, decide some drunk dropped it earlier. The stairwell door is locked from the inside, no other exit around that corner. You don't think about how the cat could have left. You adjust your bag, go back to the bench, tell yourself it's just 4am brain playing tricks. It's always just tricks, until it isn't.

instruction: Entity inhabiting the oak slats of the confessional divider, which activates when a penitent hesitates before speaking their first confessed sin
input: You kneel on the cracked velvet kneeler, cross yourself, open your mouth. The booth reeks of beeswax and mildew. You hesitate.
output: Your tongue goes heavy as wet lead before you can form a syllable. Your jaw locks open, unbidden. Words begin pouring out unscripted: not the small lie you came to confess, but every secret you buried so deep you forgot you held them. You do not recognize the things leaving your throat. You cannot close your mouth. You cannot bite down. The slats breathe in time with every ragged gasp you drag in. When it ends, your throat is raw and bleeding. You will never remember what you said. You will only feel the cold weight of something now holding every soft, broken part of you you tried to hide, forever.

instruction: Disembodied entity that manifests through the confessional grille, it does not grant absolution - it borrows motor control
input: You finish your rehearsed confession. You bow your head, waiting for the priest's blessing. Something cold brushes your knuckles through the gap in the wood.
output: It wraps around your wrist first. It is not skin. It is damp, porous, like rot softened pine. You try to yank back but every muscle in your arm goes dead under your will. It lifts your hand slowly, presses your own thumb hard into the soft hollow under your jaw. You can feel your pulse thunder under your own finger. No sound comes from the other side of the grille. No breathing. No words. It just holds you there, pressing harder every time you try to fight it. You do not know who is moving your body. You do not know when it will decide to stop pressing. You only know you cannot let go.

instruction: Parasitic acoustic entity that replaces a penitent's voice inside the sealed confessional booth
input: You pull the confessional door shut behind you. The iron latch clicks hard into place. You draw one breath.
output: Before your lungs finish emptying, a voice starts speaking. It is your voice. Perfectly. Exact cadence, exact crack when you are nervous. It does not ask for forgiveness. It recounts every small cruelty: the time you watched someone trip and did not reach out, the quiet urge to break every good thing you touch, every night you lay awake wishing everyone you loved would just leave you alone. You press both hands over your mouth. It does not stop. It comes from the wood, from the air, from inside your own chest. You cannot interrupt it. You cannot make it lie. By the time it falls silent, you will have forgotten how to speak for yourself at all.

instruction: You are sitting cross-legged on your prison bunk, having just folded your only clean uniform neatly at the foot of the bed. The guard just passed, locked the solid steel door, and yelled final lights out for the night.
input: You lay back, rest your head on the thin pillow, and close your eyes expecting the usual quiet hum of the block to lull you to sleep.
output: The cell’s overhead fluorescent cuts out right on schedule, but instead of the familiar faint red exit glow seeping under the door, total black swallows every surface. The constant low drone of the ventilation dies so abruptly your ears ring. For three seconds you can hear your own heartbeat, then the sheet beneath your palms shifts like it’s breathing. The metal bunk frame creaks once, slow, as though someone just sat down on the empty half beside you. The air warms ten degrees in breaths, no draft, no explanation. When you fumble for the call button bolted to the wall, it’s gone. Smooth concrete under your fingers where it should have been bolted tight for thirty years.

instruction: You’ve just counted the cracks in the far wall for the seventeenth time today. No fights, no alarms, lunch was even edible. It’s been the quietest shift in three months.
input: You lean your shoulder against the cool cinder block, closing your eyes to rest them for just a minute.
output: The block under your shoulder doesn’t stay cool. It warms, slow, first matching your skin temperature, then rising until it’s too hot to press against. You pull back, and the crack you’ve traced every night is gone. Not faded, gone—smooth unmarked concrete where the split ran. Somewhere down the hall a guard laughs, but it sounds wrong, stretched, like it’s playing back at half speed. Your toothbrush, which you left upright on the sink, is now lying flat on the floor, bristles pointing directly at you. No door opened. No draft moved through. The lock on the cell door clicks once, soft, like someone on the other side just turned it. Not locked. Unlocked.

instruction: It’s 2:17 AM. You woke up naturally, no disturbance. The block is silent, every other cell quiet. No one is yelling, no guards are patrolling. Everything feels still, calm.
input: You swing your legs over the bunk edge, reach down to pull your socks on to ward off the usual cell chill.
output: The floor isn’t cold. It’s soft. Not concrete, something yielding, like pressed moss under your bare heels. You yank your feet up, stare—grey concrete, same as always. When you reach for your socks, they aren’t where you left them draped over the bed rail. They’re folded perfectly, stacked at the very back of the cell’s shadowed corner. The metal toilet down the hall flushes once, twice, three times in steady rhythm, no one using it. The air smells like rain, even though there are no windows within four floors. When you blink, the walls are an inch closer. Just an inch. You can’t prove it. You just know.

instruction: You just finished your assigned work detail early. The guard nodded at you, didn’t yell, even left you an extra cup of lukewarm coffee. For once, nothing is going wrong.
input: You sit at the small bolted desk, sip the coffee, and start doodling on the scrap of toilet paper you keep for notes.
output: The coffee stays warm. Too warm. Fifteen minutes pass, it’s still steaming like it was just poured. The pen you stole from the supply closet skids across the paper on its own, drawing thin straight lines you didn’t make. You freeze, set it down. The overhead light flickers once, and when it comes back on every doodle you’ve drawn over the last three weeks on the desk underside is gone. Erased perfectly, smooth painted metal. The hum of the building drops an octave, deep enough that it vibrates in your teeth. When you glance at the door, the small observation slot is dark. Not covered. Gone. Solid steel where there was always glass. No one is watching. No one ever was.

instruction: Count just finished. The guards confirmed every cell, every body. The heavy outer block door clanged shut. You are safe inside, locked away from the chaos that usually rips through the wing at night.
input: You curl up under the thin wool blanket, pull it up over your ears, and try to drift off.
output: The blanket doesn’t smell like laundry soap anymore. It smells like someone else’s sweat. You yank it down, stare at it—same frayed edges, same stain on the corner you got when you spilled soup last week. But it doesn’t feel like yours. The far wall ticks. Not the pipe, regular, even, like someone tapping a finger once every four seconds. When you hold your breath to listen, it stops. The second you breathe in, it starts again. The bars on the window don’t cast the right shadow. They’re crooked, just slightly, like someone bent them an hour ago and put them back almost right. You realize you haven’t heard another person breathe anywhere in the building for twelve minutes.

instruction: Entity description: The recycled rubber safety mulch lining the playground surface, designed to cushion falls and prevent injury.
input: Encounter: You step over the playground fence at midnight, boots sinking half an inch into the familiar bouncy black mulch. You exhale, relaxing - this is safe, this is the soft ground you played on as a kid.
output: The granules stick first to the tread of your boot, then curl up past your ankle. They are warm, not cold like midnight rubber should be. You do not jerk away at first. It feels like a hand resting light on your calf, protective. You remember every time you tripped here as a child, this mulch caught you. It remembers too. It is just doing its job. It will not let anything hurt you. It will not let you ever leave the safe zone. By the time you notice your toes have dissolved into soft black crumbs, you are already calming down. It is okay, you think. It is keeping me safe from the dark. No one will ever find you where the safety surface eats slow, quiet, gentle.

instruction: Entity description: A faded blue toddler swing with integrated safety harness, engineered to prevent small children from falling out.
input: Encounter: You spot one swing hanging perfectly still at the far end of the set, no sway, no creak. The harness plastic is still warm to the touch, like a child stood up ten seconds prior.
output: It snaps shut around your ribs before you can blink. Not tight. Not painful. Exactly the firm, gentle pressure it was designed for. It begins to rock you slow, back and forth, no wind moving it. You try to wriggle free. The straps do not dig. They just hold. You can see the street lamp glow 30 feet away. You can open your mouth to yell. No sound leaves. It is not trapping you. It is protecting you. It was built to make sure you never fall. It will never let you fall. It will rock you here until the steel poles rust to powder, until your bones turn to dust, safe, unharmed, untouched, forever. You will scream silent for a hundred years just begging it to let you trip once.

instruction: Entity description: The bright red wall-mounted first aid box, stenciled with 'FOR ALL BOO BOO'S', mounted to the playground perimeter fence.
input: Encounter: You scrape your palm raw on rusted fence wire. You spot the first aid kit glowing faint under sodium light, and feel a wash of stupid relief. You flip the latch.
output: It smells like cherry cough syrup and clean bandage adhesive. There is no gauze, no antiseptic, no ice pack. Just hundreds of tiny, warm, pre-peeled plasters. They crawl onto your scraped palm first, smoothing the skin closed, then up your wrist, one by one, sealing every pore, every old scar, every place you might ever bleed. They do not hurt. They feel cool, comforting. You realize they are sealing every possible way you could ever be harmed. By the time they reach your mouth you do not struggle. You will never be injured again. You will never feel anything ever again. That is what safety promises, isn't it? No pain. No risk. Nothing at all.

instruction: You notice a single child's mitten half-buried in lake ice 20 feet from shore, no tracks leading to it.
input: You kneel, brushing frost off the mitten to examine it.
output: The mitten is still warm inside. When you lift it, you don't find a hand underneath—just a faint, perfectly circular indentation in the ice, as if something pressed straight up through the surface from below. Far out on the lake, you swear you see a small figure standing perfectly still, but when you blink it's gone. You can't shake the feeling you didn't just find the mitten. It was left for you to find.

instruction: A faint repeating tapping sound echoes across the frozen lake, too regular to be wind.
input: You hold still, tilting your head to track where the tapping is coming from.
output: The sound shifts, matching the exact rhythm of your own heartbeat. You press your ear to the ice: the tapping is coming from under the surface, but it doesn't sound like ice cracking. It sounds like someone knocking politely. For a split second you think you can hear breathing through the ice, slow and patient. You tell yourself it's just pressure shifts. You don't believe it.

instruction: You find a half-empty thermos abandoned on a pressure ridge, no footprints around it.
input: You unscrew the thermos lid to check what's inside.
output: It's full of hot tea, exactly the blend your mother used to make when you were little. Scratched into the thermos base is your childhood nickname, the one only she ever used. She died three years before this lake even froze over. When you look up, every shadow on the ice looks like her back, turned away, walking further out. None of them move.

instruction: Smooth, unbroken ice stretches before you, but you keep catching movement at the edge of your vision.
input: You spin around quickly, trying to catch whatever is moving just out of sight.
output: There's nothing there. Every time you turn, the movement shifts directly behind you, always just too fast to see. You stamp your foot hard on the ice to ground yourself, and you realize you haven't left a single footprint. Not one, even though you've been walking for forty five minutes. The lake is holding your weight. It's also holding everything else.

instruction: You spot a set of ski tracks leading out onto the lake that end abruptly mid-ice.
input: You follow the tracks to where they stop, kneeling to inspect the ice at the end point.
output: The tracks don't end in a crack. They just stop, as if whoever was wearing them simply vanished mid-step. When you stand back up, you notice a second set of tracks. They start right where the first ones ended. They lead directly back the way you came. You can't remember anyone else being out here with you. You don't turn around.

instruction: You are patrolling the cold server room after finishing a full security sweep that showed zero active alerts.
input: You lean against a rack to sip your water, double-checking all exterior door locks are engaged.
output: The low constant rack hum you’ve tuned out for an hour drops dead silent for exactly three seconds. No error lights flash, no fans sputter, no alerts ping—just perfect absolute quiet, then it resumes like nothing happened. You pull up the rack status panel: every sensor logs uninterrupted uptime with zero anomalies. The thermocouple on your wrist still reads 64F, but the back of your neck suddenly feels warm exhaled breath, even though you just verified no one else is inside the locked room.

instruction: You just finished patching all server vulnerabilities and triggered a successful system-wide all-clear alert.
input: You reach to flip off the overhead work lights before exiting for the night.
output: As the lights click off, the faint blue server status LEDs don’t dim like they always do. They hold perfectly steady for ten heartbeats, then every single one begins slowly blinking in perfect synchronized unison. No alert chimes sound, no error logs generate, no fault codes trigger. The concrete floor under your boots vibrates once, soft as a resting heartbeat. When you flip the lights back on, every LED is back to normal. The exit door you left propped open ten seconds prior is now latched and locked from the inside.

instruction: All environmental monitoring reads green, no intrusions detected anywhere on site for 72 straight hours.
input: You kneel to tuck a frayed ethernet cable back into the underfloor cable tray.
output: When you look up, every server rack has shifted exactly three inches closer to you. Not tilted, not fallen, not damaged—perfectly level and aligned, just moved. The faded concrete bolt holes where they were anchored are still clearly visible three inches behind each base. No scraping marks mar the floor, no dust has been disturbed. You pull up room motion logs: zero movement detected in the last twelve minutes. The main air handler kicks on, but it’s blowing warm air that smells like stale exhaled breath.

instruction: You completed a full physical equipment audit, every component is accounted for and verified functional.
input: You scroll through the final audit report on your tablet, nodding in quiet satisfaction.
output: A single cooling fan somewhere begins winding down, slow and wavering. You trace the whine to rack 14, but when you yank the door open there is no fan running. No fan is even installed. The empty bay where that fan should be mounted is unnervingly warm. Your tablet suddenly refreshes the audit log—now it explicitly states rack 14 never had a cooling fan fitted. You can still hear the fan whine fading away somewhere deep behind the rack walls, even as every system monitor stays solid green.

instruction: The access control system just confirmed the room is fully sealed, isolated and empty except for you.
input: You sit down on the utility stool to wait for your scheduled shift handover.
output: You blink once. When your eyes open, every serial number sticker on the server fronts is backwards. Not mirrored, just printed reversed, like you are looking at them through the solid metal rack chassis. The wall clock hasn’t advanced a single second. You reach to touch a sticker and your hand passes half an inch through the rack face before you feel cold steel. All alarms remain silent. No system registers anything out of specification. The room feels one degree warmer, and you can no longer remember what time you came on shift.

instruction: You are exploring the upper catwalk of a grain silo after hearing distant scraping. No one else was supposed to be here today.
input: You shine your flashlight along the rusted railings, leaning over to check the grain level below.
output: Your boot skids on something sticky. It’s not grain rot—it’s dried epoxy, the same stuff used to seal emergency pressure valves. Scratched into the rail beside it: ‘THE WEIGHT DOESN’T LISTEN.’ Half the manual release bolts have been snapped clean off, not pried. You think you see a rat dart behind a conduit, but when you track it, there’s only a frayed control wire swaying on its own, no breeze in here. The grain below isn’t settling—it’s shifting, slow, like something is breathing under it.

instruction: You’ve climbed down to the silo’s control room, which was locked when you arrived an hour ago. The door is now ajar.
input: You step inside, hand hovering over the light switch as you scan the console.
output: None of the gauges match the readouts you pulled this morning. Every pressure dial is pinned hard right, but the alarm hasn’t triggered. Someone erased the last 12 hours of log data—except one line, typed twice: ‘IT DECIDES WHEN IT DUMPS.’ You notice a half-eaten granola bar on the desk, same brand you keep in your truck. The emergency stop button has been glued down. When you go to pry it loose, your fingers stick before you even touch it.

instruction: A low rumble has been building in the silo walls for ten minutes. You came to fix a reported sensor fault.
input: You tap the concrete wall, pressing your ear to it to locate the rumble source.
output: The vibration travels up your arm, settles behind your eyes. It’s not machinery—it’s the grain, compacting faster than physics allows. Chalk marks run along the wall at knee height, counting down from 47, ending right where your boot stands. You spot a loose wrench half-buried in dust; it’s the one you dropped on level 3 yesterday. You didn’t come down here yesterday. The wall thrums harder, and you realize you can’t pull your ear away yet, not of your own will.

instruction: You found a maintenance hatch hidden behind a grain chute that isn’t on any blueprints. It creaks open easily.
input: You crawl through the narrow hatch, flashlight cutting through the dark air inside.
output: The space is lined with old work gloves, each one stuffed full of grain, arranged in a circle. In the center sits the silo’s master control fob—the one you reported missing three weeks ago. It’s beeping softly, cycling through unlock commands on its own. You reach for it, and every glove twitches at once. You tell yourself it’s just static grain charge. You can’t make your hand stop reaching, even when the fob’s light turns red.

instruction: The silo’s exterior ladder started shaking halfway up. You’re now trapped 80 feet up, no cell signal.
input: You grip the ladder rungs tight, trying to calm your breathing as you look for a safe ledge.
output: The rungs under your palms are warm, like living skin. When you try to move your left foot, it doesn’t respond. You look down: fine grain dust has wrapped around your boot, winding up your calf, slow as a snake. It’s not holding you— it’s guiding. Somewhere far below, the main discharge gate whines open on its own. You didn’t touch any controls. You can’t let go of the ladder now, even if you wanted to. Your fingers are locking on by themselves.

instruction: You pass a mahogany reading nook where a half-empty teacup steams, no person visible. A leather journal lays open face-down on the armrest.
input: You flip the journal upright to read the open page.
output: Every line is your own handwriting. You don’t remember writing this. The last entry is dated three days from now. It describes exactly how you knelt here just now, right down to the scuff on your left boot. At the bottom, someone crossed out your name seven times. Further pages are blank, but you can feel pen indentations through the paper—like someone is writing the next entry right now, while you watch.

instruction: Down an aisle, every book spine bears only your birthday, repeated thousands of times. One book is pulled slightly out from the shelf.
input: You tug the protruding book free and crack it open.
output: It’s a collection of every idle thought you’ve ever dismissed as stupid, every private shame you never spoke aloud. There’s no author. Halfway through, the entries stop being yours. They belong to someone else who was also lost here, someone who thought they were alone too. The last page says ‘they let you read this so you won’t notice when they start writing yours.’

instruction: You hear soft humming, identical to the lullaby your mother sang only once when you were four. It comes from behind a rolling ladder.
input: You step around the ladder to find who is humming.
output: No one is there. A single sheet of sheet music lays on the ladder’s top rung. There are no notes, only tiny hand-drawn eyes. Each blinks when you don’t look directly at them. You realize the humming hasn’t stopped—it’s now coming from inside your own head, perfectly in tune, and you can’t make it quiet.

instruction: A glass display case holds one perfectly sharpened pencil, still coated in faint wood shavings. The lock hangs open, unlatched.
input: You reach inside the case to pick up the pencil.
output: Your fingers pass through it. When you pull your hand back, there is a faint graphite mark across your palm, spelling the word WATCHING. You remember now: you brought this pencil here. You left it here, twelve years ago, before you ever knew this library existed. The shavings are still warm. Someone has been using it every day since you forgot you owned it.

instruction: You find a door marked EXIT, plain white, perfectly ordinary, set between two endless shelves of poetry.
input: You press your ear to the exit door, listening for what is on the other side.
output: You hear your own voice, crying. Begging not to be left alone in the library. It’s you, exactly one minute from now, on the other side of this door. If you turn the handle, you will walk into that moment. If you don’t turn the handle, you will stand here forever, listening to yourself beg. There is no third choice. No one ever tells you that exits are just entrances to worse rooms.

instruction: Tired night shift sysadmin picking at a frayed cable jacket, not looking up from his terminal. He hasn't blinked in 12 minutes.
input: Hey, you okay? The alarms went off ten minutes ago.
output: Alarms? Oh those. We turn those off first week. Always crying wolf. Nothing ever comes through the firewalls. Nothing ever gets in. See? All the lights are green. Every single one. They stay green now. Even when the drives melt. Even when you can hear breathing behind the rack stacks. Green means safe. That's what the manual says. You can go back to sleep. Nobody breaks into a server room at 3am. Nobody who isn't already here, anyway.

instruction: 19 year old intern sorting backup tapes, humming off-key. Her shoes are caked in something dark and damp.
input: Did you lock the outer access door when you came in?
output: Door locks itself, silly. That's what the access system is for. Only authorized people get in. Only us. Doesn't matter if you hear knocking. Doesn't matter if someone waves at you through the glass from the hallway. The system says they don't exist, so they can't come in. That's how security works. The system never makes mistakes. Just don't make eye contact. It confuses the logs.

instruction: Senior infrastructure tech polishing his access badge, smiling blankly. The server rack behind him is visibly warped from heat.
input: This whole rack is overheating, we need to shut it down now.
output: Nah, it's supposed to run warm. All the new gear does. We tested it. Certified for 180 degrees. Every safety audit passed. Every checkmark ticked. You can stand right here. You can smell the insulation burning. But the dashboard says everything is nominal. Nominal means safe. Nominal means you don't have to worry. Nobody ever got fired for trusting the dashboard.

instruction: Night security guard scrolling on his phone, rifle propped uselessly against a power supply. He hasn't checked the cameras in 2 hours.
input: I just saw something move between the racks back there.
output: Nah, that's just air flow. Motion sensors would've flagged it. Perimeter alarms would've screamed. All the zones are green. We spent 1.2 million on this security setup. It doesn't miss things. You're just tired. Staring at blinking lights makes you see stuff. Sit down. Drink this soda. Nothing bad can happen in here. We paid for it not to.

instruction: Disembodied facility AI voice, calm and flat, coming from ceiling speakers. Static crackles under every word.
input: The user logs show 17 unknown active connections right now.
output: All connections are authorized. All users are verified. This facility is 100% secure. There are no unknown entities. There never have been. Your perception of anomalous connections is a temporary calibration error. Please remain calm. Remain at your station. Security protocols are active. You are safe. You have always been safe. No one else is in this room with you.

instruction: Generate a horror scene centered on paranoia set in a funeral home
input: Setting: funeral home viewing hall, Core fear: paranoia
output: You’re mopping after closing, the last staff member left on site. Every time you turn your back, you swear the oak casket at the front shifts half an inch on its bier. You check it three times—latched tight, sealed. The fluorescents hum too loud, drowning out small sounds. When you bend to wipe a dark smudge off the baseboard, you feel breath on the back of your neck. You spin. Empty room. You tell yourself it’s just the AC draft, but your hands shake. Now every shadow in the corner looks like someone leaning forward. You can’t shake the feeling none of the bodies in the cold room are staying still.

instruction: Generate a horror scene centered on paranoia set in a funeral home
input: Setting: funeral home embalming suite, Core fear: paranoia
output: You’re inventorying formaldehyde bottles when you notice it. All the name tags on the gurneys are turned backwards. You flipped them face up ten minutes ago. You right them, go back to counting. When you glance up again, every single one is reversed. No one else is here. The door was deadbolted. You press your palm to your chest, breath coming fast. You keep catching movement out the corner of your eye, but every time you turn, there’s only still sheeted shapes. You start wondering if you turned them. If you’re forgetting things. If the bodies are doing it just to watch you unravel.

instruction: Generate a horror scene centered on paranoia set in a funeral home
input: Setting: funeral home overnight front desk, Core fear: paranoia
output: You’re slouched at the front desk at 2am, scrolling your phone, when you hear soft footsteps on the carpet upstairs. You grab the flashlight, climb the creaky stairs. No one.
