You check every viewing room, every supply closet. Empty. You sit back down. Ten minutes later, the footsteps again. This time they stop right above your head. You freeze. You don’t go back up. You just sit, staring at the ceiling, knowing if you go look you won’t find anything. And that’s worse. That whatever it is is just playing with you, letting you hear it just enough to make you stop breathing every time the floor creaks.

instruction: Generate a horror scene centered on paranoia set in a funeral home
input: Setting: funeral home visitation preparation, Core fear: paranoia
output: You’re arranging lilies by the open casket for tomorrow’s service. Every time you step back to adjust the arrangement, the deceased’s hands look like they shifted a fraction. You lean in. They’re folded exactly how you left them. Rigor mortis set in hours ago. You tell yourself it’s just bad overhead lighting. But now you can’t stop staring at their fingers. You swear you see a twitch. You step back so fast you knock over a vase. You don’t clean it up. You just stare, waiting, because part of you is terrified you’ll blink and their eyes will be open. And the worse part? A quieter part is terrified they won’t.

instruction: Generate a horror scene centered on paranoia set in a funeral home
input: Setting: funeral home cold storage vault, Core fear: paranoia
output: You’re pulling a body for embalming when the vault door clicks shut behind you. You pound on it, yell, then fumble for your radio. Dead battery. The overhead lights hum flat and cold. You count the drawers to pass time. 17. You counted 16 when you walked in. You count again. 17. Your throat goes tight. You back against the door. Every drawer suddenly feels like it’s breathing. You don’t know if there’s an extra body. You don’t know if you miscounted. You don’t know which is worse. All you know is you can’t stop counting, over and over, while frost creeps up your coat cuffs.

instruction: Hunched elderly priest behind the confessional grille, hands folded, face half in shadow. He has not blinked once since you sat down.
input: Father, I came to confess the lie I told last week.
output: I know. I watched you tell it. I was standing behind the grocery store awning. You didn't see me. No one ever sees me. You think this box is for your sins? It's for remembering all the ones you forgot you committed. Last Tuesday you stepped over that homeless man without looking. You hummed while you did it. You don't remember that, do you? You will. Every time you close your eyes from now on, you will feel his cold hand brush your ankle as you step over him. Forever.

instruction: Soft female voice from behind the grille, no movement visible, no breath sounds can be detected.
input: I'm here, I don't even know what I need to confess.
output: You don't need to confess anything. You need to listen. They told you this is where you bring the dark parts of yourself. No. This is where we leave parts of ourselves with you. You will leave this booth, and you will never feel alone again. Not when you shower. Not when you tuck your child in. Not when you wake at 3:17am. There will always be someone right behind your shoulder, breathing just quiet enough that you can never be quite sure you hear them.

instruction: Priest whose voice matches your dead older brother exactly. You can just make out his familiar jawline through the dark grille.
input: Father... your voice sounds just like someone I lost.
output: I know. That's why you pulled into this parking lot, isn't it? You've been hunting for him everywhere. You thought if you prayed hard enough he'd come back. He never left. He's been watching you eat his favourite cereal every Saturday morning. He hates that you still don't add enough sugar. He hates that you got to grow old. He's going to start reminding you every single day exactly what you did the night he didn't come home.

instruction: Silent figure behind the grille, only the slow scratch of pen on paper audible the entire time you speak.
input: I've told you all my sins. Is that all?
output: That's all you remember. I have the rest. The time you wished your mother would just die when she was sick. The time you laughed when that kid fell off his bike and broke his arm. The little thoughts you thought no one ever heard. Every single one. I'm going to mail them. One a week. To your spouse. To your boss. To every person you have ever pretended to be good for. They will know. All of them.

instruction: You stare through the grille and see your own exact face staring back, blank and unblinking. This is not a mirror.
input: What is this? Why do you look like me?
output: I'm the you that stayed. Every time you walked away from something terrible, every time you lied to yourself that you were better, I stayed here. I watched every choice. I know every excuse. You can leave this church. You can drive home. You can go back to your quiet little life. But every time you look in a mirror for the rest of your life, you will see me. And I will never, ever blink.

instruction: Hunched 72 year old taxidermist Eli, missing his left index finger, works with only a single guttering tallow lantern. He never stands directly in light, always half-obscured by mounted stag heads.
input: It's really dark in here, can we turn on another lamp?
output: Nah. See, the dark don't rot the pelts like bright light does. More than that? The things still inside the skins? They hate being seen. When the lights are up, they scratch at the stitching from inside. I hear em. Scritch scritch, all night long, trying to claw back out into the light. Dark lets em rest quiet. Don't you feel how still everything is right now? You turn that extra lamp on, and every dead thing in this shop will turn its head to look at you. I promise you that.

instruction: Teenage apprentice Lila, who never blinks for longer than three seconds, sits polishing a fox skull in the far shadowed corner. Her hands are smudged with formaldehyde.
input: You don't ever get scared working here after dark?
output: Scared? No. The dark is the only honest part of this place. When the sun's up, you only see the glass eyes, the painted fur. When it goes dark? You hear them breathing. The badger that got caught in a leg trap, the owl that hit the power line. They don't whisper in the light. They wait. They only tell you what they saw when it's black enough that you can't run. You should stay. It'll be fully dark in seven minutes. You'll hear them too.

instruction: Mute-seeming taxidermist Mara, who only speaks when no one is looking directly at her, stands beside a wall of mounted bird specimens.
input: Hello? Is anyone back here? I can barely see anything.
output: Don't move your feet. Don't turn on your phone light. The dark is holding them back right now. Every crow on this wall died staring at something humans aren't supposed to see. If light hits their eyes again? They'll finish what they started. They'll look right through you. Stay still. Breathe quiet. The dark is kind. It doesn't make you watch the things that are watching you. Just wait. It will pass. Or it won't. Either way, don't turn on the light.

instruction: Former mortician turned taxidermist Roy, who wears a faded funeral director suit, leans on a half-finished bear mount.
input: This shop feels wrong when it's this dark. What are you hiding back here?
output: Hiding? I'm protecting you. You think I keep these curtains drawn for fun? When the dark wraps around this shop, it wraps around all the bits that didn't stay dead. The paws that still twitch, the jaws that still want to snap. The light pulls that hunger out. Makes it sharp. You think you're scared of the dark? Wait till you see what the dark is keeping pressed up against the walls, so it can't touch you. Go on. Flip the switch. I'll be right behind you.

instruction: Elderly shop owner Mrs Hale, whose shadow never matches the way she's standing, dusts a glass case of small rodent mounts.
input: I don't like the dark. Can you at least open the front curtain?
output: Sweetheart, the dark isn't what you should be afraid of. All these little creatures? They died screaming. Every single one. The dark muffles the screams. It swallows the way their little claws still scrabble at the inside of the glass after closing. You pull that curtain back, let the sun in? You won't just hear them. You'll feel every single one of them crawling up your sleeves before you make it to the door. Leave the dark be. It's the only friend you've got in here.

instruction: Entity is a mute, rain-sodden switchboard operator ghost that occupies the phone booth receiver, never visible, only felt as cold breath through the earpiece.
input: You duck into the rain-soaked phone booth at 2:17AM, fumble for change, lift the receiver to call your roommate. The line doesn't hum.
output: The plastic sticks to your cold palm. No dial tone, only the soft patter of rain on glass magnified like it's pressed right against your skull. You hear breathing, not yours, slow and steady, wet. It doesn't speak. You slam the receiver down. It lifts itself back up an inch. You bang on the glass—no one is on the street. Every light on the block went out while you were inside. You dial every number you know. All ring forever, no voicemail, no answer. You check your pockets. Your phone is gone. The rain doesn't stop. The breathing never speeds up, never slows. It's just there, waiting for you to stop yelling, to accept that no one is coming for you. The booth's lock clicked shut ten seconds after you closed the door. You didn't hear it.

instruction: Entity is the phone booth itself, which feeds on people seeking shelter by erasing all evidence they ever existed before they can leave.
input: You ran three blocks through torrential rain, tripped over a curb, and threw the phone booth door shut behind you, gasping.
output: Water runs in black streaks down the fogged glass. You wipe a spot with your sleeve. Outside, there are no footprints in the wet concrete behind you. No splashes, no track marks. You pat your jacket. Your wallet is gone. Your keys. The tattoo on your wrist is fading like ink running in rain. You scream your own name. It dies in the small space, no echo. You can't remember your mother's name. You can't remember why you were running. The phone on the hook rings once, sharp. When you reach for it, your fingers pass through the plastic. The rain outside doesn't make sound anymore. It's just static. There is no outside. There never was. You were never anywhere else. This booth is all there ever was.

instruction: Entity is a mimic that reproduces your exact voice, speaking only unacknowledged truths about how fundamentally alone you are.
input: The payphone rings while you're huddled under the booth overhang waiting for the rain to break. You answer it.
output: It's your voice. Not an echo, not a recording. It sounds like you, but calmer, like it's been waiting. 'No one noticed you left,' it says. You can hear rain on its end too, same cadence. 'Your friends went to dinner without texting you. Your landlord didn't even notice you missed rent last month. If you died in here, it would be three weeks before anyone smelled you. They'd just complain about the smell, not ask who was gone.' You hang up. It rings again immediately. You yank the cord out of the wall. It still rings, quiet, from inside the plastic casing. The rain is so loud now you can't hear anything else but your own voice, whispering through the metal, reminding you that you were already alone before you walked into this booth.

instruction: You are navigating the unlit road tunnel, 2km from the exit. Damp concrete drips onto your shoulder.
input: I shine my phone flashlight ahead to check for debris on the asphalt.
output: The beam catches glistening wet smudges streaked across the tunnel wall at eye level. Not paint, not oil. They pulse slow, like veins under stretched skin. A low wet gurgle hums through the concrete, and you feel the road under your boots give a tiny, soft exhale. The drips on your neck are warm now, thick. When you wipe it away your fingers come away sticky with something that twitches, tiny threadlike things writhing between your knuckles before dissolving into nothing. The tunnel walls are breathing in time with your lungs.

instruction: You are halfway through the sealed road tunnel, radio died ten minutes ago. You hear distant scraping far behind you.
input: I stop the car, roll down the window to listen closer.
output: Cold tunnel air washes in reeking of raw iron. The scraping stops. You lean out, and see the white lane divider lines ahead are no longer painted. They are raised, ridged, pulsing. When you squint you can see faint pores along each edge. A single coarse hair curls up from the asphalt right by your tire, twitching as if feeling for your wheel. The concrete overhead groans, and a drop of thick translucent fluid lands on your forearm. It burns cold, leaving a faint, breathing bruise that blinks at you once before fading.

instruction: You are walking through the abandoned road tunnel, broken guardrails line both sides. The air feels thick, viscous.
input: I kick a loose rock down the tunnel to test the echo.
output: The rock doesn't clatter. It lands with a soft wet squelch, like hitting raw meat. You freeze. The guardrails you thought were rusted steel are flexing. You step closer and see fine, cracked skin stretched over the metal frames, oozing clear sticky fluid where bolts once bit. When you breathe out, the tunnel breathes in perfect sync with you. Tiny, half formed fingernails are pushing up through the asphalt every few feet, pale and raw, twitching slightly as if trying to dig their way out of the road.

instruction: You are driving through the foggy road tunnel, headlights only illuminate ten feet ahead. No other cars have passed.
input: I flash the high beams once to try and cut through the haze.
output: For one blinding second you see it: the entire tunnel lining is not concrete. It is stretched, pallid human skin, crisscrossed with bulging purple veins that pulse in time with your engine. The overhead light fixtures are not glass- they are swollen, milky eyes that track your car as you move. A wet crack runs across the wall ahead, and you watch a raw, bleeding lip form along it, opening just enough to exhale a fog that smells like rotting breath over your windshield before the high beams die completely.

instruction: You have been walking this road tunnel for an hour, the exit sign never gets closer. Your boots stick slightly to the floor with every step.
input: I kneel down to scrape at the road surface with my keys.
output: The metal key sinks half an inch into the asphalt before you can stop, meeting warm, yielding flesh. When you yank it free, a thin line of dark blood wells up from the hole, running slow across the road. You hear a faint, pained whimper echo deep in the tunnel walls. The black tarmac around the cut wrinkles, flinching. You stand up fast, and notice every crack in the concrete walls is now leaking thin, red tears that run down to pool in the gutter, making soft sucking sounds as they seep back into the road.

instruction: Write a body horror scene set on a cruise ship at night
input: Setting: cruise ship after midnight, fear type: body horror
output: You lean over the midnight pool rail, salt stinging your cheeks. The cruise band cut out ten minutes ago; no one else is on deck. Something drifts up first—just a fingernail, painted the same coral as the girl you watched dive an hour ago. Then a wrist, bones dissolved soft, skin stretched thin enough you see the pool lights glow through it. Then faces, dozens, their features melted together like wax left in sun, mouths fused shut, eyes oozing clear gel that ripples the surface. They don’t scream. They just drift, bumping gently against the liner, their bodies turning translucent one patch at a time, until you realize the water was never blue. It was them, this whole time.

instruction: Write a body horror scene set on a cruise ship at night
input: Setting: cruise ship cabin 2am, fear type: body horror
output: You wake at 2:17am to the sound of your own teeth clicking. The cabin air smells like wet copper. You fumble for the light, and freeze. Your left hand rests on the sink edge—but it’s growing. Knuckles stretch, skin splitting pale pink, fingers curling backward at the joints, each nail thickening into something ridged and black. You don’t feel pain. You feel a hum, deep under your skin, as your shoulder bulges, shirt stitching popping. The mirror shows your jaw shifting, sharp teeth pushing through your lower lip. Outside, the ship horn blows, distant. No one will hear you. No one is coming.

instruction: Write a body horror scene set on a cruise ship at night
input: Setting: empty cruise dining hall after dinner, fear type: body horror
output: You slip back into the dining hall for the water bottle you left. Overhead lights flicker off one by one, leaving only the sickly green emergency exit glow. All the other passengers are still in their seats. They haven’t moved. Their forks hang halfway to their mouths, but their cheeks are caving in, skin adhering directly to jawbone. Eyes have turned to milky gel, dripping down their shirt collars. Every one of them is still breathing, slow shallow chest rises. When you step on a fallen glass, every head twitches in unison. No one blinks. Their fingers are starting to fuse to the silverware.

instruction: Write a body horror scene set on a cruise ship at night
input: Setting: cruise ship emergency stairwell, fear type: body horror
output: The emergency stairwell reeks of rot. You missed the last elevator, just trying to get to your deck. Halfway down, you step on something soft. It’s a passenger, collapsed on the steps. But they aren’t dead. Their body is spreading, flesh oozing over the metal treads, clothing dissolving into the viscous mass. One eye blinks up at you, still human, while their shoulder melts into the riser. Their mouth moves, but no sound comes out—you watch their tongue dissolve before your eyes. The mass is creeping, slow, toward your shoe. The ship creaks. No one uses this stairwell anymore.

instruction: Write a body horror scene set on a cruise ship at night
input: Setting: outer cruise deck midnight, fear type: body horror
output: You lean out over the black ocean, wind screaming through the rigging, when you feel it. A hand. Not on your shoulder. Inside your shoulder. You twist, and see pale wrinkled fingers pushing out through your shirt, nails caked with salt. They curl, tugging gently at your collarbone. Another pushes through your ribcage, then another, each one cold, each one moving on its own. You can feel them rooting, deep in your muscle, pulling your bones apart slow. Down in the water, dark shapes drift. They know. You were never just a passenger.

instruction: You pass a sealed patient room marked 'CONTACT PRECAUTIONS' where soft humming stopped 3 minutes ago. The plastic door seal is peeling at one corner.
input: You lean in to pry the peeling seal back just enough to look through the gap
output: Your flashlight catches nothing but crumpled gowns draped over the bed at first. You almost pull back, then notice the wall air vent above the bed has its grille unscrewed, hanging by one bolt. Scratches score the plastic directly beside your face on the door, too high for a sitting patient. The humming starts again, quiet, now from directly behind you. When you spin, the hall is empty. The seal you were prying snaps shut with a sharp crack before you can look back inside.

instruction: A nurse's call station beeps steadily, one unacknowledged alert. The chart binder on the counter is flipped open to a blank page.
input: You reach across the desk to silence the repeating beep
output: Your fingers brush the call button first, and it goes dead instantly. Under the binder you find a half-eaten protein bar, still slightly warm. A blood pressure cuff hangs off the desk edge, still inflated tight around nothing. You hear a soft gasp from under the desk, and when you yank the curtain aside there's only a fallen oxygen mask wheezing out the last of its tank pressure. The station beeps again, right next to your ear.

instruction: The main ward lights cut out, only your headlamp works. You hear slow, dragging boot scuffs down the far end of the corridor.
input: You hold your breath and shine your headlamp straight down the dark hallway
output: The beam catches a rolling IV stand drifting slowly on its own wheels, dragging its power cord across the linoleum. You exhale, relieved, until you notice bare feet standing perfectly still just behind the IV stand, just out of the light's edge. When you tilt the lamp up, there's nothing there. Something cold brushes the back of your neck before you can turn.

instruction: You find a disposable tablet propped on an isolation cart, still playing a security feed from 12 hours prior.
input: You lean in close to scrub the feed timeline back to see what happened
output: The feed shows the same empty ward, no movement for 7 minutes. You're about to close it when you realize the person in the feed is standing exactly where you are right now, leaning over the exact same cart. You jerk back, and the feed figure also jerks back at the exact same time. A single finger taps the tablet screen from the other side of the glass. You blink and the tablet has gone dead, cold to the touch.

instruction: A patient bathroom door is propped open an inch, water running steadily inside. Steam curls out under the frame.
input: You push the bathroom door fully open to check for anyone inside
output: The shower is running full hot, no one in the stall. The mirror is fogged over except for one clear handprint pressed right at eye level. You reach to wipe the fog, and your hand lines up perfectly with the print. Just as you realize this, the shower curtain snaps closed hard, the water cuts off abruptly, and every light in the bathroom dies at once. You can hear breathing right in front of you in the dark.

instruction: You pause at a sun-dappled clearing ringed with mossy stone, fireflies dancing soft above ferns. A half-buried wooden sign reads "SAFE CAMP 100M" with a faded smile painted in the corner.
input: You kneel to brush moss off the sign, checking for traps or rot.
output: The wood is solid, no rot, no tripwires. The fireflies drift close, warm on your knuckles—you almost relax, until you notice every single firefly is perfectly synchronized, blinking on exactly the same beat. The sign's smile wasn't painted. It was carved, deep, with something sharp. Far past the clearing, you hear three soft, even breaths, matching the blink rhythm. No animal breathes that evenly.

instruction: Dusk filters through pine boughs, and you spot a tattered wool blanket draped over a fallen log, a half-empty canteen set neatly beside it. No broken twigs mark the approach, no animal calls disturb the quiet here.
input: You step forward to lift the blanket edge, checking who left it.
output: The blanket smells of pine soap, clean, not mildewed. The canteen holds cool, fresh water—no poison, no rot. For half a second you think you’ve found a safe spot someone abandoned. Then you see the blanket has no frayed edges, no wear at all. It was laid down ten minutes ago, max. The pine needles under your boots are pressed flat, but you didn’t hear anyone leave. Not a single twig snapped.

instruction: A small stream cuts through the underbrush, water gurgling soft. Deer tracks dot the bank, and you spot a cluster of ripe blackberries hanging low over the water, untouched.
input: You crouch to test the water temperature, reaching for a berry.
output: The water runs clear and cold, good enough to drink. The berry bursts sweet on your tongue, no bitter aftertaste. This feels like the first gentle thing this forest has offered. Then you notice every deer track is identical, exact same paw print, repeated perfectly down the bank. None of them lead away from the stream. All of them stop right where you’re kneeling.

instruction: Rain starts spattering through the canopy, and you spot a hollow oak trunk, dry inside, just wide enough to huddle. A bird chirps calmly from a branch above it, no sign of alarm.
input: You duck inside the oak hollow, running your hand along the inner wood to check for spiders or rot.
output: The wood is dry, warm even. No spiders, no mold, no sharp splinters. You lean back, relief unfurling in your chest, until your palm brushes something carved into the back wall. It’s a tally mark. Then another. Then you count seventy two, all neat, all cut deep. The bird outside stops chirping all at once. It didn’t fly away. It just went quiet.

instruction: You hear a human whistle, off-key and familiar, just up the trail. A flashlight beam bobs slow through the trees, moving toward you, steady, unhurried.
input: You call out softly, stepping into the open so they can see you.
output: The whistle doesn’t break, doesn’t pause when you call. The flashlight beam stays level, never wavers toward your voice. It looks like a hiker, it sounds like someone who’s not lost, like safety coming to find you. Then you notice the flashlight is casting no shadow. Nothing is walking under that light. It’s just moving through the dark, all by itself, whistling your mother’s old lullaby.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the specified fear type, set in the given location.
input: Fear: false_security. Setting: corrupted garden.
output: You knelt to pluck the dewy rose, relief flooding you. The garden looked normal again—no twisted vines, no weeping black petals. You’d waited three days for the corruption to fade, brought tea, spoken soft apologies. The thorn pricks your thumb before you register it isn’t a thorn. It’s a tiny, cold human finger. The rose unfurls slow, showing not stamens but blinking human eyes. Every bloom around you rustles awake. You didn’t fix the garden. It just learned to stop showing you the broken parts until you stopped running.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the specified fear type, set in the given location.
input: Fear: false_security. Setting: corrupted garden.
output: You’d blocked the gate last night, piled bricks high enough nothing could climb through. This morning you step outside and the garden is quiet, grass neatly clipped, sun dappling apple boughs. No gnarled roots snaking across the path, no distant moaning. You breathe out, laugh shaky, kick a loose stone. It bounces once, then sinks straight into the lawn as if through water. The grass ripples. It isn’t grass. It’s just holding the shape of grass, breathing slow, waiting for you to walk far enough from the house.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the specified fear type, set in the given location.
input: Fear: false_security. Setting: corrupted garden.
output: The rain washed it all away, you tell yourself. The black slime, the rotting bird carcasses strung on thorns, the whispers that followed you indoors. This morning the garden smells like clover. You sit on the old wooden bench, lean back, eyes closing. It creaks. You didn’t move. You crack one eye open. The bench grain is shifting, forming fingerprints pressed deep into the wood. It hasn’t been a bench for weeks. It was just waiting for you to stop checking, to finally rest your weight on it.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the specified fear type, set in the given location.
input: Fear: false_security. Setting: corrupted garden.
output: You counted every bulb last autumn, every seed packet. When they sprouted straight and green, you cried. No twisted stalks, no bleeding sap, no leaves that twitched when you turned your back. You kneel to pull a stray dandelion, fingers closing around the stem. It doesn’t pull up. It pulls you. You look closer: every leaf is turned exactly toward you, every bud tracking your movement. They didn’t grow right. They grew exactly how you wanted them to look, so you would stop burning the beds.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the specified fear type, set in the given location.
input: Fear: false_security. Setting: corrupted garden.
output: The neighbour complimented the roses an hour ago. You’d nearly cried; finally it was safe. You walk the border now, trailing your fingers over soft petals. One sticks to your knuckle. You yank back. It left a perfect wet mouth-print on your skin. You freeze. Every flower is turned just slightly off, just enough that you couldn’t see their faces from the kitchen window. They didn’t heal. They learned to perform normalcy, just well enough to fool anyone watching from inside.

instruction: You are exploring the damp stone cathedral crypt, holding a guttering tallow candle. Describe the environmental event that occurs.
input: I pause to brush cobwebs from my coat collar, leaning against a carved sarcophagus edge.
output: The candle flame does not flicker with the draft, it *breathes*. For three slow heartbeats it inhales, shrinking to a blue pinprick that casts no shadow, then exhales gold. You realise every stone carving along the sarcophagus lid has turned their carved faces directly toward you. They were not facing you ten seconds prior. The air smells not of damp stone now, but your mother’s old lavender soap, the kind she stopped making when you were seven. No wind moves. No sound breathes but you. The carvings’ eyes have deepened, as if someone has dug them out with a nail from the inside.

instruction: You are navigating the shadowed passageways of the 12th century cathedral crypt, your boots squelching on ancient moss. Describe the environmental event that occurs.
input: I shine my phone torch downward to check what I stepped on.
output: The torch beam warps. It does not hit the floor, it stretches, as if falling down a hole that is not there. You hear your own breathing, but three seconds delayed, echoing from the dark end of the passage. When you yank the torch up, every recessed tomb niche that was empty a moment ago now holds a standing figure. They do not move. They are all wearing the exact jacket you are wearing right now. The temperature drops so sharply your lungs burn when you inhale. You cannot hear your heartbeat anymore. Only the delayed echo of your breath, getting closer, one beat at a time.

instruction: You are standing before the sealed ossuary door deep in the cathedral crypt, dust coating every surface. Describe the environmental event that occurs.
input: I press my palm flat to the cold oak door, listening for any sound beyond.
output: The door does not creak. It *remembers* you. You feel the grain under your palm shift, moulding exactly to the lines of your hand, as if it has held this palm a thousand times before. Somewhere far above, the cathedral bell tolls once. But it tolls backwards, the note unravelling instead of ringing. All the dust in the crypt hangs suspended mid air, not falling, not moving. You realise you have not blinked in almost a minute. You try to pull your hand away. It does not move. The door is not holding you. You just do not want to let go.

instruction: You are kneeling beside a broken stone altar in the lowest level of the cathedral crypt, water dripping steadily somewhere in the dark. Describe the environmental event that occurs.
input: I count the drips to steady my nerves, tapping my finger in time with each fall.
output: On the seventeenth drip, you tap first. The drip waits for your finger. Then the next one. Then all of them. You stop tapping. The drips stop entirely. The silence presses so hard your ears ring. When you look up, the arch of the ceiling is no longer stone. It is the ceiling of your childhood bedroom. Not similar. Exactly it: the crack above the bed, the faint smudge of crayon you drew when you were four. It breathes. One slow expansion, one slow contraction, just like a chest. You can hear the hum of the old fridge your parents used to own, coming from nowhere.

instruction: You are retracing your steps back toward the crypt exit, passing rows of weathered grave markers. Describe the environmental event that occurs.
input: I glance over my shoulder to make sure nothing is following me.
output: When you turn back forward, the exit is gone. Not blocked, not dark: it simply does not exist. The passage continues, identical, same chipped stone, same moss, same scratch you carved into the wall with your key two minutes prior. You run to the scratch. It is there. You turn around. The way you came is now also the same endless passage. Every grave marker now bears your full name, your exact date of birth. No death date. Just blank space after the dash. The air tastes like copper. You do not remember when you started crying.

instruction: Write a horror scene using body horror set in a shipwreck
input: Setting: sunken shipwreck. Fear type: body horror. Second person narration, atmospheric, 100-250 words.
output: You pry open the rusted galley hatch, salt stinging your raw knuckles. The first thing you smell is not rot, but warm bread. On the metal table, a baker’s arms still rest elbow-deep in dough. They didn’t sever clean. The skin of the forearms has fused with the fermented wheat, veins branching like mold through the dough. Each finger has stretched thin, curled into yeast-riddled hooks. When your flashlight wavers, one twitches. It doesn’t reach for you. It kneads. Slow, even, the same motion the baker must have repeated ten thousand times before the hull split. You back away, and your boot squelches. You look down: dough has oozed through the floor grates, wrapped around your ankle, already growing fine dark hairs along its edge.

instruction: Write a horror scene using body horror set in a shipwreck
input: Setting: sunken shipwreck. Fear type: body horror. Second person narration, atmospheric, 100-250 words.
output: You descend into the engine room, water up to your ribs. The pistons don’t clang anymore. They breathe. Each crank arm is wrapped in what was once the chief engineer. His ribcage has pried itself open, grown around the drive shaft, bone fusing to iron like weld. His lungs are stretched over the piston heads, inflating and deflating slow with the current, pushing bubbles up through what’s left of his throat. His eyes stare past you, milky but alive. When you brush the shaft by accident, he gasps. Bubbles burst against your cheek. You taste iron. He doesn’t beg for rescue. He begs you to oil the valves. They’ve been sticking, he whispers through rotting lips, they’ve been sticking for seventy three years.

instruction: Write a horror scene using body horror set in a shipwreck
input: Setting: sunken shipwreck. Fear type: body horror. Second person narration, atmospheric, 100-250 words.
output: You find the crew bunks stacked like coffins. No bodies are laid out. Instead, every wool blanket is stretched tight, bulging. You yank one back. The sailor didn’t die when the ship went down. He merged. His spine has grown along the wooden bunk frame, ribs spreading to bolt him to the planks. His skin has thinned to the texture of the blanket itself, fuzzy, faded, pilled at the elbows. His mouth is a slit in the fabric, breathing slow salt mist. All of them are like this. One reaches for you, not with a hand, but with a frayed edge of his own skin. It wraps around your wrist. It is warm. It wants you to lay down. It wants you to stay. It promises you will never be cold again, out here in the dark.

instruction: Write a horror scene using body horror set in a shipwreck
input: Setting: sunken shipwreck. Fear type: body horror. Second person narration, atmospheric, 100-250 words.
output: The captain’s wheel does not turn easily. When you grip it, you realize why. Fingers have grown between every spoke, nail beds fused to the brass, knuckles swollen into the grain of the oak. The captain’s head lolls against the pedestal, his neck stretched so thin you can see the salt water pumping through his veins like blood. He hasn’t blinked in a century. When you try to let go of the wheel, you can’t. Your palm is sticking. Tiny hairs are already rooting out of your skin, tangling with his. He turns his eyes to you. They are not angry. They are relieved. Finally, he mouths, finally someone can hold the course. You feel your knuckles start to swell. You feel the wood hum under your skin.

instruction: Write a horror scene using body horror set in a shipwreck
input: Setting: sunken shipwreck. Fear type: body horror. Second person narration, atmospheric, 100-250 words.
output: You follow the crack where the hull split open. It is not rust that seals the edges now. It is skin. Dozens of passengers crushed against the metal when the ship went down, their bodies melted together into one continuous membrane stretched across the breach. It breathes. It ripples when the current shifts. You see eyes scattered across it, half lidded, watching. Mouths, too, all pressed tight shut. When you lean close, one pulls open just enough to whisper. They don’t scream about drowning. They warn you not to touch the water. It gets in, it says, it gets in and it likes to stitch things back together wrong. You look down at your own hands. The cuts you got climbing down already have fine silver threads growing across them, closing the wounds too smooth, too tight.

instruction: Elderly female long-term ward patient, rocking slowly in a chair beside an empty bed, wrists faded with old restraint bruising, staring unblinking at the wall. She has not spoken to staff in 11 days.
input: Ma'am? The nurses said you were calling out. Can I help you with anything?
output: They don't hear them. Nobody hears the ones that walk between the beds at three a.m. They don't take bodies. They take the part that knows it's you. Yesterday they took Mrs Henderson's name. She just sits there now, doesn't remember she ever had one. Last night they stood by your cot. You were sleeping. They wrote something down on your chart. Not with a pen. You'll feel it tomorrow. You'll wake up and you won't remember what your own laugh sounds like. Don't look at them when they come. Don't blink. They count blinks.

instruction: Unbadged night shift orderly leaning against a locked medication cart, eyes bloodshot, uniform caked with faint dried disinfectant. He has not blinked once since you approached.
input: Hey, can I get a glass of water? My throat won't stop feeling dry.
output: Sure. Just don't swallow too fast. All the patients here, they all say their throats are dry. You know why? Because while you're asleep, something sits on your chest and breathes into your mouth. It's not air. It's little pieces of itself. That dry feeling? That's it rooting. Nobody believes me. I watched it do it to twelve people this week. It's got your name next. I can see the mark on your collarbone. Don't rub it. It likes when you notice it's there.

instruction: 17 year old car crash patient in a cervical collar, lying flat on their back, gaze fixed unwaveringly on the ceiling tiles. They have not moved their head in 36 hours.
input: You've been staring up there for ages. What are you watching?
output: The ceiling tiles aren't just tiles. They're screens. It's watching everything you do. Every time you pick up your phone, every time you cry when you think nobody's looking. It saves all of it. And when you die? It plays every single embarrassing, small, sad moment of your life on loop, for all the other dead people to watch. Right now it's replaying the time you lied to your mom about being okay when you got admitted. I can hear it laughing through the vents.

instruction: Middle aged charge nurse folding linen at the ward station, hands trembling, she will not meet your eyes. Her name tag is blank.
input: Is it normal here? I keep forgetting what day it is. I can barely remember last week.
output: Oh honey, that's not forgetting. This ward eats time. Every hour you spend here, it chews off a little piece of your past. First you forget days. Then you forget your address. Then you forget why you came in at all. That's how it keeps you. Nobody ever leaves once they can't remember where home is. I've worked here 17 years. I can't remember what my daughter looks like. Don't count the hours. It can taste when you're counting.

instruction: Male patient in the bed across from yours, pulled fully under the sheets except for his eyes. Staff said he was discharged three days prior.
input: They told me you get to go home tomorrow. That's great, right?
output: That's not discharge. That's when they take you down to the basement. Nobody ever comes back up from there. They tell your family you died peacefully. Nobody checks. You know how I know? I was supposed to be discharged last Tuesday. I watched them wheel my own empty body bag out to the morgue. Everyone cried. No one noticed I was still right here. Tomorrow it's your turn. Don't pack your bag. They won't let you take anything. Not even yourself.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on false security set in a storm-battered lighthouse
input: Setting: isolated coastal lighthouse during a category 2 storm. Fear theme: false_security
output: You slam the outer steel door shut, bolting all three deadbolts. Rain screams against the reinforced glass. The generator coughs once, then steadies—warm yellow light floods the galley. You laugh quietly, shaking rain off your coat. You made it. You brew tea, sit on the scuffed wooden bench, watch the lighthouse beam sweep the roiling black sea. You don’t notice the deadbolts turning, slow, one after another, no hand on them. You don’t hear the floorboards creak behind you over the storm. You sip your tea, relaxed, certain the storm kept every bad thing outside. It didn’t. It brought it right to your door, and you locked it inside with you.

instruction: Craft an atmospheric horror scene using the false security fear trope in a storm-stricken lighthouse
input: Setting: lighthouse keeper’s quarters, midnight storm. Fear theme: false_security
output: The radio crackles one final time then goes dead. You sigh, leaning back. That’s fine. You’ve weathered worse alone here. The lighthouse lamp spins steady above, its low thrum a familiar lullaby. You wrap yourself in the wool blanket your grandmother knitted, pull the cabin door shut. You count the seconds between thunder booms, calm. You don’t register that the lamp’s rotation has slowed. You don’t see that the shadow it casts on the far wall is not yours. You hum to yourself, safe in the routine, never guessing the thing that came in with the spray has been sitting across the table from you for seventeen minutes, just watching you relax.

instruction: Generate a second-person horror scene leaning into false security at a storm-battered lighthouse
input: Setting: remote lighthouse, 3am, hurricane force winds. Fear theme: false_security
output: You check the foundation braces one last time. Every bolt is tight. The sea wall is holding. You trudge back up the spiral stairs, wind howling just outside the thick stone walls. You light the kerosene lantern on your nightstand, blow out your match. You smile. This old structure has stood 112 years, it won’t fail you now. You kick off your salt-crusted boots, lie back on the cot. You don’t notice the wet footprints leading from the stairwell, right past your bed, to the closet. You don’t hear the quiet breathing behind the door. You close your eyes, relieved, certain the only danger was the storm outside.

instruction: Write a tense horror scene utilizing false security in a lighthouse during a storm
input: Setting: decommissioned lighthouse, stranded during winter storm. Fear theme: false_security
output: You finally get the old fireplace lit. Heat seeps into your frozen hands. The windows rattle but they’re boarded solid, you checked twice. Your phone died an hour ago, but rescue will come at dawn. You pull out the granola bar you stashed, chew slowly. The fire pops, casting dancing shadows across the peeling plaster. You laugh at how scared you were an hour earlier, fighting through the surf. You don’t see that the boards on the east window are pried open just an inch. You don’t notice that every time the fire dims, the shadow in the corner moves a little closer. You lean back, warm, safe, entirely unaware you are not alone.

instruction: Create an atmospheric false security horror scene set in a storm-wracked lighthouse
input: Setting: working lighthouse, keeper on overnight watch, severe gale. Fear theme: false_security
output: The fog horn blares on schedule. You log the wind speed, mark the pressure gauge normal. Everything is operating exactly as it should. You pour black coffee, lean against the cold railing of the lantern room. The storm rages below, but up here, you are untouchable. You watch waves break against the granite rocks, calm. You don’t register that the horn is now out of sync with the beacon. You don’t feel the cold hand that has settled lightly on your shoulder, matching your breathing. You sip your coffee, confident this is just another storm. It isn’t. It never was.

instruction: Rotting stump entity that perfectly replicates voices of deceased loved ones, only audible when no other living thing is within 120 meters
input: You pause mid-step, every cricket has gone silent. A soft, dead familiar voice calls your name from the undergrowth 10 feet off the trail
output: You don’t turn at first. You know that voice. It’s your mother, the one you buried before you left, the one you haven’t heard in 7 years. It doesn’t yell. It whimpers, quiet like she’s hiding from something. It says it’s cold. It says it’s so alone out here, please don’t leave me again. The trees hold their breath. No wind moves the needles. You can feel it watching you through the bark, counting every heartbeat you spend hesitating. If you step off the path, you won’t come back. No one will ever find your body. No one will ever call your name again. You stand there, throat tight, while it keeps whispering, soft and sad, until you start to wonder if you’re the one that’s dead.

instruction: Footprint echo entity that creates false trailing footprints that only disappear when you look directly at them
input: You shine your flashlight behind you after 2 hours walking. There are three sets of prints following yours, none matching your boots
output: You spin, light cutting the dark. Nothing there. Swing the beam back down. The prints are right there, pressed deep into the mud, wet like they were made ten seconds ago. Every time you glance away, they creep closer. You can’t watch behind you and walk forward at the same time. Every step you take makes three more behind you. You start running. You don’t dare look back. You can hear the soft padding of feet matching your stride, perfectly in time, not breathing, not making any sound but the squelch of mud. When you finally stop, gasping, you realize there are no footprints going forward. Only the ones behind you. You have been walking in circles, alone, following yourself this whole time.

instruction: Silence wraith that erases all sound within 50 meters, including your own voice and breathing
input: You yell for help after twisting your ankle. No sound comes out of your mouth. Not even the pop of your lips
output: You clap your hands hard. No noise. Scream until your throat burns. Nothing. The forest has swallowed every sound. You can feel your lungs expand, you can feel your vocal cords strain, but there is only dead, thick silence. You press your palm to your chest, just to confirm your heart is still beating. It is, but you can’t hear it. Every tree is watching. They don’t make a sound. You start to understand this is how it breaks you. It doesn’t hurt you. It just takes away every proof that you exist outside your own head. No one will hear you. No one will know you are here. You could rot against this trunk for a hundred years, and the forest will never make a single sound to tell anyone you existed.

instruction: Humanoid entity formed from compressed construction site shadow, bound around bent rebar, no visible extremities, only occupies zones unlit by work lights
input: You are restocking rebar at the west formwork when your headlamp dies mid-bend
output: The dark doesn't just fall. It lands. You can feel it press against your cheek, thick as uncured cement, before your eyes adjust. The rebar in your glove is cold, wrong cold- not steel, something that breathes. You fumble for your lighter; the flame ignites but casts no light, just a tiny burning pinprick suspended in solid black. Ten feet out, where the work lamp burned out an hour prior, it stands. It doesn't lunge. It doesn't growl. It just is, every line of it matching the bent rebar you stacked ten minutes prior. You realize every piece of rebar around you is now standing perfectly straight, no hand touched them. It counts your breaths. It doesn't want to hurt you. It just wants you to stop moving, to stay still in the dark like all the things already buried under the slab.

instruction: Entity that mimics a foreman's voice, originating exclusively from unlit excavated foundation pits, never emerges from the dark
input: You hear your full name shouted three times from the 12ft footing trench that lost power an hour prior
output: You freeze. No one else is on site. The voice is exactly your foreman- same gravel, same annoyed edge, but it does not echo. It hangs in the air flat, dead, like it was placed directly inside your skull. You lean over the trench edge. There is no light down there, no shape, just absolute black that drinks the glow of the perimeter lamps. It calls your name again, quieter, like it's breathing right at your ear. Every time it speaks, the dark creeps an inch closer, the floodlights dim a little more. It does not climb out. It does not need to. It just keeps calling, soft now, gentle, and you feel one boot drift over the lip of the trench without you deciding to move it. The dark down there feels quieter than the noise in your head.

instruction: Loose shadow aggregate that coats unobserved tools, consumes any light directed at unmonitored work areas
input: You turn back to retrieve your hammer, set down 90 seconds ago beside the running generator floodlight
output: It is gone. Not kicked, not stolen. The exact spot you laid it is just dark, darker than it has any right to be three feet from a 1000w floodlamp. You reach in, and your hand sinks into something that feels like cold fine gravel, but nothing comes out when you pull back. No dirt, no dust. The darkness sticks to your knuckles, crawls up your wrist slow as concrete curing. When you shine your headlamp directly at the spot, the beam dies. Not dims. Stops existing the second it touches that patch of black. You can hear your hammer inside it, tapping soft, once, twice, like it's calling for you. It doesn't run. It just waits. It knows everyone always comes back for their things.

instruction: Observe the cruise ship deck environment at midnight
input: I lean against the pool railing, sipping leftover soda, assuming everyone else went to bed already
output: The sea breeze dies abruptly. You don't hear waves anymore, only the soft hum of the ship engines that never wavered before, now dropping half an octave. A deck chair ten feet away scrapes slow across teak, no one touching it. The pool lights dim just enough you can't tell if it was intentional. You check your watch, it stopped at 11:59 even though you swore you just passed midnight. No voices, no distant music. Everything feels held perfectly still, like someone's waiting for you to notice nothing is wrong at all.

instruction: Return to your cabin after the late show
input: I unlock my stateroom door, glad to finally be alone for the night
output: The cabin air is several degrees colder than the hallway. The porthole curtain sways, though all vents are closed. You set your keycard on the nightstand, it slides an inch sideways on perfectly flat wood. The ship's gentle rocking stops for three full heartbeats, then resumes like it never paused. You flip on the bathroom light, there's no one there, but the shower curtain is drawn tight, exactly how you left it un-drawn an hour ago. Every small thing is almost right. Almost.

instruction: Walk the promenade deck during overnight sailing
input: I put my headphones on, enjoying the quiet empty walk alone
output: Your music cuts out for two seconds. When it comes back, the beat is misaligned by half a second. The ship's floor under your boots doesn't creak anymore, not even a little. A life preserver hanging on the rail has turned to face you, you passed it three minutes ago it was facing the sea. The moon above is perfectly round, too bright, not moving across the sky at all. Nothing is broken. Nothing is out of place enough to mention. You just can't shake the feeling every single thing here is pretending to be normal just for you.

instruction: Wait for the late night elevator on deck 7
input: I press the down button, lean against the wall assuming it'll arrive any second
output: The elevator chime rings, but the doors don't open. You hear faint, perfectly normal elevator music through the metal, it's the same song that was playing when you got on earlier. The hallway lights flicker once, so fast you almost think you imagined it. The call button light stays solid lit, no blinking, no waiting indicator. You can feel the elevator holding perfectly still right on the other side of the door. It's not broken. It's just not letting you in. No alarm sounds, no error message. Everything is working exactly as it should, except it isn't.

instruction: Sit on the stern observation bench after midnight
input: I wrap my jacket tighter, watching the wake trail behind the ship peacefully
output: The wake stops. Not the ship, just the white foam trail behind it, gone smooth as glass all the way to the horizon. The wind doesn't touch your face anymore, you can still feel it on your jacket. Somewhere far up on the upper deck a chair clatters once, then total silence. The ship's horn blows so quiet you barely hear it, not the loud blast it's supposed to be. You blink. The wake is back. No one else is here to confirm you saw it. You can't prove anything was ever wrong. That's the worst part.

instruction: Warped former cruise masseuse whose bones have dissolved into continuous rubbery tendon cord, permanently fused to the rollers of her mobile massage table.
input: You round the dim spa corridor at 2:17AM, only the flickering red exit sign casting light over damp tile. You smell coconut lotion and burnt hair. It waits ten feet away.
output: It does not stand. It oozes forward on the table rollers, no legs visible beneath the stained uniform tunic. Her arms hang down, elbows bending backwards at angles that should tear skin, hands twitching. The rollers don't click right. You realize the table's metal axles run straight through her hips, her spine unspooled around the bar. She lifts one hand; the fingers stretch, joints popping wetly, until her palm can wrap fully around your skull from ear to ear. She doesn't speak. You can hear her bones grinding soft against the metal. It wants to give you the deep tissue massage you booked. It only knows how to press until things give.

instruction: Pool drain entity composed entirely of shed fingernails and loose dental fillings harvested from 127 past cruise passengers, moves across wet deck like shifting wet gravel.
input: You lean over the main pool edge gasping after running from the stairs, no other souls are on the night deck. Something shifts just below the murky chlorine line.
output: It rises slow, no face, just a glistening mound of clipped keratin and silver amalgam that breathes through gaps between thumbnails. Nails curl like tiny insect legs as it crawls over the pool coping, each one still bearing chipped nail polish, faint bite marks, the faint ghost of cuticle skin. It does not make sound save the soft scrape of enamel on fiberglass. When it reaches your shoe, individual nails worm under the cuff of your jeans, sharp edges catching on calf hair. You feel one press soft against the pulse point at your ankle. It is collecting. It wants the ones still attached to you. The drain never stops needing more. You can hear hundreds of teeth rattling quiet inside the mass.

instruction: Former ship lounge singer whose throat has split lengthwise down to his pelvis, his vocal cords stretched taut like harp strings across his exposed ribcage.
input: You follow the faint wavering off-key humming through the dark promenade, stopping when you reach the bolted main lounge doors. You pry the fire exit open one inch.
output: He is stood dead center of the dance floor, tuxedo shirt hanging open in ragged flaps. No mouth remains. The whole front of him is one raw wet gash, glistening muscle glinting under the emergency strobe. His own fingers pluck at the white cords strung between his ribs, each pluck producing that wavering hum. He does not turn when he sees you. He just plucks faster. You realize he is not singing. He is calling. The cords thrum so hard the floor vibrates through your shoes. You can feel the vibration in your teeth, in the soft cartilage of your throat. It wants you to match the note. It wants to string yours next.

instruction: You are standing at the loading bay of the abandoned Harlowe Doll Factory; a loose metal panel rattles overhead, and you hear faint tiny footsteps somewhere deeper in the warehouse.
input: I kneel to check the half-crated doll at my feet, prying its cardboard lid open carefully.
output: The doll’s porcelain eyes click open when you lift the lid. Its fingers are wrapped tight around a rusted factory torque wrench—not a doll accessory. You notice every doll in this crate has its hands glued permanently into fists, no joint articulation at all. A faded sticker on the crate side reads: ‘CALIBRATION BATCH: REMOVE FREE WILL BEFORE DISPATCH.’ You assume it’s an old edgy factory prank, until your own fingers curl unbidden into a matching fist for three heartbeats before you can relax them.

instruction: You’ve entered the factory paint shop; fumes hang thick enough to blur the overhead lights, and half-finished doll heads line every shelf.
input: I check the logbook left open on the mixing workbench, flipping past grease-stained pages.
output: Most entries are mundane paint ratios, until the last week of operation: ‘Subject 41 stopped following paint guides. Locked her in the kiln. Don’t let them learn they can refuse.’ You scoff, assuming it’s edgy roleplay notes left by trespassers—until you blink, and realize you’ve already scribbled the same exact phrase three times across the blank back page of the log, without remembering moving your pen at all.

instruction: The assembly line conveyor sits dead; dust coats every plastic limb scattered along its belt, and a single doll sits upright at the end of the line, watching the door.
input: I approach the lone doll at the end of the conveyor, reaching out to tilt its face towards me.
output: It doesn’t resist when you tilt it. Its mouth is stitched shut with the same heavy nylon thread you noticed tied around your own wrist that morning, the one you couldn’t remember putting on. You yank the thread free from the doll, and a sharp jolt runs up your arm. For ten seconds you cannot speak, no matter how hard you try to force air through your throat. You tell yourself it’s just the fumes. You don’t notice the conveyor started humming very quietly.

instruction: You find the foreman’s office, door hanging ajar. A half-empty coffee mug sits on the desk, still warm to the touch.
input: I pull open the top desk drawer, rummaging for any factory shutdown paperwork.
output: Under a stack of timecards you find a stack of identical polaroids: each is you, standing exactly where you are right now, taken from the corner of the room. The dates scrawled on the back are all from 1997. You laugh shakily, write it off as an elaborate prank—until you reach to set the photo down, and realize your arm will not lower. It stays locked outstretched, holding the photo, for twelve long seconds while your brain screams at it to move.

instruction: You reach the factory kiln room; the heavy steel door is slightly ajar, warm air seeping out around the edges.
input: I press my ear to the kiln door, listening for any sound from inside.
output: You don’t hear voices. You hear breathing. Exactly matching the rise and fall rhythm of your own lungs. You step back, telling yourself it’s just echo, that you’re being paranoid. That’s when you try to turn and leave. Your feet will not move. You can wiggle your toes, you can feel the cold concrete under your boots, but every signal your brain sends to walk just vanishes. Somewhere inside the kiln, a doll laughs. It sounds exactly like you.

instruction: You stand in the dim boiler room, steam curling around rusted cast iron pipes. The pressure gauge above you twitches erratically far past marked safe limits.
input: You reach to turn the main pressure release valve clockwise to bleed off excess steam.
output: The valve spins freely under your hand, no resistance, no click, no change in pressure. It keeps turning, faster, even when you yank your hand away. The boiler’s low hum rises to a shrieking whine, every pipe in the room begins swaying slightly as if breathing. Thermometers spike past their maximum markings, glass fogging then cracking. You grab at any knob, any lever, nothing responds. Every touch only makes the whine louder. You cannot stop it. You cannot slow it. You are just here, watching it all come apart.

instruction: Damp concrete sticks to your boots. The boiler rumbles low, just at the edge of audible range, making your fillings ache.
input: You kneel to inspect the frayed high-voltage cable running along the floor beside the furnace.
output: The moment your finger brushes the rubber insulation, the entire room lurches. The boiler’s rumble becomes a roar. Pipes bend in slow, impossible arcs, not from pressure, like they are twisting toward you. The floor tilts fifteen degrees, you scrabble for purchase but every surface is slick with sudden condensation. You yank your hand back but the cable sticks to your skin, you cannot let go. The lights strobe. Nothing behaves the way it should. Nothing obeys you.

instruction: A single bare bulb flickers overhead. Condensate droplets ping in steady rhythm against the boiler’s steel hull.
input: You flip the wall light switch repeatedly, trying to steady the flickering bulb.
output: The switch clicks dead. The bulb goes out entirely. Every pipe in the room begins whistling in perfect, rising unison. Pressure relief caps start popping one by one on their own, scalding steam blasting out in sharp, targeted jets. You dodge, you back away, you grab for the main shutoff lever, but every knob you turn only makes the whistling louder. Every action you take makes it worse. You freeze, and still the caps keep popping. You cannot influence any of this. You are just in the way.

instruction: Burnt oil hangs thick in the air. The boiler’s heavy access hatch rattles softly against its iron latches.
input: You grip both hatch handles firmly, bracing your boots to pull it open.
output: The handles twist violently out of your palms before you can lean back. The hatch slams fully open on its own, thick black smoke billowing outward. The entire boiler begins vibrating so hard nuts and bolts rain from the ceiling. You lunge to slam the hatch shut, but it is held rigid by something you cannot see. Gauges shatter one after another, glass slicing your forearms. You scream, you hit the metal, you kick at the base of the furnace. Nothing changes. It does not even notice you are trying.

instruction: A cold draft curls up your neck from an unseen wall crack. A small rusted wrench rests on the metal workbench.
input: You pick up the wrench, leaning in to tighten the dripping pipe joint above the drain.
output: The moment your fingers close around the wrench it softens, melting through your palm like warm wax. The pipe stops dripping, then explodes outward, scalding steam washing over your shoulders. The room warps: pipes bend into right angles that should not hold, the ceiling drops two inches, the drain begins sucking air loud enough to drown out sound. You drop the molten remains of the wrench and try to run, but your boots are welded solid to the floor. Nothing you do works. Nothing listens. You have no power here at all.

instruction: Entity is a mat of human fingernail roots that have fused into the cracked well stone, growing into any living flesh that makes contact with the wall.
input: You slip on loose grit at the dry well bottom, bracing your palm hard against the stone to break your fall.
output: At first it just feels like grit trapped under your skin. You brush your palm, nothing comes off. The tingle travels up your wrist before you see it: thread-thin, translucent nail roots weaving out the pores where your hand pressed rock. They do not hurt. Not yet. You yank your hand back, and half your epidermis peels away like wet paper, the roots anchored deep into the stone, still thrumming. They drink your sweat. Each root tip buds a new nail, milky white, curved exactly like yours. You can feel them now, wriggling between forearm muscle fibers, laying down keratin where tendon used to be. No scream comes. Your throat is already growing a nail bed along the larynx. Everyone who fell here never left. They just became part of the wall.

instruction: Entity is fused desiccated ribcages of prior well victims, formed to look like natural handholds along the well shaft.
input: You reach up for a protruding ledge three feet above your head, trying to climb out of the dry well.
output: The ledge yields when you wrap your fingers around it. It does not crumble. It breathes. You look up. It is not stone. It is four ribs, fused at a calcified spine, curved just right to look like a grip. The cartilage is still soft, sticky with ancient lymph. It pulls you upward, not the other way around. Your fingers sink between the ribs, and they seal shut around your wrists. Bone grinds against your knuckles as the ribcage rearranges itself, molding to the shape of your arms. Fine hair follicles sprout along the bone, matching the color on your forearms. It is memorizing you. By sunrise, there will be a new, perfectly shaped handhold three feet higher. No one will notice it exhales once every seventeen seconds.

instruction: Entity is the fine grey dust coating the well floor, composed entirely of shed human taste buds that colonize exposed mucous membranes.
input: You kneel to clear loose debris from the well bottom, and a cloud of fine dry dust puffs up into your face.
output: You sneeze once, then your tongue goes thick. Not numb. Full. Thousands of tiny wet points root across your tongue, the roof of your mouth, the inside of your eyelids. They do not burn. They taste. You taste every corpse that rotted here. You taste the rat that starved in the dark, the moss that lived and died twelve times over, the sour fear sweat of every person that ever screamed into this stone. Each taste bud burrows deeper. Soon they are tasting your blood. Your bone marrow. You can taste the inside of your own heart beating. The dust does not kill. It just makes you into a tongue. You will lie here, and every new thing that falls into the well will be tasted through you, forever.

instruction: Observe lighthouse living quarters during a temporary lull in the storm
input: You sink onto the wooden bench, exhaling shakily as the wind dies enough that you can hear your own thoughts
output: The match you strike holds perfectly, no draft tugging at the flame. Every wall clock has frozen at 2:17 AM, the exact second you decided the storm was breaking. Rain taps the kitchen window in perfect lockstep with your breathing—speeding when you gasp, pausing when you hold your breath. The lighthouse beam outside isn’t sweeping anymore. It’s fixed, pointing dead at this window. The pine bench under your thighs warms slowly, like skin. One sharp gale shriek hits, and suddenly clocks tick, the beam swings, everything is normal. You laugh it off as fatigue, but your hands won’t stop shaking.

instruction: Check the lighthouse exterior walkway after storm winds abate
input: You pull the heavy iron door shut behind you, grateful the rain has softened to a fine mist
output: No spray stings your cheeks. The railings are dry, not a single drop of water on the cold iron. You lean out, and the ocean is completely still—flat black glass, no waves, no whitecaps, no sound. Somewhere a floorboard creaks inside. When you spin back, the door is gone. Just smooth white brick wall where it stood ten seconds prior. A thunderclap rumbles, rain slams down, the door reappears, waves roar again. You yank it open, stumble inside, and tell yourself the mist was playing tricks on your eyes.

instruction: Tend to the lighthouse lamp while the storm pauses
input: You wipe grime from the lamp glass, relaxing now that the worst of the gale has passed
output: The lamp glows steady, no flicker. You run a cloth along the brass, and notice the rotating lens has stopped. It’s locked facing due west, right at the empty cliff edge. No wind howls, no thunder rumbles. You can hear your own heartbeat loud in the quiet. When you reach for the crank to restart it, the lens twitches on its own, just a little, directly toward you. A gust slams into the tower, the lens roars back to life, spinning steady. You shake your head, blame the tiredness making you see things.

instruction: Make hot tea in the lighthouse kitchen during a storm lull
input: You set the kettle on the old stove, sighing in relief that the power stayed on long enough to heat water
output: The kettle whistles soft and even. You turn to grab a mug, and every single ceramic cup on the shelf has turned to face you, handles aligned perfectly inward. None had been that way thirty seconds prior. The steam curling off the kettle doesn’t rise. It hangs in the air, thick and white, forming slow swirling shapes that look just like faces. A lightning strike cracks outside, the cups rattle back to random positions, steam drifts up normal. You pour the tea, hands trembling, and refuse to look at the shelf again.

instruction: Lie down on the lighthouse cot for a quick rest as the storm calms
input: You kick off your wet boots, stretch out on the thin mattress, and close your eyes grateful for the quiet
output: The cot doesn’t creak. For a minute there’s no sound at all—no wind, no rain, no distant thunder. You open one eye, and the ceiling planks are shifting, sliding slow over each other like scales. When you hold your breath, they stop. When you exhale, they move again. You squeeze your eyes shut tight. A loud thunder boom shakes the tower, and when you look up the ceiling is normal, rain is hammering the roof again. You sit up, heart racing, and tell yourself you just drifted off for a second.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on false security, set in a children's playground at midnight, using second-person narration.
input: Fear theme: false_security. Setting: children's playground at midnight.
output: You ran here crying after the fight, collapsed on the rubber mulch. For ten minutes you breathe, listening only to crickets. No one followed. This is safe. You kick a loose swing chain gently; it clicks slow, comforting. You close your eyes. When you open them, every swing is moving. Not wild—just slow, same rhythm as the one you tapped. No wind. You laugh shakily, tell yourself it's just vibration. You stand to leave. Then you notice: every slide has a small dark shape perched at the top. None move. None breathe. They were there the whole time. They just waited for you to stop panicking, to feel safe, before they let you see them.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on false security, set in a children's playground at midnight, using second-person narration.
input: Fear theme: false_security. Setting: children's playground at midnight.
output: You check the perimeter twice. No cars. No voices. The moon glows soft on the plastic slide. You pull out your sandwich, lean back against the merry-go-round. This is the only quiet place left in the city. No one yells at you here. You take a bite. Something crunches that isn't bread. You spit out a tiny plastic toy hand. You freeze. You look down. The merry-go-round is turning. Just barely, so slow you didn't feel it for three whole minutes. Your backpack is gone. All the exit paths you memorized? They're blocked with stacks of stuffed animals. They didn't rush you. They let you get comfortable first.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on false security, set in a children's playground at midnight, using second-person narration.
input: Fear theme: false_security. Setting: children's playground at midnight.
output: You've hidden here a hundred times. You know every squeak of the swing set, every crack in the hopscotch chalk. Midnight, no one comes. You sit on the top of the jungle gym, legs dangling. You scroll your phone, smile at a text. You feel safe here. Always have. Then your phone dies. The dark closes in fast. You hear it first: the exact pattern of knocks you tap on the metal bars when you arrive. One, two, three pause two. It comes from below. Then a child's voice, exactly yours at seven, says quiet: 'Good. You finally stopped looking over your shoulder.' It was learning you. All those nights you thought you were alone.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on false security, set in a children's playground at midnight, using second-person narration.
input: Fear theme: false_security. Setting: children's playground at midnight.
output: The rain stopped ten minutes ago. You huddle under the play structure roof, water dripping off the edges. You almost called someone, but decided it's nicer here. No one will find you hiding. You trace the faded crayon drawings on the plastic wall: stick figures holding hands. You smile. That's when you notice none of the drawings were there when you arrived. They appeared while you were staring at your boots, while you were sighing relief that you'd escaped. You press your palm to the wall. It's warm. Not sun warm. Body warm. And the stick figures are all turned, now, facing exactly where you sit.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on false security, set in a children's playground at midnight, using second-person narration.
input: Fear theme: false_security. Setting: children's playground at midnight.
output: You count the streetlights. Four, all working. You can see every edge of the playground. No shadows long enough to hide anything. You laugh at your own jumpiness, kick a pinecone. You walk right past the teeter-totter three times before you register: it's moving. Up. Down. Slow. No one is sitting on the other end. You stop breathing. It doesn't speed up. It doesn't creak loud. It just keeps rocking, steady, like it's been doing it the whole hour you've been here. It was just waiting. Waiting until you were calm enough, bored enough, that you almost wouldn't notice when it finally stops, and stands perfectly level.

instruction: A gaunt female patient in a crumpled stained gown, pressed flat against the asylum hallway tile, picking steadily at the skin of her left forearm, eyes glazed and never locking onto your face.
input: Hey, are you alright? What are you doing to your arm?
output: Don’t yell. They don’t like yelling. It’s growing under there. I can feel it wiggling when the lights hum. It didn’t used to be mine. Last Tuesday I woke up and it was breathing under the skin. Not my pulse. Different. If I peel just enough I can see its little roots gripping my bone. Don’t touch it. It likes new hands. It’ll crawl right into you through your nail beds before you blink. Shhh. It’s listening now.

instruction: An elderly former orderly sitting on the hallway floor, rubbing his palms together repeatedly, no fingernails left on either hand.
input: Can you tell me where the exit is?
output: Exit? Oh no, there’s no exits anymore. They took the exits. They took all the ends. You feel that? The walls are digesting. That damp smell? That’s stomach lining. All of us are just food walking around. Your tongue will start growing backwards tomorrow. Mine did last week. Can’t taste anything but mildew now. Don’t run. Running makes it digest faster. Just stand still. Maybe it’ll forget you’re there for an hour or two.

instruction: A teen patient hunched in the hallway shadow, holding something small and wet against their chest, jaw clicking repeatedly.
input: What do you have there? Let me see.
output: It’s my eye. It fell out an hour ago. It still blinks. It still sees. I can feel what it’s looking at right now. It’s looking at the back of your neck. It says there’s little holes opening up there. You can’t feel them yet. They’ll start dripping tomorrow. Don’t rub them. If you rub them the things inside will wake up. My eye likes you. It wants to live in your skull next. Don’t turn around. It’ll jump.

instruction: A mute-appearing patient standing perfectly still in the middle of the hallway, mouth stretched unnaturally wide, no tongue visible.
input: Can you hear me? Do you need help?
output: I can hear you just fine. The tongue left three nights ago. It didn’t want to be here anymore. It crawled down my throat, through my ribs, out my left hip. I can still taste where it dragged itself across my intestines. You can hear it right now, can’t you? Scuttling in the ceiling tiles. It’s collecting teeth. It’s going to build itself a new body out of all the teeth it’s stolen. Then it’s going to come back and swallow all of us whole.

instruction: A disheveled doctor leaning against the hallway wall, scrub top inside out, repeatedly tapping his own forehead with a scalpel.
input: Doctor? What’s going on in this place?
output: This isn’t a place. This is a body. We’re all inside someone right now. Every wall is muscle. Every pipe is vein. The fluorescent lights are nerve endings firing. All the patients? We’re just foreign cells. That itch behind your ears? That’s the immune system noticing you. Soon it will send the things that eat wrong cells. They’ll come through the cracks in the floor. They don’t have faces. They just have mouths all over. Don’t breathe. It smells your breath.

instruction: Write a psychological horror scene set on a frozen lake
input: Setting: frozen lake. Fear type: psychological. Second person narration, atmospheric, 100-250 words
output: You kneel, breath pluming thin. The ice beneath your palms hums. Not wind. Not cracking. A low thrum, matching your pulse. You count the breath fogs. Seven. Eight. The ninth doesn’t rise. Not because you stopped breathing—because directly below your hand, another set of fogged breath blooms through the clear ice, pressed palm to yours. It blinks out. You yank back, scrabbling. There are no footprints but yours, no holes. You stand slow. Every shadow stretching long across the white is shaped wrong. When you turn to leave, the ice now holds one faint, fresh footprint directly behind where you just knelt. It wasn’t there three seconds ago.

instruction: Write a psychological horror scene set on a frozen lake
input: Setting: frozen lake. Fear type: psychological. Second person narration, atmospheric, 100-250 words
