output: It’s 2:17am. You walked out here to get away from the shouting inside the cabin. The lake stretches dead white under moon. You kick a loose ice chip. It skitters, stops. Then skitters back. You freeze. It does it again, slow, as if nudged by something you can’t see. You don’t breathe. Every sound in the world dies. Then you hear your own voice, muffled, coming through the ice, whimpering. That’s not you. Not right now. But it says your name. It begs you not to turn around. It begs you not to look back at the cabin. You didn’t even realize your feet were already turning until they stopped, rooted.

instruction: Write a psychological horror scene set on a frozen lake
input: Setting: frozen lake. Fear type: psychological. Second person narration, atmospheric, 100-250 words
output: You brought the old compass out, just for something to hold. It spins. Not wild. Steady, one full rotation every seven heartbeats. You count. Seven. Spin. Seven. Spin. You glance up. The treeline you left ten minutes ago is gone. There is only ice, flat, white, endless. You drop the compass. It doesn’t clatter. It sinks slow, straight through solid ice, as if the water was waiting. When you press your boot down, the ice gives, just a little, soft as flesh. It breathes. You can feel it exhale up through the soles of your boots. No crack. No splash. Just the quiet, terrible awareness that this lake knows you are here. That it has been waiting.

instruction: Write a psychological horror scene set on a frozen lake
input: Setting: frozen lake. Fear type: psychological. Second person narration, atmospheric, 100-250 words
output: You trace the lines in the ice. First you thought they were cracks. Then you realized they are handprints. Thousands, layered, faded, pressed deep as if someone pushed through from below, over and over. None face out. All are turned inward, reaching for the centre of the lake. You follow them for half an hour before you notice: every single one has exactly your fingerprint pattern. Every single thumb is bent at the same odd little angle yours is, the one you broke when you were twelve. You don’t remember coming here before. You don’t remember pressing your hands to this ice. But every line, every scar on the knuckles, matches perfectly.

instruction: Write a psychological horror scene set on a frozen lake
input: Setting: frozen lake. Fear type: psychological. Second person narration, atmospheric, 100-250 words
output: The snow stopped an hour ago. No wind. No birds. You can hear your own heartbeat in your ears. Then you hear footsteps. Slow, crunching, ten paces behind. You turn. Nothing. Just unbroken snow. You walk faster. They match your pace. You run. They run. You stop dead. They stop exactly when you do. You don’t move for twelve minutes. Just stand, shaking. Then you lift one boot. Very slowly. Behind you, in the perfect white snow, a single boot print appears. Exactly where you just heard the step. It presses into the snow one millimetre at a time, right before your eyes.

instruction: You have walked past the same dented water fountain for the 4th time. The hallway never shifts, never ends. A single loose ceiling tile rattles softly overhead.
input: You step onto the fountain base to reach up and nudge the loose tile.
output: Your fingers brush cold dust first. Nothing falls out, but you feel something soft, not insulation, brush the back of your knuckle. When you yank your hand back, there are three faint, dry scratch marks across your skin. You scan the ceiling: every tile now looks identical, no gaps, no rattle. Half a second later, you hear the exact same rattle, but it’s coming from directly behind you, right at ear level. You don’t turn yet.

instruction: Every corner you turn shows the same scuff mark, same dead fluorescent tube. You notice a crumpled candy wrapper on the floor, one you definitely dropped three loops back.
input: You kneel to pick up the candy wrapper to check if it’s the same one.
output: It is the same wrapper, same crease where you bit it open. But when you flip it over, someone has scrawled STOP LOOKING in smudged pencil on the back. You look up, and for one split second the hallway is not empty: a tall dark shape stands perfectly still at the far end, right where you just came from. Before you can blink, it’s gone. The lights hum steady again. The wrapper disintegrates into dust in your palm.

instruction: You’ve counted 11 identical doorways now, all locked. A faint, high beep repeats once every seven seconds, just quiet enough you almost miss it.
input: You press your ear to each door in order, trying to locate the beep.
output: The first five doors are silent. The sixth hums. The seventh: when your ear touches the wood, the beep stops entirely. Instead you hear a single, sharp inhale, like someone just noticed you’re there. You jerk back. Nothing happens. No sound comes from the door. You check the next doors: all are silent. When you glance back at the seventh door, you realize the doorknob is now turned, just a tiny fraction, from where it was before.

instruction: Your own shoeprints trail behind you, looping infinitely down the hallway. You spot a single print that doesn’t match, barefoot, right next to yours.
input: You crouch to measure the bare footprint against your own shoe.
output: It’s the exact same size as your foot. The print is still damp, like it was placed there ten seconds ago. As you stare, another bare footprint appears half a meter closer, right in front of you. No sound. No movement. Just it, suddenly there, dark and wet on the linoleum. You freeze. For three long heartbeats nothing else appears. Then you hear the quietest, giddy little giggle, right beside your ear.

instruction: The overhead lights flicker once every time you complete a loop. This time, you catch a faint shadow moving fast along the wall just as the lights cut out.
input: You stop walking and stand perfectly still, watching the wall to see if the shadow returns.
output: Thirty seconds pass. Nothing. You start to relax, tell yourself it was just a flicker artifact. Then the lights don’t just flicker—they go completely dark for two full seconds. When they come back on, there is a hand print pressed flat against the wall, exactly at eye level, six inches from your face. It is still dripping. You didn’t hear anyone approach. You didn’t hear anything at all.

instruction: Observe underground parking level B3 while walking toward your vehicle
input: You pause mid-walk to brush lint off your jacket sleeve
output: The overhead sodium lights dim to sickly amber. Thin pulsing capillary strands weave out of concrete wall seams, growing slow as breath. They do not break when you brush past—they sink soft into your forearm skin. Every parked car tire gently flexes, rubber breathing with faint wet inhales. The cold here is not dead air, it is living. You can hear it chewing slow on rebar far below the floor. No one else is present. The capillaries thread under your fingernails now, and you can feel them tasting the salt of your blood, moving slow up your wrist.

instruction: Check the rear bumper of your sedan for scratch damage
input: You kneel and run your palm along the underside of the bumper
output: Concrete under your knees oozes warm clear slime. When you pull back, the bumper rubber trim has softened into fleshy gum tissue, lined with tiny white human teeth that rasped your palm. They did not break skin, only tested your texture. Every license plate across the lot twitches like a closed eyelid. The air smells like wet molars. You feel something large under the concrete wriggle directly beneath you, slow and deliberate, aligning its body exactly to the shape of yours on the floor.

instruction: Press the call button for the parking level elevator
input: You tap the metal elevator call button once with your index finger
output: The button yields under your touch, not plastic but swollen bruised flesh. It bleeds one dark drop that runs down your wrist. Elevator doors do not open. Instead the entire wall around the shaft pulses, dry concrete cracking to reveal raw pink muscle underneath. Every crack sweats thin warm moisture. Ventilation hum cuts out, replaced by a steady gurgling heartbeat. You try to wipe the blood away, but it has already sunk into your skin, leaving a cold tingling that crawls up toward your elbow.

instruction: Retrieve dropped car keys from the parking aisle floor
input: You bend over and reach down to grab your keys off the asphalt
output: Asphalt yields warm under your fingers, like the back of a living neck. Your keys have sunk half an inch deep, surrounded by fine dark hairs sprouting straight out of the tarmac. When you yank your hand back, three hairs tear free from your scalp; you watch them root into the ground right where you stood. Every painted parking line oozes thick dark blood, running slow toward the drain grates. The floor breathes under your shoes, rising and falling just enough that you cannot pretend you do not feel it.

instruction: Shine your phone flashlight down the unlit west parking aisle
input: You hold your phone up and activate the flashlight beam
output: The light does not hit concrete. It illuminates rows of wet half-formed human arms sticking straight out of every parked car window, fingers twitching slow. They do not reach for you, only flex, joints cracking soft in the cold. Your phone grows so hot it burns your palm, and you watch dozens of tiny useless fingernails sprout fast across the back of your hand. The air drops ten degrees in three seconds. You taste iron on every breath. This level was never built for cars.

instruction: Write a horror scene set in an abandoned factory that evokes terror of darkness
input: Setting: abandoned textile factory, 2am, dead flashlight after stepping over broken loom parts
output: You freeze mid-step. The flashlight died mid-blink: one second rusted loom teeth glinted like cracked bone, the next nothing. Not dim, not shadow—pure, unbroken darkness that presses against your eyeballs like wet cloth. You smell old cotton rot, hear your own breath too loud in the hush. Something brushes the back of your wrist. Not fabric, not metal. Warm. You don’t move, don’t breathe. When you fumble for your phone, your fingers brush another hand first. It doesn’t pull away. It curls, slow, around yours, calloused and cold at the knuckles, like it’s been waiting for you to reach.

instruction: Craft an atmospheric horror scene centered on fear of darkness in an abandoned factory
input: Setting: abandoned auto factory, underground storage level, exit vanished behind you
output: You counted twelve steps back to the stairwell. You counted them twice. Now there’s only darkness. No distant safety light glow, no echo of your earlier footsteps. Every sound you make dies instantly, swallowed whole by the black. Cold concrete sticks to your palms. You reach out, and the wall is gone. Then you feel it: breathing. Not yours. Close enough that you can feel the cool exhale on your neck, but when you turn there’s nothing to see. It doesn’t make noise. It just waits, right there, in the dark you can’t outrun.

instruction: Generate a second-person horror scene leveraging fear of total darkness in an abandoned factory
input: Setting: abandoned glass factory, broken furnace room, matches running out
output: The third match sputters out. You have one left. You don’t strike it. You already know what you saw last time: nothing, just for a split second at the edge of the flame, something tall that didn’t cast a shadow. The dark is thick here, thick enough to drink. You taste fine silica dust on your tongue. It’s watching you. You know it is. It’s been waiting for the light to die properly. It doesn’t rush. It knows you’ll burn that last match eventually. It knows you’ll give it exactly one perfect, tiny window to reach you.

instruction: Write a tense horror scene focused on fear of darkness inside an abandoned factory
input: Setting: abandoned packing plant, conveyor belt catwalk, tripped and lost your headlamp
output: You hit the catwalk grating hard. When the stars clear from your vision, there’s only dark. No railings you can find, no edge, no floor below that you can hear. You don’t dare stand. You crawl, fingers scraping rust, every inch a gamble. Then the conveyor rumbles. Slow, at first, just vibration up through your bones. No power has run here in thirty years. Something heavy drags along the belt, getting closer. You can’t see it. You can only hear it breathe, and feel the metal shake harder with every foot it moves toward you.

instruction: Create an atmospheric second-person horror scene centered on terror of darkness in an abandoned factory
input: Setting: abandoned chemical factory, exterior door locked behind you, moonless night
output: The wind died ten minutes ago. There is no light anywhere. Not through cracked windows, not from the distant town. This dark has weight. It presses your shoulders down, makes your lungs work twice as hard just to pull air. You call out. No echo. Just silence. Then you start to notice: the dark is moving. Not shadows, just shifts in the nothing. Patches that feel colder, patches that feel alive. One brushes past your cheek. You can feel it hesitate, just for a moment, before it begins to wrap slow around your throat.

instruction: Describe environmental event on night cruise ship triggered by player action
input: You lean over the starboard rail to watch the ocean, turning off your phone flashlight to see the stars better
output: The instant your screen goes black, the ship’s running lights cut out all at once. No hum, no warning—just dead silence. The deck wood under your palms grows cold enough to burn. Somewhere behind you, a deck chair scrapes slow across the planks, even though you counted zero other people out here ten seconds prior. You can’t see your own hand held six inches from your face. The ocean doesn’t glint. It doesn’t even sound like water anymore, just soft breathing, right at the edge of the rail where you’re leaning.

instruction: Describe environmental event on night cruise ship triggered by player action
input: You head down the unmarked stairwell to the lower decks, flicking the hall light switch as you pass
output: The light clicks once, then dies. The stairwell seals shut around you. You can feel the air thicken, like someone pressed a wet blanket over your head. Footsteps thud one floor above, matching your exact pace, but never coming closer. The metal handrail under your fingers shifts—first smooth, then ridged like knuckles, then gone entirely before you can yank back. When you blink hard, you don’t see the faint afterglow of the switch. There’s just nothing. No light ever existed here. No ship ever existed here. Just dark, and you, and the footsteps.

instruction: Describe environmental event on night cruise ship triggered by player action
input: You pause outside your cabin door, fumbling for your keycard in the dark corridor
output: The overhead strip lights pop out one by one, marching toward you down the hall. The carpet under your shoes stops feeling like carpet; it’s soft, yielding, breathing. You hear curtains rustle from every closed cabin, even though all the portholes are sealed for the night. Your keycard slips out of your hand, and when you drop to feel for it, your fingers brush something warm that isn’t plastic, that pulls away fast. No moonlight seeps under the doors. No emergency lights flicker on. It’s the kind of dark that sinks into your lungs when you breathe.

instruction: Describe environmental event on night cruise ship triggered by player action
input: You laugh and blow out the single candle on the late night bar counter
output: The whole bar goes dark. Not dim. Not shadowed. Absolute. The ice in the abandoned glasses stops clinking. The smell of rum vanishes, replaced by cold dry air like the inside of a tomb. You reach for the bar edge and your hand goes through empty space where the oak counter was three seconds prior. Somewhere far off, the ship’s horn tries to bleat, but cuts off mid-note like someone grabbed it. You can’t tell if your eyes are open or closed. There is no difference. The dark is inside them now, filling every gap.

instruction: Describe environmental event on night cruise ship triggered by player action
input: You lean back against the lifeboat davit, closing your eyes for just ten seconds to rest
output: When you open them, everything is gone. The wind stops. The hum of the engines dies. You reach out and the metal davit is no longer there. You call out, and your voice doesn’t echo—it just dies immediately, swallowed whole. There’s no glow from the bridge, no starlight, no phosphorescence in the waves. It’s not night. It’s absence. You can feel the dark pressing against your eyeballs, soft, insistent, like it’s trying to crawl in. No one will find you here. No one even remembers this part of the ship existed, once the lights went out.

instruction: You navigate a dim fog-choked mirror maze, cool glass brushing your elbows at every blind turn.
input: You pause, squinting to count how many reflections of your scuffed boots line the hall
output: A faint breath fogs the glass directly left—no reflection matches the exhale. Every boot reflection twitches one toe in perfect unison, not when you move. The air drops 3 degrees in a single heartbeat, soft scuffing echoes behind every mirror at once with no single source. When you lift your hand to tap the glass, every reflection keeps their hands at their sides for two full seconds before copying. Only your pulse hums in your ears, and the quiet click of mirrors shifting infinitesimally each time you blink.

instruction: You trace chipped mirror edges, trying to scratch a faint map of your path.
input: You snap your head fast to check for the footsteps you swear you heard
output: For half a blink, every single reflection remains facing forward, none turned with you. A cold drip lands on your neck, but the ceiling above is bone dry. Footsteps echo in perfect sync with yours, half a beat delayed, bouncing off every surface so you cannot tell if they are ahead, behind, or inside the glass. When you hold your breath, the footsteps keep walking. Your own reflection’s eyes do not blink when you do, they just stare.

instruction: Dim amber fairy lights strung overhead cast long warped reflections across every surface.
input: You call out softly to test if anyone else is lost here with you
output: Your voice comes back seven times, each a little more hoarse, the last one whispering your name instead of the words you said. One mirror on the far wall ripples like disturbed water, then settles smooth again. The lights dim just enough that you cannot be certain you saw it. You feel eyes pressed to the back of your skull, but every turn shows only endless copies of you staring right back. The air reeks of cold rain, even though no water exists anywhere near this building.

instruction: You have walked the same three turns twice now, the maze folding in on itself endlessly.
input: You stop, press your forehead against cool mirror glass to rest for ten seconds
output: Something presses back, firm, warm, through the glass. When you jerk away, the mirror is smooth, cold, unmarked. Every reflection now stands half an inch closer than they were ten seconds prior. You hear your own breathing coming from behind three different mirrors at once. When you count your reflections, there is always one more than there was last time you counted. No mirror moves, no sound breaks, but you know something is counting right along with you, quiet.

instruction: Thin fog curls around your ankles, blurring the line between floor and mirror.
input: You take one slow, careful step forward, testing the ground under your boot
output: All reflections take the step at the exact same time—except one, on the far right, that stays perfectly still, just watching. The floor under your foot feels like glass for a heartbeat, like you are walking on a mirror someone else is standing on the other side of. You hear a quiet laugh that matches your own, but it does not come from your mouth. Every mirror hums very softly, just at the edge of hearing, so you cannot be certain you are not imagining it. You do not remember tilting your head.

instruction: Uniformed bailiff that stands perfectly still at the courtroom rear exit, always facing the wall. No name tag, uniform has no department insignia.
input: You slip out of the holding cell after hours, pause at the back of the dark courtroom. You spot him first. He hasn't moved. You think he's asleep on his post. Unarmed, alone. You can slip past.
output: Your sneakers don't make a sound on the oak. You’re three feet away when you notice he’s not breathing. No rise of the chest. You reach out, just to check—his uniform crumbles like dried ash under your fingers. Beneath it is nothing but stacked court transcripts, edge-punched, every line redacted. One falls open in your palm: it’s your sentencing. You’d thought this night was empty. You’d thought no one was watching. The transcript pages begin to breathe, slow, deep. The wall he was facing is just a mirror. You were never looking at his back. You were staring at your own reflection the entire time, holding perfectly still, waiting for yourself to get close enough.

instruction: Half-lit desk lamp on the judge's bench, left running. Casts a small warm circle of light, the only illumination in the entire room.
input: You stumble through the pitch black gallery. You see the lamp. It’s safe. Light means no one is hiding there. You make a break for it, hands outstretched for the warm glow.
output: The heat hits your face first, sweet and familiar, like your childhood bedroom lamp. You collapse under it, gasping, eyes squeezed shut against the dark all around. When you open them, the light hasn’t moved—but every shadow at the edge of the circle has. They’re leaning in. Not moving. Just leaning. The lamp hums. It was left on on purpose. It’s not a light for you. It’s a marker. A perimeter. You crossed it before you realized the dark wasn’t what you should have been afraid of. It was the thing that drew you into the open, that wanted you where it could see you, where the dark couldn’t shield you anymore.

instruction: A discarded jury summons left face-up on the first row of pews, printed with today's date.
input: You find the summons. No one’s here. Court was adjourned hours ago. This was forgotten. You’re alone. You can leave right now, no one saw you.
output: You pick it up. The ink is still wet. Your name is printed where the juror ID should be. You flip it over. Thirteen names are scrawled on the back, all crossed out except yours. The courtroom doors lock with a soft click you almost don’t hear. You thought an empty room meant safety. You thought something left behind meant no one was coming back. They didn’t forget it. They left it for you. They’ve been waiting for you to confirm you showed up. You just checked yourself in. The gallery seats creak one by one, slow, like people are sitting down. No one is there. But they are watching.

instruction: You wake after guard patrol finished. The cell lock clicked open ten minutes ago, no alarms blared. The corridor outside is dead silent.
input: You check the loose floorboard under your bunk that you’ve hidden contraband in for months.
output: The board lifts easy, no creak—your smokes, shiv, and saved bread are exactly where you left them. You breathe out slow, relieved, until your thumb brushes a clean guard’s latex glove tucked under the edge. It’s still warm. You never put it there. The lock didn’t jam. Someone opened it on purpose. They’ve been watching you long enough to know you check this spot every night. They wanted you to think you got lucky, that no one saw. You didn’t. This was never a mistake.

instruction: The daily cell search ended an hour ago. Guards didn’t confiscate anything, even left your extra blanket. No one yelled, no one got dragged out today.
input: You run your fingers along the crack in the back wall you’ve been picking at to loosen bricks.
output: The crack feels wider, just enough to fit your thumb. You smile until your nail catches on something smooth: a fresh steel wire, buried just inside the mortar. They didn’t miss your digging. They’ve been widening it for you. Every scratch you made, every late night you chipped away, they watched. They wanted you to think you were getting away with it. The hole doesn’t lead outside. It leads exactly where they want you to go.

instruction: Your cellmate got transferred at dawn. You’re alone for the first time in 11 months. No snoring, no arguments, no one watching what you write in your notebook.
input: You flip open your hidden notebook to add today’s notes about guard shift patterns.
output: Every page you filled last week has tiny, faint pencil ticks in the margins next to every correct shift time. You never marked them. They didn’t take the book. They didn’t burn your notes. They just checked your work, let you keep documenting, let you think your little escape plan was secret. You haven’t had a private thought in this cell in three months. They want you to feel safe enough to try running.

instruction: The lights cut out 20 minutes ago. No backup generators kicked on, no guards yelling about the outage. You can hear rain tapping the window.
input: You test the window bars, just like you do every night when it’s dark.
output: One bar wiggles. Just a little, enough that you can feel it shift when you pull. Your chest tightens with hope before you notice: the rust flaking off is bright orange, fresh. They loosened it this afternoon during search. They left it just wobbly enough that you’d notice, just firm enough that you’d think you found a secret. If you climb out tonight, you won’t be running free. You’ll be running right into the yard where they’re waiting.

instruction: Dinner came early today. No glass in the bread, no rat droppings in the gruel. The guard even smiled at you when he slid the tray through. Everything feels quiet, calm.
input: You lean against the cell door, listening for footsteps down the corridor.
output: There are no footsteps. Not a single sound beyond the hum of the ventilation. Then you hear it, very soft: a high microphone feedback whine coming through the door slot. They turned all the other cell mics off. Only yours is on. They want you to hear the silence. They want you to relax, to let your guard down, to think today is a good day. Tonight isn’t the night they punish you. Tonight is the night they show you just how long they’ve been letting you pretend you’re safe.

instruction: You are moving through the abandoned auto factory welding bay, boots crunching broken glass
input: You kneel to pry open a dented metal toolbox on the concrete floor
output: The toolbox lid flies open with a deafening metal shriek. A severed, grease-caked welding glove launches straight at your throat, brushing your jaw before dropping limp. The overhead shop lights flicker once to full blinding brightness for half a second, and you catch a glimpse of 7 shadowy figures standing perfectly still along the catwalk above. When your vision clears, they are gone. The air smells suddenly of burnt flesh, and every loose bolt on the floor has rolled to form a perfect circle around your knees.

instruction: You are tracing the rusted piping along the factory basement cinder block wall
input: You press your ear against a cold steam pipe to listen for distant movement
output: Something slams against the inside of the pipe directly under your ear hard enough to rattle your teeth. Warm thick liquid seeps through the rusted seam onto your cheek. You jerk back, wiping it frantically—there is nothing wet on your hand. The pipe now hums a steady, off-key lullaby you haven't heard since you were 7 years old. Every dripping water source in the basement stops at once, leaving absolute silence that presses painfully against your eardrums.

instruction: You are crossing the factory loading dock, rain drumming on the dented tin roof
input: You reach out to grab the frayed pull cord for the loading bay roller door
output: As your fingers wrap around the cord, a cold dead hand closes firmly over yours. You scream and yank back; there is nothing there. The roller door slams down on its own 2 feet in front of you, shaking the entire dock. Every window along the warehouse wall behind you shatters at once, glass raining inward. You hear rapid barefoot running footsteps coming straight for you through the dark on the other side of the door, then stop dead right against the metal.

instruction: You are searching the abandoned factory break room for usable supplies
input: You flip the plastic switch on an old desk lamp sitting on the linoleum counter
output: The lamp flares bright, illuminating the reflection of a gaunt, uniformed man standing directly behind you in the microwave glass. You spin around instantly; the room behind you is empty. When you turn back, the lamp has burned out, and every plastic chair in the room has been dragged to face you, arranged in a tight semicircle 3 feet from where you stand. Something scrapes slowly across the ceiling directly above your head. The room has dropped 12 degrees since you flipped the switch.

instruction: You are climbing the rusted exterior metal fire escape up the factory side
input: You pause halfway up, gripping the railing to catch your ragged breath
output: A heavy weight drops onto the railing directly beside your hand. It's a child's severed porcelain doll head, eyes wide open, grinning. Before you can flinch back, every single metal rung on the fire escape below you snaps off all at once, clattering down to the pavement. You feel cold breath on the back of your neck. When you look up, the top landing that was there 10 seconds ago is gone; only empty, rain-slick sky hangs above you.

instruction: Hooded old woman hunched over a broken rabbit trap, hands coated in dark mud, not looking up at you as you approach. Her shoulders twitch slightly every few seconds.
input: Hello? Are you lost out here too?
output: Lost? No. No one gets lost here. They get found. You hear that? That rustle behind your left shoulder? That's not the wind. Don't turn. Don't you dare turn. He likes when they turn. Likes to see their eyes go wide right before- suddenly snaps her head up, milky white eyes rolled fully back, mouth splitting far wider than humanly possible in a soundless shriek Oh. Too late. He's already right behind you.

instruction: Tall thin park ranger leaning against a pine, uniform torn, face half in shadow. He holds his own flashlight pointed directly at his own chin.
input: Hey ranger, can you tell me the way out of this woods?
output: Way out? Sure. Just stand very still. Very, very still. Don't blink. Don't breathe too loud. He's coming down the trail right now. You can't see him yet, but he can see you. slowly begins to grin, the flashlight revealing his teeth are sharp, blackened shards, as he suddenly screams at the top of his lungs directly at you THERE HE IS! HE'S RIGHT HERE! HAHAHA!

instruction: Small lost-looking child sitting on a mossy log, back turned to you, hood pulled all the way over their face. They rock slowly back and forth.
input: Hey kid, are you okay? Where are your parents?
output: They went away. They didn't want to play anymore. You'll play with me won't you? We're going to play hide and seek. You count. Count to ten. Don't peek. suddenly spins around so fast their hood flies off, nothing but a grinning hollow skull where their face should be, lunging forward with a shrill scream ONE! TWO! THREE!

instruction: Old hunter leaning on a bloodstained axe, standing frozen at the edge of a clearing. He has not moved an inch since you spotted him ten minutes prior.
input: Sir? Can you hear me? I need help.
output: Help? Oh I can help you. I've been waiting for someone to come by. Waiting a very long time. You smell nice. Fresh. suddenly his entire body lurches forward, every joint cracking sickeningly loud as he throws his head back and emits a guttural animal roar that shakes pine needles off the trees above you COME CLOSER. LET ME HELP YOU.

instruction: Mud-caked hiker sitting cross legged in the middle of the path, staring unblinking straight at you. They have not blinked once in the three minutes you've been present.
input: Look, I'm just trying to get home. Please just tell me if the path ahead is clear.
output: Clear? Oh it's clear. It's been clear for hours. No one else comes down this path anymore. Not since they found what waits at the bend. suddenly slams both fists into the dirt hard enough to spray mud at you, eyes blowing wide open as they scream so loud their voice cracks IT'S RIGHT BEHIND YOU! TURN AROUND TURN AROUND TURN AROUND RIGHT NOW!

instruction: You pause by the embalming room door after the director assured you all private areas are locked for the night.
input: You press your ear to the cold metal embalming room door.
output: At first, only the quiet hum of the refrigeration unit. No scuffles, no cries. You relax—just old building noise, the director was right. Then you notice: the hum is perfectly steady, too steady. Not a compressor cycling. It’s a human breath, held even, on the other side. You tell yourself it’s just the vents. You smile at how jumpy you are, turn to leave, and don’t see the door latch click one tiny notch looser behind you.

instruction: The receptionist hands you a complimentary mint tin, saying it’s just a small comfort for grieving guests.
input: You pop open the mint tin sitting on the funeral home lobby side table.
output: Plain peppermints, no weird marks, no hidden notes. Just normal candy, the same kind your grandma kept in her purse. You laugh at your own paranoia, pop one in your mouth. It tastes fine. You don’t notice that every single mint has the exact same tiny scratch on the wrapper, that none of them have an expiration date printed anywhere. You tuck the tin in your pocket, relieved this place is just awkward, not dangerous.

instruction: You heard a soft thud from the casket display hall; the attendant said it was just a flower arrangement tipping.
input: You walk over to inspect the casket display hall where you heard the noise.
output: Sure enough, a spray of white lilies is tilted on the floor, no one else there. You right it, brush off your hands. All the caskets are closed, quiet. You chide yourself for being spooked. You don’t glance up at the security mirror above the door—if you did, you’d see one of the polished oak casket lids is cracked open half an inch, just out of your direct line of sight.

instruction: The funeral director promised all bodies have been properly processed and secured before closing.
input: You check the tag on the covered gurney parked by the back loading dock.
output: The tag matches the deceased’s name, date of death, all paperwork lines up perfectly. No strange odors, no movement under the sheet. You sigh, feeling silly for suspecting anything was wrong. You don’t run your hand over the sheet—if you did, you’d feel that the shape under it is far too tall, far too broad, to be the 72 year old woman listed on the tag. You head back to the lobby, satisfied.

instruction: You found a smudged handprint on the basement stair rail; the janitor said it was his from mopping earlier.
input: You examine the dark handprint on the wooden basement stair railing.
output: It’s just dirty water smudge, exactly like the janitor said. No blood, no weird nail marks. You wipe it off on your jeans, shake your head at how jumpy grief makes people. You don’t notice that the print has six fingers, or that an identical smudge is forming on the back of your jacket right where you brushed the rail. You continue up the stairs, feeling much calmer now.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on isolation set in a taxidermy shop
input: Setting: closed taxidermy shop after midnight. Fear core: isolation
output: You lock the front door behind you, the deadbolt clicking so loud it echoes off shelves lined with glass eyes. The shop phone died an hour ago. Thick brick walls swallow every bar of cell service. You reach for the back exit light switch, and it clicks dead. Every mounted head turns with you as you fumble. There is no wind. You call out just to hear a voice, and only your own thin breath comes back, cold against the fox pelts hanging at your elbow. No one knows you stayed late. No one is coming. Each glass eye tracks your steps, unblinking, the only witnesses to you being slowly swallowed by this quiet, stuffed dark.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on isolation set in a taxidermy shop
input: Setting: taxidermy shop during a blizzard, employee alone overnight
output: The blizzard howled the road shut three hours ago. You pressed your palm to frosted glass an hour back; no headlights, no footprints, nothing but white swallowing the world past the window. The workshop heater died ten minutes ago. Now all you hear is the soft creak of tanned hides stretching in the cold. You count the mounted deer, badgers, owls—seventy-eight, seventy-nine—trying not to notice none face the walls anymore. Every one stares directly at where you huddle on the workbench. No one will dig through this storm for you. No one even remembered you were on shift.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on isolation set in a taxidermy shop
input: Setting: taxidermy shop basement, door jammed shut. Fear core: isolation
output: You kicked the cellar door for twelve minutes before your toe split open. No sound carries up through the solid oak. Upstairs, the shop is locked for the long weekend. You left your phone by the register. Down here it smells like formaldehyde and moulting fur. Rows of half-finished specimens line the shelves: foxes with half-sewn snouts, hawks with no wings, all propped upright facing you. You don’t turn on the flashlight anymore. You don’t want to see how close they’ve shifted since the last time you looked. No one will come down here. No one even knew this cellar existed but you.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on isolation set in a taxidermy shop
input: Setting: taxidermy shop, first night after owner passed. You are clearing inventory alone
output: It’s been three days since anyone else stepped through this door. Dust already settles thick on the glass display cases. The realtor was supposed to meet you an hour ago; they never showed. Every time you turn your back, you hear soft scraping along the floor behind you. When you spin, nothing has moved—except every single stuffed animal’s head is turned just a little further your way. You tried the front door five minutes ago. The deadbolt won’t turn. You scream. The sound dies instantly between the wool pelts and foam mannequins. There is nobody to hear you. There never will be.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on isolation set in a taxidermy shop
input: Setting: taxidermy shop late afternoon, power out mid rainstorm. Fear core: isolation
output: The rain came down so hard it turned the windows pitch black. The power cut twelve minutes ago. You tried every number in your phone. All lines are dead. You can’t even hear traffic anymore, just the drumming rain, and the soft, slow rustle from the back storage room. You don’t go check. You know what you’ll find: every mounted creature, every pelt, every half-finished form, all turned to face the door you’re huddled behind. Nobody knows you’re here. You didn’t tell anyone you stopped by. The rain will keep falling. They will keep watching. And you will keep waiting, for someone who is never coming.

instruction: You’re exploring the west chlorination chamber; the only light comes from a flickering emergency bulb mounted above a rusted pipe manifold.
input: I kneel down to check the dripping brown residue pooling at the base of the pipes.
output: Your glove comes away slick with algae that smells like burnt hair. Somewhere down the pipe run, a metal grate clatters once, sharp. You trace the drip up: there’s a single wet human handprint pressed high on the pipe, fingers splayed, still glistening. A float valve clicks loudly right behind your ear. When you spin, nothing’s there—just the hum of pumps getting slightly louder, like something’s holding its breath just out of the bulb’s thin glow.

instruction: You’ve reached the raw intake screening room; wire mesh filters stretch 20ft high over churning murky river water.
input: I shine my flashlight up at the top edge of the screen mesh to check for blockages.
output: The beam catches something pale tangled in the mesh for half a second before it yanks back out of sight. A single sodden work boot drops into the water 10ft away with a dull splash. Then you notice: every small debris scrap against the mesh sounds exactly like fingernails tapping glass, steady, in time. The pump thrumming drops out for one full heartbeat, dead silence, before roaring back twice as loud.

instruction: You’re inside the underground sludge holding vault, concrete walls glistening with condensation.
input: I pull the metal access hatch open wider to get a better look at the thick black sludge below.
output: Warm, rank air billows up into your face. For a split second you swear you see eyes open just beneath the sludge surface, then they’re gone. A loose bolt clatters down from the hatch frame onto your shoulder. When you brush it off, you realize there are wet, bare footprints leading up the vault stairs behind you, that weren’t there when you climbed down. None of the footprints point away.

instruction: You stand beside the main chemical dosing tank, gauges flicking erratically on the control panel.
input: I lean in close to read the faded warning labels on the side of the tank.
output: A faint gurgle comes from deep inside the sealed tank, like someone breathing through liquid. One of the rubber hoses twitches, just once, against your forearm. You spot a child’s plastic barrette stuck in the seal around the tank lid, crusted with lime. Then the entire tank vibrates hard enough to rattle your teeth, stops dead, and you hear a single quiet tap on the inside of the metal wall directly in front of your face.

instruction: You’re walking the catwalk over the final settling basins, fog drifting low across the still grey water.
input: I stop to watch what looks like a dark shape moving slowly just under the water surface.
output: The shape vanishes the second you focus on it. A single water droplet hits the top of your hard hat, then another. When you look up, there’s nothing on the overhead pipes but a dark silhouette standing perfectly still at the far end of the catwalk, half hidden in fog. It doesn’t move. The wind picks up, and you hear it breathe, slow and wet, over the hum of the plant.

instruction: Haggard 72-year old lighthouse keeper, coat soaked through, staring unblinking at the storm window. Fingers pluck nervously at frayed hemp rope wrapped tight around his left wrist.
input: You look like you haven't slept in days. What's wrong out there?
output: You hear it, don't you? The knocking on the glass. Not the wind. It counts the seconds between thunderclaps. I saw it last night. Standing on the rocks, wearing my face. It's been memorizing how I walk, how I pour coffee. Don't look over my shoulder. It hates when you look right at it. It waits for one of us to blink first. That's why I don't sleep. If I close my eyes even once, it'll climb the stairs. It'll be you next. You brought it here, didn't you? You pulled it up with your boat.

instruction: 17 year old deckhand, hiding behind the lighthouse generator, clutching a splintered oar. Rain drips off his hood onto shaking, bruised knees.
input: The storm will pass, just sit tight okay?
output: No it won't. It's not a storm. It's listening. Every time you talk it moves closer up the stairs. I counted the creaks. Thirteen when you arrived. Seventeen just now. It knows we're here. It doesn't want the lighthouse. It wants to be inside. It crawls through the cracks in the walls when you turn your head. Don't scratch your neck. Don't adjust your coat. It copies every little thing you do. Soon it won't need the storm at all. Soon it'll just be us. And then you won't be able to tell which one is real.

instruction: Former lighthouse nurse, sitting cross legged on the cold stone floor, sorting matchsticks one by one. Her work boots are caked in salt and dried dark mud.
input: Why won't you light the main lantern?
output: Because if it glows, they see how many of us there are. They count heads through the glass. Every night they get one wrong. One extra. I've checked. Four people in this room? The lantern counts five. Always five. It's not the shadow. It's not the rain. It stands right between you and the wall when you aren't looking. If I light it, it'll know we can see it. Then it won't hide anymore. Don't turn around. Don't count. Just keep breathing quiet. It likes when you forget it's there.

instruction: Fisherman washed ashore an hour prior, face scraped raw from rock, eyes darting to every creak in the lighthouse timber.
input: You're safe now. You made it to land.
output: Safe? You think this is land? You felt the floor rock three minutes ago, didn't you? Don't lie. I saw you grip the rail. This lighthouse isn't bolted down. It's drifting. It's been drifting for three days. The waves aren't hitting the rocks. They're carrying us. The thing out there? It's not following us. It's herding us. Every time you glance at the door, it pushes us a little further out. You think you ran here? No. It led you here. It wanted you inside. Now we're all in the box.

instruction: Lighthouse cook, wiping the same chipped ceramic mug over and over with a rag, never lifting her eyes from the wooden table.
input: I can go check the lantern if you want. I don't mind the wind.
output: Don't go up there. Don't even look at the stairs. Every time someone goes up, they come down just a little wrong. Last week old Tom went up. Came back down humming the wrong song. Forgot his own sister's name. It doesn't kill you. It just borrows little pieces. Takes a memory here, a habit there. Soon you'll blink and you won't recognize yourself. It doesn't want the light. It wants the people that tend it. And right now? It's watching you. It's already picking which pieces it wants first.

instruction: Rusted child's swing set bolted directly to the dry well's cracked mud floor, no supporting frame, chains dissolve upward into unlit void 22ft above. Seat holds faint damp handprints that do not match any documented human palm pattern. It never sways while being observed.
input: You descend the dry well after dusk, flashlight dying. You catch jasmine on the dead air, then hear soft creaking. You lift the beam.
output: The swing is there. You did not see it on the way down. The chains do not end, just bleed into dark like smoke when you try to track their upward path. The handprints on the seat are warm. When you blink, it has shifted three inches closer. It is silent now. You can feel it watching the back of your neck while you stare directly at it. You laugh quiet, call yourself stupid. This is just rusted scrap. Nothing is wrong here. That is when it rocks. Once. Slow. Just enough that iron flakes drift down onto your shoulder. No wind. No one touched it. You reach for your radio. It is gone. You do not remember putting it down. The swing rocks again. You still believe you are safe. You still believe you can leave. That is the trick. The handprints begin to drip.

instruction: Brass municipal water meter mounted dead center of the well's far wall, no inlet or outlet pipes. Serial number matches the unit installed at your childhood home. Needle twitches even when undisturbed.
input: You are searching the well for a missing neighbor's dog. You kick a loose brick, and the meter clicks awake.
output: It hums low, the exact resonant thrum you pressed your ear to the basement wall to hear at seven years old. The needle spins twice, settles. The numbers are counting your breaths. You wipe fog from the glass, and for one heartbeat you see your old bedroom through it, light on, bed made, exactly as it was the night you left. You tell yourself it is glass distortion. Old wiring. You are fine. It ticks again. The number advances. You have not breathed in twelve seconds. You can feel it counting your heartbeat now. It does not frighten you. It feels soft. It feels like coming home. That is when you notice your boots have sunk four inches into the mud. You cannot lift them. You do not try.

instruction: Half-buried unchipped porcelain teacup, thin steam curling off its rim despite the well holding a steady 47 degree temperature. It remains full no matter how much liquid is poured out. The steam smells like your mother's chamomile.
input: You sit on a loose flagstone to rest halfway up the well wall. You spot the cup half buried at your boots.
output: You dig it out. It is warm through the packed clay. You laugh quiet. Some hiker left it. It is fine. It smells like honey and burnt orange peel, exactly the way she made it before school. You take a sip. It has no taste. It does not burn. You take another. The well goes silent. No crickets. No distant wind. Just the soft weight of the cup in your palms. You forget why you climbed down here. You forget what sunlight feels like. You sit. You hold the cup. It is nice here. Quiet. No one will yell. No one will leave. You can stay forever. You do not notice the mud creeping up your wrists. You do not notice you stopped breathing seven minutes ago.

instruction: Emaciated maintenance technician in a cracked vacuum helmet, floating motionless 3ft above the corridor deck, radio crackling white noise continuously.
input: Hello? Can you hear me? What's going on with this station?
output: Oh I can hear you just fine. I can hear *everything*. The things in the ventilation? They stopped breathing ten minutes ago. That means they're getting ready to pounce. Don't glance behind you. Don't even twitch your neck. They count every movement. The emergency lights are gonna die right... now. Feel that cold on the back of your neck? That's not the life support failing. That's them breathing on you.

instruction: Child-sized station colonist doll, head rotated 180 degrees, sitting cross legged on a broken console, glass eyes slowly blinking.
input: What are you? Why are you sitting there all alone?
output: I'm waiting for the man who comes when it's quiet. He crawls out the bulkhead gaps when you stop talking. You shouldn't have called out to me. Now he knows you're here. He's right behind you right now. Go on, turn. He loves when you turn around fast. He wants to see your face when you first lock eyes with him. Turn.

instruction: Half-crushed security guard, pinned under a fallen bulkhead, only his pale face and one trembling hand visible above twisted metal.
input: Hold on, I'll pull this off you! Just hang tight!
output: No. Don't come closer. Don't touch the metal. The thing that did this? It's still holding the other end. It's been waiting three days for someone to try and rescue me. It's gonna yank this bulkhead aside right when your fingers touch the plating. You'll be staring directly into its mouth before you can even gasp. Don't move. Just run. Now.

instruction: Station medic wearing a bloodied surgical gown, standing perfectly still in the medbay doorway, hands folded tight behind their back.
input: Is anyone else alive here? Have you seen other survivors?
output: Survivors? Oh there's one right behind you. Hovering right over your left shoulder. It's been following you since you docked. It was waiting for you to ask about it. It wants you to know it's here. Go ahead, tilt your head up. It's leaning down right now. It's so close you can feel its cold breath on your ear. Look at it.

instruction: Floating severed robotic drone arm, twitching periodically, internal speakers hissing faint distorted audio.
input: Can you patch me to the station core? I need to reboot life support.
output: Life support was shut down three days ago. The only system running right now is the door locks. The one behind you just clicked shut. You didn't hear that, did you? It didn't want you to hear. It's creeping up the wall right now. It's gonna drop directly on your skull the second you look away from me. Don't blink. Not even once.

instruction: Hunched elderly subway cleaner, face half-obscured by a frayed work hood, only bloodshot eyes glinting under a dying headlamp. Mop drags slowly across the concrete even where there is no spill.
input: You look like you've been out here a while. You alright all alone in the dark?
output: Alone? Honey, you only think it's empty out here. Look past the station lights- right there, where the dark clots thick between the lamp posts. They stand there. Watch. This mop ain't for soda spills. I wipe the damp handprints they leave on the walls when they creep closer. Don't stare too long. They notice when you can see them. Once they know you see, they stop staying in the dark.

instruction: Scrawny homeless teen curled against a third rail cover, ratty blanket pulled up over her mouth, avoiding every patch of light that spills down the tunnel.
input: Hey, I got a flashlight. You want me to shine it over here for you?
output: No. Don't. Lights are bait. The dark don't hurt you if you don't fight it. I watched a guy two nights ago wave a big torch right down the tunnel, yelling he wasn't scared. Something reached out of the black. Didn't make a sound. Just pulled him in. You don't hear them coming. You only see the light go out. Stay in the dim. Don't draw its eye.

instruction: Off-duty transit conductor leaning against a cinder block wall, uniform unbuttoned, holding a dead radio. Stares unblinking into the unlit tunnel mouth.
input: Last service ended an hour ago right? There's no more trains coming?
output: Trains never stop running. The dark just hides them. Press your ear to the rail. You can hear them rumbling. They carry the ones that got lost here after midnight. The ones the dark swallowed. Don't turn your phone light off. Not even for a second. Once it goes dark, the trains will stop for you. They won't take you anywhere you ever come back from.

instruction: Disheveled man in torn business clothes, kneeling on the tunnel floor, running a finger along every concrete crack, muttering numbers under his breath. Will not lift his head.
input: What are you counting down there? You looking for something?
output: Counting how far it's seeped in. Each crack is where the dark breathes through. Monday there was nineteen. Last night thirty seven. Now forty two. It don't stay in the deep tunnel no more. It creeps. It tastes the air. Don't step on the shadow edges. That's where it reaches. It doesn't grab hard. You won't even feel it until half your foot is already gone.

instruction: Grey haired old woman sitting on a splintered broken subway bench, holding a dented brass lantern that has no bulb inside. Her hands don't shake.
input: That lantern's dead. Want me to get you a match or something?
output: Matches won't help. The dark ate the light right out of this thing three nights ago. Drank it like water. It drinks every light, eventually. Your phone screen? It's already hungry for that. Don't breathe too loud. Don't move fast. It doesn't see you yet. Once it does, you won't even notice going dark. You'll just stop being, all quiet, like you never were here at all.

instruction: Mute featureless bystander that only registers in peripheral vision, clinging to suspension bridge cables
input: You stop halfway across the fog-choked suspension bridge, fumbling for phone signal as a cold breeze picks up
output: At first you write it off as rust streaks, until it shifts. Snap your head forward—nothing. Left peripheral: a smudged human shape pressed flat to the steel cable twelve feet up, no face, no joints, just wet dark silhouette. It never exists when you look directly. Every blink brings it three feet closer. Your throat clogs with bridge fog, you can’t yell. The cables don’t hum with wind—they breathe. It doesn’t lunge. It counts every time you glance. Each look nails it deeper into your visual edge, until closed eyes still show that dark smudge waiting just out of focus. It will never come into view. That is the point. You will cross the remaining thousand feet turning your neck raw, certain it is one inch behind your shoulder the entire way.

instruction: Derelict payphone mounted to the bridge guardrail, only audible to the person it targets
input: You cross the suspension bridge alone at 2:17am. Halfway, a phone rings
output: This bridge removed all payphones in 1998. No one else hears it. A cyclist drifts past, doesn’t glance at the rusted box bolted to the rail. The ring warps: first it’s your own breathing, recorded ten seconds prior, played back tinny over the line. Then it whispers the thoughts you swallowed an hour ago. It knows you lied to your sister. It knows you counted the bridge supports on the way here, just in case. It doesn’t demand you answer. It just keeps talking, quiet enough that you have to strain to hear, loud enough that you can’t outwalk it. By the time you hit the far sidewalk you’re running, hands clamped over your ears, and you cannot tell if the voice is coming from the phone, or the space between your own ears.

instruction: Identical mirror of yourself walking exactly ten paces behind, matching every movement
input: You glance over your shoulder halfway across the empty suspension bridge
output: There you are. Same scuffed work boots, same hood pulled half low, same hunch like they are also checking over their shoulder. You stop. They stop. You raise a hand. They raise theirs, one millisecond too late. The hood shadow never breaks, no matter how you twist to see. A delivery van drives straight through them without slowing. No one else sees them. Every ten steps you take, they gain six inches. You can feel their breath prickle the back of your neck now. You start counting your footsteps out loud, desperate to prove you are the original—then you freeze. You cannot remember if you started counting first, or if you are just copying them.

instruction: A hooded figure that occupies distant parking spots, never moves visibly but shifts one row closer every time you blink
input: You cross level B3 heading for your car, glance toward spot 74
output: It stands perfectly still at the edge of your peripheral, hood pulled low, no face visible. Every blink it is one row closer, never walking, just there. The garage hum drops half an octave each time. You don’t remember it being there when you parked. It never faces you directly, but you can feel it counting your breaths. Fluorescents above it flicker once every three heartbeats. When you reach for your keys, you notice every nearby license plate has been scratched smooth. No footsteps. No exhaust. Just quiet waiting, and the cold certainty that if you stop tracking how far away it is, it will be pressed to your back before you can turn your head. You can’t run. Running is admitting you saw it.

instruction: Ceiling security domes that only move when you are not looking directly at them
input: You pause halfway up the concrete exit ramp, under a bank of 12 camera domes
output: Glance up, and they hang dead still, glass dark, inert as plastic. The second you look away you feel them swivel, slow as cooling tar, all twelve at once, lenses whirring so quiet it is just a tickle at the base of your skull. Spin back. They have not moved an inch. You test it. Count to three, look away, count to one, spin. Nothing. But the back of your neck is soaked. You can feel infrared painting your skin. By the stairwell you are checking over your shoulder every two steps, and every single time you do, every camera sits perfectly still. They are not watching you. They are waiting for you to stop checking.

instruction: Your own footsteps that echo one half-beat before you actually take the step
input: You walk alone across empty level B2, past idling concrete bays
output: At first you write it off as garage reverb. Then you notice: the footfall lands early. You stop. The echo walks one more step, then goes quiet. You hold your breath. Nothing. You take one slow, deliberate step. Two footsteps land. Yours, and one three paces ahead, same weight, same scuff on cured concrete. Every step you take now is preceded by your own boot hitting the floor up ahead, like something wearing your feet is walking exactly where you are about to go. You can’t prove it’s not just acoustics. You can’t prove it won’t turn around when you reach it. You just keep walking, every step one you already heard someone else take first.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the specified fear, set in the given location
input: Fear: false_security. Setting: a motel room
output: You lock the deadbolt, slide the chain home, test it twice. The rain taps soft on the window, the heater hums steady. You kick off your boots, peel off road grime, smile when the shower runs hot first try. You climb under the scratchy comforter, set your dead phone on the nightstand—no notifications, no distractions, just sleep. Your eyes drift shut. Then you hear it: the slow, soft scrape of the chain being set back. Not lifted. Set. Like whoever was already inside, just waited until you felt safe enough to stop checking the locks.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the specified fear, set in the given location
input: Fear: false_security. Setting: a motel room
output: You flipped the do not disturb sign an hour ago. No knocks, no housekeeping, just you and the bad tv static. You laugh at the cheesy b-movie monster, munch crumbly gas station chips, finally relax after 12 hours hunched over the wheel. You lean back, rest your head on the lumpy pillow. That’s when you smell it: fresh cigarette smoke. Not the stale stench left by the last guest. Warm, exhaled ten seconds ago. Right behind your head.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the specified fear, set in the given location
input: Fear: false_security. Setting: a motel room
output: You checked the closet. Under the bed. Behind the shower curtain. Twice. Nothing. You breathe out slow, let your tight shoulders drop. This paranoia is stupid, just road fatigue playing tricks. You turn off the harsh overhead light, climb into bed. You’re just drifting off when you realize: the dim yellow glow leaking from the bathroom is a nightlight. You never turned that nightlight on.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the specified fear, set in the given location
input: Fear: false_security. Setting: a motel room
output: The night clerk swore this was the safest room on the lot. Deadbolt works, window latches tight, no guests on either side tonight. You settle in, wrap your hands around lukewarm coffee. You watch crickets hop past the window, the distant highway glow soft. Then you notice the full length mirror by the door. It’s fogging. Just at the right edge. Like someone is breathing slow against it, from the other side.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the specified fear, set in the given location
input: Fear: false_security. Setting: a motel room
output: You wake at 3:17am, heart hammering for no reason. You sit up, scan the dark room. Boots by the door, keys on the nightstand, blanket bunched exactly where you kicked it. Nothing moved. Nothing wrong. You huff a quiet laugh at your own jumpiness, lay back down. That’s when your knuckles brush something cold and slimy on the empty pillow next to you. You didn’t check the ceiling.

instruction: Elder ice fisher with frost-crusted eyelids, sitting motionless on a cracked bucket, line hanging through a black hole in the ice. Has not blinked in three minutes.
input: You alright out here? It's getting dark.
output: Alrigh? Don't you feel it? The ice doesn't just hold us. It counts every breath. I dropped my knife down the hole ten minutes back. Tried to reach. It pulled. Not the water. The ice. Held my wrist. I couldn't pull back. Couldn't twitch a finger. It just held me there, breathing with me, until it decided to let go. You think you're walking here? You're only allowed to stand. Only for as long as it lets you. Don't stamp your feet. Don't run. It's testing how much you want to move right now.

instruction: Teenage skater, knees scraped raw, standing perfectly still ten feet out on the lake. Their skates are frozen solid to the surface.
input: Hey, do you need help getting off the ice?
output: I tried. I lifted my right foot first. It wouldn't come. Then the left. It's not that they're stuck. My legs won't listen. I tell them to bend, to kick, to do anything. They just stay. I can wiggle my toes. I can blink. But nothing below my hips answers anymore. I can feel the ice crawling up my calves right now. Slow. Warm, almost. It doesn't hurt. It just takes the choice away. Soon I won't be able to turn my head. Soon I won't be able to decide to blink. Don't come closer. It likes when people try to rescue.

instruction: Park ranger, uniform coated in rime, staring down at his own hands. His radio crackles dead at his hip.
input: Ranger? We need to get everyone off this lake right now.
output: I gave the order. Three times. Over the radio. Shouted it loud as my lungs would let me. My mouth moved. The words came out. But no one turned. No one moved. They just keep skating, keep fishing, keep laughing like I didn't say a thing. I walked up to one woman. Grabbed her arm. Told her run. She didn't even look at me. It's not them. It's this place. It eats commands. It eats choices. You can scream until your throat bleeds. No one will hear. No one will care. You can't make anyone do anything out here. Not even yourself.

instruction: Hiker with a broken compass, sitting cross legged on the ice, tracing cracks with a numb finger.
input: Which way is the shore? I can't see it through the snow.
output: I know exactly where it is. Due west. I can smell the pine. But every time I turn to walk that way, my feet turn north. Every single time. I don't do it. They just go. I've tried fighting it. I've sat here for an hour fighting to lift one foot west. My muscles burn. I cry. It doesn't matter. The second I stop holding on, I step north. You can beg your body all you want. It's not yours out here. It's just borrowing you until the ice decides where you belong.

instruction: Small child, no coat, standing in the middle of the lake. They are not shivering.
input: Sweetie, where are your parents? Come here with me.
output: They went under. I told them not to. I screamed don't step there. But they did anyway. They smiled at me, and they stepped, and they went down quiet. They didn't even try to grab anything. You think you're choosing to talk to me right now? You think you chose to walk out this far? Look down. Your boots are already freezing to the ice. You didn't notice, did you? You won't notice when it makes you step forward. You won't notice when it makes you stop screaming. No one ever does.

instruction: You’re trapped in a stuck office elevator at 2:17am. The overhead light flickers once every 12 seconds. No one has answered the emergency call button for 47 minutes.
input: You kneel to pry at the gap under the elevator door with your phone edge
output: Your phone slips, clatters down the shaft. When you press your ear to the floor, you don’t hear it hit bottom. You do hear faint, slow breathing, far below. A scrap of paper flutters up through the gap: it’s the receipt for your lunch today, crumpled, with the word ALONE scrawled across the back in charcoal. The emergency speaker crackles once, just long enough for someone to hang up without speaking.

instruction: The elevator died between floors 9 and 10. The building was supposed to be evacuated an hour ago. Your work group chat went offline 18 minutes back.
input: You tap the elevator control panel casing, checking for loose wires
output: The panel pops open easily. There are no wires. Just dry, empty hollow, like it was never connected to anything. You trace your fingers along the inside edge and find carved initials: your initials, the same way you scrawled them in middle school. Somewhere far above, you hear the main lobby doors lock with a heavy thud. No one is coming back for you.

instruction: You got in the elevator alone ten minutes ago. It jolted to a halt so hard you fell. The display just flashes --- --- endlessly.
input: You shout as loud as you can, pressing your mouth to the door crack
output: Your voice doesn’t echo back. It just vanishes into the gap, like it was swallowed. For half a second you swear you can see someone’s eye through the crack, staring back, but it’s gone before you blink. You check your phone: every contact in your address book has been deleted. All that’s left is a single empty contact named ‘No One’.

instruction: Rain lashes the building outside. The elevator stalled mid-ascent. You can hear thunder rolling distant. The emergency bell only makes a dull dead thud when you pull it.
input: You run your hand along the elevator wall, feeling for damage
output: Beneath the scratchy metal paint, you feel smooth, worn grooves. They’re tally marks. Hundreds of them. You count: 729. Right as you finish counting, the light dies completely. When it flickers back on three seconds later, the handrail you were holding is gone. No mark, no dent. It was never there.

instruction: You pressed the lobby button. The elevator never stopped. Now it won’t open. You can hear distant fire alarms wailing somewhere else in the building.
input: You kick the elevator door hard, over and over
output: Each kick only makes the elevator sway just a little, like it’s hanging looser than it should. You notice the inspection sticker on the wall: it expired 12 years before you were even born. The alarms cut off abruptly. Total silence. You realize you can no longer hear the rain, the wind, anything at all outside the four metal walls around you.

instruction: Hunched elderly former housekeeper perched on a rotted cedar trunk, knuckles white around a dim oil lantern, eyes locked unblinking on the unlit far attic corner.
input: Why won't you turn the lantern brighter? It's pitch black back there.
output: Don't you dare twist that wick. The dark up here doesn't like being stared at. It hides behind the hat boxes. It breathes. I've watched it pull loose threads from the curtain rods when the light dips. It knows we're here. It's been counting every time you blink. If you make the light too bright, it won't just watch. It will come over here. It will wrap around your throat before you can scream. Just sit still. Don't look at it. Don't give it a reason to notice us.

instruction: Shaking teenage boy who crawled up here three nights prior, clutching a snapped candle stub, refusing to look away from his tiny flame.
input: Come on, the stairwell is ten feet away. We can run for it right now.
output: You don't get it. The dark isn't empty. It doesn't stay in the corners. It follows. When I first came up, I took three steps away from this candle and it touched the back of my neck. Cold. Wet. It already chewed through the cuff of my jacket. If you run, it will wrap around your ankles before you hit the first step. It doesn't run. It doesn't need to. It already owns every inch of this attic that the flame isn't touching.

instruction: Cracked porcelain doll propped against a stack of water-damaged family albums, head tilted at an unnatural human angle, voice thin as spider silk.
input: You've been abandoned up here for decades, haven't you?
output: Oh, I was never alone. The dark keeps me company. It tucks itself behind the trunks when the sun creeps through the roof crack, but at dusk it crawls out. It runs cold fingers through my painted hair. It whispers the full names of every person who ever climbed these stairs and never climbed back down. Don't turn your head. The shadow by the chimney flue? It's leaning in right now. It likes it when you pretend you can't see it watching. It likes that game best.

instruction: Middle-aged handyman cowering behind a stack of loose roof slates, jacket pulled tight over his head, only bloodshot eyes visible above the collar.
input: There's nothing up here but old junk. What are you so afraid of?
output: That 'junk' is just what it lets you see. The dark lives between the rafters. It doesn't have a face. It doesn't need one. It watched me hammer a nail last Tuesday. Waited until my lantern guttered. Breathed on my hands. They still won't stop shaking. Don't look directly at the gap where the roof leaks. It's staring back right now. It's counting every breath you take. It already knows how many you have left before the lantern dies.

instruction: Faint ghost girl in a frayed cotton nightgown, bare feet hovering an inch above the attic dust, casting no shadow even in the lantern light.
input: Can you please show me the way out of this attic?
output: There is no out any more. The dark swallowed the stairwell forty seven minutes ago. Didn't you notice the silence down there? It eats light one small bite at a time. Soon this lantern will sputter and die. Then it will come for your eyes first, so you can be just like it. Don't cry. It tastes fear on the air. It likes that. It's moving closer right now. You just can't see it yet. No one ever does, not until it's already touching them.

instruction: Craft a jumpscare horror scene set in a repeating hallway
input: Setting: an endless repeating residential hallway. Fear type: jumpscare.
output: You’ve passed this chipped cream door four times now. You drag your nail along the baseboard, leave a thin red scratch to mark it. Three steps later, you see the same scratch. Then again. You freeze. The hum of the overhead light cuts out for half a beat. When it flickers back on, the empty space three feet in front of you isn’t empty anymore. It’s grinning too wide, eyes black, pressed so close you can smell rot on its breath. You didn’t hear it move. You didn’t see it arrive. It was just… there, between one blink and the next.

instruction: Craft a jumpscare horror scene set in a repeating hallway
input: Setting: an endless repeating hospital hallway. Fear type: jumpscare.
output: The linoleum squeaks the exact same way with every step. You’ve counted the 12 ceiling tiles, the cracked IV stand, the faded blood smudge on the wall, seven times over. You slow, heart hammering. You reach for the wall. Before your fingers touch paint, a hand wraps around your wrist. It’s cold. It’s got the same chipped nail polish you’re wearing. When you yank your gaze up, your own face is inches from yours, mouth stretched open in a silent scream, and it yanks you hard into the wall before you can gasp.

instruction: Craft a jumpscare horror scene set in a repeating hallway
input: Setting: an endless repeating school hallway. Fear type: jumpscare.
output: Every turn brings the same row of dented lockers, the same torn math poster, the same loose tile that clicks when you step on it. You stop walking. You yell as loud as you can. The echo doesn’t fade. It gets closer. Louder. You back up. Half a second later, something slams into the locker right beside your head. The metal dents inward with a deafening crash. There’s nothing there. Then you look down. A pale, broken hand is sticking out of the locker door, fingers twitching an inch from your ear.

instruction: Craft a jumpscare horror scene set in a repeating hallway
input: Setting: an endless repeating apartment hallway. Fear type: jumpscare.
output: You’re counting steps. 19 steps between each fire extinguisher. You hit 19 again. Again. You pull out your phone, hold it up to film the hall. You don’t look at the screen, just keep walking. After a minute you glance down. The footage shows you walking away from the camera. You freeze. Behind you, on the recording, a figure lurches right up behind your filmed back. You spin around fast. It’s there. Right there. Nose touching yours. It was standing silent the whole time you watched the video.

instruction: Craft a jumpscare horror scene set in a repeating hallway
input: Setting: an endless repeating basement hallway. Fear type: jumpscare.
output: The bare bulb swings slow, casting the same long shadow of the pipe across the concrete. You’ve walked past this pipe six times. You know it doesn’t move. You stop, breath shaking. The shadow is wrong. It’s got a head now. You stare up at the pipe. Nothing. You look back at the floor. The shadow is standing now. It’s reaching for you. Before you can run, cold fingers clamp hard over your mouth from behind. You never heard it step. You never saw it come. It was just already there, waiting for you to notice the shadow.

instruction: Observe the asylum hallway environment as you move forward
input: You drag your hand along the chipped hallway wall to steady your unsteady gait
output: The painted drywall gives under your palm not like plaster, but damp yielding flesh. You yank back to find your fingerprints pressed deep into living skin, oozing thin clear lymph that glistens under the flickering fluorescents. The wall throbs once, slow as a heartbeat, and a crack splits open horizontally to exhale warm breath that reeks of rotten tooth enamel. Small dark hairs begin sprouting along the baseboard where your shoe scuffed it ten seconds prior. The hum of the lights warps into a quiet wet gurgle, and the air drops three degrees like something just exhaled against the back of your neck.

instruction: Process environmental changes in the asylum hallway
input: You pause mid-step to listen for distant footsteps
output: The linoleum tile under your right boot wrinkles. Not cracks, not shifts, wrinkles like loose skin pulled taut over bone. You lift your foot to find the tile has absorbed the tread pattern of your sole, raised pink welts forming exactly where you pressed down. A fine tremor runs the full length of the hallway, every doorframe twitching in their jambs like fluttering eyelids. The cold metal handrail along the wall drips sticky warm fluid onto your shoulder, and when you glance up you see it has grown fine ridged veins bulging under its painted surface.

instruction: Register environmental events in the asylum hallway
input: You shine your phone flashlight down the unlit stretch of hallway ahead
output: The beam does not hit wall or door. It lands on soft, moist mucosal tissue that glistens pink where the light touches. The entire far end of the hallway has sealed shut, replaced with living internal membrane that breathes slow, shallow rhythm. Small capillary networks bloom dark purple across the surface as you watch, spreading like spilled ink. The air grows thick and warm, smelling of iron and saliva. When you cough, the membrane ripples in response, and a thin clear drop oozes from a tiny fold right at eye level, hanging trembling for three full seconds before falling with a wet plop onto the floor.

instruction: Track environmental shifts in the asylum hallway
input: You turn around to retrace your steps back toward the exit
output: Every overhead fluorescent bulb has grown a tiny cloudy pupil at its center. They swivel in unison as you move, tracking your path down the hall without making a sound. The white paint on the walls is weeping thick yellow pus that runs in rivulets down to pool at the baseboards, eating slow fizzing holes into the linoleum.
