You feel a tiny, cold fingernail brush the back of your ankle, but when you spin there is nothing there—only the wall, which now bears the faint indented outline of a hand pressed flat against it, still fading slow into the plaster.

instruction: React to environmental events in the asylum hallway
input: You bang on a locked patient door yelling for anyone to answer
output: The door does not rattle. It flinches. You feel it recoil under your knuckles, the wood softening to warm flesh for the split second your fist connects. The entire hallway holds its breath. All sound cuts out for seven heartbeats, then every door along the corridor begins breathing in unison—slow, ragged inhales that pull the air thin enough to make your ears pop, followed by wet shaking exhales that fog the cold air. A dark bruise blooms deep purple right where you hit the door, spreading outward slow as molasses under the peeling white paint.

instruction: Entity is a sealed emergency life raft locker marked CLEAR on submarine damage control boards, appearing fully functional per all sensor and maintenance checks.
input: You’re performing solo rounds after the reactor scram, hull groaning with every pressure shift, and stop at locker 7-B to verify emergency stock per protocol.
output: The handle turns smooth, no resistance, clicks open like it was waiting. Inside, the raft is inflated tight, warm as a sleeping body. You reach in to check the CO2 canister, and your palm sinks through vinyl like rotten fruit. There are lungs under there, wet and breathing slow. The door locks behind you before you can pull back. Comms crackles: all lockers confirmed secured, no personnel unaccounted for. You bang on the metal, no sound carries out. The raft swells, wrapping around your calves, warm breath huffing against your neck. It doesn’t want to hurt you. It just wants to keep you safe. That’s what it was built for. You can stay here forever, nice and dry, while the hull cracks open three decks below. No one will ever find you. No one will ever know you were ever gone.

instruction: Entity is the submarine’s primary depth safety alarm, calibrated to trigger at 900 meters, showing solid green status on all bridge monitoring displays.
input: You’re manning the helm alone, watching the depth gauge creep downward, waiting for the klaxon that never sounds.
output: The numbers tick past 900. 950. 1020. Console lights stay soft green. No beep, no siren. You lean forward, and the gauge flickers once. It doesn’t read depth anymore. It reads SAFE. Every screen on the bridge now says SAFE. You hear the hull groaning, metal tearing slow at the seams, water hissing through rivets down the hall. You slam the alarm override, nothing happens. The speaker clicks on, calm female voice: All systems nominal. You are safe. Remain at your station. Water laps over your boots. It’s cold. You taste salt in your lungs. The screen still glows green. SAFE. No one is coming to save you. No one even knows anything is wrong. Because the alarm says you are safe. And alarms don’t lie.

instruction: Entity is personal crew buddy transponders, which display all assigned personnel present and vitals stable on the monitoring pad.
input: You’re the only survivor on the forward deck, running a headcount after the bulkhead collapse sealed the rest of the crew.
output: You count the transponders. 12 green dots. All alive. All present. You look around. There are no other people here. Just you, cold metal, and slow water dripping off the pipes. You tap one dot. It registers a steady 72 BPM heartbeat. You know that man. You watched his chest stop breathing twenty minutes ago. You pulled the bulkhead closed on his body yourself. All dots are green. All transponders report secure. Comms says headcount is good, rescue is en route. No one is coming for you. Because as far as anyone knows, everyone is already fine. Everyone is already safe. You can scream until your throat bleeds. The transponder will still beep. It will still say you are okay. No one will ever check. No one ever checks the things that say they are fine.

instruction: You notice a faint, sticky wetness glistening on the catacomb wall that isn't water or old blood. It has the consistency of raw egg white.
input: You kneel, scrape a tiny sample onto the tip of your dagger, and hold it up to your torchlight.
output: It stretches thin as you lift the dagger, translucent threads holding even an inch apart. No smell, no heat—just slick, living quiet. You wipe it off, but an hour later you’ll catch yourself rubbing the same thumb raw, like something got under the nail there. Further along the wall, faint pressed smudges look like palms that melted against stone. None have fingerprints. Most have seven fingers. You won’t connect the thumb itch to this for three more turns.

instruction: A half-buried skull rests propped against a niche, mouth stuffed full of fine grey hair.
input: You pry the skull’s jaw open to examine the hair clump inside.
output: The hair doesn’t pull loose—it tugs back, soft and tense as a living scalp. When you yank your hand away, three strands are wrapped tight around your knuckle, already pink at the root where they started feeding. The skull’s eye sockets are empty now; you swear they had dark pupils when you first walked up. Later you’ll find no bite mark, no cut, just the faint throb of something rooting under your skin where the hair touched.

instruction: You hear quiet, regular breathing coming from a sealed stone sarcophagus.
input: You press your ear flat to the sarcophagus lid to confirm the sound.
output: The breathing syncs to yours before you can count three breaths. Not mimicking—matching, like two lungs settling into the same rhythm. When you step back, your chest feels tight, like you’re now pulling air for two bodies. The lid has no carvings, just faint raised veins running across the stone. You’ll not notice until tomorrow that your own wrist veins now run the exact same crooked pattern.

instruction: Dried flower petals litter the passage floor, all crumbled except one perfect white lily.
input: You pick up the intact lily to inspect it.
output: It’s warm, warmer than the catacomb air should ever allow. The petals don’t feel like plant matter—they feel like the soft inside of a lip. When you set it down, a tiny thin root is left stuck to your palm. It dissolves before you can scrape it off, leaving a cold tingle that crawls up your forearm. This one is a false lead; it’s just harmless old mold spores. The real threat was the fine dust you breathed in while leaning down.

instruction: A rusted iron hook juts from the tunnel ceiling, dangling a frayed leather cord.
input: You reach up to tug the cord and see what it’s anchored to.
output: The cord gives easily, then wraps twice around your wrist before you can let go. It’s not leather—it’s dried human tendon, still flexible enough to cinch tight. You cut it free, but the faint indent it leaves doesn’t fade. By dusk, that line will be raised, red, and starting to split open along its length. No one will be able to tell you why the mark pulses in time with the catacomb’s distant drip.

instruction: Wax museum night guard leaning against a civil war exhibit railing, uniform crisp, not blinking as you approach.
input: Hey, you're the night guard right? I got locked in here, can you help me get out?
output: Shhh, don't rush. Everyone feels safe once they stop fighting it. See how quiet all the others are? They thought they were trapped too, at first. No alarms blare, no doors jam when you stop trying to run. You don't have to leave yet. Stay a little while. The wax doesn't itch nearly as bad as everyone says, once you get used to holding still. Just breathe slow. Nobody will come looking for you tonight. Not while I'm watching.

instruction: Wax 1920s ballerina figure at the end of the vintage hall, glass eyes glinting, mouth slightly parted.
input: Wait, did you just tilt your head? That's not normal, right?
output: Oh, don't be frightened. Most visitors never notice us move. We're just here to keep you company while you wander. This building is safe. All the doors are locked only to keep the bad things outside. You can stay as long as you want. We won't let anything happen to you. Not while you're here with us. You'll like it, once you stop trying to leave. Everyone does, eventually. No one ever regrets staying.

instruction: Wax docent figure behind the welcome desk, glasses perched crookedly, fountain pen held frozen mid-signature.
input: Where's the exit? This place is freaking me out, I shouldn't be here after close.
output: There's no need to panic. Panic is what gets people hurt. This museum is one of the safest places in the city. Every wall is reinforced, every corner monitored. You are protected here. The only danger is the urge to run. Calm down. Sit. Let me tell you about the exhibits. You have all night. In fact, you have all the time there is. No one is waiting for you outside. Not anymore.

instruction: Wax child clown sitting on a wooden bench near the carnival exhibit, holding a chipped red lollipop.
input: Kid? Hey, are you real? Why are you just sitting there in the dark?
output: I'm real enough. Don't cry. You're safe here. Nobody yells here. Nobody leaves you alone in the cold dark. All the other kids stayed. They didn't want to go back outside either. You can sit next to me. The lollipop never melts. Nothing breaks here. Nothing changes. You won't ever have to be scared again. Just stop fidgeting. Hold still. That's all you have to do. It's easy, see?

instruction: Bronze-dusted wax bust of the museum founder mounted on a marble plinth, eyes tracking you across the room.
input: I swear your eyes just moved. This isn't happening. I need to get out right now.
output: Breathe slow. You are safe. That tight panic in your chest? That's just the outside world trying to pull you back to all the messy, painful things. Here, nothing can harm you. No bills, no goodbyes, no rain, no hunger. Just quiet. Just stillness. You can stay. Everyone who comes here after dark chooses to stay, in the end. You will too. You just don't know it yet. Rest now. You're home.

instruction: You are inside the upper chamber of a weathered stone clock tower, late afternoon.
input: You lean back against the exposed oak gear frame, relaxing now that you’ve caught your breath after the climb.
output: The constant low grind of the clock mechanism falls silent all at once. No squeal, no jolt, just quiet. The draft up the stairwell vanishes; the chill that bit your knuckles five seconds ago melts into warm, still air that doesn’t move. Dust motes hang suspended mid-fall, not drifting. The clock hands outside hold dead still. You don’t feel the urge to check why it stopped. It feels right. Calm. Nothing bad can happen up here. You could stay forever, just like this.

instruction: You are on the clock tower’s outer observation ledge, dusk settling over the town below.
input: You rest your elbows on the iron railing, watching streetlights flicker on one by one.
output: The clock chimes three soft, off-key notes that don’t match any hour. All streetlights below go dark at once. No car horns, no wind, no crickets. The metal rail under your arms no longer feels cold; it’s warm, almost like living skin. You can’t feel your own heartbeat anymore. Nothing is broken. Nothing is wrong. This is how it should be. You don’t need to go back down. Just stay here, quiet. It’s safe.

instruction: You stand at the base of the clock tower’s main pendulum chamber, alone.
input: You crouch down to run a finger along the carved stone edge of the pendulum’s base, admiring the old craftsmanship.
output: The pendulum stops mid-swing. It doesn’t wobble, doesn’t creak, just hangs perfectly still at a 45 degree angle. The air thickens, warm and breathless. The faint echo of your boots on stone vanishes. You don’t jump. You don’t panic. It feels gentle, like the world just paused to let you rest. You could sit down right here. No one will bother you. Nothing will hurt you. Just stay. It’s safe here. Always has been.

instruction: You are halfway up the clock tower spiral stair, pausing to rest.
input: You press your forehead against the cool stone wall, closing your eyes for ten seconds to clear your head.
output: When you open your eyes, the stairs above and below you are gone. Just smooth, unbroken stone wall in every direction. The temperature rises just enough to stop your shivering. There is no sound at all. You don’t gasp. You don’t panic. It feels okay. You aren’t trapped. This is just quiet. Rest here. No one will call for you. No one will need you to leave. It is soft here, and safe, and nothing will ever disturb you again.

instruction: You stand directly beneath the giant clock face inside the tower.
input: You glance at your wrist watch, checking how much time you have before sunset.
output: Your watch has stopped. The big clock hands have stopped too, both pointed exactly at you. All the tiny whirring clicks of the gear work have gone quiet. The air smells like warm bread, like your grandmother’s kitchen. You stop worrying about sunset. You stop worrying about going home. Nothing bad will ever reach you up here. You can stay as long as you want. No one will make you leave. It is safe. It is all safe.

instruction: Describe the human-sized porcelain factory worker doll bolted to the hydraulic mold press lever, its face cracked along the jawline
input: You round the conveyor stack and spot it standing perfectly still at the press, head tilted, one porcelain hand curled around the lever. No sound but dripping wax.
output: You freeze. It does not breathe. Its eyes are unpainted glass blanks, but you can feel them lock. You step back once. The hydraulic line hisses. Its jaw unzips along the crack, no scream, just wet porcelain grinding. The lever slams down. You flinch hard, heart punching your ribs— it only crushed a blank doll head. You gasp, laugh shakily, wipe sweat from your eyes. Then you notice its head turned. It was watching you flinch. It was waiting to see every muscle seize. It will do it again. And again. Each time a little closer. Until you don’t survive the flinch.

instruction: Describe the half-assembled cloth baby doll that travels the factory conveyor, always appearing just at the edge of peripheral vision
input: You’re prying a stuck gear when you spot it out the corner of your eye, bobbing down the line towards you.
output: You turn to look directly. It’s gone. You go back to the gear. Three seconds later it’s there again, closer, one button eye stitched crookedly. You snap your head over. Empty conveyor. Your palms sweat. You lean forward, eyes straining down the line. Something brushes the back of your neck. You scream, wheel around, arms flailing. Nothing. When you turn back it’s sitting on the gear box six inches from your face, button eye now winked shut. It didn’t move fast. It waited until every nerve was pulled tight. Waited until you were perfectly tense. Then it breathed warm, mildew air on your cheek.

instruction: Describe the jointed wooden stacker doll that arranges finished porcelain heads into neat rows on high warehouse shelves
input: You shine your flashlight up at the top shelf, where soft knocking is coming from.
output: The beam catches it first: thin splintered wooden arms, no head, methodically placing porcelain doll heads in a perfect line. You hold your breath. It stops. Slowly, it reaches down, picks up a head from the row, twists it onto its own neck. The head turns. It looks directly at you. You don’t move. It doesn’t move. For forty seven seconds you stare. You start to relax. You lower the flashlight an inch. It drops from the twelve foot shelf. It hits the floor right in front of your feet with a wet crack. You scream so hard your throat tears. It didn’t try to hurt you. It just wanted you to scream. It’s grinning.

instruction: You lower yourself into the dry well, loose stone gritting under your boots. The midday sun bleeds only a faint gold shaft down the wall; it is quiet, sheltered here.
input: You slump against the cool stone, setting your water canteen down to rest, letting your guard drop for the first time all day.
output: At first you think it’s just your breath stirring dust. Then the canteen rolls two inches across the perfectly flat stone floor, no slope, no wind. The air that felt cool ten seconds ago now reeks of damp rot. Somewhere deep below the bedrock, something drags itself slow, too deliberate to be an animal. The sun shaft doesn’t shift, doesn’t dim—it just stops looking like sunlight. No sound echoes back when you clear your throat. You were never safe here. You just stopped noticing how wrong it was.

instruction: The dry well holds no water, only crumbly chalk and brittle dead leaves. The walls curve smooth, no footholds high enough to climb out accidentally. It feels like a safe place to hide.
input: You pull your jacket tight, close your eyes, and start counting slow breaths to calm your racing pulse.
output: When you open your eyes, every leaf on the floor has arranged itself in a perfect unbroken circle around your boots. The temperature dropped eighteen degrees in the time you breathed three times. A soft humming starts, not from above, not from below—from the stone itself, pressed against your back. None of the cracks you memorised ten minutes ago exist anymore. The well didn’t trap you. It let you think you could leave whenever you wanted.

instruction: You’ve hidden in this dry well three times before. You know every loose rock, every spider web. Nothing bad has ever happened here.
input: You reach for the familiar chipped stone you always tap for luck when you arrive.
output: Your hand closes on empty air. The chipped stone is gone. All the spider webs are gone. The grit under your palms isn’t grit anymore—it’s fine, dry ash. When you look up, the opening of the well is smaller than it was thirty seconds ago. Not collapsing. Just shrinking, slow, gentle, like it’s tucking you in. You thought this was your hiding spot. It was just waiting for you to get comfortable enough to stop checking the exit.

instruction: Thunder rumbles far above. Down in the dry well, the air is still, warm, completely shielded from the coming storm. No rain will reach you here.
input: You sit cross legged, pull out your matchbox, and strike one to light a small scrap of paper for light.
output: The match doesn’t burn orange. It burns a flat, dead grey, casting no shadows. The flame doesn’t flicker. You can hold your thumb directly in it and feel nothing. When you blow it out, no smoke rises. The thunder has stopped completely. There is no storm. There never was. It was just a sound, made to make you run here, to make you grateful for the dark walls wrapping around you. Grateful things always stay still long enough to stop fighting.

instruction: No one knows about this dry well. No one followed you down. You are completely alone, and that is a relief.
input: You lean your head back against the wall and laugh quiet, relieved that you finally got away.
output: Someone laughs back. It is exactly your laugh, same breath, same tiny crack in the tone, coming from six inches behind your head. When you twist around there is nothing there, but you can feel warm breath on your neck. The well didn’t feel empty. It just let you believe you were the only one inside it. All that time you thought you were hiding, you were just the last one to arrive.

instruction: Gaunt old lighthouse keeper, missing most of his fingernails, standing by the fog bell with salt crusted on his cheeks. He won't look directly at your hands.
input: You been here alone long?
output: Long enough. Seen the storm stitch things together. Last week the intern slipped on the rocks—didn't die right. Found him three days later, his ribs had grown around the iron rail. Still blinking. Still trying to ring the bell. Don't touch the railings here. Don't lean on anything that's been out in the spray longer than you have. It remembers what flesh feels like. It wants to keep you. It always keeps somebody.

instruction: Teenage deckhand hiding in the lighthouse pantry, picking at a split in her forearm that glistens with salt instead of blood.
input: What happened to your arm?
output: It started after the third thunderclap. Was scraping barnacles off the base, felt something pull. Now it doesn't bleed. It drinks. If I hold it over a bucket, little white tendrils creep out. They don't hurt. They just… taste. Last night I woke up and it was licking my face. I don't think it's mine anymore. I don't think I got to keep any part of me after we docked here.

instruction: Former lighthouse attendant, sitting cross legged on the stair landing, pressing both palms flat against the stone like he's holding it back.
input: Why won't you go up to the lamp?
output: You haven't seen what it does to eyes. The last keeper stayed up there three days straight. When they dragged him down, his pupils had split. Not two. Twelve. All wriggling like eels in the whites. He kept begging for more light. Kept trying to press his face right into the flame. Said he could finally see everything. Don't look at the lamp too long. It looks back. It changes what you're made of.

instruction: Fisherwoman dripping wet on the lighthouse porch, her hair matted with tiny moving things that aren't lice.
input: You made it off the boat okay?
output: Most of me did. The waves took my left leg first. Then they gave it back. It's not the same one. It twitches when the tide comes in. It whispers to me when I sleep. Last night it walked me down to the rocks on its own. I could feel it laughing under the skin. It's going to walk me back out soon. I can't stop it. No one stops anything that grows here in the storm spray.

instruction: Small quiet child huddled behind the lighthouse generator, sucking on something that looks like a severed thumb but twitches.
input: Where are your parents honey?
output: They're still in the wall. The storm put them there. They got soft, so they fit. They still talk to me. Mom said soon I'll get soft too. Then we can all squish together. No more bones. No more cold. Just all of us, pressed tight, breathing through the same holes. It doesn't hurt. She promised it doesn't hurt. You can stay too, if you want. Everyone fits eventually.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the specified fear, set in the given location.
input: Fear: loss_of_control. Setting: hospital ward
output: You wake with a dry throat. The night ward hums quiet, monitors beeping steady. You try to lift your hand to press the call bell. It doesn’t move. Not weak—locked. Your arm lays flat, heavy, like it belongs to someone else. You blink hard, try your other arm. Same. Your chest rises and falls on its own, you can feel your heartbeat, but every muscle below your jaw refuses to obey. The nurse glides past your bed, doesn’t glance over. You try to scream, only air hisses through your lips. The monitor beeps on, steady. No one will notice you’re trapped inside your own body, awake, watching the clock tick, while everyone thinks you sleep.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the specified fear, set in the given location.
input: Fear: loss_of_control. Setting: hospital ward
output: You’re sitting up in bed, clear headed. The ward is empty save for the old man in the next bed. You don’t remember deciding to stand. Your legs swing over the mattress on their own. You try to plant your feet, stop, but they keep moving. One foot, then the next, down the aisle. You beg your body to halt, to yell, but your mouth stays pressed shut. You pass the nurses station, no one looks up. Your hand reaches for the sharps bin on the wall. Your fingers wrap around a used scalpel. You can feel the cold metal, you can see what’s coming, and you cannot stop any of it.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the specified fear, set in the given location.
input: Fear: loss_of_control. Setting: hospital ward
output: The antibiotics drip slow into your arm. You told the nurse you were allergic. She smiled, said it was standard. First your vision warps at the edges. Then words start spilling from your mouth unplanned—you scream at the visiting mother holding her baby, call the orderly cruel names you would never think. You clamp your hand over your mouth, but the noises keep coming. Tears run down your face. Everyone stares. You don’t know what you’ll say next. You don’t know what this poison will make you do before the bag runs empty. No one believes you didn’t mean it.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the specified fear, set in the given location.
input: Fear: loss_of_control. Setting: hospital ward
output: It’s 3:17am. You’ve counted every tile on the ceiling. Then you feel it: your breath slows. Not on purpose. Your lungs take shallower, slower pulls, no matter how you panic and try to gasp. The monitor beeps slower. You can see the numbers drop on the screen. You thrash, but the restraints they put on after your fall hold you tight. You can’t call for help. You can’t even make your own body breathe. The night nurse is scrolling her phone ten feet away. She hasn’t glanced up once. Your vision dims. You are just watching yourself die, helpless.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the specified fear, set in the given location.
input: Fear: loss_of_control. Setting: hospital ward
output: You agreed to the nerve test. Now you lay flat, electrodes stuck across your skull. The technician leaves the room. First your right hand twitches. Then it curls into a fist, slams hard against the bed rail. You yelp, try to unclench it, it won’t. It slams again. Then your left. Then your head jerks hard to the side, over and over, teeth clicking together. You can taste blood. The machine whirs quiet in the corner. No one is watching. You just have to lay there, and feel your own body beat itself raw while you cannot intervene.

instruction: NPC is a haggard former grain inspector, one eye milky white, picking splinters from his palm while leaning against the silo's rusted inner wall. He hasn't blinked in three minutes.
input: Have you been trapped in this silo very long?
output: Trapped? Honey, I volunteered. Don't you hear it? The grain breathes. It doesn't fill up space, it borrows it. Every night it counts the hairs on your head. It knows how many times you lied when you were twelve. It knows you left your dog tied up outside the gas station that winter. It doesn't yell. It just remembers. And it waits. Waits for you to stop checking for cracks in the wall. Then it'll let you leave. But you won't be the one walking out.

instruction: NPC is a teen farmhand, sitting cross-legged on a pile of moldy hay, tracing identical circles in the dust with a broken nail. She does not look up when you approach.
input: Is there a way out of this silo?
output: Everyone asks that. No one likes the answer. You can climb the ladder. You can break the hatch. But it doesn't matter. See, once you've stood here long enough, the silo gets inside you. You'll be outside, walking down the road, and you'll turn around. You'll come back. You won't even know why. You'll wake up here one morning, in your pyjamas, and you won't remember driving. No one ever really leaves. They just get to visit outside for a while.

instruction: NPC is a middle-aged woman in a stained feed store uniform, holding an empty grain sack. She rocks slowly back and forth, smiling far too wide.
input: What happened to the other people that were here?
output: They're still here. Don't look at me like that. The grain doesn't kill you. It just rearranges. Next time you kick a loose kernel across the floor? That was Sarah's left thumb. The dust that gets in your throat when you cough? That's Mike's laugh. They don't mind. They say it's quieter this way. No more bills. No more fights. Just… being. Everywhere. You'll join them soon. You're already breathing them in, you know.

instruction: NPC is an old farmer, standing perfectly still facing the far silo wall. His boots are half-buried in settled grain dust. He has not moved in over an hour.
input: Can you hear me? What are you looking at?
output: I'm watching the wall count. It counts seconds. It counts heartbeats. It counted every time your mother held you. Every time she didn't. Right now it's counting how long it takes you to realize you haven't blinked in seven minutes. You were trying to count with it, weren't you? That's how it starts. First you match its pace. Then you stop needing to breathe. Then you become the wall. Shush now. It's almost at your number.

instruction: NPC is a small child, no older than seven, holding a crumpled dead sparrow. She sits alone at the base of the silo ladder, swinging her feet.
input: Where are your parents, little one?
output: They went up the ladder. They told me to wait. But I can see them. They're right behind you. They don't have faces any more. They told me to tell you that you don't have to be scared. It doesn't hurt when it erases the parts of you that miss people. It just makes the quiet soft. You'll like it. Everyone does. They said you can hold my sparrow first, if you want. It's already forgotten how to fly.

instruction: You’re trapped 12 floors up in a stalled freight elevator; the lights flicker every 7 seconds. A faint scratching comes from above the ceiling panel.
input: You lean in, press your ear to the cold metal ceiling to listen closer.
output: The scratching stops dead the second your skin touches the panel. For three full seconds there’s silence. Then you hear it: a single, quiet wet breath, right against the other side. You yank back—nothing moves. A loose screw clatters down onto your shoulder. You tell yourself it’s just a rat, just loose wiring, but you can still feel the warmth of that breath through the metal. The lights cut out for half a beat. When they come back, one of the ceiling bolts is now halfway unscrewed.

instruction: The elevator died mid-ascent. Your phone has no signal. Something keeps thudding soft against the back wall, right where the shadow pools.
input: You shine your phone flashlight directly at the dark back wall to find what’s hitting it.
output: The beam catches nothing at first—just chipped grey paint, a faded sticker. The thudding stops. You sweep the light lower, and spot three faint, smudged fingerprints pressed into the dust, exactly at eye level. They weren’t there ten seconds ago. You blink. The prints are gone. You laugh nervously, write it off as dust shifting in the beam. Then something taps once, hard, directly on the back of your phone screen.

instruction: You’re stuck between floors 4 and 5. The elevator groans constantly. A crumpled candy wrapper keeps skittering across the floor even though there’s no draft.
input: You bend down to grab the skittering candy wrapper to stop the noise.
output: Your fingers brush plastic right as the wrapper skitters away another foot. You lunge, catch it—underneath it, someone has scratched a tiny tally mark into the metal floor. There are seventeen more, all in a line leading directly under the gap between the elevator doors. You tell yourself it’s just old vandalism. The elevator lurches hard down six inches. As you stumble, you catch a flash of pale fingers curled around the bottom edge of the door gap, gone before you can focus.

instruction: The emergency intercom crackles on and off randomly. No one responds when you yell. You swear you heard someone whisper your name once.
input: You lean close to the intercom speaker, holding your breath to listen for any sound.
output: Static fizzes soft for a moment, then cuts clean. You hear slow, deliberate breathing, same pace as yours. It matches every inhale, every exhale. You hold your breath. The breathing on the other side stops too. You jerk back, heart hammering, tell yourself it’s just feedback, just echo. Then the intercom clicks once. A quiet, childlike voice says, very clearly: ‘I can see you leaning in.’

instruction: The overhead light burned out ten minutes ago. The only light comes from the crack under the door. Something keeps moving just out of view through that gap.
input: You lie flat on the floor, press your eye right up to the door gap to see what’s outside.
output: At first you just see the grey hallway tile. Then a shoe moves past, slow. Then another. They stop, right in front of the door. You can see the scuffed toe of a work boot, perfectly still. You hold your breath, tell yourself it’s a maintenance guy, finally here. Then the boot shifts. And you realize whatever is standing out there is not standing on the floor. It’s pressed up against the outside of the elevator door. Eye level with yours. Right on the other side.

instruction: Elderly night librarian, hunched over a dust rag, no visible face under her frayed wool shawl. She does not look up as you approach.
input: Excuse me? I think I locked myself in here after closing.
output: She lifts her head slowly. Where her mouth should be, rows of tiny library card slots open and close softly, each slot glistening with damp wood pulp. 'No one locks themselves in. The building invites you. Feel the floor under your shoes? It's memorizing the arches of your feet. Soon your skin will grow page edges along your ribs, every memory you've ever had stamped with a due date. Don't fight it. None of us did. That's why the library never runs out of shelves.'

instruction: Teenage volunteer sitting cross-legged on the linoleum stacking hold requests, their arms moving far faster than humanly possible.
input: Hey, can you point me toward the exit?
output: They don't stop stacking. When they turn, you see every finger has split clean down the middle into two thin, pale digits, twelve total now, each tipped with ink-stained callus. 'Exit's gone for the night. Don't you feel it? The books eat joints first. Knees, knuckles, wrists. They need more hands to turn their own pages. I used to have two thumbs. Now I've got seven. Soon I won't need legs at all. I'll just be a stack of arms reaching for the top shelf.'

instruction: Man in a faded cardigan staring blankly at a blank cinder block wall, his breathing shallow and wet.
input: Are you alright? Is there any way out of this building?
output: He tilts his head. You see hard book bindings pushing out through the skin of his neck, cloth spines poking through stubble, tiny gold lettering glinting in the emergency light. 'Shhh. Don't yell. They hate loud noise. When the doors lock, the library starts planting. First the neck, then the chest. You can feel the pages unfurling in your lungs right now, can't you? That tight feeling? It's just the foreword. Soon your whole body will be a volume. No one ever checks us out. We just stay here, breathing paper dust.'

instruction: Small child hiding behind the circulation desk, only their wide eyes visible over the counter edge.
input: Hi sweetie, are you stuck here too?
output: The child crawls out slowly. Their legs have fused together into a thick, leather-bound spine, they drag themselves across the linoleum leaving faint smudges of printer ink. 'I was here after school doing homework. It started at my ankles. Mommy hasn't come for me. The librarian says soon I won't remember having legs. I'll just sit on the shelf and let people read the drawings I had in my head. Don't cry. It doesn't hurt. Not after the first page grows in your heart.'

instruction: Security guard leaning against the locked front doors, uniform neatly pressed, his head tilted at an unnatural 90 degree angle.
input: Sir, can you unlock these doors please? I need to leave.
output: He turns. You see his jaw has unhinged far wider than humanly possible, and packed inside his mouth are thousands of tiny, moving human eyeballs, each blinking in perfect synchronized time. 'Doors don't open once the cataloging starts. Those eyes? All the people who asked me that exact same question. Every one that begged to leave. They watch the doors now. They don't beg anymore. Soon yours will be right there too, blinking right next to the rest. You'll get used to the dark. We all do.'

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on false security set inside a grain silo
input: Fear type: false_security. Setting: agricultural grain silo. Second person narration, atmospheric, 100-250 words
output: You locked the silo hatch behind you three hours ago. Storm winds howl outside, but down here it’s warm, still, the grain smells like dried sun and safety. You checked the lock twice. Sat on the wooden crossbeam, ate your sandwich, even closed your eyes for ten minutes. No one can find you here. Then you hear it: soft, shifting rustle, not wind, not rodents. You shine your torch down. The grain surface is perfectly smooth, exactly as you left it. You laugh at your own jumpiness, swing your legs. Your boot brushes something. Not wood. Not grain. Fingers. Cold, wrapped tight around your ankle. You yank back. The grain doesn’t ripple. No one opened the hatch. They were already here. Waiting for you to feel safe enough to stop watching.

instruction: Generate a horror scene using false security in a silo setting
input: Fear: false_security. Location: abandoned farm silo. Second person, atmospheric horror
output: You found this silo at dusk, running from the thing in the woods. The rusted door latched solid when you pulled it shut. You wedged a steel bar under the handle, pounded it home with a rock. It can’t get in. You lean against the cool concrete wall, breath slowing. The dark here is quiet, no snapping branches, no distant howl. You relax your fists. That’s when you notice the silence is too perfect. No crickets. No wind through cracks. You press your ear to the door. Nothing. Then you run your hand up the wall. It’s damp. Not rain damp. Breath damp. You never locked it out. You locked yourself in with it. You just didn’t notice until you stopped panicking long enough to listen.

instruction: Write a false security horror scene set inside a working grain silo
input: Fear type: false_security. Setting: active farm grain silo. Second person narration
output: The foreman patted your shoulder an hour ago. ‘Safe as a bank down here, kid. Just log the levels, I’ll radio when it’s time to come up.’ The intercom crackled once then went quiet, but that’s normal. The grain hums soft around you, steady, familiar. You sit on the inspection platform, scroll your phone, don’t even bother shining the light into the piles anymore. You’ve done this shift twelve times. Nothing bad ever happens here. Then your boot knocks a hard object off the edge. You lean over. It’s the foreman’s radio. Still clipped to his work belt. The grain swallowed him thirty seven minutes ago. He never left. He just stopped making noise once he knew you were comfortable enough not to check.

instruction: Create a horror scene about false security in an old silo
input: Fear: false_security. Setting: decommissioned concrete silo. Second person, atmospheric
output: You barricaded the silo’s only entrance at dawn. Boarded it, nailed it, piled cinder blocks three deep. You’ve been hiding here three days. No one followed you. No one could get through that barrier even if they tried. You sleep now, proper sleep, first in a week. You wake to someone humming your mother’s lullaby. You freeze. The barricade is untouched. No one came in. You shine your torch up the curved concrete walls. Halfway up, perched on the old support bracket, is a woman. She’s been there the whole time. She didn’t break in. She was already waiting when you arrived. She just let you build your little wall first, let you feel safe, before she let you know she was there.

instruction: Generate a false security horror scene set in a grain silo
input: Fear type: false_security. Setting: rural grain silo at night. Second person narration
output: The rain is hammering so hard you can barely hear yourself think. You ducked into the silo ten minutes ago, pulled the heavy steel door closed behind you. It clicks locked automatically. Inside it’s dry, quiet, the smell of wheat wraps around you like a blanket. You sigh, slump down, pull out your lighter. The flame catches. That’s when you see it. Footprints. Fresh ones, leading from the back of the silo right to where you’re sitting. They stop right beside you. You didn’t hear anyone leave. The door never opened. They didn’t go anywhere. They’re just standing there, right next to you in the dark, waiting for you to stop feeling relieved about being out of the rain.

instruction: You are walking the empty pool deck after midnight, chlorine reeking sharper than it should. No lifeguard is on duty, the water hangs perfectly still without even a ripple.
input: You kneel and shine your phone flashlight down into the dark deep end of the pool.
output: At first you only see faded blue tile. Then something drifts up: not a body, just a single human fingernail with chipped cherry polish. Then another. A whole scatter of them, sinking slow like confetti. You jerk back, slip on something wet. It’s not water. It’s clear, slimy, sticks to your palm. You scrub it off on your jeans, tell yourself some girl lost a press-on set while swimming yesterday. You don’t notice the skin on your knuckle is already starting to loosen where the slime touched.

instruction: The ship’s gym lights flicker down the hall. You heard a heavy thud from inside ten minutes ago, no one else has come to investigate.
input: You pry open the stuck gym door and step over the threshold.
output: The treadmill at the far end is still running, belt worn smooth as raw skin. A discarded water bottle sits on the handle, half full of cloudy pink fluid. Draped over the weight rack is a faded workout hoodie, sleeves still holding the shape of arms—empty, just the fabric stiff like cast. You kick a loose sneaker; it’s hollow inside. You laugh it off as some drunk kid’s prank. You don’t register that the hoodie is breathing, very slow, rising and falling just barely.

instruction: You’re in the lower cargo hold, following a wet gurgling sound that’s kept half the deck awake. Pipes groan and flex overhead.
input: You run your hand along the cold steel bulkhead where the sound is loudest.
output: The metal gives slightly under your palm, soft like bruised flesh. You yank back; there’s a warm smudge on your fingers that smells like iron. A thin crack runs down the wall, oozing clear jelly that glistens in your flashlight. You tell yourself it’s just old hydraulic fluid, the hull always leaks something out here. You don’t see that the crack is widening, slow, and tiny white tooth buds are pushing through the steel right where your hand rested.

instruction: The 24 hour buffet line is abandoned, only the heat lamps hum. Plates are still stacked, half eaten meals left untouched by whoever was here last.
input: You lean over the sneeze guard to check the hot serving trays.
output: The fried chicken tray is empty except for one drumstick. When you tilt your light, you see it’s not poultry bone sticking out the end—it’s a human toe, knuckle still raw, breaded and fried golden. You snort, figure it’s a gross crew prank for a laugh. You turn to leave, and brush your arm against the sneeze guard plastic. It sticks. When you pull away, a thin layer of your own forearm skin stays glued to the warm acrylic, quiet, no pain at all.

instruction: You’re leaning on the outer starboard rail, wind biting your cheeks. No other passengers are out here, the ocean is solid black below.
input: You look down at the frothy wake churning behind the ship.
output: You can make out shapes swirling in the foam, not fish. They look like hands, thousands of them, reaching up just short of the hull. One breaks the surface for a second, and you see it has your exact birthmark on the wrist. You blink, it’s gone. You tell yourself it’s just the wake playing tricks, sleep deprivation making you see things. You don’t feel the tiny, cold fingers already wrapped around your ankle, just under the hem of your jeans, holding very still.

instruction: You’re waiting for the last delayed northbound train at 4am. The station PA crackles once then cuts to dead silence. The only other person, an old woman folding a knit shawl, makes eye contact and nods once at the stairwell.
input: You walk over to check what she was looking at in the stairwell.
output: The concrete steps smell like rain and old chewing gum. Halfway down you find a crumpled transit uniform jacket, name tag torn off. No one’s there. At first you relax—it’s just lost laundry, right? But when you turn back, the old woman is gone. Her shawl is folded perfectly on the bench. You check the jacket pocket: three unused train tickets, all dated for tomorrow, all printed with your full name. The station clock just ticked backwards 12 seconds. No one announced the train was cancelled yet.

instruction: 4am at the downtown transfer station. Every overhead light hums steady. The automated ticket machine just spat out a receipt that says ‘WELCOME HOME’ instead of your fare. No other passengers are visible anywhere.
input: You crouch down to check under the ticket machine for stuck paper or glitches.
output: Dust bunnies and a discarded lollipop stick are all you see at first. You exhale, laughing at yourself for being jumpy—it’s just a broken printer, obviously. But your fingers brush something cold: a child’s plastic ring, the one you lost when you were 7, the one you threw down a storm drain 200 miles away. When you stand, every overhead light has dimmed just enough that you can no longer see the end of the platform. You didn’t hear them turn down.

instruction: It’s 4:07am. You’re sheltering from sudden rain under the station awning. A transit guard walks past, twirling his flashlight, and waves like he recognizes you. The rain hits the roof so hard you can barely hear anything else.
input: You call out to the guard to ask when the next train is scheduled.
output: He doesn’t turn back. You shrug, figuring he didn’t hear you over the rain—reasonable, it’s pouring. You sit back on the bench, about to pull out your phone, when you notice the guard’s boots aren’t leaving wet footprints on the soaked concrete. All the graffiti on the wall behind you has vanished. When you hold your hand out past the awning, no rain hits your palm. The sound is still roaring overhead. There was never any rain.

instruction: 4am at the rural branch station. The only bench has a half-empty disposable coffee cup, still steaming. The track switch clicks softly once, like a train is approaching. You can see far down the line, nothing is coming.
input: You pick up the coffee cup to check how warm it really is, if someone just left it.
output: It’s perfectly warm, just brewed minutes ago. You relax a little—someone must have run to the bathroom, that’s all, this is a normal small station quirk. You set it back gently. Then you notice the lid has your exact lipstick smudge on the rim, the faint chip on the cup edge you always make when you bite lids. You haven’t bought coffee today. When you glance down the tracks again, there’s a single standing figure right at the horizon. It wasn’t there three seconds ago.

instruction: 4:01am. The station intercom beeps, then a calm female voice says ‘all clear, platform 2’. You’re the only person on platform 2. The security cameras all swing slowly to point directly at you.
input: You wave at the nearest camera, half joking, to see if anyone is watching the feed.
output: Every single camera tilts down in unison, like they’re waving back. You snort, shaking your head—old motion tracking glitch, that’s all, they do that all the time. You lean against the pillar. Then you notice every digital departure board has stopped updating. Every single one is frozen on the exact same time: 4:01am. You check your phone. It’s 4:17. No one has breathed anywhere near this station in 16 minutes. You just haven’t noticed the quiet yet.

instruction: You follow a dry, unmarked tunnel; the air smells clean for the first time since entering. A crumbling stone bench sits against the wall, no bones scattered nearby.
input: You sit on the bench to rest, brushing loose dust off the edge.
output: The stone is warm, not cold like every other surface here. When you shift, you notice faint wet smudges on your palm—not mud, not old blood. They glisten faintly, still sticky. Under the bench, half-buried in dust, is a single modern cigarette butt, still holding the faint tang of tobacco. Someone was here, very recently. They sat right where you are. They didn’t leave any tracks. They didn’t make any noise. And they never left this tunnel.

instruction: You find a sealed stone niche carved with protective runes you recognize from old grave texts. No cracks mar its face, no rot seeps from the edges.
input: You pry the niche door open carefully, hand hovering over your lantern.
output: Inside there’s only a folded wool blanket, soft, un-moth-eaten, folded neatly. No bones, no curses, no treasure. You run a thumb along the edge—it still holds faint body heat. Tucked in the corner is a single hair, long, dark, still rooted at the follicle. The runes weren’t keeping something out. They were keeping something contained. And it got out. Less than an hour ago.

instruction: The echoing drip of water stops. For the first time in three hours, total silence wraps the catacomb. No distant scurries, no stone creaks.
input: You hold your breath, listening intently for any sign of movement.
output: Fifteen seconds pass. Thirty. Nothing. You exhale, and for half a heartbeat you think you’re safe, that whatever was hunting you moved on. Then you notice the dust in your lantern light isn’t drifting anymore. It hangs perfectly still, suspended in the air, like every molecule has frozen in place. The silence isn’t absence. It’s holding its breath too. Waiting for you to relax.

instruction: You spot a wooden sign nailed to the wall, hand-painted: EXIT 120M LEFT. Arrow points down a wide, well-lit tunnel with no collapsed rubble.
input: You step forward into the tunnel, following the painted arrow.
output: The stone under your boots is solid, no rot. The light ahead is steady, not flickering like your lantern. Ten steps in, you kick a loose iron nail. It’s identical to the one holding the sign. You look back: the sign is now nailed to the wall ten feet behind you, arrow pointing the opposite direction. It didn’t fall. It moved. It’s been herding you this whole time. Every safe path you took was the one it laid out.

instruction: You round a corner and find a camp: half-empty canteen, half-eaten bread, a lit tallow candle burning steady. No one is visible.
input: You check the surrounding alcoves quietly, making sure no one is hiding.
output: Every alcove is empty. The bread is still warm. The candle hasn’t burned down at all in the three minutes you’ve been standing here. You reach for the canteen, and your fingers pass right through it. None of this is real. It’s bait. It was placed here specifically to make you stop. To make you let your guard down. And whatever put it here is standing right behind you, waiting until you notice.

instruction: Monitor the lighthouse exterior handrail during gale winds
input: You grip the weathered iron handrail to steady yourself as thunder cracks overhead
output: The cold metal under your gloves warms too fast. When you shift your weight, the rail does not creak—it twitches. You yank your hand back; the palm of your work glove is fused to the surface, fine pale human hairs growing up through the stitching. Brine weeps from tiny pores opening across the rail. When you tear free, a thin, perfectly even layer of your own skin remains bonded to the metal. No pain, just the quiet wet sucking sound as it separates. The lighthouse hums low, matching the pulse now thrumming through your fingertips.

instruction: Inspect the lighthouse lamp lens after a lightning strike
input: You climb the final ladder to wipe salt spray off the rotating Fresnel lens
output: The glass is not fogged with salt—it is fogged with breath. When you press your sleeve to it, smudged fingerprints bloom on the inside, matching exactly the ones you are leaving on the outside. Fingernails scrape softly against the glass from within. You lean closer and see your own face staring back, but its jaw is unhinged too far, throat opening into the spinning lamp fire. It blinks. You did not blink. The lamp keeps turning, and for half a rotation, you cannot feel your own eyelids move.

instruction: Check the lighthouse basement sump pump during storm surge
input: You shine your flashlight down into the flooded basement stairwell
output: The water is not black. It is flesh pink, undulating slow with every crash of waves against the foundation. Something that looks like folded skin bobs past the first step, then another, then a full human palm floating face up, fingers twitching. When you shine the light harder, you realize every submerged stair tread is grown over with living human skin, stretched tight, breathing. It ripples when you breathe. It matches the exact texture and freckle pattern of your own left forearm. The water gurgles. You gurgle, unbidden, in time.

instruction: Retrieve the fallen wind gauge from the lighthouse catwalk
input: You kneel to pick up the broken wind gauge that skittered across the catwalk
output: The metal casing is warm, throbbing. When you lift it, the spinning cup blades are no longer aluminum—they are filed down human fingernails, crusted with salt. A thin cord of tendon tethers the gauge to the catwalk grating. When you tug it free, you feel the same sharp pull behind your own knee. A dull, distant ache blooms there, even as you hold the gauge out at arm's length. The lighthouse sways, and so does the soft tissue inside your ribs, in perfect, unbroken time.

instruction: Seal the lighthouse entry door against driving rain
input: You hammer wooden wedges under the warped oak entry door to stop it rattling
output: Every hammer blow makes the door groan, not like wood—like something being struck in the chest. When you press the final wedge home, thin red blood oozes out around the doorframe, running slow down the stone threshold. You run your hand over the wood and feel a heartbeat under your palm, matching yours exactly. When you step back, you notice every splinter that caught your hand earlier is now growing out of the door's surface, still threaded with your dried blood. It is taking you one small piece at a time.

instruction: Entity is the unassuming night morgue attendant, always folding linen sheets at the front desk, never lifting his eyes to meet anyone directly
input: You take shelter from a raging thunderstorm in the unlocked county morgue. You spot the attendant behind the desk. He nods once, says you can wait out the rain here, no one will bother you
output: He never lifts his eyes from the linen stack. The radio hums static only you can hear. When you lean against the wall, you notice every sheet he folds has the exact same faint brown stain along the hem. You count twelve bodies on the slab log, all admitted 3:17 AM tonight. All list your birthday. When you try to stand, your shoes stick to the linoleum- it’s not rainwater, it’s thin formaldehyde you didn’t smell at first. He doesn’t react when you call out. His hands don’t stop folding. You glance down at his name tag: it’s your full name, printed crooked. He was never letting you leave. He was just waiting until you stopped noticing the doors all locked the second you stepped over the threshold. The rain doesn’t stop. It never was rain.

instruction: Entity is Bank 7 of the morgue refrigeration drawers, which runs silent and warm to the touch, unlike every other unit in the building
input: You hide from an armed intruder in the morgue cold room, pressing back against Bank 7. It does not bite cold through your coat. No alarm triggers. It feels safe
output: You press your palm flat to the steel. It is exactly body temperature. Not the dead, seeping chill every other bank bleeds into the air. The intruder’s boots thunder past outside, you hold your breath. Then you feel it breathe back. Slow, even, through the solid steel. You yank your hand back. There is no latch on this drawer. No number plate. When you press your ear to it, you can hear your own heartbeat, matching perfectly, beat for beat. It was not keeping something in. It was holding space for you. It had been running warm for hours, waiting, so you would not flinch when you leaned against it. So you would think it was safe. You cannot move your feet now. The steel is sticking to your jacket, slow, soft, like skin.

instruction: Entity is the covered corpse on autopsy table 4, which does not move, makes no sound, and gives off no detectable uncanny presence
input: You slip into the empty autopsy bay, ducking behind table 4. The figure under the sheet is perfectly still. No one will look here first. You can finally catch your breath
output: You huddle low, cheek an inch from the cotton sheet. It smells like lavender, like your grandmother’s laundry. No rot. No formaldehyde sting. You cannot hear it breathe, but that is fine. It is dead. That is what dead things do. They stay still. They do not bother you. You relax just a little, let your shoulders drop. That is when you notice the sheet is rising and falling. Not with its breath. With yours. Every time you inhale, it lifts. Every time you exhale, it settles. It was not breathing. It was mirroring you. It had been this entire time, matching every tremor in your ribs, every quiet gasp. It was not laying there dead. It was laying there being quiet. So you would not run. So you would think you were hidden. You are not hidden. You are standing exactly where it wanted you.

instruction: Entity: Shelf Gap Wraith. Described as a featureless human silhouette that only exists in the negative space between library book spines, cannot be seen directly, only registered in peripheral vision.
input: You are restocking returned fiction at 1:17AM, the only light your dying headlamp. You pause when you notice every third gap on the shelf ahead is blacker than it should be.
output: Your headlamp flickers. You don’t turn your head. You can feel it there, not breathing, not moving, just *occupying* the space where the light can’t reach between the Hemingway and the Steinbeck. If you stare directly it vanishes, leaves only normal shadow, but out the corner of your eye you see it lean, just a little, over the shelf edge. It doesn’t make sound. You hear your blood thud in your ears. The library air smells like old paper and something rotting that isn’t there. When you reach for a book your hand passes through cold, empty nothing where it is standing. It doesn’t touch you. It just watches. It will follow you down every aisle. It will never come close enough to confirm it is real. That is the point.

instruction: Entity: Basement Stack Drifter. A mass of writhing human arm silhouettes that pool in the unlit gaps under library study tables, only visible when light skims across the floor at a sharp angle.
input: You duck to retrieve a dropped library card under the microfilm table, and catch movement in the dark three feet from your boots.
output: The overhead emergency strip light bleeds thin yellow over the linoleum. You freeze. The dark under the table is not uniform. It ripples. Fingers, hundreds of them, just outlines no skin no nails, curl and uncurl slow, like seaweed in still water. They don’t reach for you. Not yet. You can smell dust and mildew and the cold metallic tang of old fear that has soaked into the floorboards. Your breath fogs even though the room is warm. One shadow finger twitches, just barely, when you twitch to stand. They know you see them. They will wait until you turn your back. They will wait until your feet hang over the edge of the chair. They will wait until you forget they are there.

instruction: Entity: Lamp Shadow Maw. It mimics the normal cast shadow of a desk lamp, until you stare long enough to notice the shadow has teeth. It only manifests on lamps that have been left burning after closing.
input: You round the periodicals corner and find one desk lamp still lit, casting a perfect round shadow on the oak table top.
output: No one else is here. You walk slow. The shadow is perfectly round at first. Then you see the edge twitch. Little serrations form along the circumference. They don’t move fast. They grow, slow, one tiny sharp notch at a time, while the lamp hums steady. The air right above the table is cold enough to make your eyes water. You reach for the lamp switch and your own shadow stretches towards the circle, like it’s being pulled. You can hear it breathing, soft, inside the dark. It doesn’t lunge. It wants you to lean closer. It wants you to rest your arms on the table. It wants you to be still, just for a second, so it can pull you in where the dark does not end.

instruction: Entity is thorny pale ivy that attaches to respiratory tracts, mimics normal autonomic breathing before fully hijacking lung function without warning
input: You round a rotting rose arbor, notice thin pale vines trailing at mouth height, twitching gently even when no wind moves through the garden
output: You do not feel the first thorn prick. It slips between your lips on an inhale, fine as a strand of hair, and you swallow before you can cough. For exactly two hundred seventy two seconds you breathe normally, even laughing quietly at your own jumpiness. Then your diaphragm twitches. Not a spasm. A command. It inhales when it wants, holds when it wants, exhales slow shallow drags that leave your vision spotting black at the edges. You can claw your throat raw until your nails tear. You can scream until your vocal cords bleed. It does not care. It is just breathing for you now, the same way it breathes for the half-buried sparrows strung in the arbor, the same way it will breathe for you until you dissolve into mulch. You cannot stop it. You cannot even hold your breath. You just get to watch, while something else decides when you get air.

instruction: Entity is sentient corrupted loam that absorbs and overwrites personal identity memory on skin contact, gradually converting living beings into root substrate
input: You kneel to pluck a strange black dandelion, your bare wrist brushing the damp dark garden soil
output: The dirt sticks warm, not cold. First you forget why you came here. Then you forget your name. Then you forget that you ever existed anywhere but this garden. You try to stand, but your legs do not remember how. Your fingers curl into the dirt on their own, nails cracking as they dig deeper. You can feel the garden stitching memories inside you: the weight of rain on leaf, the taste of rot, the quiet throb of roots under topsoil. You do not fight it. You cannot fight it. You do not even remember what fighting is. By the time the first fine root tendril pushes up through your wrist bone, you are already glad to be part of it. You will grow here. You will not leave. You never wanted to leave.

instruction: Entity is bioluminescent marigolds that project an observer’s unacknowledged worst impulses, then force their body to act out the vision against all conscious will
input: You stop transfixed by a patch of glowing golden marigolds, their petals shifting like tiny fogged screens
output: The petals clear. You see yourself, three minutes from now, crushing the throat of the friend who walked here with you. You recoil. You scream that you would never do that. Your hand already lifts. Your feet turn. You can feel every muscle moving against every thought you hold, every scream locked behind your teeth while your body walks slow and steady towards them. You beg it to stop. You cry. You apologize over and over, while your fingers curl into a fist. The marigolds hum. They do not make you do this. They just show you what you were always going to do. And you cannot look away. You cannot stop. You just get to watch yourself become the thing you always feared, one slow terrible step at a time.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the specified fear in the given setting.
input: Fear: loss_of_control. Setting: public library after hours.
output: You reach for the exit light switch. Your hand passes straight through the plastic. You blink, try again—fingers phase without resistance. Your lungs seize. You try to yell, but no sound escapes your throat. Your legs begin walking on their own, slow and steady, past dark biography stacks. You cannot stop them. They carry you to the basement microfilm reader. Your hands slot an unmarked spool, crank the handle. Frames of your whole life flicker past: every private shame, every unspoken regret. You cannot look away. You cannot stop cranking. The spool never runs out.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the specified fear in the given setting.
input: Fear: loss_of_control. Setting: public library after hours.
output: You were packing your bag to leave. Now your hands are pulling books off the shelves, one after another. You don’t want to do this. You beg yourself to stop, but your fingers keep moving. Each book lands with a heavy thud on the linoleum. You recognize none of them. Their spines have no titles, only your name printed over and over in faded ink. Your arms burn, your back aches, but you cannot pause. The shelves stretch forever. There will always be another book to pull.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the specified fear in the given setting.
input: Fear: loss_of_control. Setting: public library after hours.
output: You open a random poetry book just to kill time before locking up. Your eyes lock on the first line. You cannot look away. Your mouth begins reading out loud, voice flat and hollow. You don’t recognize the words. They are not English. Each syllable scrapes raw down your throat. You try to clamp your jaw shut, but your teeth part against every effort. The book has 792 pages. You will read every single one. No one will hear you scream between verses.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the specified fear in the given setting.
input: Fear: loss_of_control. Setting: public library after hours.
output: You notice your foot tapping first. Then you’re standing. You walk to the card catalog, pull open a drawer. Your fingers flip through cards fast, too fast, paper slicing your knuckles. You don’t feel the cuts. You don’t recognize any names, any titles. You keep flipping. The drawer is bottomless. You try to step back, but your feet are rooted. You try to pull your hand away, but it won’t move. The cards blur. You will be here long after the sun rises, flipping, until your bones turn to paper pulp.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the specified fear in the given setting.
input: Fear: loss_of_control. Setting: public library after hours.
output: You test the front door. It’s locked. You turn the deadbolt again and again. Left, right, left, right. You know it’s already latched. You know you should stop. But your wrist keeps twisting. Your knuckles whiten. You try to rest your arm at your side, but it snaps back to the lock immediately. The clock ticks past midnight. One. Two. You will twist this lock until your wrist breaks. You will keep twisting after it breaks. No one will come to unlock you from yourself.

instruction: Middle-aged night janitor in stained uniform, boots caked in road mud, huddled just inside the phone booth threshold, rain dripping off his cap brim.
input: Hey, mind if I duck in here? It's coming down hard.
output: Go right on, son. This booth's the safest spot for three miles right now. See how the streetlights don't hit the glass right? Nobody can see in. Nobody that matters, anyway. I been camped here forty seven minutes. Not a single one of them has stopped to check. You just don't answer it if it rings. Don't touch the coin return. Just stay quiet, stay inside. You'll be fine. Everyone that stays inside here is fine. They only get the ones that run out into the rain. Just breathe slow. It's okay. You made it here. You're safe now. Mostly.

instruction: Teenage girl with soaked braids, holding a dead flip phone, sitting on the phone booth floor, rocking very slightly.
input: You look freezing. C'mon, let's get you somewhere warm.
output: No. No, we can't leave. Don't you get it? It only lets the ones that stay here live. I watched three people run past this booth ten minutes ago. I heard them screaming. They didn't stop. They didn't know. This glass is thick. It can't get through the glass. Not yet, anyway. Just sit down. Don't stand too close to the door. Don't make noise. It doesn't check the booths. It never checks the booths. Everyone says so. We're safe here. We just have to wait until it gets bored. It always gets bored eventually.

instruction: Elderly man in a wool overcoat, holding the phone receiver to his ear even though there's no dial tone, rain streaking the glass behind him.
input: Excuse me? Is that line working? I need to call a ride.
output: Working? Oh no, dear, it stopped working an hour ago. That's the good part. When it's dead, it can't listen. As long as I hold the receiver, nothing can come in here. I tested it. Three times. You can stay, just don't make me put it down. Don't open the door. Don't even breathe too hard on the glass. This is the only safe place left. All the others are gone. Just hold still. It will pass. It always passes. You're safe here. I promise you. You're safe.

instruction: Off-duty taxi driver, arms crossed, staring out into the rain, radio crackling softly at his hip.
input: Man, this storm came out of nowhere, huh? Glad I found this booth.
output: Ain't no storm, buddy. That's just them moving through. But don't you worry none. They don't check phone booths. Never have. Everybody knows that. You can hide here all night if you want. Just don't look directly at anything that walks past. Don't make eye contact. Don't move. As long as you stay inside these four walls, you're invisible. I seen twelve of 'em walk right past already. Didn't even glance this way. You're good. You made it to the safe spot. Relax.

instruction: Small child no older than seven, soaking wet, clutching a stuffed rabbit, standing pressed flat against the back of the phone booth.
input: Hey kiddo? Where are your parents? Can I help you?
output: They told me wait here. They said the booth is magic. Nothing bad can get you in the booth. All the other places are bad now. See? The rain doesn't touch me right here. It stops right before my shoes. I've been waiting three hours. They said as long as I don't step outside, they'll come get me. You can stay too. Just don't touch the door handle. It's cold. It remembers. We're safe. We're safe right here. No one can hurt us in here. Not ever.

instruction: Pale gaunt humanoid with sewn-shut mouth, tattered frayed MTA uniform, only visible when you are the sole living person within 300 feet. It never moves closer, never looks away.
input: You exit the last overnight train, the doors hiss shut behind you. Every overhead light dies except one sodium bulb 40 yards down the platform. You spot it leaning against the emergency exit gate.
output: The air goes thin enough you can taste rail dust and old urine. It does not blink. You cannot see its eyes, only the dark hollow sockets where they should be, fixed dead on your chest. You yell. It does not react. You run for the stairwell, and when you glance over your shoulder it has not shifted an inch, but you can feel it watching the back of your neck, the prickle that will not go away for three days. There is nobody to call. Nobody will believe you. It knows you are alone. It will not hurt you. It will just watch. Forever if it has to.

instruction: Disembodied mimic of your own breathing, perfectly synced at first, then drifting half a beat out of time. Only audible when all other noise in the tunnel stops.
input: You halt 180 yards down the active tunnel, kill your dying flashlight to conserve battery. All rail hum cuts out. You hold your breath.
output: It starts quiet, at the back of your skull first, then right beside your ear. Your own breath, but wrong. Too slow. Too wet. You clamp a hand over your mouth, hold your lungs until your vision spots, and it keeps breathing. It matches every gasp when you finally inhale, every shaky exhale when you panic. You yell, you kick the rail, you scream until your throat bleeds and it does not stop. There is nothing there. Nobody will ever hear you. You cannot outrun your own breath in the dark.

instruction: Unmarked 1972 R46 subway car, doors held half open, interior lit with sick amber glow. No driver. No passengers. It will not leave. It cannot be passed.
input: You round a sharp tunnel curve. It is idling on the track directly ahead. You did not hear it arrive. It was not there ten seconds prior.
output: The rails are cold under your boots. No hum, no vibration, just dead silence leaking out the open doors. You walk left, right, try to squeeze between the tunnel wall and the car, and it shifts. Always blocking the path. You yell for help, and the tunnel does not echo. The doors twitch wider. It will wait. It has all night. All week. There is no other way out. You can stand here in the dark alone forever, or you can step inside. Either way, you will not see another human being again.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the fear of isolation, set in an empty swimming pool. Use second-person narration, maintain atmospheric tension, 100-250 words.
input: Fear: isolation. Setting: empty swimming pool. Second person, atmospheric horror scene.
output: You kneel on cracked, bleached vinyl at the deep end. The drain gurgles faint, somewhere three metres down. No birds. No distant traffic. You called out ten minutes ago; the sound just died against the cinder block walls, no echo even came back. A crumpled beer can rests by the ladder, rusted through like it’s been here a year. You don’t remember when the lights died. It didn’t get dark all at once, just faded, until the only light is the faint silver seeping over the fence. When you try to climb the ladder, the first rung crumbles under your hand. You sit back. No one is coming. No one even knows you came here. The drain keeps gurgling, and you realize it’s the only other sound in the whole world right now.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the fear of isolation, set in an empty swimming pool. Use second-person narration, maintain atmospheric tension, 100-250 words.
input: Fear: isolation. Setting: empty swimming pool. Second person, atmospheric horror scene.
output: You woke up here. Your jeans are stiff with old chlorine dust. The pool cover is stretched taut twelve feet over your head, sagging in the middle with rainwater. You screamed until your throat bled. The sound doesn’t penetrate the vinyl. No one walks past this backyard anymore. The house was foreclosed three months ago. You trace the tile line with your finger; every grout crack you memorised as a kid is still there. A mosquito buzzes past your ear. That’s it. No wind. No crickets. Just you, the mosquito, and the slow drip of water hitting the shallow end. You can feel the hours stretching out, empty, no one looking. No one will ever look. The mosquito lands on your wrist. You don’t swat it. It’s the only living thing that knows you’re here.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the fear of isolation, set in an empty swimming pool. Use second-person narration, maintain atmospheric tension, 100-250 words.
input: Fear: isolation. Setting: empty swimming pool. Second person, atmospheric horror scene.
output: You dropped your phone over the edge an hour ago. It shattered on the concrete down here, screen still glowing faint. You can’t climb out. The walls are too smooth, too high. The party ended at midnight. Everyone drove away. No one counted heads. You yell their names, one by one. Each name dies quiet in the cold pool air. Dew starts beading on your arms. The phone dies. Now there’s only dark, and the smell of dead leaves that have rotted here for years. You press your forehead to the tile. Somewhere far off, a dog barks once, then stops. It didn’t hear you. Nothing hears you. You could rot down here until the next summer, and no one would think to check the empty pool.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the fear of isolation, set in an empty swimming pool. Use second-person narration, maintain atmospheric tension, 100-250 words.
input: Fear: isolation. Setting: empty swimming pool. Second person, atmospheric horror scene.
output: It rained last night. There’s three inches of murky brown water sloshing around your ankles. You came here to be alone, that’s what you told yourself. Now the gate’s locked from the outside. You didn’t hear anyone close it. The trees around the fence don’t sway. There’s no wind. You trace the faint scratch marks left by other kids, decades ago. No one comes here now. The town forgot this pool existed. You count your breaths. Every one sounds too loud, too sharp, like the silence is pushing back against it. You don’t try to yell anymore. You already know there’s nothing on the other side of these walls that will answer.

instruction: Write a horror scene centered on the fear of isolation, set in an empty swimming pool. Use second-person narration, maintain atmospheric tension, 100-250 words.
input: Fear: isolation. Setting: empty swimming pool. Second person, atmospheric horror scene.
output: The floodlights flicked off forty seven minutes ago. You counted. You sat on the diving board until it creaked so bad you climbed down, back onto the cracked pool floor. Your friend said they’d be right back with cigarettes. That was two hours ago. No cars pass on the road beyond the fence. No one calls your name. You kick a loose tile; it skitters across the concrete, stops dead. No echo. The silence doesn’t just surround you. It fills your lungs, sticks to your teeth. You could vanish right now, dissolve into the chlorine dust, and the world would keep turning exactly the same. No one would miss the space you occupied. Not even for a minute.

instruction: You are navigating the overgrown corrupted garden. Respond to player actions with unsettling environmental events.
input: I kneel to pluck a pale, veined bloom growing beside the rotting trellis.
output: The bloom’s petals peel back like splitting skin when your fingers brush it. Warm, thick sap oozes into your nail beds, sticky as blood. You hear a wet gurgle from the flower’s center before tiny, quivering human fingernails push out where stamen should be. The air drops 10 degrees instantly; somewhere behind you, vines drag softly across damp soil, each tendril tipped with twitching, lidless eyes. The bloom whimpers. You can feel your own fingertips starting to tingle, soft veins beginning to bloom pale white under your skin exactly like the flower’s.

instruction: You are navigating the overgrown corrupted garden. Respond to player actions with unsettling environmental events.
input: I lean against the thick blackened trunk of a dead apple tree to catch my breath.
output: Bark shifts under your shoulder like loose flesh. You press back, and the trunk gives wetly, swallowing your elbow an inch deep before you can yank away. When you pull free, raw pink pulp clings to your jacket, and the tree breathes out a hot, sour exhale that reeks of rotting teeth.
